### Raiken3712 — 2019-11-01T04:32:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycloset-and-fbg-update-oh-my.2927/post-281657>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=85166)
>
> Story from 1990's
> Publication number US5635512 A
> Publication type Grant
> Application number 08/460,858
> Publication date Jun 3, 1997
> Filing date Jun 5, 1995
>
> That was before Cycloset.
> \--------------------------------------------
>
> \=============================================
>
>
> [http://www.google.com/patents/US5635512](http://www.google.com/patents/US5635512)
>
>
>
>
>
> \============================
>
>
> Claims
> Having described the invention, what is claimed is:
>
> 1\. A method for modifying or resetting the prolactin rhythm of an insulin insensitive or diabetic animal or human subject which comprises:
>
> (a) administering to said subject a prolactin-inhibiting compound once a day, at a predetermined time within a 24-hour period;
>
> and
>
> (b) additionally administering to said subject a prolactin-stimulating compound;
>
> wherein each compound is administered in an amount sufficient, and for a period of time sufficient to accomplish at least one of the following: decrease insulin resistance, decrease hyperglycemia, decrease hyperinsulemia in said subject, and increase in glucose tolerance.
>
> 2\. A method for modifying or resetting the prolactin rhythm of an insulin insensitive or diabetic animal or human subject comprising:
>
> (a) administering to said subject a **prolactin-inhibiting** compound daily at a predetermined time of day designed to cause the daytime prolactin bloodstream level of said subject to decrease, thereby approaching the low daytime prolactin level of a lean, insulin sensitive subject; and
>
> (b) administering to said subject a **prolactin stimulating compound daily** at a predetermined time of day designed to cause the nightime prolactin bloodstream level of said subject to increase thereby approaching the high nightime prolactin level of a lean, insulin sensitive subject;
>
> wherein each compound is administered in a dosage amount sufficient and for a period of time sufficient to achieve in said subject at least one of the following modifications in glucose metabolism: decrease in insulin resistance, reduction of hyperinsulinemia, improvement in glucose tolerance and reduction of hyperglycemia.
> \-------------------------
>
>
> there is more in article
>
>
>
> ..

Interesting....loving the information in that publication. One thing I noticed is this....

"Exemplary of prolactin stimulators are dopamine antagonists, e.g. metoclopramide, haloperidol, pimozide, phenothiazine, sulpiride. chlorpromazine and serotonin agonists, i.e. MAO inhibitors, e.g. pargyline, synthetic morphine analogs, e.g. methadone, antiemetics, e.g. metoclopramide, antipsychotics, e.g. sulpiride, estrogens and others, e.g. tryptophan and 5-hydroxy-tryptophan."

When it mentioned Serotonin Agonists I immediately thought of Cymbalta aka Duloxetine a medication I am taking for Depression. I wondered if I looked up Cymbalta if it would say that it increases prolactin and lo and behold it does. So that made me wonder should I try metoclopramide or one of the others.

I want to taper off Duloxetine as soon as possible with all the negative I've read about it on the Cymbalta Hurts Worse Facebook Group. I've heard you have to taper slowly though as quicker can cause large withdrawal effects.

I'm wondering if somewhere in this information is the answer to faster safer tapering.

Also I'm wondering if I should take a different dose of metoclopramide or other medication because I'm taking Duloxetine...or will the duloxetine itself work as a stand in...I'm on 90 mg a day.

Before I found all of this I've been wondering what the best time/times to take my Duloxetine is. I started at all night time according to the doctors prescription, but then tried to split it between three times during the day....morning...noon...and night.

Now with this information I'm wondering if all at night before bed makes the most sense since it increases prolactin at the appropriate time that way.

Also I was curious about something my family nurse practitioner said...she said it was ok to take it all at night because the medication is designed to work over the course of a full 24 hours....anyone have any information if that is true? I've read the medication is delayed release, but is it really that delayed...and is the dose well spread out if it is?

Also another concern I had....someone said something about sublingual dosing of Cycloset...wouldn't that be bad....? If the dosages are based on taking the capsule by mouth with first pass metabolism...wouldn't we be getting too much from sublingual?

I read first pass metabolism by the liver ends up causing 96% of the Cycloset to be excreted so normally you only get 4% of the tablet...if I were to dissolve it under my tongue...eep?

Also...I'm about to start taking Low Dose Naltrexone again...is that a concern in using these dopamine and serotonin agonists?

### Raiken3712 — 2019-11-05T21:30:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycloset-and-fbg-update-oh-my.2927/post-281860>

[@DrEttinger](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/22307/) [@Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) Would low dose Naltrexone work against me in using Cycloset and Metoclopramide? I was wondering because I read something that made it sound like Endorphins that are increased by taking LDN at night are increased the next day are related to neurotransmitters which is what Serotonin and Dopamine are.

Also I'm used to going to sleep at midnight-2 a.m. and waking up anywhere from 10 a.m. to 1 p.m. Would taking Cycloset when I wake up not be effective because of how wonky that schedule is?

Do I need to go to bed sooner/wake up sooner in order for it to work...should I set an alarm to wake up earlier and then go back to sleep?

I work from 4 p.m. till midnight most of the time recently so really not sure what I should do. What would you do if you had no choice, but to keep that schedule?

Thanks

### DrEttinger — 2019-11-06T00:38:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycloset-and-fbg-update-oh-my.2927/post-281866>

### JanSz — 2019-11-06T01:06:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycloset-and-fbg-update-oh-my.2927/post-281867>

> [DrEttinger said:](/goto/post?id=281866)

> Those are questions that would be taken up in a medical consultation, which would also include labs, food log, and lifestyle/symptom questionnaires.

I live in New Jersey. To the best of my knowledge scripts from a chiropractor are not acceptable here.
LabCorp also is charging very high prices for the simplest tests (because they charge insurance).
But I can buy a script from outfit selling scripts, then drive to Pensylvania and have blood drawn there, test results are e-mailed.
I have used successfully, (once) this outfit:
[https://www.mcssl.com/store/bltsystem/catalog/search?keyword=Homocysteine](https://www.mcssl.com/store/bltsystem/catalog/search?keyword=Homocysteine)
Prices for those scripts are rather reasonable, way cheaper than what they charge insurance.
Problem is, this outfit sells scripts for a limited number of tests.
They do not sell
potassium RBC
magnesium RBC
that I was interested in recently.

[@DrEttinger](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/22307/) how would you be able to help in arranging for testing, someone like me?

..

### DrEttinger — 2019-11-07T16:27:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycloset-and-fbg-update-oh-my.2927/post-281968>

$70 RBC potassium, draw fee $10 (credit card fee $4)

They do offer RBC magnesium. I rarely run RBC potassium, because recommend potassium to almost all of my patients, so they will be on it after their first visit. I have rarely found anyone who does not need some extra potassium.
[https://www.mcssl.com/store/bltsystem/electrolyte-testing/magnesium-rbc](https://www.mcssl.com/store/bltsystem/electrolyte-testing/magnesium-rbc)

Their pricing is the same as my pricing. This is as good as anyone is going to get.

### Raiken3712 — 2019-12-07T23:49:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycloset-and-fbg-update-oh-my.2927/post-283278>

[https://newsnetwork.mayoclinic.org/...rate-most-likely-caused-by-medical-condition/](https://newsnetwork.mayoclinic.org/discussion/elevated-heart-rate-most-likely-caused-by-medical-condition/)

Not sure of my resting pulse of 112 is the 4.375 mg of Bromocriptine I'm on or Anemia. My Serum Iron is around 65 when it should be around 130 from what I've read.

Is this concerning. I don't feel overly concerned about it as sometimes I've seen my pulse in the 70s, and I want the reset of my circadian system though maybe I should just get that by getting morning sun and going to bed at night instead....

I started walking and getting sunlight pretty consistently and I feel quite a bit better when I go out and do that.

Maybe I should just stop the Bromocriptine...even if the 112 pulse isn't dangerous maybe it's not doing anything for me but causing a high pulse.

[@Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/)

By the way I currently work 4-12 or 2-10:30 most days so earliest bed time would be 11 to 12:30 depending on the day. Guess I should go to bed as soon as I get home.

# Thread: 23andme mutations
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/23andme-mutations.2928/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-08-09T04:29:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/23andme-mutations.2928/post-43802>

I've seen a couple of you on here mention your 23andme mutations. Can you direct me to where to find those and start researching what they mean? I've not seen this! Perhaps I'm not a mutant?

### freshveggies — 2012-08-09T17:47:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/23andme-mutations.2928/post-43899>

Here is a table someone has made to convert the 23 and me snips to the Yasko snips that she uses to make her methylation protocol.

[https://docs.google.com/spreadsheet/ccc?key=0Ar76dNWyEQLIdExKZUhfXzFUWHpkWnZObk1TM1dxX0E&hl=en#gid=0](http://https://docs.google.com/spreadsheet/ccc?key=0Ar76dNWyEQLIdExKZUhfXzFUWHpkWnZObk1TM1dxX0E&hl=en#gid=0)

Use the raw data browser at the upper right corner of the 23 and me and then copy and paste each snip of line 2 of the table in the the raw data brower and it will give you a gene altell. ( I think that is what it is called.)

Then you look at the 3rd line of the gene you just took from the table and read what it says about + & - A=T & C=G

Under D it says for gene CBS C699T +A-G---if you are GG you would be -/- If you are AG you would be +/- if it said TT you would be +/+, and so on. A is not always + and Gis not alwasy -. You need to look at the table to determine which each means. If you have any questions, feel free to post them.

this graph was also helpful.

\# rsid gene snp genotype positive genotype negative

rs1801181 CBS A360A AT GC

rs234706 CBS Y233Y (C699T) AT GC

rs4633 COMT H62H AT GC

rs4680 COMT V158M AT GC

rs769224 COMT 61 P199P AT GC

rs6323 MAO A R297R AT GC

rs1801133 MTHFR A222V (C677T) AT GC

rs1801131 MTHFR E429A (A1298C) GC AT

rs2066470 MTHFR 03 P39P AT GC

rs1805087 MTR A919G (A2756G) GC AT

rs10380 MTRR H595Y AT GC

rs162036 MTRR K350A GC AT

rs2287780 MTRR R415T AT GC

rs1801394 MTRR A919G (A66G) GC AT

rs1802059 MTRR 11 A664A AT GC

rs1544410 VDR BSM/TAQ AT GC

rs3741049 ACAT1 0 N/A AT GC

rs819147 AHCY 01 N/A GC AT

rs819171 AHCY 19 N/A GC AT

rs567754 BHMT 02 N/A AT GC

rs617219 BHMT 04 N/A GC AT

rs651852 BHMT 08 N/A AT GC

rs1979277 SHMT C1420T AT GC

I cant keep this graph lined up properly when paste it in.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-09T15:24:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tonights-webinar.2932/post-43873>

If you are not one th emailing list you will never hear about these offers. Many of the people who signed up never even read the email's instructions........I cant solve that problem. Several people signed up and then cancelled and never realized all the parts they were getting........and now they want back in.......I strongly suggest you read before your decide. This deal was probably one of the best ones we will ever offer. After Sept 15 you will see why. Things are radically going to be changing. Our membership is going to be strengthened and supported to a serious degree. Members will be rewarded going forward. Non members are going to find the costs much steeper. There also will be a new website and forum and lots of the technical issues will be eliminated. I love the chaos of change.

### vkiernan — 2012-08-09T15:53:47+0300

Got it. I actually signed up for it but apparantly missed the deadline for sending labs because they weren't back yet. Must not have read the instructions thoroughly. Anyway, Misty e-mailed me and we decided she would refund my money for that e-mail special and sign me up the consult only. I agreed and never heard back as to whether my money was refunded or not. So hopefully I have another consult waiting for me besides the 2 for 1. I really wanted the e-mail so I could take it to my doc and perhaps she wouldn't think I was blowing smoke. Besides that, with the thought of panhypopit lingering, I wanted the tests outlined so I knew exactly what I needed and could give that to my doc as well.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-10T17:00:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tonights-webinar.2932/post-44126>

I'll go. . . I thought it was most fun to see people on the vid's. Ok, there, I'm lame. My attention wanders when it's just audio - my preference with audio only stuff is go be able to go back and listen, and then please please make them avail for Idevices.

I think question/answer in small groups by topic would be best. I think when you get too many different "issues" going - it's not as informative (read my attention wanders) as when I know there is a topic so I can either prepare or save notes and learn after. With too many different things coming up I find I can't keep up.

I like the idea of a Q&A with consult people so you can actually dispense advice and answer in ways you can not on the forum though, but if you are brainstorming options for future Q&A's, perhaps pick a general topic for if. . . .like one time will be on CT, another will be on "What to eat" another on Hormones. . . etc.

Also, it's not purely selfish on the video options - you want to build community, having a face/person behind all the names will serve that goal quite nicely.

Misty, I was referring to my new doc blowing me off on labs for the panhypopit; not you not following thru with something. I would have e-mailed you back if I needed something. Although, I thought you were going to charge my cc for the $49 and refund the $99. I can't sign up for a consult without you charging my cc and the offer was overwith so no way could I get the $49 consult anymore. My point was you didn't and I was fine with that and nothing more. ### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-11T15:23:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tonights-webinar.2932/post-44307>

> **tellmisty;44593 wrote:** Easy, sister. I don't blow anyone off. I can't sign you up for a consult ... you have to sign up yourself (we don't have access to anyone's credit card info). I honored your request. If you ever have questions, just email me. I respond very fast and take care of everything I can ... I'm sure many here will attest to that.

I was wondering how you would handle that.........LOL. Even the even keeled Misty gets hot. I would have went ballistic.......LOL. We can not read for you then comprehend for you and then of course read your mind to get your true intent........This is what I mean by thin slicing a person without words. Many times what happens between the words tells you a lot about their real issues. We want to help but I really think you must realize you MUST do your part and READ THE DAMN emails. We tried the email consult thing because YOU ALL asked for it. I have to be honest.........It was one of the best deals one could get. The amount of grief we got from about 15, tells me never again will we go this route..........next time it is going to happen on my terms because of the amount of time we had to spend on return emails and crazy complaints because people simply did not read.

Some of you did great once Misty responded to you and we appreciate it.......when she explained how it was going to work. If you think you can find a doc to help hold your hand to review a history and then help you consider about what you and your PCP might order to get to the core issues of your problem all for 99 bucks with a free Q & A to boot.........well, you best post a link for all to see that doc. You wont find one.

I decided to try it once against my better judgement.........I was willing to try it because you guys wanted it but I have to tell you some of the responses we got back.........just were unreal. To get this kind of service in the real world you are looking at 400-600 dollars........minimum. The flack we got was unreal. So I instructed Misty any complaints I wanted her to refund them ASAP. We are trying to help you all not screw you over. This process is very labor intensive. Sorry, but this had to be said. I have been pretty hot over it for sometime.

# Thread: Very interesting new article on cold showers.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/very-interesting-new-article-on-cold-showers.2939/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-09T21:59:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/very-interesting-new-article-on-cold-showers.2939/post-43956>

[http://www.thisviewoflife.com/index.php/magazine/articles/take-a-cold-shower](http://http://www.thisviewoflife.com/index.php/magazine/articles/take-a-cold-shower)

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-10T00:29:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/very-interesting-new-article-on-cold-showers.2939/post-43973>

The last paragraph hits home regarding everything you've been talking about

"Cost-effective methods of indoor climate control have only been developed within the last century and any behavioral symptoms rooted in a lack of “thermal exercise” would clearly be exacerbated by climate-controlled lifestyles. It would be interesting to determine whether diagnoses of anxiety disorder or other conditions predicted by noradrenergic dysfunction have increased following the introduction of affordable climate control systems in the mid-to late 20th century. We live in a time where climate-controlled conditions are common, and it is clear that we are living in conditions much different from that of our ancestors. Whether this evolutionary incongruity is responsible for the prevalence of anxiety and depressive disorders has yet to be determined, but the findings presented here suggest that cold temperatures can potentially treat depression and anxiety in humans."

### Dali Dula — 2012-08-10T00:53:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/very-interesting-new-article-on-cold-showers.2939/post-43978>

Love the phrase "thermal exercise". Sounds so normal. As in-" Of course you're sick, you haven't been getting any thermal exercise."

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-10T01:24:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/very-interesting-new-article-on-cold-showers.2939/post-43985>

He is another shocker.......Robb Wolf just told AHS 2012 that metformin is great for people.......Hey Gretchen did you hear that? Robb is now on board with the Epi paleo Rx education consults........can you imagine when he reads about cycloset?

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-10T01:42:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/very-interesting-new-article-on-cold-showers.2939/post-43993>

> **Jack Kruse;44280 wrote:** He is another shocker.......Robb Wolf just told AHS 2012 that metformin is great for people.......Hey Gretchen did you hear that? Robb is now on board with the Epi paleo Rx education consults........can you imagine when he reads about cycloset?

WOW! talk about direction change! he'll be amazed, just like my PCP!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-10T01:44:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/very-interesting-new-article-on-cold-showers.2939/post-43996>

It's not about who is right.......it is about getting people healthy the way mother nature intended. We need to reverse the modern behaviors that fast forwarded our circadian clocks.........and sped up the shortening of our telomeres.

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-10T02:30:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/very-interesting-new-article-on-cold-showers.2939/post-44014>

> **Jack Kruse;44291 wrote:** It's not about who is right.......it is about getting people healthy the way mother nature intended. We need to reverse the modern behaviors that fast forwarded our circadian clocks.........and sped up the shortening of our telomeres.

you are 100% right...but as I see it the big thing is Robb's paradigm is shifting, he's looking at things through a different lens - and that's taking his thinking in different yet positive directions...

you planted the seed. Some of us grabbed on w/both hands and held on for dear life - while others shook their heads and called both you and us crazy...yet here we stand, changing things through quiet revolution.....

# Thread: Natural labor and vaginal delivery activates UPC2, C-Sections don't.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/natural-labor-and-vaginal-delivery-activates-upc2-c-sections-dont.2940/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shijin13 — 2012-08-10T00:25:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/natural-labor-and-vaginal-delivery-activates-upc2-c-sections-dont.2940/post-43971>

[http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0042911](http://http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0042911)

Pretty interesting article.

Dd was delivered vaginally (after a natural labor - not induced)

Ds was delivered via C-section (after a natural labor - not induced, but with maternal distress)

My question is UPC2 activated during labor? or is it activated during the transition through the birth canal?

If its activated by labor - would there be a difference in UPC2 in a baby born vaginally vs one via C-section after natural labor (due to complications)?

If transition through the birth canal is the activator of UPC2 - then activation of the hippocampus for optimal function isn't fully activated - given my own history (C-section after natural labor due to fetal distress) - I'm guessing the UPC2 is activated via transition... might just explain what started the cascade in me - which means that for dd who was vaginally delivered- even though she physically couldn't nurse and got breast milk (expressed) - she's ahead of the game; where ds - who was a C-section after natural labor and maternal distress, and is still nursing is behind the power curve b/c his hippocampus isn't fully turned on....

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-10T02:07:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/natural-labor-and-vaginal-delivery-activates-upc2-c-sections-dont.2940/post-44005>

So question. Is it the Labor and natural methods that trigger the Upc2? Or is it transition/vaginal delivery that triggers it? As well as the skin-to-skin contact and the baby breast crawling to the breasts to nurse.

I know the birth canal squeezes all the placental fluid out of their lungs & vaginally delivered babies don't have as big of weight drop. Where as w/C-Sect babies that doesn't happen & they trend toward inflated birth weights from not only the placental fluid, but from the epidural, pitocin and iv fluids & a trend toward a drop of at least 10% of birth weight.

This is all so fascinating how it all ties together.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-23T15:53:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/natural-labor-and-vaginal-delivery-activates-upc2-c-sections-dont.2940/post-46521>

C section babies also lack the benefit of bacterial colonization from mom's vagina.

### TribalSpice — 2012-08-29T00:55:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/natural-labor-and-vaginal-delivery-activates-upc2-c-sections-dont.2940/post-47946>

There are massive differences between babies born via spontaneous vaginal birth and removed in surgery. From gut colonization from the mothers vagina and anus (OA babies come out facing mums butt for a reason that is not just the shape of the pelvis) to compression of skull, chest etc. and all that goes with that but also the composition of breast milk is different. On day 3 there is 1/3 of the levels of beta endorphin compared to VB.

### nonchalant — 2012-08-29T01:03:29+0300

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-29T02:04:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/natural-labor-and-vaginal-delivery-activates-upc2-c-sections-dont.2940/post-47987>

So I'm guessing anyone here is down with real birth vs c-section. . . the real pearls here are what can we do when it doesn't work out?

My son is 6 now, so it may be too late for me. . . but perhaps I can help others.

Sometimes you get a C-section, then knowing all this - what to do? Following that, when things go the way of C-section, Breast feeding is sometimes not happening either. Assuming one can't breastfeed, what's the next best option?

I think we are preachin to the choir here on optimizing the birth thing. . .but lets use this info to figure out how best to handle when things don't work out?

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-29T02:52:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/natural-labor-and-vaginal-delivery-activates-upc2-c-sections-dont.2940/post-47998>

I wonder for myself - I was a c-section baby who was breastfed. Maybe that's why I have low levels of bifidus bacteria showing up in me now . Doesn't bifidus normally colonize the colon? And I think exclusively breastfed babies have predominantly bifidus bacteria in their guts until they start eating solids. I wonder if it's the same for c-section babies versus vaginal babies. So how do we get the bifidus that we missed out on during delivery (I assume) into our colons where it belongs? Here we go again back to fecal transplants I suppose.

Now how about pondering group B strep. I was positive for it for 2 of my babies, and declined the iv antibiotics both times. I knew my deliveries in the past had been pretty straight forward affairs and couldn't imagine adding in the antibiotics. What is the likely outcome for babies born to this scenario, other than crazy amounts of yeast?

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-29T03:50:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/natural-labor-and-vaginal-delivery-activates-upc2-c-sections-dont.2940/post-48008>

I attempted natural drug free birth w/both kids. Wasn't successful w/either dd was a vaginal delivery but she was unable to nurse so I pumped for 14 mths for her. Ds natural labor that ended up in CSec b/c my blood pressure got too high ds nursed w/30 min of delivery and hasn't stopped yet

As for optionsif you can't bf milk share via human milk 4 human babies is a great option if you can't make milk I donated me xtra milk from ds to a woman w/aoGT she got milk for her son all over the eastern seaboard from 15 different women She's going to do the same thing for her next baby. I wish it had been around for dd b/c we would have supplemented w/other moms milk instead of supplementing w/formula when I no longer made enough to keep up w/dd

### KiwiLauren — 2012-08-29T04:30:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/natural-labor-and-vaginal-delivery-activates-upc2-c-sections-dont.2940/post-48015>

> **Cavemam;48438 wrote:** So I'm guessing anyone here is down with real birth vs c-section. . . the real pearls here are what can we do when it doesn't work out?
>
>
>
> My son is 6 now, so it may be too late for me. . . but perhaps I can help others.
>
>
>
> Sometimes you get a C-section, then knowing all this - what to do? Following that, when things go the way of C-section, Breast feeding is sometimes not happening either. Assuming one can't breastfeed, what's the next best option?
>
>
>
> I think we are preachin to the choir here on optimizing the birth thing. . .but lets use this info to figure out how best to handle when things don't work out?

Well this is too late for those of us whose children are born, but I have a friend who is an OB and believes in natural birth but works almost exclusively in the OR because she believes there is a LOT that can be done to support a mother and baby when they've had a C-section. One of the things she says is really important is to NOT wash the baby in any way, especially teh baby's hands. They have amniotic fluid on them and baby will lick them and use that to 'find' the mother (and the mother's breast)... she talks about how everything the baby is covered with helps the baby recover the normal process. So she'll take a C-section baby and place that baby down at the base of the mother's body and let the baby rest her/his head on mum's lower abdomen and then take it from there.

I guess what I've learned from her is sort of what we're learning here... if we missed the window, how do we recreate it as close to nature as possible? So if you're not breastfeeding, you can still feed your baby topless and the baby naked... this activates the oxytocin, too. Mimic and follow nature whenever possible. Don't throw in the towel when things don't go as planned.

### TribalSpice — 2012-08-29T04:38:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/natural-labor-and-vaginal-delivery-activates-upc2-c-sections-dont.2940/post-48016>

First off, prevention is always the best way to deal with problems. A woman will birth without complications 95% of the time with:

\*good preconception health (exactly the sort of stuff I have been reading here) and

\*quality prenatal care and by this I don't mean testing for all the things that could be wrong, but watching body signals and making adjustments as needed. Looking at chemical (nutrition, toxins, hormones), Structural (mobility, position of baby, flexibility of the pelvis) and emotional (relationships, fears, etc.)

But \*\*\*\* does happen, so if you are being wheeled of for a c/s swab your vagina, perineum and anus then rub it in your nipples and breasts ( I find this less gross than a mackerel head smoothie). Do as much skin to skin with your baby as possible, wear your baby, cosleep (within arms reach)

If you cannot breastfeed (wow, awesome effort pumping for 14 months, that is a lot of hard work and dedication) as mentioned HM4HB is worldwide and growing all the time. Where I am we have a virtual village where there are a couple of babies feed by other mums, sometimes from the breast sometimes with donations and lactaids. I would love to know what their gut flora is like with such a diverse breast milk supply. Formula does not have ogliosaccharides for feeding gut flora, it should be the very very last option.

I think if your child has chronic health issues stemming back to their birth via c/section, then fecal transplant might be the way to go. I would look for a donor who's family have a good birth history.

I love this blog post: [http://www.livnaked.com/we-came-barefoot-naked-and-covered-in-bugs/](http://http://www.livnaked.com/we-came-barefoot-naked-and-covered-in-bugs/)

# Thread: Take a look at my labs please
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-a-look-at-my-labs-please.2941/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Eve — 2012-08-10T03:19:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-a-look-at-my-labs-please.2941/post-44032>

These are some of my recent labs:

I'm in Canada but converted the results to conventional values, so I could use the conventional ranges given by LEF and other resources.

These were taken on Day 4 of my cycle.

Jul 30/12

Estradiol 40.04 pg/mL LOW

lef follicular \[12.5 - 166.0 pg/mL\]; below 90 is a deficiency (but not sure if is for any time during cycle)

earlymenopause.com \[25- 75 pg/mL\]; below 50 is low

Progesterone 0.38 ng/mL LOW

lef range follicular \[0.2 - 1.5\]

I calculated by hand my P:E ratio and it was 8.22:1......If I did that right, that's really low!

DHEA-S 275 ug/dL OPTIMAL (BARELY)

lef's optimal range 275-400ug/dL

Testosterone 37.46 ng/dL (couldn't find the conventional range for this one)

standard value 1.3 nmol/L \[0.5 - 2.6\]

Jul 10/12

HDL 78mg/dL

LDL 137 mg/dL

Vitamin D 59 (up from 17 in January)

TSH 2.42

free T3 and free T4 were just below range...I had switched to Erfa natural thyroid 60mg in May approx. I didn't increase fast enough I think, so I became hypo again. After increasing 30mg at the end of July, my hypo symptoms went away. (Because my TSH was in the normal range, my doctor tested for prolactinoma but my prolactin was normal. She suspected the TSH was normal because natural thyroid contains varying doses of thyroid hormones. She is against me taking it and handed me papers on the Boxed Recall of natural thyroid in the States). Retesting thyroid in September.

May? 2012

HS-CRP 1.6

Thoughts:

I am surprised by DHEA is so good, considering my super low estrogen. I also was suspecting low DHEA cause I have a really bad leaky gut...?

The low progesterone I was expecting cause that is associated with leaky gut and I was born premature (by ~5 weeks).

Btw, I am only 26. When I started GAPS last Feb, I gained 20-25lbs in 4 weeks but I also came off a calorie restrictive diet (although gluten-free) and allowed myself fats and went binge crazy on almond butter (which my leaky gut can't handle). I have been 175-185 (5'7) the past year and can't lose weight at all.

My next steps:

I am having a consult at the compounding pharmacy next week and am hoping to get connected to a doc who does BHRT. Hopefully, I will find one taking patients.

Tomorrow I am doing my diurnal cortisol kit.

All comments appreciated

Thanks for reading

### KiwiLauren — 2012-08-10T07:41:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-a-look-at-my-labs-please.2941/post-44082>

I'm not so good with hormones, but having T3 and T4 at the low end/below range is problematic. You don't have a RT3 test or cortisol testing, but do you have any concern about cortisol rhythms/levels? I see you're doing the test, so that should be helpful I'd think, to the overall picture. How is your energy? Are you following light cycles strictly?

Your hsCRP is too high (should be as close to zero as possible) which means you've still got inflammation on board (me, too!). Slow weight loss can be linked to the poor hormone panel and, ultimately, the inflammation. As long as your gut is leaky, you'll have problems righting your hormone levels/balance. If you didn't hear the BHRT webinar, I'm guessing you'd find it very helpful.

In the webinar, Dr K said he wants DHEA in the top quartile, FYI.

How long have you been doing the Leptin Rx? You may need to give yourself some time to reset things. Sounds like you've already ditched nuts and dairy, but going hard with epi paleo diet and Leptin Rx should help. And of course CT.

I'm sure others with more expertise will chime in.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-10T17:05:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-a-look-at-my-labs-please.2941/post-44128>

You should also test your thyroid antibodies if you haven't done that already: TPO and TgAB. This will test for Hashimotos. Also agree with Kiwi (on everything she said), you should check your rT3 and at the same time recheck FT3 as it should be in the top of the range. And it appears your hormones are trashed and so is your gut. Not a hormone expert but you definitely need help and probably need a pregnenolone test too. Hopefully you can find a doc to work with both your leaky gut and hormones.

### Eve — 2012-08-10T18:04:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-a-look-at-my-labs-please.2941/post-44141>

Thanks guys for the comments.

In Canada, they don't have a RT3 test (as much as I want one). I am seeing an Internal Medicine Doctor in October(! after a 4 month wait), so maybe he will order one for me from somewhere.

In 2008 when I was first diagnosed with hypothyroid, my doc tested for Hashi's and said I didn't have it. Unfortunately, I don't have a copy of the results. I think I was tested again for it by a different doc later as well in 2009.

I have been doing the Leptin Reset since January...yes, going on 8 months now. I would like to do the Epi-Paleo and load up on fish...unfortunately I am still on the hunt for fish that I don't react to...I was eating fish for a while and then realized I was reacting to the sodium phosphate in it. I tried organic salmon and had a reaction to that too...Also, I have a corn sensitivity and I read that all fish as soon as it's caught is stored in ice that has citric acid in it. Maybe local will be better...I'll try that next.

I don't think they have a pregnenolone test at least in my province. I looked at the online Lab catelogue online and it wasn't listed. But once again, perhaps the internal medicine doc can order it for me.

For the DHEA, is that upper quartile for even the "optimal" range? Or is it upper quartile for just the standard ranges?

After I get my cortisol test back, I'm also considering booking a consult with Dr. K.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-10T18:32:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-a-look-at-my-labs-please.2941/post-44145>

Yes DHEA s/b in the top quartile of the optimal range. You could raise yours a little, I would say. Keep doing what you are doing as it sounds like you are working at finding the right solutions for your situation. I hope you are able to get the tests you need.

# Thread: Coconut Oil how much is too much?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-how-much-is-too-much.2943/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-10T05:44:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-how-much-is-too-much.2943/post-44068>

For awhile I was using coconut in my hair and on my skin daily...and nothing seemed bad about it. I was wondering though if coconut oil too much or too often could be bad though because it will kill good bacteria as well? I've heard about things ...maybe it was coconut oil that for some reason don't destroy the good bacteria but only the bad..not sure how that would work..can't remember what it was.

Anyway so I'm making Water Kefir and I'm not supposed to make it with honey because Kefir uses a bacteria/yeast...can't think of name... so was wondering if I should limit use because of this. There are supposed to be beneficial bacteria on both skin, in hair, in the stomach..pretty much everywhere in the body so will coconut oil kill these?

Thanks

### Michael — 2012-08-10T13:48:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-how-much-is-too-much.2943/post-44092>

I think you can rest easy on that score.

There are people who've been annointing their skin with coconut oil (as a kind of sun-block among other things) for centuries, so if it killed the bacteria that, so to speak, belong there they'd have run into problems and learnt to stop doing that a long time ago.

Similar considerations would apply to food use. Recently CO has even been used with AIDS patients. Reports are it's quite good for their troubled systems. If it killed off beneficial bacteria in the gut, then it would create a niche for harmful ones, which would then run riot, os that the patients would get worse not better.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-11T00:10:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-how-much-is-too-much.2943/post-44202>

> **Michael;44390 wrote:** I think you can rest easy on that score.
>
>
>
> There are people who've been annointing their skin with coconut oil (as a kind of sun-block among other things) for centuries, so if it killed the bacteria that, so to speak, belong there they'd have run into problems and learnt to stop doing that a long time ago.
>
>
>
> Similar considerations would apply to food use. Recently CO has even been used with AIDS patients. Reports are it's quite good for their troubled systems. If it killed off beneficial bacteria in the gut, then it would create a niche for harmful ones, which would then run riot, os that the patients would get worse not better.

You're right....makes quite a bit of sense....does that mean coconut oil ignores all beneficial bacteria though...and if so now I"m curious about the mechanism.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-on-ct-and-pain.2946/post-44086>

I'm curious about something and was wondering if some experienced CTers might weigh in. I often get bad neck pain with migraines. Since I've learned about CT, I've been trying to treat them with ice packs on my neck but it really doesn't help me. But, if I use a heating pad, it does relieve the pain. Temporarily, at least. Why is that?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-on-ct-and-pain.2946/post-44114>

Every time you feel miserable, there is present an excellent opportunity to make a big step forward

you need to wrap the ice packs around your carotid arteries in the neck to actually get relief using ice. another thing that works well is head into a bucket of ice water.

I've used the ice packs wrapped around my neck several times - and it works wonders... it relieves the migraine to the point I can funciton... which is critical when you work fulltime and you've got little ones running about your life....

# Thread: Hcg yes or no?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/>
**Subforum:** HCG Protocol
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-08-10T15:34:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/post-44109>

At a webinar last night Jack said something regarding who should not take HCG (i used all my tough love yesterday so i won't repeat his colorful insults) .

Summary is if you have hpa axis issues (eating disorders do) then its a bad idea.

I had not heard this reason yet and found it useful.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-10T22:25:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/post-44195>

on most hcg forums it's generally suggested that folks with eating disorders deal with tht first before trying hcg. few listen but it's a pretty standard recommendation. hcg definitely will not fix an ED and may worsen it (food restrictions etc)

### cantweight — 2012-09-21T15:41:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/post-53153>

I did multiple rounds of HCG and lost a substantial amount of weight, however I gained it back within weeks of stopping and major hormonal fallout after each round. Perhaps I could have negated some of that with activated charcoal to help clean out whatever scary stuff all that fat released. I have low progesterone...another factor that to my understanding works against one using HCG. And my most recent finding is that my liver is not functioning as well as it should so while my estrogen is showing up very low in blood work, it is not low, my liver is processing it and storing in fat cells. I just smile and nod as I hear all these things.

Doing the leptin RX combined with CT gave me much better full body results than HCG. The improvement in skin, mood, hair, sleep, body shape etc, made up for the slower number decline on the scale. I actually looked better, felt better, and ate bacon!

>
> Doing the leptin RX combined with CT gave me much better full body results than HCG. The improvement in skin, mood, hair, sleep, body shape etc, made up for the slower number decline on the scale. I actually looked better, felt better, and ate bacon!

I so agree - after HHCG - I had a very hard time maintaining but since doing the leptin RX and CT I am down 2 sizes. That might not sound like a big deal - but I am not very big - 60K - so 2 sizes is huge. I didn't realize it was happening even tho my clothes were falling off me. I was also pretty bummed that I went up 2K on the leptin RX and didn't realize my body comp was changing. Sounds dopey not to realize - but when you have been dieting most of your life - things like that don't happen - not to me anyway! Dr. K. says women focus too much on the scale - I am shocked at how right he is! who knew.....

### Zorica Vuletic — 2013-01-29T21:30:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/post-73200>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=54896)
>
> I so agree - after HHCG - I had a very hard time maintaining but since doing the leptin RX and CT I am down 2 sizes. That might not sound like a big deal - but I am not very big - 60K - so 2 sizes is huge. I didn't realize it was happening even tho my clothes were falling off me. I was also pretty bummed that I went up 2K on the leptin RX and didn't realize my body comp was changing. Sounds dopey not to realize - but when you have been dieting most of your life - things like that don't happen - not to me anyway! Dr. K. says women focus too much on the scale - I am shocked at how right he is! who knew.....

I have always obsessed about body comp and never weight. Luckily b/c I always knew what made sense best. I figured my clothes would tell me one way or anther...So even since a teen I knew it was in the body comp not the 'size' per se. Also I would compare and observe people who are quite 'large' but I just looked at them and they had a better body comp (particularly very nice legs). I knew then also that it's the composition not the size.

### Charlotte — 2013-01-29T21:40:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/post-73203>

I do not quite agree to that Caroline and Zorica. I can be 5 kg up and my pants still fit. The scale is a much finer tuned instrument to see a tendency. What is not good is obsessing. But what speaks against getting on the scale in the morning to evaluate where you are right now with weight. Is the tendency up or down or stable? What did you eat last day? If you can use that simply to evaluate instead of getting emotionally involved I think the scale is a perfect instrument.

### diane — 2013-01-29T22:07:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/post-73212>

Charlotte I'm so envious if you can use it without emotional involvement!  That's one reason why I'm trying to find other ways right now. But I also think that more than just food can cause weight fluctuations. Perhaps that knowledge keeps you from getting emotionally involved.

For me, I read a lot about HCG, and I almost did it (even ordered some, but can only get the homeopathic kind here). But reading some of these forums, I saw how obsessed that people can get, and knowing my personality, I also feared that's what I would do. I also saw a lot of people that have to do it multiple times because they gain the weight back - and that's always my problem as well. I also just had a feeling that somehow I would be creating bigger problems for myself down the line. No evidence, but sometimes I have to go on my gut. But I'm happy for the people that it works for!

### caroline — 2013-01-29T23:17:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/post-73227>

For me - weighing every day is just something I need to do.....but I realize that so many things effect it. if I didn't drink enough water - If I had a terrible sleep my weight will always be up. All my overweight life \[pre JK\] I would avoid the scales like the plague if I knew I was gaining ..... and that is the worst case scenario because it gets out of hand so fast. If you have gained 5 lbs - you can just take care of it ...but when you have gained 15-20lbs it is a major deal to get rid of it. Not getting on the scales when I knew I was gaining was way, way more stressful for me ..... and when I just went by how my clothes were fitting I was in big trouble because I could gain 10 -12 lbs and my clothes still fit fine - it just got distributed differently. When I weight everyday it makes me feel like I am in control of this journey for the first time in my life and I am not going to let anything derail me - so I just suck it up and do it ...and sometimes I get a nice surprise! my 2 cents.....

### Charlotte — 2013-01-29T23:24:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/post-73228>

yes Diane, I agree. Most important is to know how you react, what you can take and what not. I also gain weight or stall when I stay up over night, as I had to do last August, when I worked three days and three nights straight without sleep. No loss at 500 kcal. Then, doing too much in terms of heavy lifting (not at the fitness studio, but at work), or having stress, not drinking enough, inflammation, something I ate that did not go down well and so on. And I think it is interesting to get on the scale in morning, and remember and then not get upset because you have not lost, but simply assess the situation. Its learning by doing for me.

### Charlotte — 2013-01-29T23:28:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/post-73229>

exactly my experience too, Caroline. For me, getting on the scale is staying in control. (maybe we are just control freaks )

### caroline — 2013-01-29T23:49:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/post-73235>

Charlotte - I really don't think we are control freaks - but after a lifetime of trying to starve my body into submission - all of this that JK is teaching us ...is working - but we need to dig in ... be 100% in. If we want to be successful we can't be afraid of the numbers on the scale - I need to know to keep on track .... just the same as looking at my labs - I need to know what's up and where I am going . JK's flashlight can only show me so much - I need to get in there and do....
I suppose this overweight thing isn't the same for everyone ..... I used to look in the mirror and feel like a thin person on the inside - but could never understand how/why all this flab had attached itself to me ....I always hear about obese people who lose weight and then they gain it back because they can't relate to the new thin person ....that was never me ...I was a thin person regardless and inspite of what the mirror was telling me .....

### diane — 2013-01-29T23:51:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/post-73236>

I like this conversation - so much to think about. Thanks ladies!

### caroline — 2013-01-30T00:10:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-yes-or-no.2950/post-73240>

Diane - I think - in the long run - that is what JK has taught us....think for ourselves and question everything...I am not a sheep - never have been and never will be...but I think - at some point - you do have to trust someone/something and go for it. All of this is falling into place for me after years of searching for answers ... that doesn't make me a sheep - it means I have finally found something that makes so much sense and seems so right. I suppose I could better evaluate if I had a medical degree - but then again - I have known Doctors who are pretty lame ....

# Thread: Hello from Lima Peru !!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-lima-peru.2953/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### karicat — 2012-08-10T16:45:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-lima-peru.2953/post-44124>

Hi everyone. My name is Karina and I'm new to this forum. Listening to yesterday's webinar made me think that it was time to introduce myself here. I still have a lot of reading to do, that sometimes seems quite complex, but incredibly interesting as well.

I'm currently on a bhrt program that is helping me, but I still have a long way back to optimal. My hormonal issues started when I was 33 and my menses stopped for good. But I suspect that I've got these issues for quite a while before, only didn't know about them as everything seemed quite "normal".

I'm really looking forward to learn all I can here to finally overcome my issues. It has been too many years living a "half-life" and I'm sure there is much more to it. For the past few months since starting the bhrt I've got glimpses of what normal should feel like, and that's what keeps me going in spite of the ups and downs, and the problems I've had so far with my program. Somehow along this road, I started to think that maybe the way I felt was what normal should be (when you are feeling lousy for too long, you just don't know what to think anymore). But at the same time, I wondered how could people do all the things they do, if they were feeling like me... So I felt bad because of my own health issues and on top of that, frustrated and bad for not being able to keep up with all the people I know.

But now I've realized that things can be totally different, if I find the correct path to get where I should be.

I'm also really looking forward to my educational consult with Jack, it was really nice to listen to him yesterday for the first time.

All the best from Peru

### shilohman — 2012-08-10T17:01:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-lima-peru.2953/post-44127>

Welcome Karina, you have come to the right place to find answers and solutions. Dr Kruse knows his stuff.

### karicat — 2012-08-10T21:42:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-lima-peru.2953/post-44188>

Thanks for the welcome

### Destiny — 2012-08-11T00:40:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-lima-peru.2953/post-44206>

> **karicat;44422 wrote:**
>
>
>
> Somehow along this road, I started to think that maybe the way I felt was what normal should be (when you are feeling lousy for too long, you just don't know what to think anymore). But at the same time, I wondered how could people do all the things they do, if they were feeling like me... So I felt bad because of my own health issues and on top of that, frustrated and bad for not being able to keep up with all the people I know.
>
> But now I've realized that things can be totally different, if I find the correct path to get where I should be.
>
>
>
> All the best from Peru

Karicat, welcome! I have to add that this hit the home! I also wondered how my grandma could have possibly lived to 97 yrs old when I felt so tired at 51! I did not considered that my tiredness was not a norm. Now, I know better!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-11T00:53:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-lima-peru.2953/post-44208>

welcome to the road to optimal

### karicat — 2012-08-12T19:05:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-lima-peru.2953/post-44549>

Hi Destiny ! I guess the important thing is that now we know better. And we have the tools to make the best out of of it. So, I'm really hopeful for the first time in many years

And thanks Jack and Caroline for the welcome !!

### caroline — 2012-08-13T00:11:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-lima-peru.2953/post-44585>

That is one of my favorite sayings - when you know better - you do better

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-13T03:51:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-lima-peru.2953/post-44614>

HOLA karicat

one of the best things I did for myself was go to Peru  I love your pais. I went by myself, signed up with a tour group to back pack El Camino Inka..... and I had the time of my life. 2 weeks Lima-Cuzco-Ollantaytambo-Macchu Picchu-Lima I made wonderful friends that I still stay in touch with, 4 years later.

It was there that I first had ceviche, pisco and chicha (uhhh, wow  not paleo LOL) trucha pina, lomo saltado... I love comida peruana. I love to eat at a local peruvian joint, they know me by now, no rice/beans/corn for the gringa, no inka cola either.

I just started BHRT too, so I look forward to following your journey

### karicat — 2012-08-15T03:35:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-lima-peru.2953/post-44981>

Hola Teenie !! So happy to read your post

Yes, we have many different interesting/beautiful places here. Cusco is the most famous, but Puno is also extremely beautiful and the Manu, in the jungle, quite awesome I've been told.

I've been to Cusco a few times (business trips), but have never made the Inka Trail, that must be quite an experience.

And it seems you know a lot about our typical food as well. Did you ever tried the anticuchos ?? Our food is very tasty, but it is true that it is extremely high in carbs. There is a dish here called combinado, which is spaghetti with huacatay sauce and potatoes a la huancaina. So basically, noodles with potatoes  But we have a great variety of very good food to choose from, so I guess you have to pick the right choice.

I've started my bhrt 6 months ago already, but I still have a long way to go. Too many years of imbalances and not knowing what to do to get better have taken its toll, but I really believe I'm on the right path now.

If you ever come to Peru again, let me know, I'd be really happy to meet you

\=========================================

Life Extension Magazine **September 2012

http://www.lef.org/magazine/mag2012/sep2012\_Fish-Oils-Health-Benefits\_01.htm?source=search&key=fish%20oils%20Magazine%20September%202012

After a dozen years or more of close examination of detailed

red cell lipid analysis at BodyBio, we have witnessed and

documented repeated over-expression of omega 3 fatty acids

EPA and DHA, with the ultimate suppression of omega 6

fatty acids. BodyBio analyzes the patient’s complex lipid

biochemistry through a computerized report, which is then

forwarded to the physician. Over 90% are reporting high

EPA and DHA levels most often from taking an excess of

to try ﬁsh oil. With the encouragement now coming from an authority, the over-expression of EPA and DHA has

reached epidemic** proportions.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-10T21:13:05+0300

Jansz youre not missing anything.......but context. DHA in supplements acts differently than it does in vivo. BG 5 laid that out. the key is the ration in the CNS.......if it is right the rest of the body follows.

The big issue with the Kane's are RBC analysis does not equal CM of neurons. It is a proxy.

### chocolate — 2012-08-10T21:13:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fatty-acids-la-la-la.2956/post-44179>

Janzz you are turning into a road bump for all of my bright ideas...

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15254232](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15254232)

> Vitamin C is a kinase inhibitor: dehydroascorbic acid inhibits IkappaBalpha kinase beta.
>
> CÃ¡rcamo JM, Pedraza A, BÃ³rquez-Ojeda O, Zhang B, Sanchez R, Golde DW.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Program in Molecular Pharmacology and Chemistry, Box 451, Memorial Sloan-Kettering Cancer Center, New York, NY 10021, USA.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Reactive oxygen species (ROS) are key intermediates in cellular signal transduction pathways whose function may be counterbalanced by antioxidants. Acting as an antioxidant, ascorbic acid (AA) donates two electrons and becomes oxidized to dehydroascorbic acid (DHA). We discovered that DHA directly inhibits IkappaBalpha kinase beta (IKKbeta) and IKKalpha enzymatic activity in vitro, whereas AA did not have this effect. When cells were loaded with AA and induced to generate DHA by oxidative stress in cells expressing a constitutive active IKKbeta, NF-kappaB activation was inhibited. Our results identify a dual molecular action of vitamin C in signal transduction and provide a direct linkage between the redox state of vitamin C and NF-kappaB signaling events. AA quenches ROS intermediates involved in the activation of NF-kappaB and is oxidized to DHA, which directly inhibits IKKbeta and IKKalpha enzymatic activity. These findings define a function for vitamin C in signal transduction other than as an antioxidant and mechanistically illuminate how vitamin C down-modulates NF-kappaB signaling.

I'm posting this in your thread til I can learn what the heck it means or someone can translate.

# Thread: Can we cause gluten intolerance in our kids?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-cause-gluten-intolerance-in-our-kids.2960/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-08-10T20:03:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-cause-gluten-intolerance-in-our-kids.2960/post-44162>

So we have some celiac disease in my family, both my grandma and uncle have it. I have in the last few months cut my kids who are 8 and 3 off of gluten and sugar. More changes are in the works, but we are cutting things gradually - those are pretty huge changes for my boys anyhow. My twin 14 month olds I'm not allowing any gluten either and this is causing one of my aunts great distress. She has apparently read that not allowing them to eat gluten from a lung age will cause gluten intolerance. Now, I don't know whether or not this is true and it really doesn't matter that much because I am still resolved to cut this out of my families diet. I am curious whether it is true though, just so I k ow how to respond. I have already told her, I don't believe it is a healthy substance for people to eat EVEN if they aren't gluten intolerant. She recently had the blood tests done for celiac disease and she is quite excited that she is all clear to keep eating gluten and I guess she doesn't want my kids to miss out on cake and pie and pizza and Mac and cheese. You know, the healthy American way of eating (ha!).

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-10T20:34:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-cause-gluten-intolerance-in-our-kids.2960/post-44166>

eating gluten causes gluten intolerance. If you don't eat it - no problems. as for cookies, cakes, pie, pizza etc - they're your kids, she needs to respect how your raising them.

If she keeps pushing the issue give her a copy of the paleo answer, ask her to read it - she'll get the picture, as cordain explains why grains, legumes, soy etc are bad for us.

the real deal is - these are you kids - rasie them how you want them raised, they are not your aunts childeren - in the end they'll be healtier than their peers.

ETA: if you have to give treats/desserts - find things that are complementary to how you eat.

fresh berries/fruit in season w/HWC

kids don't need candy or cake - last weekend we went to a 4yo bday party - neither of our kids had cake. Our frend did make macarones for them - and they each had 1. While not ideal - at least they participated. The 4yo now ask if something has wheat, and attempts to avoid eating it.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-08-10T21:18:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-cause-gluten-intolerance-in-our-kids.2960/post-44182>

> **Shijin13;44470 wrote:** eating gluten causes gluten intolerance.

+1000 This is how we flipped that epigenetic switch isn't it?

and +1000 to what Gretchen said about these being YOUR children. When my children don't like a choice I've made for them, I simply tell them (after explaining why I made the choice) that I am responsible for their health and I MUST do what I believe is right, regardless of what others say. When they become adults they can make their own choices.

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-10T22:06:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-cause-gluten-intolerance-in-our-kids.2960/post-44192>

After my 2nd was diagnosed celiac at age 5 (while I was pregnant with #4), I didn't allow #4 to eat any gluten, except she had exposure through breastmilk until I cut gluten completely myself when she was 9 months old. He had allready been diagnosed with Hashi's at age 3.5. She obviously has not developped celiac, however, she did develop Hashi's at the same age he did (3). I'm pretty confident full blown celiac is in the cards for her should she start eating gluten. In fact I'm nervous because I'm not as rigid about cross-contamination issues with her or my other two as with her brother.

That all said, my in-laws used to be really distressed for the other children in my house having to endure eating gluten free all the time (the house is gluten free) just because one child has an issue... They blamed me of course, for imposing such a henous rule. But, 4 years have passed, and they have really come around. At least they respect the reason for the rule, not that any of them ever stopped eating gluten or anything lol!! My kid ws the 1st on either side of the family to get the diagnosis. Since then, I have a cousin and his mother (my aunt) diagnosed. But still nobody else wants to see the writing on the wall. Not my sister or my brother are concerned for their children... sigh...

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-11T01:24:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-cause-gluten-intolerance-in-our-kids.2960/post-44212>

I'd say the human species is gluten intolerant. We don't become it. We are. No matter if you eat it or not.

### NWgirl — 2012-08-11T02:12:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-cause-gluten-intolerance-in-our-kids.2960/post-44221>

Both my daughters have the celiac gene. One has just one of the genes and the other (they are fraternal twins) has BOTH of the genes. However the twin that has only the one celiac gene actually developed celiac disease at 15 years old. The other twin (with two genes) does not have it (yet).

My point is, the genes don't always switch on and sometimes it takes an illness or stress to turn them on. The girl that got celiac had mono and we suspect that illness switched on the gene.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-08-11T03:01:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-cause-gluten-intolerance-in-our-kids.2960/post-44231>

> **Souldanzer;44519 wrote:** I'd say the human species is gluten intolerant. We don't become it. We are. No matter if you eat it or not.

If you read Bill Davis (Wheatbelly) or many others in paleo land, they'd agree with that. And Davis points out repeatedly that our current testing for celiac is only ONE way to pick up major issues with gluten. He has worked with people who had completely incapacitating symptoms that resolved with gluten elimination but tested negative for celiac. I think it's pretty clear that not consuming gluten is required for optimal growth and development for all human beings.

### kathylu — 2012-08-11T03:15:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-cause-gluten-intolerance-in-our-kids.2960/post-44236>

> **KiwiLauren;44539 wrote:** If you read Bill Davis (Wheatbelly) or many others in paleo land, they'd agree with that. And Davis points out repeatedly that our current testing for celiac is only ONE way to pick up major issues with gluten. He has worked with people who had completely incapacitating symptoms that resolved with gluten elimination but tested negative for celiac. I think it's pretty clear that not consuming gluten is required for optimal growth and development for all human beings.

I tested negative for celiac, several times. However, I developed debilitating arthritis that cleared up completely with grain elimination. So, I'm certainly gluten/gliadin intolerant no matter what the blood tests show. Just sayin'.

### ColdBren — 2012-08-11T21:46:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-cause-gluten-intolerance-in-our-kids.2960/post-44377>

There is a subtlety of the question that doesn't seem to be addressed directly. Most of us agree gluten is bad overall. The question is does avoiding it completely at a young age create a greater sensitivity. I would like to know this too. It doesn't mean I will let my kid et it more, I just want to know th truth.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-08-12T00:04:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-cause-gluten-intolerance-in-our-kids.2960/post-44409>

> **ColdBren;44685 wrote:** There is a subtlety of the question that doesn't seem to be addressed directly. Most of us agree gluten is bad overall. The question is does avoiding it completely at a young age create a greater sensitivity. I would like to know this too. It doesn't mean I will let my kid et it more, I just want to know th truth.

My guess would be that avoiding it completely in childhood would mean that a person would not develop the symptoms (many of which become subtle, underground and not-apparently-linked-to-food) that go along with gluten consumption. In other words, creating a stronger, more optimal foundation of health. However, if that person started to eat gluten later in life, they may actually appear more 'sensitive' to it because the symptoms hadn't gone underground. So given what it does to gut health (make it more leaky), someone who consumes gluten earlier in life will have a longer time under their belt creating gut leakiness. They may not show symptoms, but the more years you pound your body with bad stuff, the more likely you are going to reap that later. But that's just my theory.

I also think it's pretty clear that some people are, because of their own genetics/epigenetics, more sensitive than others and will experience a much more obvious and greater negative effect immediately and in the long term. The problem is, I don't think we can easily tell where we/our children fall along that spectrum.

### Joann — 2012-08-16T10:27:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-cause-gluten-intolerance-in-our-kids.2960/post-45173>

I think the bottom line with gluten is that it causes inflammation whether or not a person gets gut symptoms.

**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis

### Adria — 2012-08-10T23:03:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/elderly-dog-in-pain-seeks-cold-water-news-article.2964/post-44198>

**Man cradles arthritic, sleeping dog in Lake Superior to ease his â€˜best friendâ€™sâ€™ pain**

John Unger holds his 19-year-old dog in cold water as he sleeps, to help cope with the dog's arthritis.

After 18 years of loyal companionship, a Wisconsin man is trying to comfort a dear friend through his final days of life.

A photo of John Unger holding his shepherd mix in a lake as the dog sleeps has gone viral, the image portraying the incredible bond between man and dog.

See the beautiful photo here:

[http://www.nydailynews.com/news/national/photo-john-unger-cradles-arthritic-sleeping-dog-lake-superior-ease-best-friend-pain-article-1.1133642](http://http://www.nydailynews.com/news/national/photo-john-unger-cradles-arthritic-sleeping-dog-lake-superior-ease-best-friend-pain-article-1.1133642)

# Thread: Understanding of Ketosis lacking

**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 13

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-11T03:49:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/understanding-of-ketosis-lacking.2967/post-44247>

So I had a bread stick and ....a piece of one and probably like four Kiwis today. I'm guessing that's enough to stop Ketosis? Does it screw up my Ketoadaption as well? Will I have to go below 20 g carbohydrates for 2 weeks to regain?

I wouldn't think something that takes 2 weeks to happen would be gone that fast...but...not sure how it works. I've probably read the answer before but I don't remember.

How much do you have to eat to stop Ketosis and/or Keto Adapation and is it the same amount of time to get back to that point every time? I'm guessing not...

Maybe one of these days I won't even feel like eating bread...but I still do sometimes at the moment. Sigh. I'm not too worried about it...I plan on eating well tomorrow just curious.

Thanks

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-08-11T05:13:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/understanding-of-ketosis-lacking.2967/post-44264>

Well I had some desserts...dang. I really don't feel for them...just had them at a function. Don't know if this helps, but I ate enormous amounts of Bubbies pickles (live cultures). It made my bowels flow, so hoping it was a quick detox of crap I put in my body. I had my usual seafood fare today as well...so hopefully it's not a 'all or none' situation.

I know Jack would not approve at all, and would imply that I have totally ruined things...that my choice was 'mediocre'. I am aware of the choice I made and try to live with it as best as possible. I am still interested in longevity.

It's such a fine line between Optimal and 'mediocre' and trying to 'make life work'. You know the life situations where you just feel compelled to to 'join in'. I try not to make excuses, and if it happens well then I just deal with it. I can't beat myself up, yet I can't let myself be mediocre either...fine line.

Overall the protocols of Dr. K are really helpful and I'm very very grateful for them! Many health problems are totally reversing for me, and not just that, but ultra health is increasing too.

This does not answer your question directly...but just thought I'd say some words in regards to some possible thoughts you might have swirling around your head regarding this whole life style as is the case with me. (But does get way easier with time...since February).

### Destiny — 2012-08-13T14:40:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/understanding-of-ketosis-lacking.2967/post-44661>

> **Raiken3712;44555 wrote:** So I had a bread stick and ....a piece of one and probably like four Kiwis today. I'm guessing that's enough to stop Ketosis? Does it screw up my Ketoadaption as well? Will I have to go below 20 g carbohydrates for 2 weeks to regain?

I believe your bread stick stopped ketosis for sure. How long it takes to get back in is very individual. Some people need to be very strick, some not as much.

I did not have any grains since last October. It took a lot for me to stop eating them. I never want to go through that again.

You can do it! Good luck!

### PrimalPam — 2012-08-14T18:04:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/understanding-of-ketosis-lacking.2967/post-44909>

The important thing about these cheats, or slip-ups (someone passed you a piece of cake at a party and before you knew it, it was gone) is that the very next time you eat, make it epi-Paleo.

Years of poor eating will take months, even years, to unravel. Just as, I believe, years of eating epi-Paleo will not unravel by one poor choice.

I was always of the "screw it, I already blew it" mindset: I'd indulge in one donut at the office, and the rest of the day would be "shot". Might as well stuff in all the garbage I can, I blew it at 8:00 a.m. These days, one slip-up leads me to, the very next time I eat (and not till I'm hungry!!!), epi-Paleo: a plate of shrimp, some CO, etc.

The thing I've found is that I can actually stop and THINK before I eat that poor choice, wherease prior to epi-Paleo, it would stay on my mind. Like, my brain now says, "No", and my body actually listens. So the desire to even eat those poor choices exists less and less.

### shilohman — 2012-08-14T18:55:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/understanding-of-ketosis-lacking.2967/post-44918>

Messing up ketosis is of little importance if you just upped your inflammation and possibly trashed any Leptin Sensitivity that you had and yes one bread stick can do that. I am speaking from personal experience here.

To quote from the character Dr. Switzer from the Bob Newhart Show, just two words, "STOP IT".

### cramernh — 2012-09-26T14:02:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/understanding-of-ketosis-lacking.2967/post-54390>

> **MooseMan;51997 wrote:** I may be wrong, but doesn't any amount of carbs end ketosis? The amount of carbs just determines how long until you return to ketosis?

I know of a patient right now who easily hits ketosis with 40g of healthier versions of CHO. I also have another patient who is about 25g and is lighting up medium-purple easily. We also have others who are 20g and are only hitting the 'trace' but we think it also has alot to do with the fact that they are water-crazy as well... they may have a false-low result because of the large amounts of water they consume.

Everyone will definitely be different.
