### kathylu — 2012-08-13T18:39:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-hope-some-of-you-go-to-this-blog-and-post-about-this.3004/post-44703>

> **Jack Kruse;45001 wrote:** One point he made that I thought was interesting.....he thinks the USA is the best system for health care. I wonder what you all think about that?

I do think our acute care is good. If I'm horribly injured in an accident I think shock-trauma will do an amazing job of stitching me back together. But talking about health care's failings in managing and treating chronic disease? It depresses me to even think about it. Every where I look I see people who are sick and slowly dying (some not so slowly) and I often think I could do a better job of helping them than most of their doctors obviously do. Is this an arrogant and misguided thought? Probably. However, is it true? Yup.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-13T19:16:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-hope-some-of-you-go-to-this-blog-and-post-about-this.3004/post-44707>

### chocolate — 2012-08-13T19:25:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-hope-some-of-you-go-to-this-blog-and-post-about-this.3004/post-44709>

> **Jack Kruse;45001 wrote:** One point he made that I thought was interesting.....he thinks the USA is the best system for health care. I wonder what you all think about that?

I think its the not the best. I keep finding studies from smaller countries that just want to take care of their own practically. It usually isn't big pharma studies and a lot more nutrition based. They don't have the USDA lobby the government and we suck as far as education goes. We have dumbasses that believe the commercials and people with a financial interest in sugar addiction fighting for the free will of people to buy it on food stamps, the real reason there is no sin tax on the stuff. Why isn't the cancer society doing PSA's on fructose and multiplication of cancer cells? The soda pop drinking crowd would believe anything on tv.

And forget the hospitals. As soon as the SEIU gets in there, the suffering increases exponentially and the place starts to smell. Can you imagine doctors with the teacher union mentality? OMG the politics involved in government posts is unbelievable. I learned that in Canada. The doc needed a little lobbying time with a higher up and my MIL was the perfect patient to get it for him.

### BJK77 — 2012-08-13T20:20:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-hope-some-of-you-go-to-this-blog-and-post-about-this.3004/post-44722>

> **kathylu;45012 wrote:** I do think our acute care is good. If I'm horribly injured in an accident I think shock-trauma will do an amazing job of stitching me back together. But talking about health care's failings in managing and treating chronic disease? It depresses me to even think about it. Every where I look I see people who are sick and slowly dying (some not so slowly) and I often think I could do a better job of helping them than most of their doctors obviously do. Is this an arrogant and misguided thought? Probably. However, is it true? Yup.

I agree that acute care in the US is pretty top notch, but considering the current state of our health chronic disease is and will become more the focal point. Unfortunately I think the US is severely lacking in both prevention and treatment of chronic disease.

### Lyndra — 2012-08-13T20:20:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-hope-some-of-you-go-to-this-blog-and-post-about-this.3004/post-44723>

> **kathylu;45012 wrote:** I do think our acute care is good. If I'm horribly injured in an accident I think shock-trauma will do an amazing job of stitching me back together. But talking about health care's failings in managing and treating chronic disease? It depresses me to even think about it. Every where I look I see people who are sick and slowly dying (some not so slowly) and I often think I could do a better job of helping them than most of their doctors obviously do. Is this an arrogant and misguided thought? Probably. However, is it true? Yup.

I also agree, acute care is wonderful but too many doctors do not pressure patients to live better, even when they know that's what is necessary. Case in point: relative, very overweight, smoker, beer drinker. He's very happy when he goes to the doc and they don't weigh him and talk to him about obesity. He's also very glad when they don't mention his smoking. They just prescribe him pills to control his high blood pressure and he thinks he's just fine. I'm a big believer in letting people make their own choices but I also believe in letting them live with the consequences.

One thought from Dr. Ernie really got me to wondering - paraphrasing, he said he couldn't just drop patients who failed to follow his advice. Why not? Imagine the psychological effect of your doctor telling you to find another doc because he wouldn't continue to "treat" you in an ineffective manner. I guess he needs those patients in his practice to make enough money to stay afloat. I'm with Dr. K, the system is going to break in a very big way and at that point we'll all have to do things very differently.

### Lyndra — 2012-08-13T20:23:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-hope-some-of-you-go-to-this-blog-and-post-about-this.3004/post-44725>

> **BJK77;45031 wrote:** I agree that acute care in the US is pretty top notch, but considering the current state of our health chronic disease is and will become more the focal point. Unfortunately I think the US is severely lacking in both prevention and treatment of chronic disease.

Dr. K may call me a negative thinker on this one, but I don't think most people really want to prevent & treat their chronic disease if it will make them uncomfortable. As in, changing their diet ("what, no bread or pasta???"), following circadian rhythms, or heaven forbid, doing CT. Just ain't gonna happen for most people.

### BJK77 — 2012-08-13T20:31:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-hope-some-of-you-go-to-this-blog-and-post-about-this.3004/post-44730>

> **Lyndra;45034 wrote:** Dr. K may call me a negative thinker on this one, but I don't think most people really want to prevent & treat their chronic disease if it will make them uncomfortable. As in, changing their diet ("what, no bread or pasta???"), following circadian rhythms, or heaven forbid, doing CT. Just ain't gonna happen for most people.

Yes and no. I agree with you that most people don't want to hear things that make them uncomfortable, i.e. no bread or pasta, go to sleep at sunset, etc, etc. However, I also think once studies have shown that these things are critical for optimal health that physicians have an obligation to inform their patients - regardless of whether or not it makes them uncomfortable.

I think many people would be more willing to listen to a physician than you or I as an average joe schmoe telling them how important these things are.

### CarolMorris — 2012-08-13T20:55:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-hope-some-of-you-go-to-this-blog-and-post-about-this.3004/post-44737>

In this PC world we live in, I for one salute and encourage bold statements from people who care. I'm sick of this "playing it safe mentality." Mediocrity begets mediocrity. have the courage to speak up and take a stand...if you're wrong you've still learned something. You may even find a gutsy neurosurgeon who's willing to take them all on and gift his wisdom to us!!I told my husband when we were dating..if you want mediocre find someone else..it's gotta be all in or nothing...30 years later and i'm still saying I'm the luckiest girl in the whole wide world!!!

### vlynnb — 2012-08-13T22:00:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-hope-some-of-you-go-to-this-blog-and-post-about-this.3004/post-44753>

Many sides to this issue. I've had very good PCPs tell me that after telling patients multiple times to quit smoking, drinking, overeating and eating SAD, etc. and having the patient ignore them that the physicians eventually do stop and just prescribe the medications they think might keep the patient alive a little longer, even if it is in a very ill state. One reasons that it is better to prescribe than to have them not come at all, not for the loss of income, but because the physicians do want to help the patient in any way the patient will accept.

Also, at least in a medically under-served area, I think it is considered unethical practice to refuse to treat a patient after he has become your patient, and could possible lead to liability. I think some do so by documenting that the patient refused to follow medical advice, much like one can check out of a hospital against medical advice (after signing lots of forms), but it is not something that physicians do without a lot of thought. Sometimes they just state that they can not do any more for the patient if they won't take advice and refer them to a specialist that wouldn't be needed if they changed their ways.

### ealachan — 2012-08-13T22:11:48+0300

I agree with you that most people don't want to hear things that make them uncomfortable, i.e. no bread or pasta, go to sleep at sunset, etc, etc. However, I also think once studies have shown that these things are critical for optimal health that physicians have an obligation to inform their patients - regardless of whether or not it makes them uncomfortable.
>
>
>
> I think many people would be more willing to listen to a physician than you or I as an average joe schmoe telling them how important these things are.

This, times 150 bazillion! I can talk to my husband and my parents about circadian rhythms until I'm blue in the face, but until they hear it from someone "official", I'm just blowing smoke to them.

### SeaHorse — 2012-08-13T22:19:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-hope-some-of-you-go-to-this-blog-and-post-about-this.3004/post-44757>

> > I learned that in Canada. The doc needed a little lobbying time with a higher up and my MIL was the perfect patient to get it for him.
>
> Chocolate...I lived in Canada for 26 years and it was definitely a mixed bag of benefits and detriments as a health system. I really liked that there was a "universal" health care...but it doesn't really work well at all for preventative wellness. The length of time it took for diagnostic testing was really criminal in many cases....people waiting months to get neuro testing (my best friends can give you an earful), and even in acute cases like my husband's 7 back surgeries it seemed like he had to wait until a disk ruptured before anybody sprang into action. He was told to wait three months for an MRI after a CATscan wasn't conclusive about needing surgery. So we actually went out of province and paid to go to a clinic in Ontario (we lived in the "have not provinces" of Prince Edward Island and Newfoundland) Lordy...I wish we had known about CT back in those days!!!! He didn't end up in a wheelchair, but that was way more good luck than good management. We were not exactly pushovers so we got good at being our own advocates.
>
>
>
> Anyway...rant over...but just to say that the shortsightedness of both the US and Canadian Health care paradigm is extreme. I really do believe that chronic diseases are too big a money maker for any systemic change to occur for a long time. However....individually we have all the power in the world to change our lives and that's how things are going to transform.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-08-13T23:57:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-hope-some-of-you-go-to-this-blog-and-post-about-this.3004/post-44774>

> **Jack Kruse;45001 wrote:** One point he made that I thought was interesting.....he thinks the USA is the best system for health care. I wonder what you all think about that?

I'd be interested in knowing what countries people would point to as having the 'best' system of health care. I live in New Zealand. Healthcare is provided for all citizens at a basic level (insurance is available for the wealthy and they get better hospitals, private rooms, etc). However, the health care provided is still the Western 'standard of care.' In addition to that, all the options Americans have available to them in terms of labs you can self-test via, cheap supplements for purchase and a plethora of alternative/BHRT/functional docs throughout the country are completely absent here. So I may have access to free healthcare but I don't have access to anything much beyond the Western standard. I think this is the typical case for those living in socialised medicine Western nations. My husband does a lot of work throughout Asia and it seems the 'Western' standard is rife there, too.

This is what makes me feel very strongly in agreement with Dr K's statement that patients must take control and responsibility. Otherwise, it is a worldwide system, with a strong pharmaceutical, money-making focus, that will not change unless we force it to.

# Thread: CT in the morning
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-morning.3006/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### NWgirl — 2012-08-13T06:20:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-morning.3006/post-44640>

Does anyone else have problems doing CT in the morning or middle of the day? I've done this about 3 times after a BAB, it feels fine doing it but I get so tired after that it prevents me from doing things during the day. Yesterday I CT'ed for almost one hour then took a cold shower to wash. Afterward I warmed up in the sun. Sounds nice, right? But I felt kind of achy and had to take a nap. This has happened 3 times.

I guess when I CT after dinner and then go to bed I'm sleeping through this phase. Anyone know what this could be?

### ealachan — 2012-08-13T12:28:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-morning.3006/post-44656>

I believe it's because CT ramps up your body's DHEA production, which can make you sleepy? I think I remember reading that, anyway...if someone calls bollocks on it, then nevermind me...

### x_danny_x — 2012-08-13T17:28:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-morning.3006/post-44688>

CT helps you sleep. It is good to have a good night rest. If you going to do CT in the morning, I will advise you to work out a little bit to help keep you from sleeping. Or just do CT at night.

I put some music or bring laptop to watch shows to help pass the time. Or bring a radio to listen to whatever you like. Make it as enjoyable as possible.

### MartiD — 2012-08-13T18:17:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-morning.3006/post-44694>

I have the opposite effect. I always do a cold bath in the morning after a cold shower and have lots of energy for about an hour or two afterwards. Still sleep solid at night too!

### Qodesh — 2012-08-13T20:34:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-morning.3006/post-44731>

I just did my first morning CT. My tub is on the back screen porch here in South Florida. I had fatty beef bacon breakfast, took about a 5 minute soak in 50 degrees, then sat in the warm morning sunshine with my coffee.

It was beautiful and made the morning heat bearable.

Not tired so far and that was about 5 hours ago

### PrimalPam — 2012-08-13T21:20:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-morning.3006/post-44743>

Happens to me too, so I'm wondering if increased DHEA is the mechanism.

CT makes me sleep really well; on the days I do CT, I sleep 8 hours straight. Other days, I will wake up in the middle of the night and fall back asleep. But on CT days, sleep straight through.

If I do CT earlier than usual then yes, it seems to make me more tired earlier.

### ealachan — 2012-08-13T21:37:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-morning.3006/post-44749>

I take a 20-minute cold shower every morning that makes me feel almost hyper, but if I take one at night right before bed, it will definitely make me sleepy. Interesting.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-08-13T22:25:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-morning.3006/post-44760>

chalk me up in the energized category. I can just feel that norepinephrine getting released into my bloodstream.

My morning CT is usually the first thing I do - directly from bed to shower or bath.

### NWgirl — 2012-08-14T03:12:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-morning.3006/post-44811>

Wow it looks like about 50/50! I will have to attribute it to the DHEA, thanks for all of your replies!

# Thread: Too Much Calcium Could Cause Kidney, Heart Problems, Researchers Say
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-calcium-could-cause-kidney-heart-problems-researchers-say.3014/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-08-13T19:50:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-calcium-could-cause-kidney-heart-problems-researchers-say.3014/post-44715>

[http://www.npr.org/blogs/health/2012/08/13/158506960/too-much-calcium-could-cause-kidney-heart-problems-researchers-say?](http://http://www.npr.org/blogs/health/2012/08/13/158506960/too-much-calcium-could-cause-kidney-heart-problems-researchers-say?)

> When it comes to a healthy diet â€” especially for women, and especially after menopause â€” nutritionists, doctors, everybody it seems, will tell you: calcium, calcium, calcium.
>
>
>
> Federal health officials recommend that women and men younger than 50 consume 1,000 milligrams of calcium per day. The recommendation goes up to 1,200 milligrams after age 70 for men and after menopause for women, when a major drop in estrogen causes bone loss.
>
>
>
> So, in a culture that often considers "more" to be "better," one might ask, if 1,200 milligrams of calcium is good, is 2,000 mg of calcium better?
>
>
>
>
>
> No, says Dr. Ethel C. Siris, director of the Toni Stabile Osteoporosis Center at Columbia University Medical Center in New York. "You need enough; you don't need extra," she says.
>
> "Extra calcium does you no good, and there is a small risk that if you take too much you might get a kidney stone," says Dr. Siris. That's because the body can only handle 600 milligrams of calcium at once. Extra calcium can build up in the bloodstream and, when excreted through kidneys in urine, it can cause a kidney stone.
>
>
>
> That's been known for a while. But recently, a few studies raised concern that excess calcium may also calcify coronary arteries in susceptible individuals and even precipitate heart attack.
>
>
>
>
>
> Robert Eckel, a cardiologist and endocrinologist at the University of Colorado, is a past president of the American Heart Association. While these studies are far from conclusive, and far more research needs to be done, he says they do raise the question about whether there's potentially some danger in over-the-counter calcium supplements that go beyond our usual dietary intake of calcium.
>
>
>
> "At this point in time, there's a bit of a signal" that should raise caution but remains highly controversial. "I don't think anyone has stepped up to say calcium supplements should be abandoned," says Eckel.
>
>
>
> That's particularly because calcium is so critical for bone health. The best plan of action, says Siris: Eat more calcium-rich foods.
>
>
>
> "If you're somebody who has a glass of milk with breakfast, that's 300 milligrams of calcium; a container of yogurt will give you another 200 to 300 milligrams; a couple of ounces of cheese will give you 200 to 300 milligrams," she says. For most healthy adults younger than 50, that's about all you need for bone health.
>
>
>
> If you don't eat dairy, Siris says there are plenty of other foods that also contain calcium. These include vegetables (like broccoli, bok choy and turnip greens), oranges, figs, salmon and sardines. Cereals and soy milk often have added calcium, along with added vitamin D, which is essential to help the body absorb calcium.
>
>
>
> So, estimate your daily intake of calcium from food, says Siris, and then calculate whether you need to take an extra supplement. You may just need 300 or 600 milligrams of calcium extra, and you may not even need that every day.

### Joann — 2012-08-13T23:55:47+0300

# Thread: GENETICS, or EPIGENTICS
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/genetics-or-epigentics.3017/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-08-13T20:49:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/genetics-or-epigentics.3017/post-44736>

[http://www.nature.com/news/tree-s-leaves-genetically-different-from-its-roots-1.11156](http://http://www.nature.com/news/tree-s-leaves-genetically-different-from-its-roots-1.11156)

> Tree's leaves genetically different from its roots
>
>
>
> Cottonwood trees show genetic differences across individuals as well as within populations.
>
>
>
> Ed Yong
>
>
>
> 10 August 2012
>
>
>
> Black cottonwood trees show a surprising amount of genetic diversity.
>
>
>
>
>
> Black cottonwood trees (Populus trichocarpa) can clone themselves to produce offspring that are connected to their parents by the same root system. Now, after the first genome-wide analysis of a tree, it turns out that the connected clones have many genetic differences, even between tissues from the top and bottom of a single tree.
>
>
>
> â€œThe variation within a tree is as great as the variation across unrelated trees,â€ says Ken Paige, an evolutionary biologist at the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign, who led the team that made the discovery
>
>
>
> â€œThis could change the classic paradigm that evolution only happens in a population rather than at an individual level,â€ says Brett Olds, a biologist in Paigeâ€™s laboratory, who presented the research at the 2012 Ecological Society of America Annual Meeting in Portland, Oregon.
>
>
>
> Such somatic mutations â€” those that occur in cells other than sperm or eggs â€” are familiar to horticulturalists, who have long bred new plant varieties by grafting mutant branches onto â€˜normalâ€™ stocks. But until now, no one has catalogued the total number of somatic mutations in an individual plant. â€œMost of the research hasnâ€™t been genome-wide. Itâ€™s usually a certain region or certain genes,â€ says Olds. â€œThis is the first one across entire genomes.â€
>
>
>
> The black cottonwood is ideal for such a study. It lives for up to 200 years and grows to 30â€“50 metres tall, so its tissues are separated by long distances and long spans of growth â€” and it forms connected clones. In 2006, it became the first tree to have its full genome sequenced1.
>
> Parent clones
>
>
>
> Olds collected samples from 11 parent-clone sets of black cottonwood parents and their connected offspring. For each one, he sequenced the full genomes of tissues from the highest buds, the lowest branches and the roots. â€œFive years ago, this kind of project would have been prohibitively expensive,â€ says Ari Novy, a plant biologist at the US Botanic Garden in Washington DC, who was not involved in the study. â€œOlds has made great use of next-generation sequencing technology.â€
>
>
>
> When Olds compared these sequences to the reference cottonwood genome, he found 188,406 mutations that were unique to just one tissue sample, differing even from other parts of the same tree. Of these, 8,629 mutations fell within protein-encoding DNA sequences and 5,529 resulted in amino-acid changes in the encoded proteins.
>
>
>
> â€œWhen people study plants, theyâ€™ll often take a cutting from a leaf and assume that it is representative of the plantâ€™s genome,â€ says Olds. â€œThat may not be the case. You may need to take multiple tissues.â€
>
>
>
> Other surprises emerged when Olds used the tissue-specific amino-acid changes to build a family tree of the different cottonwood tissues. In one tree, the top buds of the parent and offspring were genetically closer to each other than to their respective roots or lower branches. In another tree, the top bud was closer to the reference cottonwood genome than to any of the other tissues from the same individual.
>
>
>
> The tissue-specific mutations affected mainly genes involved in cell death, immune responses, metabolism, DNA binding and cell communication. Olds think that this may be because many of the mutations are harmful, and the tree reacts by destroying the mutated tissues or altering its metabolic pathways and the way it controls its genes, which leads to further mutations.
>
>
>
> **The findings have parallels to cancer studies. Earlier this year, scientists showed2 that separate parts of the same tumour can evolve independently and build up distinct genetic mutations, meaning that single biopsies give only a narrow view of the tumourâ€™s diversity3.**

### chocolate — 2012-08-15T01:51:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/genetics-or-epigentics.3017/post-44967>

[http://www.psypost.org/2012/08/remaking-history-a-new-take-on-how-evolution-has-shaped-modern-europeans-13350](http://http://www.psypost.org/2012/08/remaking-history-a-new-take-on-how-evolution-has-shaped-modern-europeans-13350)

Remaking history: A new take on how evolution has shaped modern Europeans

> Investigators reporting in the Cell Press journal Trends in Genetics say that new analytical techniques are changing long-held, simplistic views about the evolutionary history of humans in Europe.
>
>
>
> Their findings indicate that many cultural, climatic, and demographic events have shaped genetic variation among modern-day European populations and that the variety of those mechanisms is more diverse than previously thought.
>
>
>
> Recent advances in paleogenetics are providing never-before-seen glimpses into the complex evolution of humans in Europe, helping researchers piece together the events that ultimately created what is now known as modern man. Following the period when ice sheets were at their maximum extension across the earth (between 27,000 and 16,000 years ago), hunter-gatherer populations re-colonized most parts of Europe.
>
>
>
> Then around 8,000 years ago, the first farming populations appeared on the continent during the so-called Neolithic transition. For several thousand years, two separate modes of life coexisted in Europe: hunter-gatherer populations continued to rely on wild food resources, while farming populations had an entirely different demographic profile and lifestyle that consisted of domesticated crops and livestock, pottery, housing, and storage technology.

### chocolate — 2012-08-20T18:08:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/genetics-or-epigentics.3017/post-45853>

[http://www.nature.com/news/photosynthesis-like-process-found-in-insects-1.11214](http://http://www.nature.com/news/photosynthesis-like-process-found-in-insects-1.11214)

Nature | News

Photosynthesis-like process found in insects

Aphids may have a rudimentary sunlight-harvesting system.

> The biology of aphids is bizarre: they can be born pregnant and males sometimes lack mouths, causing them to die not long after mating. In an addition to their list of anomalies, work published this week indicates that they may also capture sunlight and use the energy for metabolic purposes.
>
>
>
> Aphids are unique among animals in their ability to synthesize pigments called carotenoids. Many creatures rely on these pigments for a variety of functions, such as maintaining a healthy immune system and making certain vitamins, but all other animals must obtain them through their diet. Entomologist Alain Robichon at the Sophia Agrobiotech Institute in Sophia Antipolis, France, and his colleagues suggest that, in aphids, these pigments can absorb energy from the Sun and transfer it to the cellular machinery involved in energy production1.
>
>
>
> Although unprecedented in animals, this capability is common in other kingdoms. Plants and algae, as well as certain fungi and bacteria, also synthesize carotenoids, and in all of these organisms the pigments form part of the photosynthetic machinery.
>
> Home-made harvesters
>
>
>
> Taking their cue from the 2010 finding2 that the high levels of carotenoids found in aphids are homegrown, Robichon and his team set out to investigate why the insects make such metabolically expensive chemicals.
>
> Related stories
>
>
>
>
>
> Carotenoids are responsible for aphid pigmentation, and anaphid's colour determines the kind of predators that can see it. The body colour of Robichon's lab aphids is affected by environmental conditions, with the cold favouring green aphids, optimal conditions resulting in orange ones and white ones appearing when the population is large and faced with limited resources.
>
>
>
> When the researchers measured the aphids’ levels of ATP — the ‘currency’ of energy transfer in all living things — the results were striking. Green aphids, which contain high levels of carotenoids, make significantly more ATP than do white ones, which are almost devoid of these pigments. Moreover, ATP production rose when the orange insects — which contain an intermediate amount of carotenoids — were placed in the light, and fell when they were moved into the dark.
>
>
>
> The researchers went on to crush the orange aphids and purify their carotenoids, demonstrating that it was these extracts that could absorb light and pass this energy on.
>
>
>
> One of the authors, Maria Capovilla, another entomologist at the Sophia Institute, insists that much more work is needed before scientists can be sure that aphids truly photosynthesize, but the findings certainly throw up that possibility.
>
>
>
> The way that carotene molecules are arranged in the animals adds weight to that hypothesis. The pigments form a layer between 0–40 micrometres deep under the insect's cuticle, putting them in the perfect position to capture the Sun's light.

### chocolate — 2012-08-21T22:34:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/genetics-or-epigentics.3017/post-46115>

[http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/08/21/research.reveals.unique.solution.gene.regulation?](http://http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/08/21/research.reveals.unique.solution.gene.regulation?)

> Research reveals unique solution to gene regulation
>
> Published: Tuesday, August 21, 2012 - 12:37 in Biology & Nature
>
>
>
> Research on a unique vertebrate called the sea lamprey shows that more than a thousand genes are shed during its early development. These genes are paradoxically lost all throughout the developing embryo except in a specialized compartment called "primordial germ cells" or PGCs. The PGCs can be thought of as embryonic stem cells and are used, ultimately, for making the next generation of lampreys. Based on computational analysis, a significant number of genes that are lost in the embryo have signatures of "pluripotency," which suggests that they could also have undesirable effects if they were inadvertently turned on in the body. In effect, by undergoing programmed genome rearrangement and gene loss during embryogenesis, the sea lamprey "seals" the genes away in the small germline compartment so they cannot be misexpressed and thereby create untoward problems (such as development of cancer, for example). The study was completed at the Benaroya Research Institute at Virginia Mason (BRI) and recently published as a featured article in Current Biology, along with an outside commentary.
>
>
>
> The article authors are Jeramiah Smith, PhD, former postdoctoral fellow at BRI and now Assistant Professor of Biology at the University of Kentucky; Chris Amemiya, PhD, Principal Investigator at BRI and Professor of Biology, University of Washington; Evan Eichler, PhD, University of Washington Genome Sciences Professor; and Carl Baker, Research Scientist, University of Washington.
>
>
>
> The discovery builds on the group's previous work published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences in 2009. "Our new research confirms that lampreys experience rampant programmed genome rearrangement and losses during early development," says Dr. Amemiya. "The genes are restricted to the germline compartment suggesting a deeper biological strategy in order to regulate the genome for highly precise, normal functioning. The strategy removes the possibility that the genes will be expressed in deleterious ways. Humans, on the other hand, must contain these genes through other "epigenetic" mechanisms that are not fool-proof.
>
>
>
> There are several implications of this work:
>
>
>
> By understanding how programmed genome arrangement occurs so pervasively in lampreys, scientists can gain insight into how vertebrate genomes can remain stable and what genetic factors contribute to this stability.
>
> Studies in distantly related species can provide unique insights into fundamental biological concepts and may be translatable to human health.
>
>
>
>
>
> Identifying the molecular and developmental mechanism of how lampreys regulate their genome may have implications for disease treatment.
>
>
>
> Sea lampreys are "basal" vertebrates that lack jaws and have unique properties that are of interest to scientists. **This includes a completely different genetic toolkit for their adaptive immune system, which was also discovered, in part, by Amemiya's group, as well as remarkable powers of regeneration that allow them to completely recover from a severed spinal cord.**

I wonder if they have lamprey pellets at the pet store?

### chocolate — 2012-08-23T03:22:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/genetics-or-epigentics.3017/post-46458>

[http://www.latimes.com/health/boostershots/la-heb-older-fathers--genetic-mutations-autism-schizophrenia20120822,0,4956716.story](http://http://www.latimes.com/health/boostershots/la-heb-older-fathers--genetic-mutations-autism-schizophrenia20120822,0,4956716.story)

Older fathers pass on more new genetic mutations to offspring

> Men who become fathers later in life pass on more brand-new genetic mutations to their offspring, a study has found — probably contributing to disorders such as autism and schizophrenia in the next generation.
>
>
>
> The finding, published online Wednesday in the journal Nature, buttresses earlier observations that rates of autism and some other disorders are more prevalent in children born of older fathers, sometimes by a factor of two or more, experts said. Though this has been observed for years from population studies, scientists had not known what lay behind it.
>
>
>
> The new research, made possible by recent advances in DNA-sequencing technology, also should help correct an overemphasis on the riskiness of women giving birth at older ages, some researchers said.
>
>
>
> Although older mothers are at higher risk for complications such as diabetes during pregnancy and are more likely to have children with chromosomal disorders such as Down syndrome, the study found that practically all of the new mutations detected in children came from the father.
>
>
>
> And the older the father, the more mutations he passed on.
>
>
>
> A man aged 29.7 at the time he fathered a child contributed 63 new mutations on average to his offspring, the authors
>
> found, and a man aged 46.2 contributed 126 mutations — a doubling, the authors calculated.
>
>
>
> Many of the mutations would confer no effect either for good or ill on the children, scientists noted. But some would — and that is significant because in developed countries there has been a shift over the decades toward older men fathering children, said study senior author Dr. Kari Stefansson.
>
>
>
> Stefansson, who is a human geneticist and neurologist at the University of Iceland and the company deCODE Genetics in ReykjavÃ­k, noted for example that the average age of Iceland's fathers at the time of a child’s conception was 34.9 in 1900, falling to 27.9 in 1980, then rising back up again to 33 in 2011.
>
>
>
> “Similar changes have taken place all over the Western world,” Stefansson said. “It’s very likely to have made meaningful contributions to increased diagnoses of autism in our society. What percentage is due to that and what percentage is due to increased focus on diagnosis, I cannot tell you.”
>
>
>
> Stefansson and coauthors sequenced the entire genomes of 78 so-called “trios” — father, mother and child — many times over to detect tiny mutations in which a single “letter” in the DNA code had been altered. Of the children, 44 had received a diagnosis of autism spectrum disorder and 21 had schizophrenia. The genetic codes of five grandchildren were sequenced, too.
>
>
>
> **The scientists were able to identify stretches of DNA that had come from the father or mother. But they also could detect new mutations in the child that did not exist in the genome of either parent.**
>
>
>
> To be sure that these were bona fide new mutations and not an error of DNA sequencing, the genome of each person was sequenced many times over and the genomes of 1,859 other Iceland residents were sequenced as well to exclude mutations that already existed in the general population.
>
>
>
> In addition to the research article, check out a podcast featuring Stefansson, an accompanying commentary by evolutionary geneticist Alexey Kondrashov of the University of Michigan, and a Nature news report about the research.

# Thread: Epi-paleo Rx beats the Paleo Solution into the American Heart Association literature
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-rx-beats-the-paleo-solution-into-the-american-heart-association-literature.3020/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-13T23:35:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-rx-beats-the-paleo-solution-into-the-american-heart-association-literature.3020/post-44772>

WOW! http://circ.ahajournals.org/content/106/3/289.full

### AKMan — 2012-08-19T19:06:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-rx-beats-the-paleo-solution-into-the-american-heart-association-literature.3020/post-45719>

Notice the study group ate mainly fresh water fish. I just spent a week in NW Ohio involved in an archeological dig of paleo indian artifacts. We found lots of fishing and fish butchering tools. The skulls showed perfect dental arches and teeth with no sign of decay. This area of the midwest later became known as the 'goiter belt' due to it's lack of iodine in the soil. I'll bet the paleo indians didn't have a problem with iodine deficiency. I'm sure their diet consisted soley of freshwater fish and mussells, venison, small game, birds, and locally harvested plants with no saltwater seaweed.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-19T19:17:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-rx-beats-the-paleo-solution-into-the-american-heart-association-literature.3020/post-45722>

Layer this with what Dali Dula posted in the Brain Gut series about his trip to Cape Cod. Can see any synergy modern man has lost?

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-20T01:12:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-rx-beats-the-paleo-solution-into-the-american-heart-association-literature.3020/post-45762>

> **Jack Kruse;46097 wrote:** Layer this with what Dali Dula posted in the Brain Gut series about his trip to Cape Cod. Can see any synergy modern man has lost?

This gives us even more incentive to get out and camp away from the bright lights and embrace nature...

### kathylu — 2012-08-20T17:29:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-rx-beats-the-paleo-solution-into-the-american-heart-association-literature.3020/post-45847>

I wish they had included a tribe with a meat based diet and no seafood. Then the associations would have been even clearer. For instance what if there is no difference in leptin levels between the seafood group and a meat based group? Then all you could infer would be that high quality, non-vegetarian sources of protein lower leptin levels. However, if the vegetarians have high leptin levels, meat eaters have moderate leptin levels and seafood diets produce the lowest leptin levels, that would be pretty spectacular.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-20T18:27:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-rx-beats-the-paleo-solution-into-the-american-heart-association-literature.3020/post-45860>

Kathylu it was done in 2002 before any of the Cunnane data was out......I still was shocked that this data made it into a AHA ACA journal ten yrs ago.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-21T02:58:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-rx-beats-the-paleo-solution-into-the-american-heart-association-literature.3020/post-45960>

> **Shijin13;46139 wrote:** This gives us even more incentive to get out and camp away from the bright lights and embrace nature...

You know, my experience living in a minimalist mountain cabin for a little over a year really brought it home to me. I never could pinpoint what happened up there to me until I started studying Dr K's blog.... and now it's just all coming together. The perfect light cycles, the cold showers, the minimal heating in the winter, no electro magnetic radiation and such.... nature sounds. Quiet. So many things and still discovering more. My eating was still so bad especially in the beginning but wow so many other things started to resolve... the bad sleep, the messy hormones, I was PMS free within a couple months or so. I think there are way more mismatches in the way we live (city etc) than we realize at this time.

> Antibody Panel:
>
> Thyr Perox Ab....... 26.4** Range 0.0 - 35.0 IU/ml
>
> Thyroglob Ab......... < 20.0** Range 0.0 - 40.0 IU/ml
>
>
>
>
>
> Dr K said during the webinar that \*any\* level of antibodies indicates hashi's/AI.... is that any level above range or any level above 0.0?
>
>
>
> My TSH went up from 1.6 in December '11. This test was taken a couple weeks after major bout with ED while the previous one was taken after I had not engaged in any ED behaviors for 2 months or so.
>
>
>
> I'm guessing I need T3. That was expected. Not sure why the TSH suddenly went up. It's been dropping for years now. I'm going to dig out my older labs. Don't know much about the reverse T3 result either. What's a desired range for that?
>
>
>
> Thanks.... any input appreciated

Your labs look very much like mine except my FT4 is a bit better. Your ratio is a bit better than mine but not by much. I am taking Cytomel.

Not sure about the antibodies. I had some like you and Dr. K did not comment on them.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-14T03:10:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-44810>

Your TPO antibodies are so close to the range, if I were you, I would act-as-if and go auto-immune all the way! I think you are doing that already, right? You FT4 s/b in the high middle to top of range and FT3 s/b at the top. You are low in both. Not too bad though in the FT4 but you may not be converting that into FT3 because that is an amount that usually causes tons of fatigue and other issues. For me fatigue always comes first if I am not getting enough meds.

You may be converting that T4 to rT3 as your ratio is 10.7 and should be >20 ([http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/)). The conversion happens in the liver. Still working on understanding that whole process myself.

It looks like you need some thyroid support but that is looking only at the thyroid and not any other issues. If you are low in progesterone, supplementing there helps the thyroid a bit too.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-08-14T03:18:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-44816>

+1 on what Patty Cakes has said. Looks like you're not converting properly and perhaps the TSH is reacting to that by trying to stimulate the thyroid more? Maybe, since Dr K said they fluctuate a lot, coinciding with a stressful period meant a rise in TSH and a shunting of T4 to RT3? Your RT3 isn't awful, just isn't great either. You're already on a strict A1 protocol, so that's not an issue. Have you had antibodies tested before? It's possible you are actually reducing this number through your diet/CT/etc. Do you already take thyroid support?

### Lyndra — 2012-08-14T23:40:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-44950>

Your situation seems to be very similar to mine. From what I understand, T3-only is the right answer as you don't need more T4 and likely conversion to RT3. You've already tried T3 in the form of Cytomel and couldn't take it. Maybe you could try a generic, but I can tell you based on my experience, that may be a problem. I tried it and had non-stop headaches (my head seems to be my achilles heel so to speak). There is a whole regimen out there on using T3 so you might want to investigate that ([http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/dosing-with-t3-only/](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/dosing-with-t3-only/)).

### tweety — 2012-08-15T04:02:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-44990>

> **Souldanzer;45308 wrote:** Thanks Patty, any idea what starting dose I am requesting and how I'm going to increase it? I'm reading stopthethyroidmadness right now.

I started compounded T3 in July, and was put on 5mcg for two weeks then bumped to 10. It worked very well, but watch out for the trap of being too low. At first 5 felt great, and 10 even better. But after a few weeks at 10 I was sleepy again. I probably should've bumped up again. I struggled with brain fog alot and completely overlooked the issue of dosing.

Now I am trying NDT. I am still on entry dose, bump up again later this week. Still groggy and not mentally awake. Others feel physically tired or something else entirely. Good luck with it, and maybe these personal experiences shed some light on the subject for you.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-15T17:58:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-45028>

What Tweety said sounds right for starting. I switched from NDT to T3 only and already had been on meds for a couple of years so I started higher than that. You want to start low and work your way up. If you start too high and your body goes hyper you will be miserable. Start low and work your way up to the necessary dose.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-18T02:47:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-45478>

older labs:

_**08/07/2006

no TSH

Free T3 266 Range 230 - 420

Free T4 1.0 Range 0.8 - 1.8

doc puts me on 1 grain Armour

_**8/30/2007

TSH: 2.770 Range 0.465 - 4.680

different doc confiscates my Armour

_**12/10/2009**_**

TSH: 2.808

**12/02/2011******_****

TSH: 1.660 Range 0.450 - 4.500

couple of months or so after starting leptin reset

08/06/2012******_****

TSH........ 3.66 Range 0.36 - 3.74 uIM/ml

preceded by high dose iodine supplementation w/o selenium supps

Free T4......1.07 Range 0.76 - 1.46 ng/dl

Free T3...... 2.41 Range 2.18 - 3.98 pg/ml

reverse T3........ 225 Range 90 - 350 pg/ml

Antibody Panel:

Thyr Perox Ab....... 26.4 Range 0.0 - 35.0 IU/ml

Thyroglob Ab......... < 20.0 Range 0.0 - 40.0 IU/ml

I feel like something is a little amiss. My TSH was steady or dropping as I was moving along in my AN recovery. I get it that my earliest labs that don't show TSH but only free T3 and T4 are kinda sad - but I was still really really sick with my eating disorder during that time.

Then comes along high dose iodine supplementation and suddenly my TSH goes sky wards? Been reading here [http://perfecthealthdiet.com/2011/05/iodine-and-hashimotos-thyroiditis-part-i/](http://http://perfecthealthdiet.com/2011/05/iodine-and-hashimotos-thyroiditis-part-i/) and apparently TSH can rise with high dose iodine.... and I went up to 150mg/day rather suddenly. Over a couple of weeks. The test was drawn a few days after I lowered it again I think. I think I lowered it to 50mg/day. Then stopped b/c of confusion whether or not I should be taking iodine. The link also talks about high-dose iodine supplementation increasing TPO antibodies and that it will go away if iodine is stopped. They talk about a link between selenium deficiency/excess and this reaction (elevated TSH and antibodies).

> Conclusion
>
>
>
> Excess iodine intake can cause an autoimmune thyroiditis that bears all the characteristics of Hashimoto’s. However, in animal studies this occurs only if selenium is deficient or in excess. Similarly, in animal studies very high iodine intake can exacerbate a pre-existing autoimmune thyroiditis, but only if selenium is deficient or in excess.

**

> With optimal selenium status, thyroid follicles are healthy, goiter is eliminated, and autoimmune markers like Th1/Th2 ratio and CD4+/CD8+ ratio are normalized over a wide range of iodine intake. It seems that optimizing selenium intake provides powerful protection against autoimmune thyroid disease, and provides tolerance of a wide range of iodine intakes.

I get it, one can acquire Hashi's at any time pretty much if we expose ourselves to gut damaging substances (is that correct?). So I guess I could have acquired it between December '11 and August '12. That is possible. A bit unlikely considering that I was taking the high dose iodine right before this test? Without supplementing selenium?

Dr K said no need to supplement selenium b/c of my seafood-rich diet. But I had anorexia for..... so long. Maybe I still am deficient?

How valid really is this thyroid antibody panel considering the "unusual" TSH result relative to my TSH history and in combination with my (very) high-dose iodine supplementation right before the test?** I did take a few selenium pills during that time..... but stopped b/c of allergic reaction.

I'm interested to see what Jack tells you about Spectracell. I had it done late last year and the results made me think I was doing great with Vit D3 but then I realized their values are a percentage compared to a control that is considered "optimal." I scored 69% on Spectracell D3 with a reference range greater than 50%, but a few months later a blood test showed I was at 43. So now I'm rather skeptical/confused about how to interpret the Spectracell results since there's no way to tell what they consider to be optimal.

not really angry at you I was kinda joking.... well, sometimes it bugs me that I'm stuck with my reaction to your answer but that doesn't make the answer invalid. It's more like I'm angry at my situation and I want it solved now and then I'm being told to wait.... which You're right, that's my choice whether or not I'm going to wait.

I guess I don't understand your reasoning here.... are you saying if I have a or various gross nutritional deficiencies that's not important right now? What is it not important for.... my road to optimal final destination or how I live my life today?

B/c how I live my life today is kinda important to me. Very important. I'm only going to live today once and once tomorrow rolls around today is going to be lost. I do want to enjoy the journey and not be too focused on the destination so I want to do what I can to make that journey enjoyable - now. I know you (and some of my significant others as well) tell me to not overwhelm myself.... well, you people all have no idea what I am capable of. None of you has ever had the kind of ED I had. It makes you tough. Very tough. We're some of the toughest people you will ever meet imo. I might face plant and I might cry out loud and I might complain and yell and scream and cry. And then I go to sleep cut my losses and start over tomorrow. I've crashed so often that crashing or failure doesn't mean much to me anymore. I count my successes at the end of the day and lately there has been a whole lot of plenty in that department. I don't care if I throw up once after a stressful medical procedure.... who cares, I used to live with my head in the toilet without any obvious stress. I usually see when the red flags for overwhelm go up or someone will tell me and then I take a breather. Willingly.

What do I need to do to show you that I \*can\* handle it all.... that in fact, that is the approach I \*need\* b/c I've had a life of waiting so far.... waiting to get better - sound familiar?

I'm sorry  Not angry at all. Just..... lost.

You just went about attacking it all at once pretty much. Maybe with some advanced physician wisdom thrown into the mix.

> Good doctoring isn't writing a Rx or a slick operation, but helping them find themselves, and their inner guru, is what we should order up.

I'm at odds with this so much b/c my inner guru is telling me different on some things.... and the things I have done so far though advised not to - I have gotten useful stuff out of them.

I can see how things getting turned into "b/c Jack told me to do so" is a bit upsetting and incorrect. I think I/we go there when I/we don't understand the why. And in this case I don't understand why I'm being advised to wait. I'd like to b/c I highly value Dr K's input and it helps my inner guru to make good decisions.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-21T05:10:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-45998>

Patty or anyone, my doc rx-ed 25mcg/day of T3 and recommended I split this up into two doses, morning and noon. Is this ok? Or should I start with only half a pill at 12.5mcg per day and see how I do with it first? How long do I stay at one dose? I think I read 2 weeks and then raise until I feel well or hyper, then back off?

The compounding pharmacist had an issue with me splitting the pills and suggested I get the exact rx from my doc instead and take two pills a day b/c splitting the pills would be inaccurate. You think that is a concern? Getting less pills at a higher dosage is half as expensive so I stuck with the 25mcg pills.

### Destiny — 2012-08-21T13:45:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-46018>

> **Souldanzer;46382 wrote:** Patty or anyone, my doc rx-ed 25mcg/day of T3 and recommended I split this up into two doses, morning and noon. Is this ok? Or should I start with only half a pill at 12.5mcg per day and see how I do with it first? How long do I stay at one dose? I think I read 2 weeks and then raise until I feel well or hyper, then back off?
>
>
>
> The compounding pharmacist had an issue with me splitting the pills and suggested I get the exact rx from my doc instead and take two pills a day b/c splitting the pills would be inaccurate. You think that is a concern? Getting less pills at a higher dosage is half as expensive so I stuck with the 25mcg pills.

I tried the first dose of 12.5 mcg and my BP and heart rate shot up. So, I split my pill into four of 6.25 mcg and started to dose at 6.25 mcg twice a day. Then, I tried to go to 6.25mcg x 3 per day five days later and had to back off to a dosing twice a day. I am still there and doing well on it. You need to try it and see what it does to you. Patty Cakes had a good recommendation to watch your BP, HR, temp. and symptoms to judge how your dosing is going.

There also is no way I will wake up during the night to take my T3 (still trying to fix my sleep!!!). Do you guys think it will be ok if I take my dose sublingually right after waking in the AM and then go and prepare my BAB? Or am I ruining it all that way?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-21T22:53:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-46121>

> **Souldanzer;46382 wrote:** Patty or anyone, my doc rx-ed 25mcg/day of T3 and recommended I split this up into two doses, morning and noon. Is this ok? Or should I start with only half a pill at 12.5mcg per day and see how I do with it first? How long do I stay at one dose? I think I read 2 weeks and then raise until I feel well or hyper, then back off?
>

You think that is a concern? Getting less pills at a higher dosage is half as expensive so I stuck with the 25mcg pills.

Sorry I was out of town and then my oral surgery hole got a piece of somethings tuck in it, got infected, and on and on. But no antibitoics here, my body (or me) somehow go it flushed out and now I am just a little chipmunked out but healing very fast! Sorry if that was gross to anyone!

Totally agree with Destiny. Start the first doses out slow and increase as you can. Don't forget to take the vitals (HR, BP, and temp) as they can be indicators of too much or not enough. You are pretty sensitive and may notice things going on in your body w/o the vitals but they usually help. I was weird and my BP would start to climb and get in the high range when it was time for a dose. It took me a while to figure that out and once I did I changed things up a bit and now my BP is normal and HR is down almost 20 pts!!!

Also I take my first dose early in the morning a couple of hours before I wake. It helps to not get it mixing with the HCL, probiotics, and other stuff you may take on an empty stomach. I do all my doses sublingually except for that morning one. I set my alarm and swallow the dose being barely awake and go right back to sleep.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-21T23:07:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-46123>

Ah, thanks everybody  You guys are awesome. I will go slow and watch my vitals. I'll take my AM dose right when I wake up (most days I don't get up right away when I wake but keep resting for an hour or so... maybe I could take it then, if I'm awake enough) and I'll do it sublingually. My rx is being shipped to me b/c it was really difficult to find a compounding pharmacy that makes T3 without a filler I'm allergic to. I'm going to be getting some good doses of probiotics, lol! It's in my progesterone AND my T3 now!

### Lyndra — 2012-08-21T23:24:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-46129>

> **Souldanzer;46508 wrote:** Ah, thanks everybody  You guys are awesome. I will go slow and watch my vitals. I'll take my AM dose right when I wake up (most days I don't get up right away when I wake but keep resting for an hour or so... maybe I could take it then, if I'm awake enough) and I'll do it sublingually. It's in my progesterone AND my T3 now!

I devised a system to help prevent me from double-dosing (which I might do if I take it when I'm too groggy and can't remember I already had a dose). After I take my Armour, I'll move the pill bottle to another location. If I try to take it again, I'll notice it's not where it should be, and that means I already had my daily dose. Of course that means you need to reset the location after you get up for the day. There's always a potential flaw in those kinds of systems...ask me how I know .

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-21T23:39:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-46136>

I set my alarm on my phone to remind me. If my alarm goes off and I just ate then I will reset the alarm for an hour later. I have so many clock times set in my alarm function on my phone that I don't use on a daily basis. I have manipulated my doing times throughout this T3 process to find the best dose and need these alarm times in case of things, like eating, conflicting with the dosing schedule. It works great for me.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-28T18:40:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-47796>

> **Souldanzer;48222 wrote:** Might be a good idea. I took half a 25mcg cap this AM and going to do a quarter one at noon. Right now I feel good

I agree w/ Destiny. If you feel good but then start to feel sluggish/hypo again you can add the third dose or up the second dose after 4-7 days. I think you can keep upping it about that same time period. But one thing you want to keep in mind is that if you are clearing rT3 it will take 8-12 weeks to get it out of your system. In the mean time those rT3 guys are clogging up your T3 receptors. I'm having a little fogginess this morn but I think you can go hyper during this time because you body may not know what to do with all that T3 since the receptors are already full.

After five months on T3 only I have yet to go hyper but I am very careful in my dosing. I always suggest taking the vitals like HR, BP, temp to help determine if another dose or raise is best. My BP and HR go up when I need a dose or a raise. I tracked it every hour for the first month or so just to make sure. I wanted to clear that rT3 ASAP! It's all gone now!

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-28T19:05:02+0300

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-28T19:23:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-47803>

I know what ya mean about backed up on reading! I know there must be other things that rT3 comes from but I only know dysfunctional adrenals and iron issues. Here's my mini explanation: Your body makes some T3 on it's own and also makes it from converting T4. I believe most of this is done in the liver. So if your body is having adrenal issues, for example, some of the T4 gets converted to rT3 which your cells take up but you do not get the benefits from like you would when the cells take up T3. I hope that makes sense since my brain is having it's own set of issues this morning in the functioning realm.

I haven't perused this site but I have the author's book and it has been helpful in working with and understanding taking T3. But his site may have some info for you.

[http://recoveringwitht3.com/](http://http://recoveringwitht3.com/)

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-29T01:32:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-47961>

> **Destiny;48408 wrote:** What is confusing for me is that some people go on natural dessicated thyroid after clearing rT3. Don't you need to stay on T3? I would think once you start supplementing both T4 and T3, the situation with rT3 would repeat, no?

not if you fix the underlying reason for why it is turned into T4

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-47968>

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-29T01:47:18+0300

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-29T01:52:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-47977>

> **Destiny;48408 wrote:** What is confusing for me is that some people go on natural dessicated thyroid after clearing rT3. Don't you need to stay on T3? I would think once you start supplementing both T4 and T3, the situation with rT3 would repeat, no?

Yes it can repeat if you don't fix the cause. I know a lot of the T4 gets converted to T3 in the liver so I am curious if the liver is a culprit too in the making of too much rT3.

You can stay on T3 only but you are not fixing the problem. I think after you fix the underlying cause then you can decide which medication route you want to take, T3 only or NDT.

### Destiny — 2012-08-29T02:02:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-47985>

>
>

The liver makes sense as well. Many of us have an elevated FBS and that is the liver too. It is all connected but not easy to figure it out. Dr. K told me fix my thyroid, hormones and get some DHEA, metformin - in that order. So that is what I am working on.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-29T02:49:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-47997>

You go girl! That's all good stuff. I've been working with my thyroid for 5 years, 3 with meds and hormones a bit but not enough. I have a head start on the thyroid for sure. My thyroid seems to be good, hormones need a little help, my DHEA is finally good, pregnenolone is good, and the IGF-1 sucks!!! I may have found a doc close to me. I'm afraid Dr. K will be out of reach for me right now price-wise since I will have to add a cross country round trip plus hotel (don't want to kill my adrenals) in addition to his fee. This doc is not cheap but definitely less expensive than the Cenegenics people.

IGF-1 is the lab test to determine the amount of growth hormone in the body. I am really low. DHEA per the blog, is supposed to help but I don't feel any different. I think I may need to test again and see if the DHEA did anything or if I need to go another route. The other route includes an expensive test and injections (I think) of growth hormone. Doesn't sound like a fun proposition but if my body needs it....

When I didn't feel well, I couldn't do much and my life changed. I used to be very active and starting to get there again but not quite yet. Slowly. I treated myself for 2 of the three years. Massive research and common sense took me in the right direction. We all know now how much it takes on our end to be educated on the issues we have before we go see the doc. And to think I'm still researching!!!

Anytime, you talk about your health problems and symptoms, it almost sounds like mine. That is why I am learning so much from your experiences.

I started searching and learning last fall. My activity level took a nose dive and I started to panic. At that time, I could not comprehend how my grandma was able to live to 97. I was thinking at that time, 47 more years of this misery? How I am going to survive this? Now, I am relieved that my state was not a normal state at 50! ### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-29T18:53:47+0300

Yes we do have similar situations from everything I have read from you. So keep up that attitude great Destiny and you will live past 97!!!

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-01T21:51:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-48783>

So I think I've been on the T3 now for 6 days and I'm taking 25mcgs split in two doses. I feel....nothing. I've been taking my BP and HR and they are both dropping. I'm down to around 90/50 at rest with an HR of 45-52, depending of how long I've been sitting.

I am \*thrilled\* that my blood pressure and heart rate have come down. It was one of the things that brought me to Dr K's blog..... those two were rising at a fast rate during my AN recovery and while I was still "normal" (up to 135/85 at my docs! with HR of around 79!) at home it was better but still not "normal" for me. My docs kept telling me that now I am "normal" aka not eating disordered vitals anymore. But I felt like crap like that. Not well at all. Even my heart palpitation lately have been improving which we never found a reason for really. Dr K said it's an imbalance between my sympathetic and parasympathetic nervous system caused by my brain injuries. I think he's correct on that. B/c as I keep eating epi-paleo, my vitals are going back to where they used to be, I'm not feeling so much on edge anymore with a racing heart and I rarely have my heart thumping in my chest so hard at night that I can't sleep. I'm also following my cards' instructions now and adding electrolytes to everything I drink.

So I don't know if my vitals are a good way for me to measure whether or not I'm taking the appropriate amount of T3. Temp doesn't appear to be a good way either b/c my temp has been quite good before.... 98.2 wake up, then rising throughout the day, hitting 98.6 usually in the mid morning, and going up to 99.2 in the afternoon, then dropping back down for the night.

Do I up my daily amount since I feel nothing? I certainly do not feel hyper. Is hyper what I'm looking for so that then I can drop it back down a bit?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-02T02:45:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-48831>

You don't want to get to hyper. You want to have good vitals and that is an individual thing so good to you is different from me. 117/70 BP and heart rate of 70 is great for me. My temp usually is 98.5 in the afternoon (which will be your highest reading, usually). My temp used to be 94.5-96.0 consistently. I was always cold. The T3 only really heats me up. I really don't know what to tell you except that 25 mcg is a really low dose for someone to be on all the time. I've heard people being on as low as 38mcg and it works for them (people who monitor and know what they are doing) but I also know people who are on 150 mcg with doc's supervison. Then there is the general area most people are at of 72 mcg-120 mcg. I'm at 85 right now and think I may have hit my sweet spot. I feel good most days. Just switched brands so have to give it a week or so to make sure. So basically I gave some guidelines. Hopefully someone else will chime in.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-02T02:58:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-48842>

Thanks, Patty. This one is also hard for me b/c of my brain not functioning well. Hard to tell if I feel better or not when my head is hurting and foggy as it is most of the time. Today I just feel tired - had a good night's sleep and then I'm awake for maybe an hour or two and then I'm exhausted again. I have no idea what to look for. Maybe my BP is actually too low. My ayurvedic doc said I'm hypo b/c of my low heart rate. I think I'm going to keep going up little by little and just watch for how I'm feeling.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-02T03:03:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-48847>

My HR used to be in the low 50s. Yes that is a hypo sign. Going up too much is a hyper sign. But there are many signs. You can probably safely go up in 6 mcg amounts or even less than that...one dose at a time.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-03T03:28:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-49088>

I'm doing 25 split in two doses now and have added another quarter pill in the afternoon. So far so good.

And just ran into this:

> For the thyroid gland to produce the most active form of the thyroid hormone T3, **selenium** is essential but also helps regulate the amount of hormone that is produced.

I don't think this one has been brought up yet.....

### mambo — 2012-09-03T09:06:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-49148>

I'm still trying to confirm whether this is correct. My TPO Ab is 16. LabCorp's reference range is 0 to 34. Can you impute anything from this score? No hypothyroid symptoms except for cold fingers (which could be Raynaud's).

> **Souldanzer;45111 wrote:** Dr K said during the webinar that \*any\* level of antibodies indicates hashi's/AI.... is that any level above range or any level above 0.0?

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-03T17:33:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-49184>

> **mambo;49650 wrote:** I'm still trying to confirm whether this is correct. My TPO Ab is 16. LabCorp's reference range is 0 to 34. Can you impute anything from this score? No hypothyroid symptoms except for cold fingers (which could be Raynaud's).

It means you probably have hashi's. You need to get quarterly thyroid testing with antibody panel to see what is going on. You want your fT3 to be in the top quarter of the range optimally. With thyroid dosing you have to go a lot by symptoms though.

### melrito — 2012-09-03T17:46:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-49185>

> **mambo;49650 wrote:** I'm still trying to confirm whether this is correct. My TPO Ab is 16. LabCorp's reference range is 0 to 34. Can you impute anything from this score? No hypothyroid symptoms except for cold fingers (which could be Raynaud's).

I have a similar situation - mid-range "normal" antibodies and Dr. K told me that he cannot rule out hashis until I have my antubodies tested several more times to be sure they are not higher and I just caught them at a low point - since the antibodies do fluctuate based on a bunch of variables (I am hypothyroid with low fT3).

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-04T00:56:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-49267>

Interesting, Destiny. I'm not sure mine came down from T3 b/c I think it happened before I ever started on T3 iirc. Why do you think that happened? Cortisol/adrenaline making up for missing thyroid power?

### Destiny — 2012-09-04T01:03:21+0300

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-22T18:19:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-53408>

Been feeling off the last few days. I'm up to 75mcg T3 most days. Mostly I've been feeling shaky, heart palps, unable to calm down and relax.

T3 was my first suspect. But then second thought.... I think it might be cortisol instead. T3 increases LDL to preg conversion so if I'm actively upping my T3 slowly at some point I would need less supplemental pregnenolone, correct?

My preg was upper end of range when I tested so I know I'm already converting on my own. I was taking 300mg preg b/c it was making me feel more alert, then dropped it to 200mg b/c I was starting to have above symptoms, yesterday I stopped all preg and T3 for a day. This morning I'm feeling a bit better (shakiness is gone, feeling more relaxed). Reason I was taking so much preg on top of already being high on labs was b/c I was trying to help my lower steroid cascade hormones out a bit..... but I think lately it's all been going to cortisol instead.

So my plan for now is to keep taking T3, reducing or dropping the preg, and keep working on stress reduction (could use some help with that part ). Sounds like a good plan? If I still feel off, I will lower the T3. But so far, after my morning dose, I feel good

### Destiny — 2012-09-22T19:21:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-53426>

Soul,

Is there a possibility to test FT3 for you? That would give you an answer if your plan is a good plan.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-22T19:45:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs.3025/post-53432>

Sometimes when cortisol is off (either low or high) the individual tolerance of T3 is less than it would be with healthy adrenals. Shakiness, heaert palps, etc could be a sign of too much T3 for your adrenals. But if you feel better maybe the combo of the preg and T3 was too much. I know there are people out there who literally cannot tolerate more than 6mcg of T3 at a time!
