### JoeBranca — 2012-08-16T21:44:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-3rd-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.3047/post-45246>

The extremeness of a habit is a relative term defined by outsiders. It's the effectiveness of a habit that matters, but no uninformed outsider can assess that.

Purposeful correctives shouldn't be judged as extreme. But even if some correctives go unnecessarily extreme, so what? Does that really damage the psyche much? The effectiveness of new habits will reveal itself over time, and there's learning to the process. (correctives to the overcorrectives). Just being thoughtfully proactive in these things matters more in the harrowing context most people here suddenly discover themselves in.

Something else that's been on my mind, isn't dabbling in extremities sometimes for its own sake a great hormetic stressor? We're living between extremes as a society, so we don't value adaptability to extremes very much. But they're still valuable; things can change. I'd like to get a better idea right now on how I can adapt to extremes before it's necessity. Sort of an investing in the unpredictability of life.

### Barry — 2012-08-16T21:48:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-3rd-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.3047/post-45247>

From Ivy on Facebook

" I have to admit,this partly is why I quit the forum. Not only due to other commitments, but the stress it put on me to constantly try to live up to the latest blog-post and other members taking it so far that it left me always feeling bad. I feel so much better without the constant chatter about how to get optimal- I really thing stress over-rides anything else you may do if it goes too far. Barry has a good point!"

This is my point, it may not apply to all but it applies to many. More on this to come..

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-16T22:19:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-3rd-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.3047/post-45250>

Stress control is important.......but replacing bad food for less mental anguish about wants and needs is not an improvement. It is a lateral move. All stress is handled the same way in the body......cortisol=NE+epinephrine and the sympathetic nervous system.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-16T22:36:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-3rd-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.3047/post-45251>

> **Barry;45583 wrote: From Ivy on Facebook
>
>
>
> " I have to admit,this partly is why I quit the forum. Not only due to other commitments, but the stress it put on me to constantly try to live up to the latest blog-post and other members taking it so far that it left me always feeling bad. I feel so much better without the constant chatter about how to get optimal- I really thing stress over-rides anything else you may do if it goes too far. Barry has a good point!"
>
>
>
> This is my point, it may not apply to all but it applies to many.** More on this to come..

as Dr K has said many times before he doesn't treat populations.... he treats patients. Those are the ones that are left after you subtract the many from the all. They are actual people who are suffering. We do exist and man am I glad to finally have found someone who actually cares about that minority. If it really is a minority. Those are the ones you find on this forum eating fish heads.

Whether or not something stresses you is really up to your own mindset. I experience my journey towards optimal much more as exciting. Not sure how stressed I would be was I still couch bound all day with my head hurting like sh\*t and pumped full of medicines that will make me even sicker. And being skin and bones with my head in the toilet all day. Unable to even walk up a flight of stairs.

No one is making you do this, Barry. You are free to do your own thing. Why don't you allow others to do their own thing? Nobody is on your back about running for 22 hours on this forum. Nobody calls you out on it and tells you to drop the stressful exercise and be less "extreme".

You excel at one thing, I excel at another. 22 hrs of running vs eating a raw fish head every morning. I don't know. I'm having a hard time seeing the difference except that one is within your comfort zone, and the other one is not.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-16T22:46:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-3rd-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.3047/post-45253>

> **Jack Kruse;45586 wrote:** Stress control is important.......but replacing bad food for less mental anguish about wants and needs is not an improvement. It is a lateral move. All stress is handled the same way in the body......cortisol=NE+epinephrine and the sympathetic nervous system.

THE MOST stressful thing I have ever experienced in my life was/is being sick and knowing that I am heading towards certain death soon if nothing big happens now.

It was waking up in the mornings knowing that I was going to keep killing myself and damaging my body, that any action I might take today could be my last. That there was nothing I could do to prevent this from happening. That every day I was less likely to survive. It was going to sleep at night wondering if I was going to wake up in the morning. Or if it was better to leave a note of good-bye for my loved ones on my nightstand.

It was living with the constant awareness of impeding death. THAT is what I call stressful. Worrying about whether or not I ate my fish head today? Yeah. I can handle that.

### Barry — 2012-08-16T23:35:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-3rd-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.3047/post-45255>

> **Souldanzer;45587 wrote:** as Dr K has said many times before he doesn't treat populations.... he treats patients. Those are the ones that are left after you subtract the many from the all. They are actual people who are suffering. We do exist and man am I glad to finally have found someone who actually cares about that minority. If it really is a minority. Those are the ones you find on this forum eating fish heads.
>
>
>
> Whether or not something stresses you is really up to your own mindset. I experience my journey towards optimal much more as exciting. Not sure how stressed I would be was I still couch bound all day with my head hurting like sh\*t and pumped full of medicines that will make me even sicker. And being skin and bones with my head in the toilet all day. Unable to even walk up a flight of stairs.
>
>
>
> No one is making you do this, Barry. You are free to do your own thing. Why don't you allow others to do their own thing? Nobody is on your back about running for 22 hours on this forum. Nobody calls you out on it and tells you to drop the stressful exercise and be less "extreme".
>
>
>
> You excel at one thing, I excel at another. 22 hrs of running vs eating a raw fish head every morning. I don't know. I'm having a hard time seeing the difference except that one is within your comfort zone, and the other one is not.

Anyone ever seen the movie "Lost in Translation" , cos I think it applies here

You and others are missing the point I am trying to make. Its simple. Chasing after something that you don't want to do but that you think is good for you is wrong. Secondly, the path to optimal does not have to be the most perfect and scenic route, idealism and perfection are not obligatory.

Let me clear this up and put it into perspective or "context" as some of you think I am forgetting about, Apparently running 100miles is extreme, maybe it is but am I doing it because somebody told me ? Am I doing it to achieve optimal ? Am I doing it because its good for me and will make me superhuman and live until I'm 150 ?? NO... The reason I;m doing it is because I love it and get immense enjoyment out of it, full stop. This is where Soul and others are misinterpreting what I'm doing. Extremities are deemed extremities by the individual and one mans meat is another mans poison, sure. But the key point to realise is that whatever extreme option you choose should only be done because you enjoy it and feel good doing it. Thats where the Epigenetics come into play. If you're doing it because someone told you it will cure all illness's and make you live longer, but you don't enjoy it, this is where it goes wrong in my book.

This is where the magic bullet scenario comes into play. Eating something or doing something, in excess and in isolation, never works. Ive posted about this before, nothing is more important than the other, everything counts. So if you think eating shrimp, roe and CT is all you need to do to be healthy, then you're likely to run into trouble. This is what occurs with extreme measures, other important factors are omitted. This is crucial. Like I said in my 1st most important post, "there ain't no"

Look, serious health issues and diseases needs to be addressed by taking advanced measures, fine. I'm not one of those people thankfully. I admire those that are creating a new normal and making big changes. I'm all about that. I'm not about "mediocrity" or always doing the same etc etc, I think that should be clear from what I;ve already written.

Understand that I;m looking at this from a different angle here than most. I work in the field and I practise what I preach. Don't hit me with false claims and knit pick at comments. I've looked into all this as much if not more than most. I've seperated the woods from the tree's and I'm looking at the big picture. I know what works for me, i'm doing my own thing and I;ve got the proof to show it. Have you ?? If not, check out your own backyard before you check out someone's else...

Next round, round IV...

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-08-17T00:03:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-3rd-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.3047/post-45258>

> **Barry;45406 wrote:** Round III
>
>
>
> I think there is a very fine line between "Optimal" and "Extremism" and I think that if this line is crossed, it will result in failure. Let me explain:
>
>
>
> So we've now moved onto Epi Paleo. While I agree with most of the theory, I'm not so sure on the practice. You see, striving to be optimal can result in people searching for and trying to reach the extremity. In most situations, extremities in any walk of life are not good places to be. Not only that, but very few ever reach the extremity and the outcome for them is then failure. Failure causes dissappointment and fustration, this leads obviously to stress, stress , as everyone should know, is the killer.
>
>
>
> I do believe that in order to improve health and achieve "optimal", that you need to create a new norm. This requires you to get out of your comfort zone and this means you need to go through a period of struggle. I'm all about that. Its what I do in life myself and my sport is defined by that. You focus on the process and not the end point. Struggling and Suffering are the essence of a life worth living. Its how to progress in life and move forward. However, you can go out of your comfort zone without having to take extreme measures. For me, the current advice and recommendations are extreme. I'm sorry, but having mackerel smoothies every morning is extreme. Eating fish regularly and sea veg is certainly good for you, and might cause people some discomfort at first, but its not extreme. This then will improve health, 3/6 ratios, Iodine, Gut etc. And thats my point. You can achieve optimal health without having to wizz up mackerel smoothies every morning.
>
>
>
> What this does then is it takes you off the path to the extremity. You are then much more likely to succeed and therefore much less likely to be stressed. Eat some good fish when you can, get some sea veg and go with the flow. I don't think you need to be worrying about the amount of fish heads you eat every week or the weight of fish consumption every month. Like I have said in my two previous posts, Epigenetics is the conductor of the orchestra here, and extremism, in my opinion, causes trouble.
>
>
>
> Finally, one thing I believe is missing from this forum is "questioning everything".... its what Jack has preached many a time, yet I feel like I'm the only one doing it ! What drove me to write this is the time I have spent recently working around Europe. I saw very elderly men and women in places like France and Italy. They were fit and active, working in vineyards and riding bikes. Many of them in their 70's and 80's. They eat bread every day. They don't have mackerel smoothies and they don't sit in baths with 40 pounds of ice on their chests. So how come they are so healthy ??
>
>
>
> "Beyond the walls of intelligence, life is defined"

Will respond before reading other comments.

1\. I think someone said that they felt better on fish roe over the mackeral smootie so that seemed like a more realistic alternative.

2\. I think these more extreme methods are for people who are already sick and in dis-repair. So the Europeans that are eating bread, yet healthy etc. obviously had less (no?) damage. So it's up to the individual to see where they are on this spectrum. This also lends to where Jack states the importance of labs/tests.

I think that's difficult for a lot of people to have to deal with 'well xyz can eat bread so you're being extreme'. That is also not fair for those trying to correct their own personal health issues. I'm pretty sure your intent was not to be offensive, but rather to 'question everything'. However I do offer the other side where one does 'question everything', but also have to contend with those who are 'lucky' and can 'get away with whatever and yet still be healthy' and for those people that they live with who can 'get away with anything without harm' simultaneously try to sabotage your effort at every step of the way....

It takes a mental strength and character to persevere through that maliciousness. The good news is of course, one CAN make it through and with everything, there's an end. So triumph results.

I think then once the person has healed themselves appropriately THEN they should be reasonable about their choices and not be too 'extreme'. For example, perhaps my journey is farther along another person's, so choosing to have wine and chocolate chip cookies with a friend last night (which was preceeded by a most excellent fish fry, sweet potatoes and salad), I think that is a choice I can make and live with and I would simply monitor over a period of time how this would affect me. See for me, without testing, I'm flying blind.

You are correct though about it being a 'fine line'.

Thanks for bringing up this topic. It's necessary.

### ColdBren — 2012-08-17T00:20:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-3rd-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.3047/post-45261>

Both sides of this discussion are amazing. They really pull me. I have found myself under extreme stress trying simply understand how to be optimal....and have fallen very short of attaining it, adding more stress. This stress is, however, completely self induced and i am thankful for every word Dr. Kruse has written and said. My hope is to learn to live by the principles while enjoying it at the same time.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-08-17T00:22:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-3rd-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.3047/post-45262>

**Souldanzer;45488 wrote:** Okay. Feeling called to respond here since raw fish heads have become a food group for me \*and\* I am European and grew up in Europe.

You cannot compare European lifestyle with American lifestyle. Allow me to give you an impression of what happened to me when I first arrived at Atlanta airport with my one-way ticket in hand.....

O.V.E.R.L.O.A.D.

There was stuff going on everywhere... things moving wherever I looked and turned, voices coming through various speakers, lights going on and off.... oh, right, that's the televisions in every corner, there isn't just one screen, there is 3 in one screens and at the bottom is a rolling text message, and the whole thing gets interrupted by ads every few minutes that are so loud your ears threaten to fail. The pictures on the screens change so fast your brain can't quite keep up and you start to think you're on a rollercoaster. To this day I cannot stand american TV, impossible for me to watch, the amount of sensory input is out of control for my brain to handle at any one time.

So I'm living in this country now. And some things are very strange to me. Americans have strange houses. They are dark, very few windows and most of them kept with pulled blinds or curtains. They use artificial lights instead, day or night. There is no difference between night and day really in this country. Where I'm from (Germany) we keep our houses bright during the day and we dim the lights when it gets dark. We don't wear blueblockers but the lighting we use is gentle and warm. Not bright white which is what I kept experiencing in the U.S. I had the hardest time outfitting my first apartment with lamps b/c I couldn't stand the bright white light at night. TV time is limited and they do not run in the background. We either watch or we don't. There is no 3 screens in one with rolling text at the bottom. Sensory input is a lot more limited.

The list goes on. What I'm saying is that life in Europe, or Germany in my case, is a lot less "mismatched" than life in the U.S. if you compare it historically at the same time. Is this changing? Yes, it is. There is a big concern about youth obesity in Germany right now. But this is years later than the problem started in the U.S. We also still cook in Europe. Lots of my friends here plain don't use their kitchen. They go out to eat or eat read meals. My mother cooked for the entire family, three times a day, every day. It was homemade food and that makes a lot of difference. Yes, Europe eats a lot of bread, Germans in particular, and I get that no bread is "optimal" but preparation methods do matter in degree of damage grains might cause. You probably won't find a more traditionally prepared bread than German bread and yes, we do eat it that way.... wonder bread is pretty much unheard of. Most people don't like it. American bread and German bread are two entirely different things.

We do "sit in ice" occasionally. Maybe not as literally as Dr Kruse is suggesting it. But there is a tradition to ice bathing in Europe. It's getting lost now, the younger ones don't do it anymore but Roman Baths still exist. My parents and sister love to frequent them. They include cold baths. My dad takes cold showers every day b/c he believes it will make him healthier. As a child my parents took me to these baths and I was participating in the cold dips. We also don't need our homes to be heated to 72 degrees in the winter. We heat just enough to be comfortable with a winter sweater.... not a t-shirt as I see people do in the U.S. . As for the mackerel heads... well, maybe not entirely so but the food we eat is much more traditional. My mother eats raw smoked fish, she loves liver, the cheese we buy is raw and of good quality.

br />We're much more active in Europe. If we can walk the distance, we will. No reason to break out the car. We carry home our groceries. The kids spend the day outside. "When the street lights come on" was my curfew always while growing up.

So, maybe with this picture in mind you will understand why Europe as a country has healthier elderly than the U.S. Unfortunately, technology and such is catching up even there and so Europe is starting to experience similar problems at this point in time. My parents are both in their sixties, and they are pretty darn healthy. Not in Dr K's terms.... but they don't have any major ailments. My dad is now developing T2D and my mother is pushing a low-fat high carb diet b/c she learned in the media that this is recommended. We are getting off the path now.

It is and it is not. That entirely depends on your perpective. Btw, I think I'm the only one who does this every morning (I do it at night, too, most days, just to make this even a bit more extreme.... )

What happened to me in life is extreme. Extreme circumstances require extreme measures. I should not be alive anymore on many counts and I still am. I am doing better than ever with my eating disorder so what do I really care if what I do is extreme in some peoples eyes. NOT doing what could save me from certain death appears extreme to me. I do not know your story, Barry.... may I suggest that by your adventure with sports you are a pretty healthy person at this time, in Dr K's terms? Do you wake up every morning wondering if you can get out of bed? Do you spend the day with your eyes shutting despite having slept 9 hours the night before? Does your head throb all day and your cognitive skills go bye-bye almost entirely? Can you still think of two things in sequence? Do you ever go for a walk and are not sure you will be able to make it back you feel so weak? Have you \*ever\* felt like life is just flowing out of you and not being replenished.... and that your body and soul is on the way "out"?

I think it's great that you can do things the non-extreme way. It's great you are that healthy and your body thrives on cooked fish fillets a couple times a week. Does my body? No. I've tried it. It got me nowhere.

So, what appears extreme to you might not appear extreme to another person.

I'm sure I could. And just getting out of my comfort zone might not be enough to save my personal life.

I entirely do not agree with that. Do I take a risk? Yes, I do. I'm aware of that. The risk of not risking right now is bigger. Whether or not failure will cause you stress is really dependent on your mental way of dealing with failure. I fail every day. I succeed more than I fail, however. B/c that is how I organize my life and mind. You need to adjust the challenge to the person's skills and then there will be oodles of success. It's just like training for a marathon. Don't start with the 100km in one go if you've been a couch potato for the past 10 years.

Nope, you're not the only one. In fact, I questioned that the paleo approach was the right thing for me to do after my skiing accident 4 months ago. I read as much as I could and basically came up with seafood being brain food. Some of Dr K's comments on blogs, some of it of my own reading. So then I started eating fish to heal my brain.... not having a clue of what was about to be revealed. I ate it every day for three months almost exclusively before BG 4/5/6 ever happened. I question things in my own way. If I think the risk to reward ratio is beneficial then I try and watch. I stick with what works. There's some things that Dr K tells me to do that don't work for me, so I don't do them (like not overloading myself trying to do all at once ) I question a lot of other things. In Life. These Days. Some of it relevant to Dr K's work, some of it not. It's gotten me to a very personal, a bit unusual lifestyle. That some people would call extreme.

Questioning you: Why not strive for the best of the best if it is available to you? Are you staying within your comfort zone?

I guess I'm a bit.... different from most people. I thrive on extreme. My personality is made to be extreme. So, to quote someone else from this forum... I fell into this like a rock star into a pile of cocaine  It's the equivalent of my AN personality applied to getting healthy instead. A much better purpose.

Congrats if you made it this far. I guess I had a lot to say

Souldanzer, you are so inspiring and a good role model. (Sorry if that embarrasses you or puts too much responsibility on you). I would put you right up there with Dr. Terry Wahls too. I am so lucky to have access to these forums and website from Jack Kruse because it has helped me tremendously over the last 7 moths.

I am constantly re-wiring the way I think too. For example, I know that another argument against people saying that someone else is 'extreme' as you (and Jack) pointed out that perhaps it's the other person's views that think it extreme. Also, the context/framework from which it is coming from might be the 'extreme'. Like Soul pointed out, life in America (and most places in Canada...but even still probably not as bad) is extreme. Lights at night and coffee and doughnuts at night is extreme....not going to bed early and taking cold showers and eating properly....sorry.

Thanks all for this thread, it's a good one and makes people think. Very good replies too!

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-08-17T00:29:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-3rd-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.3047/post-45265>

Oooooo...just remembered another thought that Dr. K advocates. Find your inner guru!! (Well, I sort of said this by saying to get testing to find out your indiv. needs). Anyways, if you end up being your own guru this happens sometimes only through the route of what seems 'extreme'. For example...you might try mackeral smoothies 'just b/c someone said it's healthy or will fix your brain problem'. Then you might stumble upon that fish roe does the same effect, yet it's much easier, less messy and tastier...you experiment... Again it's ALWAYS a 'fine line'. How do you know one drink doesn't lead to two, or three, or oops you're drunk...etc.?

### SeaHorse — 2012-08-17T01:13:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-3rd-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.3047/post-45271>

Great thoughts from everyone...and I do understand the point about stress. I see this in my equine therapy work. People may yearn for change but are not ready to make it. They want to be healthy, or fit, or creative or stress free or any number of things that they don't feel they are at the moment. Stressing about how other people take their journeys of change and transformation do not help at all. There is a huge growth industry around personal development etc. A lot of it's based on selling people a "dream" of how life can be different....think yoga, spirituality, health, fitness etc etc. What is missing from all of this is a key component you can't really MAKE somebody be different by telling them to change.AND people usually talk about change for a long time before they make any.

What "wakes" us up and lets us begin to change is so individual and is so unpredictable. Given the same information one person will be inspired and change profoundly and another will feel inadequate and attacked. Those responses don't change the information....they just point to how different we all are. As Soul pointed out JK's work is not aimed at fulfilling a marketing plan...it's aimed at finding the people that are willing and able to jump on board because it makes sense to them and feels right. And this group will have a range of approaches within it. I'm sure there are many people who read and are influenced by the forum who don't post...that's one approach and another is to write your heart out everyday....there is no right or wrong about any of it.

Barry, it sounds like you are very comfortable with your choices....that has to be an important part of remaining stress free. People who compare themselves to others negatively will always have stress around difference.....but learning to celebrate our own process no matter how that appears to others is something that can't be handed out. It's up to each of us to get there on our own.

?????

Look, serious health issues and diseases needs to be addressed by taking advanced measures, fine. I'm not one of those people thankfully. I admire those that are creating a new normal and making big changes. I'm all about that. I'm not about "mediocrity" or always doing the same etc etc, I think that should be clear from what I;ve already written.

What is clear from what you have written is that you are having trouble putting yourself into someone else's shoes and taking their perspective for a moment. Being the runner that you are, you and I have a similar personality trait. You can't run 100mi without a certain degree of will involved. I am 99.9% sure, if you woke up one day disabled, not being able to get out of bed, sick like a dog, wanting for everything to be over NOW you feel so bad..... and then someone came along and gave you the tools to make yourself better (I assume you'd go see a doctor probably... my point) you'd change what you are saying here radically.

> Understand that I;m looking at this from a different angle here than most. I work in the field and I practise what I preach. Don't hit me with false claims and knit pick at comments. I've looked into all this as much if not more than most. I've seperated the woods from the tree's and I'm looking at the big picture. I know what works for me, i'm doing my own thing and I;ve got the proof to show it. Have you ?? If not, check out your own backyard before you check out someone's else...

My English skills are leaving me for the last sentence but yes, I've done my work. I've been sick my entire life and I've been disabled for most of it. I have been looking for a solution for more than 20 years to my problems. Please explain to me what you mean with if I have proof to show it? Would you like me to send you over my medical records for the last 20 years..... I got plenty of proof that what I'm doing right now is working for me. If your sports accomplishments are your proof that things are working for you..... I never doubted that. I said it's not working for me. I will not achieve any sports goals at an emaciated weight of 70lbs (I'm not short either) with the bones of a 90year old. I need to get to a point of health first before I will be able to do any of that.

_I'm better

We're together now

I'm better

So much better now

Look to the skies, give me life

We're together now

We've only just begun

Hypnotized by drums

Until forever comes

You'll find us chasing the sun

They said this day wouldn't come

We refused to run

We've only just begun

You'll find us chasing the sun

When Daylight's fading

We're gonna play in the dark

'Til it's golden again

And now it feels so amazing

Can see you coming

And We'll never grow old again

You'll find us chasing the sun

I'm never

I'm never down

Lying here, staring up

And you're looking down

I'm never

I'm never down

Live forever, forever

With you around

_

# Thread: You Want Me To Do What? - Greetings, everyone!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-want-me-to-do-what-greetings-everyone.3050/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### RespectTheProcess — 2012-08-16T02:40:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-want-me-to-do-what-greetings-everyone.3050/post-45112>

A new member to the forum (though I feel as if this has been my sole internet site for the past month), my name is L.P. My journey and paths to optimal have each started with a dumfounded look at my boyfriend as he presented each idea to me -

"You want me to not eat any wheat...at all?!"

"You want me to put....butter...IN my coffee?"

And finally...

"Um, excuse me, you want me to get into that horse trough of ice water?????????"

We've been paleo for about a year, strict bulletproof for the past 6 months, and have now transitioned to the Leptin Reset. Fully involved in CT and darnit if I can't claim it's the best thing I've ever done. We've been so blessed to have all the info from Dr. Kruse and intelligent members posted on this website. Thanks from two nurses, who despite years of education, were never taught any of this stuff!

### Lyndra — 2012-08-16T02:42:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-want-me-to-do-what-greetings-everyone.3050/post-45113>

Welcome and good to have you participating! Every time I tell someone about CT (and I tell very few because I know what the response will be), they look at me like I'm crazy. At least in this community I know I'm not .

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-16T04:15:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-want-me-to-do-what-greetings-everyone.3050/post-45141>

welcome.......and I think you might really like the next blog.........Candida meets Michael Jackson.

### nonchalant — 2012-08-16T05:22:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-want-me-to-do-what-greetings-everyone.3050/post-45154>

# Thread: The Good, the Bad, the Ugly of Supplements
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-good-the-bad-the-ugly-of-supplements.3051/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### RespectTheProcess — 2012-08-16T02:50:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-good-the-bad-the-ugly-of-supplements.3051/post-45117>

Between the podcasts, blog, and forum, my computer sees more of me than anyone else currently. However, there's so much information out there that I greatly appreciate the "Mega Threads." Although perhaps I'm just that generation that wants everything "now"!

My question is regarding supplements - what to look for in them/where to buy/what brands are respected and used by you guys. I've read that Mg Stearate is bad, the cellulose could possibly be corn derivative, etc. Essentially what I'm asking is for the veterans who've learned the hard way to share some of their lessons - or to guide us in the general right direction. We've always supplemented, but after reading many posts, I went through our cabinet and figured each of them contained something "not optimal." Very frustrating to say the least.

Appreciate the words of advice in advance!

L.P.

Edit: spelling

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-16T04:21:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-good-the-bad-the-ugly-of-supplements.3051/post-45143>

I like different ones and I test the one I use on me and my family. Right now I like Life Extension and I use others for certain things I tend to take few supplements these days unless something is acting up or I have new biohack. Right now I just finished a 6 month bio hack on topical DHEA. I also have been doing a gut reengineering hack too.......we will see how that one goes. So far so good.

### BJK77 — 2012-08-16T19:46:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-good-the-bad-the-ugly-of-supplements.3051/post-45225>

I used to react to nearly everything - foods, supplements, etc, so I became nazi-like in my approach to evaluating supplements. I look for the purest form I can possibly find. In some cases you can get pure powdered supplements (I do this for DHEA), but when that's not an option I look for the least amount of fillers and the least harmful fillers.

Some of the brands I've found to be good and of high quality are Carlson (Vit D drops), Nutricology, Pure Encapsulations and a few others. And as much as I'm not a fan of Dr Mercola, some of his supplements seem to be pretty decent. His Ubiquinol/COQ10 was the only one I could find that didn't have a ton of fillers.

### Destiny — 2012-09-14T13:13:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-good-the-bad-the-ugly-of-supplements.3051/post-51615>

Are there supplements we should be always taking once you reach the middle age?

For example curcumin, resvetrarol, iodine, etc.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-14T14:31:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-good-the-bad-the-ugly-of-supplements.3051/post-51620>

Ive had success w/LEF, nutraceuticals, VRP (ultra K2), now brands, & biotiva

If I had a bigger house I'd buy supplements like Soul does

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-14T19:59:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-good-the-bad-the-ugly-of-supplements.3051/post-51659>

Please share the topical DHEA!! Many of us bought a bunch of raw DHEA and tried making creams - did not work out! Any save for this?

### vkiernan — 2012-09-14T20:06:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-good-the-bad-the-ugly-of-supplements.3051/post-51661>

> **Cavemam;52257 wrote:** Please share the topical DHEA!! Many of us bought a bunch of raw DHEA and tried making creams - did not work out! Any save for this?

I mixed it with my current skin cream and added co and aloe to it. I think it works great. If it isn't, I am unaware. It must be working cuz when I have been in the sun too long I have used it all over my body and I haven't peeled when everyone else peeled horribly. This has happened a couple of times the last month. I am not a chemist so no way would I be able to figure this stuff out without a lot of research; so the simplest thing that seemed to me was use something that is already most of the way there.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-14T20:08:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-good-the-bad-the-ugly-of-supplements.3051/post-51663>

> **vkiernan;52259 wrote:** I mixed it with my current skin cream and added co and aloe to it. I think it works great. If it isn't, I am unaware. This has happened a couple of times the last month. I am not a chemist so no way would I be able to figure this stuff out without a lot of research; so the simplest thing that seemed to me was use something that is already most of the way there.

I tried all kinds of things to get it to dissolve, and it just didn't. I heard from others a similar experience. . .what kind did you use?

### vkiernan — 2012-09-15T15:07:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-good-the-bad-the-ugly-of-supplements.3051/post-51788>

> **Cavemam;52261 wrote:** I tried all kinds of things to get it to dissolve, and it just didn't. I heard from others a similar experience. . .what kind did you use?

I used Pure Encapsulations - micronized. I use L'Bri skin care products but that might not really matter. Seemed to mix quite well. The only thing I am finding now is that since it is cooler, the co in it is hardening. I might look for a different oil to add. Really love the softness of this though. I also wear a little loose powder makeup and have no problem applying it shortly after. It did not mix well with aloe alone though.

### angieonthegogo — 2012-09-15T17:05:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-good-the-bad-the-ugly-of-supplements.3051/post-51808>

# Thread: Got a problem with your doctor?  Post it here and let us discuss.

**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 45

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-16T15:37:05+0300

[http://www.boston.com/lifestyle/health/blog/shortwhitecoat/2012/07/disagreeing\_with\_your\_doctor\_i.html](http://http://www.boston.com/lifestyle/health/blog/shortwhitecoat/2012/07/disagreeing_with_your_doctor_i.html)

### debbiemikus — 2012-08-16T15:59:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-a-problem-with-your-doctor-post-it-here-and-let-us-discuss.3057/post-45188>

Good Morning Dr. K.

You had a consult with my husband Mike Mikus on Sunday evening and offered suggestions for dealing with cardiologist who won't give clearance for C6 epidural. He called and they gave him an appt and wanted to see his latest cortisol result. It was normal but hsCrp was high. They asked to see his cortisol. Is high HsCrp also an indicator for heart attack? Thanks

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-16T16:16:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-a-problem-with-your-doctor-post-it-here-and-let-us-discuss.3057/post-45189>

Yes it is.......if you look at the HS CRP number right next to it is should have a range and an explanation......0-1 is OK......1-2 is moderate risk and 3 and above is high risk...........Any cardiologist should know this. Moreover the biggest factor in cardiac survivability after heart surgery of heart attack is the free T3 ratio.......it should be top 25% if not ask why this has not been addressed either?

### chewingthefat — 2012-08-16T17:38:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-a-problem-with-your-doctor-post-it-here-and-let-us-discuss.3057/post-45204>

First , I'd like to say that I love my Dr--she is a breath of fresh air and wonderful to work with....that being said, I've requested (lots of) tests recently and she was very receptive to my requests. However, the mag was not RBC and I was given the cCRP, not the hsCRP. I don't know if this is something to make another request on, if it isn't a big deal? Should I make another request?

### MartiD — 2012-08-16T18:45:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-a-problem-with-your-doctor-post-it-here-and-let-us-discuss.3057/post-45218>

My Dr wouldn't order the RBC Magnesium because it wasn't on the order form and he said no one does that test! Whatever! The RT3 wasn't on form either and it wrote in into "other" section. I'm getting regular magnesium but don't know if that will really tell anything.

### fitness@home — 2012-08-16T19:58:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-a-problem-with-your-doctor-post-it-here-and-let-us-discuss.3057/post-45226>

Now my _Former_ doctor, but yes I had a problem with her. Why would a doctor suggest you get comprehensive and expensive out-of-pocket testing and then not go over the results with you to help understand them? Her comment at my follow up appt was that it would take her at least an hour to go over it before she could tell me anything. Ummm, why didn't you do that before my follow up?

Then she sent all of the result over to a compounding pharmacy for the head pharmacist to review and make recommendations. Again, shouldn't the doctor have enough knowledge to do this herself?

There is a silver lining in this situation though...the pharmacist and I have been emailing each other over the past few weeks. Found a keeper there! She took the time to really go over the results and made all of her recommendations to me first. Also, the supplement suggestions were all in line with the goals I am working toward.

Have an appt scheduled with a new doctor next week...

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-16T20:13:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-a-problem-with-your-doctor-post-it-here-and-let-us-discuss.3057/post-45228>

> **chewingthefat;45540 wrote:** First , I'd like to say that I love my Dr--she is a breath of fresh air and wonderful to work with....that being said, I've requested (lots of) tests recently and she was very receptive to my requests. However, the mag was not RBC and I was given the cCRP, not the hsCRP. I don't know if this is something to make another request on, if it isn't a big deal? Should I make another request?

the HS CRP is a big deal.....the other Mg test can be a big deal if it is a serious issue based upon your history. Mg is 98% an intracellular ion and the serum test that docs use is a serum only assay meaning we are not measuring the right part.......for the disease we look at.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-17T03:12:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-a-problem-with-your-doctor-post-it-here-and-let-us-discuss.3057/post-45287>

I think it may be time for me to find a new BHRT doctor. I've been learning a lot and getting a good feel for doctors. I was happy when I found a doctor who would at least prescribe natural thyroid. But she wanted to wait 3 months before re-evaluating my thyroid or discussing other hormonal therapy. So I moved on to the next doctor. She is much better. She got me started on estrogen, progesterone & testosterone and increased the thyroid. Very easy to work with and ordered any test I wanted. But at my last visit, she didn't want to even consider adding T3 when my TSH increased again & my FT4 is just about perfect, but my FT3 is still at the bottom. It kinda bugged me that she wouldn't even consider it. Then when I asked about trying to get my cycles regular, she said that it was normal for me to have irregular cycles at my age and I should try stopping the estrogen & progesterone if I start to feel crampish to see if I would start. That just doesn't sound right. She also said weight loss has nothing to do with hormones. She did order a new set of hormone labs, but I'm thinking it may be time to move on. I'll decide after I see my labs and how she responds to any deficiencies.

I know it should not be that difficult! Either they don't know, or they cannot connect the dots or have their own dogmas. In addition, they don't have time to analyze anything. Hi and bye ..... here is your statin, synthroid, etc.

### debbiemikus — 2012-08-18T01:13:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-a-problem-with-your-doctor-post-it-here-and-let-us-discuss.3057/post-45457>

Just a quick note to all of you regarding Dr. K and his awesome ability for looking at a few labs, a little history and pulling a rabbit out of a hat, or a hole....lol. He consulted with my husband and told me to start giving him Testosterone Cypionate 100mg on Monday and Friday. When he told his PCP that, his response was" well, I will let you try it but if your PSA goes up in 6 months you will be sorry". I printed out the mens comprehensive panel and his Pcp gave him the order. His testosterone is in the toilet, and Jack knew that from other things that his pcp did not even connect! Once again, Jack you are an effing genius and I am so grateful for all you do!

### fitness@home — 2012-08-18T01:45:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-a-problem-with-your-doctor-post-it-here-and-let-us-discuss.3057/post-45465>

Endocrinologists - they frustrate me!

Saw mine this afternoon. Went prepared for this appt - with recent thyroid tests, a lot of other information and ready to discuss it with him. FT3/RT3 ratio is 11.3, TSH is 2.15, FT3 and Total T3 are very low.

Asked him for a T3 only treatment protocol. His repeated response was, "The only result that needs to be considered is the TSH".

Will be searching for a doctor in the Houston and/or The Woodlands area that will treat with T3 for clearing out excess RT3. Anyone with suggestions?

I did walk out with an order for a Thyroid/Parathyroid/Partoid ultrasound so it wasn't all for naught. It will be used as my baseline, as there is a strong family history of thyroid issues.

### kathylu — 2012-08-18T14:48:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-a-problem-with-your-doctor-post-it-here-and-let-us-discuss.3057/post-45518>

My doctor is pretty good, very open minded. My frustration is with my husband's doctor. I have asked my husband to request certain tests for the last two years, simple things like a vitamin D level and his doc's reply was "that seems to be quite the fad these days, doesn't it?" and then won't order the test. I have also asked my husband to switch to my doctor (or anyone really) but because of habit and inertia, he has taken no action. Also, he believes himself to be perfectly healthy and invulnerable. He looks terrific, 6'1", 178 lbs, has never had a weight problem or other serious issue. So, nothing can happen to him! However, I have noted some changes over the last few years, less energy, poorer sleep, has lost some muscle, a small fat pad developed around his waist, etc. So, for his recent check up I sent him with a "dumbed down" list of Dr. K's labs, since I knew there is no way that the doc would order the whole set. However, I felt that the basics would give an indication if there were any problems and we could go from there. This guy literally took my list and marked through half the tests and sent it back to me! He wouldn't do a VAP and substituted a lipid panel. He wouldn't order hs-crp, saying that he didn't know what the test was used for! At least we got a total and free testosterone, which was low, no surprise there (the doc did give him a script for testosterone). Wouldn't do a thyroid panel, just TSH...and on and on and on. Just incredible.

I don't mean to make my husband sound dumb, he's not. However, he doesn't know much about medicine and is not that interested in it as a subject, so he leaves that to me. He trusts me to know what I'm doing. He finally agreed that he has to switch docs.

I'm just stunned though...don't docs have to take CME credits? How did they miss this guy?

Btw, the tests that Dr. Oldschool did agree to run showed a few things, but I think I'll make that the subject of another post. The doc seemed to feel all was well except for the low testosterone...another stupid mistake in my opinion.

However, I felt that the basics would give an indication if there were any problems and we could go from there. This guy literally took my list and marked through half the tests and sent it back to me! He wouldn't do a VAP and substituted a lipid panel. He wouldn't order hs-crp, saying that he didn't know what the test was used for! At least we got a total and free testosterone, which was low, no surprise there (the doc did give him a script for testosterone). Wouldn't do a thyroid panel, just TSH...and on and on and on. Just incredible.
>
> I don't mean to make my husband sound dumb, he's not. However, he doesn't know much about medicine and is not that interested in it as a subject, so he leaves that to me. He trusts me to know what I'm doing. He finally agreed that he has to switch docs.
>
> I'm just stunned though...don't docs have to take CME credits? How did they miss this guy?
>
> Btw, the tests that Dr. Oldschool did agree to run showed a few things, but I think I'll make that the subject of another post. The doc seemed to feel all was well except for the low testosterone...another stupid mistake in my opinion.

It took listening to my consult with Dr. K to get my husband to see our GP so he could run his labs. Of course, he is not as healthy as he had thought.

My husband is interested in this when I talk about it or send him some excerpts but his passion is in his profession. So I have to be the driving force in this. It is not always easy but we need to be patient and keep going.

I know what you mean Destiny, my husband relies heavily on me to do the research and tell him what to do. He did have a consult with Jack and he was blown away with how he could read his labs.

# Thread: DHEA and LabCorp reference ranges - are they age adjusted?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-and-labcorp-reference-ranges-are-they-age-adjusted.3060/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ATL_Paleo — 2012-08-16T20:46:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-and-labcorp-reference-ranges-are-they-age-adjusted.3060/post-45235>

Recently I had a question ... were LabCorp DHEA reference ranges age adjusted? So I emailed my question to Life Extension. Below is their response.

The reference ranges provided by LabCorp for DHEA are age adjusted.

Life Extensionâ€™s optimal ranges are not absolute and are meant to be used as a guideline. Due to genetic biochemical variances some people may never be able to reach certain optimal levels. For hormones, we encourage individuals to maintain levels of a 20-29 year old, which is often the upper 1/3 of the range provided by LabCorp. Our most up to date optimal range of DHEA for men is 350-490ug/dL.

Keep in mind that every individual has a unique biochemical makeup, so the optimal level for certain blood measures in one person may be different than another. Also, it is important to look at the balance of all your hormones, the symptoms you are experiencing, and improvements you have made from baseline values. In addition the optimal ranges are in constant flux as they can change due to fluctuations in testing methods by the labs. Also these ranges are only applicable for the lab Life Extension uses, which is LabCorp.

Here is a link to our DHEA restoration protocol for additional information:

[http://www.lef.org/protocols/metabolic\_health/dhea\_restoration\_01.htm](http://http://www.lef.org/protocols/metabolic_health/dhea_restoration_01.htm)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-17T15:12:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-and-labcorp-reference-ranges-are-they-age-adjusted.3060/post-45326>

There are times I like to go above the ranges too based upon the history and the other labs.......this is the art of medicine......this is especially true if there is a SNP involved that we know about from 23andme. For example, CYP SNP cause me to push B12 levels over my normal ranges.....

I just took an adrenal test from osumex (by the way there are some awesome tests here that you can do yourselves without having to send out to a lab). It looks at salt and these are the values for results:

5 or less =hyper

approx 10 = moderate hyper

approx 20 = normal

over 25 = adrenal fatigue

My result showed 9. Does this mean my adrenals are actually functioning very well? I don't really understand the implications of my results.

What do you all think?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-17T01:40:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hyperadrenalism.3062/post-45276>

everyone assumes cortisol is all about adrenal function.......its not. Its about the circadian cycle that control it in the brain.......called light and dark. More on that when we get to this months Webinar.

# Thread: Mouse study finds clear linkages between inflammation, bacterial communities and canc
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mouse-study-finds-clear-linkages-between-inflammation-bacterial-communities-and-canc.3063/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-08-17T00:12:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mouse-study-finds-clear-linkages-between-inflammation-bacterial-communities-and-canc.3063/post-45260>

Mouse study finds clear linkages between inflammation, bacterial communities and cancer

[http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/08/16/mouse.study.finds.clear.linkages.between.inflammation.bacterial.communities.and.cancer?](http://http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/08/16/mouse.study.finds.clear.linkages.between.inflammation.bacterial.communities.and.cancer?)

> What if a key factor ultimately behind a cancer was not a genetic defect but ecological? Ecologists have long known that when some major change disturbs an environment in some way, ecosystem structure is likely to change dramatically. Further, this shift in interconnected species' diversity, abundances, and relationships can in turn have a transforming effect on health of the whole landscape -- causing a rich woodland or grassland to become permanently degraded, for example -- as the ecosystem becomes unstable and then breaks down the environment.
>
>
>
> For this reason, it should come as no surprise that a significant disturbance in the human body can profoundly alter the makeup of otherwise stable microbial communities co-existing within it and that changes in the internal ecology known as the human microbiome can result in unexpected and drastic consequences for human health.
>
>
>
> A report published in the August 16 online edition of the journal Science gives evidence for such a chain reaction. It has long been known that gut inflammation is a risk factor for cancer. The new study suggests that this may be in part because inflammation disturbs gut ecosystems leading to conditions that allow pathogens to invade the gut. These pathogens may damage host cells increasing the risk of the development of colorectal cancer.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-17T01:39:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mouse-study-finds-clear-linkages-between-inflammation-bacterial-communities-and-canc.3063/post-45275>

then you are going to love the next blog post..........just wait.

# Thread: So my wife said we're doing this new diet...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-my-wife-said-were-doing-this-new-diet.3066/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chipmeister — 2012-08-17T07:39:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-my-wife-said-were-doing-this-new-diet.3066/post-45308>

Hello all. I'm one of those husbands that easily agrees with diets and most (not all) other ideas my wife has. She had been researching this "paleo leptin thing" for a while and then stated this is what we would be doing. Being the supportive husband I am, I agreed. We started the Leptin Reset PX in February of this year. Well, of course I showed all the signs of healing and getting fixed as well as the accompanying weight loss before she did...but she wasn't far behind me. It's now mid August and I've lost 40 lbs (she has lost 50) and my elbows are as smooth as a baby's bottom. My friends in the IT world are amazed at the transformation and are constantly asking me what I did and how I did it. It's fun to try and explain it when I'm not really the one with all the information.

Anyway, we've done most everything that Jack has prescribed and it has all worked! My wife finally registered me on the forum so I could do an email consult and start to get tested according to Dr Kruse's recommendation.

Thanks babe for taking the stand and fixing us. Love you always and thankfully we'll be able to live longer and healthier lives now that we're on our way to optimal.

### caroline — 2012-08-17T13:13:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-my-wife-said-were-doing-this-new-diet.3066/post-45313>

I love your story - Welcome. You sound like great life partners! Please tell us more

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-17T15:03:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-my-wife-said-were-doing-this-new-diet.3066/post-45322>

Awesome news and welcome to Optimal. Your wife sounds like a Starfish!

### PrimalPam — 2012-08-17T19:02:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-my-wife-said-were-doing-this-new-diet.3066/post-45368>

Hi Chip! SOOO funny, I just made this comment to my friend: My elbows are so smooth now! I've had the scaliest elbows for decades, and one day, I accidentally brushed one hand against my elbow and was like...wait, is that MY elbow? LOL.

### nonchalant — 2012-08-17T19:36:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-my-wife-said-were-doing-this-new-diet.3066/post-45380>

### chipmeister — 2012-08-20T20:51:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-my-wife-said-were-doing-this-new-diet.3066/post-45894>

> **Nonchalant;45744 wrote:** Welcome, Chip! Glad to have you here. Are you two doing any CT?

Thanks. Great to be here and part of the optimized community.

We are doing some CT but not as much as we should. We both have gradually gone from our traditional hot showers in the morning down to warm, then to cool, and I believe we are both at the point where we are done with our showers before the hot water ever makes it up to the bathroom.

Of course we have the best CT option available...the pacific ocean. Life has just been throwing us curve balls lately and we haven't had the best opportunities to go for a dip. But that should be changing soon and we'll be frequenting the beach more often.

We've also been spreading the word about optimized living. People who see us always comment on how thin we're getting and how much younger we both look. And then of course they ask us what we are doing which then leads to a conversation to which they realize how screwed up they themselves must be. Although I do love the look on their face(s) if we're sitting down at a meal and we inform them as to why we don't eat what they are eating at that moment

### nonchalant — 2012-08-20T21:20:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-my-wife-said-were-doing-this-new-diet.3066/post-45901>

Yeah, just the CONCEPT of not eating lots of healthywholegrains is stunning, to most.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-20T22:18:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-my-wife-said-were-doing-this-new-diet.3066/post-45915>

### Destiny — 2012-08-17T13:22:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/suggestions.3067/post-45314>

Here are the labs for my hubby:

First, the good or so so stuff:

cardio CRP **0.6

Fasting Glucose **83

HB1C **5.4

Fasting insulin **3

B12 **1500

folate **18.8

iron **147

saturation **38%

TIBC **383

Second, not so great category:

Total cholesterol **178** - was 150

HDL 41** - was 34

LDL 77** - was over 100

Triglycerides 301** - this happened before - needed a medication for bring it down; this is driven by Questran medication taken every day in the past 25 years

Vitamin D - 35**

Testosterone 330** (250-1100 ng/dl)

Free testosterone 46.2** (35 - 155 pg/ml)

TSH 1.22** (0.4-4.5 mIU/ml)

FT3 2.8** (2.3-4.2 pg/ml)

FT4 2.8** (2.3 -4.2 pg/ml)

Any suggestions? What it the best way to bring testosterone up?

The thyroid doesn't look that hot either, right?

He'll need a medication again to bring the triglycerides down for sure.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-17T14:58:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/suggestions.3067/post-45321>

the T and free T are pretty damn low. So is the D. The cholesterol issue seems to be tied to the low free T3 as usually. Since his HD CRP is low this tells one there is a huge cortisol issue likely diurnal to cause the hormonal disruption and this implies that the TSH is not an accurate test for his hypothyroid due to a CRH issue turning things off. I bet his rT3 is way up because of these numbers.........needs a 4 panel ASI to see for sure to elucidate further but he has only one number that is really concerning.......to me.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/suggestions.3067/post-45362>

He has a leaky gut.

He needs to get his omega 6/3 ratio checked.

Change his diet to epi-paleo with very low carb and his triglycerides will come down.

Take the money he saves on the TG medication and get a consult with Dr. K.

# Thread: weird glucose monitor reading
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weird-glucose-monitor-reading.3068/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaleoDentist — 2012-08-17T14:57:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weird-glucose-monitor-reading.3068/post-45320>

I check my blood sugar with a glucose meter to see how my glucose control is like.

Last evening I ate a Epi Paleo dinner : broiled seafood/spinach/broccoli/ grass fed butter at 7 pm

at 9pm my reading was 79. which was good

upon waking it was 96.

I thought it would be in the 70's

not sure why the jump as I did not eat anything since dinner.

anyone have an insight?????

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-17T18:07:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weird-glucose-monitor-reading.3068/post-45360>

dawn syndrome? Basically, you're IR/LR.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-17T19:26:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weird-glucose-monitor-reading.3068/post-45375>

Not weird at all.......answer is coming in this series later I promise.

### PaleoDentist — 2012-08-18T14:24:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weird-glucose-monitor-reading.3068/post-45514>

I think it has to do with cortisol release in the morning as well as glucose release by the liver/ gluconeogenesis. basically the body making/releasing the necessary glucose in a fasted state. I wish I didi a ketone test. I bet i was in ketosis as well.

yesterday I tested 2 hours after lunch and my glucose reading was 82.

Dr K am I in the ball park???

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-02T02:46:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weird-glucose-monitor-reading.3068/post-48832>

That's not weird at all. I had readings just like that for almost a year. Started doing the leptin reset and for 3 or 4 months saw no change. Then I started getting lower fasting bg readings upon waking on some days. Now they are in the 80s 4 or 5 days a week and in the 90s 2 or 3 days a week. If they're in the 90s, I eat breakfast then jump in the cold tub (about 65 deg. this time of year) and take a reading afterward. It's usually in the 70s after cold exposure.

### kovita — 2014-12-03T19:20:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weird-glucose-monitor-reading.3068/post-149121>

is your overall carbs intake low or very low? Longterm? if you are ketotic you will get most ketotic during the night and this means you will see numbers like is in the morning. Physiological insulin resistance and dawning phenomenon, I know nothing about your background, but normal insulin and leptin sensitive people would see this numbers if ketotic as a result of glukoneogenesis.

# Thread: For our European Readers:  ALERT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/for-our-european-readers-alert.3069/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-17T15:16:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/for-our-european-readers-alert.3069/post-45327>

[http://www.antiageingconference.com/aacl2012.jpg](http://http://www.antiageingconference.com/aacl2012.jpg)

Dr Sergey Dzugan Md PhD

Co-founder and Chief Scientific Officer of the Dzugan Institute of Restorative Medicine, Deerfield Beach, FL. Dr. Dzugan is a former heart surgeon. International Academy of Creative Endeavors (Russia) awarded Dr. Dzugan with the honorary title of Academician for outstanding contribution to the development of new methods of hypercholesterolemia and migraine treatment. He performed presentations multiple times at the prestigious International Congress on Anti-Aging Medicine. He is the author of 143 publications in medical journals, author of 3 books, holder of 3 patents, and author of numerous articles in health related magazines. Dr. Dzugan is a Member of the Editorial Board of the Neuroendocrinilogy Letters and a member of the Medical Advisory Board at Life Extension Magazine.

Dr. Dzugan is the author of 143 publications in medical journals and these publications include surgical, oncological, academic and anti-aging topics. Also, several articles were published in Life Extension Magazine and The South African Journal of Natural Medicine. He is the author of 3 books and holder of 3 patents (all related to heart surgery). Dr. Dzugan is a Member of the Editorial Board of the Neuroendocrinilogy Letters and a member of the Medical Advisory Board at Life Extension Magazine.

Dr Dzugan will be giving the Thursday Morning Pre-ConferenceWorkshop on:

Bioidentical Hormones in Clinical Practice: How to do? Potential problems and safety.

Professor Giovanni Scapagnini Md PhD

Professor Scapagnini attended the University of Catania School of Medicine and Surgery in Catania, Italy and graduated in 1992 with a medical degree. He continued his education by obtaining a Ph.D. in Neurobiology also from the University of Catania in 2000. Since completing his education, Dr. Scapagnini has conducted research with the Institute of Pharmacology School of Medicine associated with the University of Catania and has worked as a Visiting Scientist with Department of Surgical Research, Northwick Park Institute for Medical Research, Harrow, UK in 1999, and with Laboratory of Adaptive Systems, National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke, National Institute of Health in Bethesda, MD, USA in 2000. Dr. Scapagnini currently holds two academic positions as Assistant Professor with the Institute of Neurological Sciences, Italian National Research Council and with Blanchette Rockefeller Neurosciences Institute, West Virginia University. He has recently obtained a visiting professorship with the Institute of Human Virology, University of Maryland, where he is in charge of a research project on HIV dementia. He is also the scientific director of the â€œResearch & Progressâ€￾ foundation, founded by Dr Robert C. Gallo. He is author of 35 indexed scientific papers and several book chapters. His fields of research regard gene expression profiles of cellular stress response and biology and molecular mechanisms of brain aging and nerurodegenerative disorders. In particular he has studied the anti-aging activities of several nutraceuticals present in the Mediterranean diet.

Professor Scapagnini will be giving the Thursday Afternoon Pre-ConferenceWorkshop on:

Are there any Effective Protocols to Predict & Prevent Brain Ageing and Neuro-Degeneration Disorders?

If you can make it may be a cool thing to go to and hook up and talk to the doctors.......

# Thread: weight gain
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weight-gain.3073/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### suray1 — 2012-08-17T19:12:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weight-gain.3073/post-45370>

Hi Dr. Kruse,

I have been following your blog as much as I'm able to with 2 special needs kids. We have been Paleo (GAPS) for over a year now and my son, age 9, has gained 20 pounds...he is now 93 lbs. I am wondering what is causing this? As an adopted child he suffered very significant traumas within his first year of life (so malnourished that he had no intestinal villi) so I know his cortisol levels must be part of the issue. We have been doing interventions for 3 years based on neuroplasticity, so he is emotionally more stable, but I know he's got gut issues. He is always hungry, even with high fat, and doesn't know when he's full. I have made an appt with an Endocrinologist in order to do testing. I'd like to make an appt with you with test results. I want to make sure I'm asking for the right tests...what would you recommend?

Susan

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-17T19:19:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weight-gain.3073/post-45372>

The first issue is the gut.......flora. Then his exposure to light then his diet. The next few blogs will be eye openers for many including you. Even though he is young a full panel and a metametrix array maybe waht he needs. Look into a PCR analysis of his gut flora and begin to chart his bristol stool chart....and record it with pictures and make a file of the photos.......then it will tell you what foods he really needs. And just for you........GAPS fails huge when eukaryotes are the offender......remember this when you read the next blog. Candida is a eukaryote. that is all the hints you will get.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-17T21:04:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/good-explainaiton-on-why-metaformin-works-for-those-who-are-insulin-resistant.3074/post-45400>

Metformin is only a relatively weak inhibitor of mitochondrail complex I, (For geeks: the incidence of life threatening lactic acidosis is very low.) Metformin generate huge amounts of superoxide at the mitochondrial membrane. Pay attn to the fact that in T2D we have IR which also produces SO. So how it works for longevity is counter intuitive to all those who dont get biochemistry. Annexin V is a marker of of bad cellular function in IR. Metformin does not stop oxphos at complex one, but it decrease our leakiness there. I believe it does this by reversing flow of electrons on the inner mitochondrial membrane there and makes them go to complex two which offers a major organic chemical advantage. I talked about that advantage in the comments of the quantum electron post. We produce more ATP but create less ROS. **This means we age less**. We also can reverse neolithic disease. This is how metformin works. So metformin poisons visceral fat cells and makes them burn their fat globs at complex two. Now here is the irony that confuses some who think Mg is the be all end all. Metformin on the surfaces causes IR in fat cells.

And many think that is why it ia poison and should be avoided in all cases. I dont, and never have. Why? because they dont understand how it works totally, just partially. Metformin's correlation with low carb diet: since glucose generates 5 times more NADH+ (cytochrome one) then at cytochrome two (FADH2) it increases complex 1 activity. By the way, more Mag is required too at cytochrome 1 chemically, so metformin is still wins if you believe the Mg dogma too. Now from Nick Lane's masterpiece, Power Sex and Suicide, he reports, it looks like there should be balanced use of Carbon 1- Carbon 4 and this leads to healthy and happy mitochondria. What is bad or off, in low T3 states, leptin resistance, PCOS, T2D, or IR?

ALL suffer from bad mitochondria! go back and re read my mitochondrial series now. June 2011. The giant circle of life! Terry L. Wahls would probably love this because she is all over minding her mitochondria. I put all my MS patients on metformin because of these intricate biochemical factors. People who do consults with me have known about this for years. The rest of the world is just waking up to it. Anti-Aging medicine being the leaders. metformin appears to correct the changes in adipocytes which lead to systemic insulin resistance. There will come a point where, as adipocytes shrink, they will become functional again and generate appropriate levels of fatty acids for the metabolic conditions prevailing and release an appropriate mix of saturated and monounsaturated fats for normal body energy homeostasis.

### chocolate — 2012-08-18T01:32:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/good-explainaiton-on-why-metaformin-works-for-those-who-are-insulin-resistant.3074/post-45464>

[http://www.diabetesselfmanagement.com/Blog/Diane-Fennell/metformin-and-b12-supplementation/](http://http://www.diabetesselfmanagement.com/Blog/Diane-Fennell/metformin-and-b12-supplementation/)

> To determine the relationship between metformin use and vitamin B12 deficiency, and to assess whether supplementation with this vitamin could correct deficiencies, researchers looked at data from the National Health and Nutritional Examination Survey (NHANES), which was conducted in the United States between 1999 and 2006. Information on roughly 1,600 adults age 50 and older with Type 2 diabetes and 6,900 adults age 50 and older without Type 2 was included.
>
>
>
> The researchers found that B12 deficiency was present in 5.4% of people with diabetes who were taking metformin, compared to 2.4% of people with diabetes not taking metformin and 3.3% of people without diabetes. They further noted that use of supplements containing B12 was not linked with a reduction of B12 deficiency in people with diabetes, compared to a two-thirds reduction in deficiency among people who did not have diabetes. It is currently recommended that people 50 and older consume 2.4 micrograms of vitamin B12 daily from either fortified foods or supplements.
>
>
>
> According to study author Godfrey P. Oakley, Jr., MD, MPSM, “It is important to conduct further research to learn how much B12 is needed to correct the deficiency and to determine whether or not raising serum B12 levels improves the clinical picture for persons taking metformin who have low serum B12 concentrations.”

We've ordered berberine. I think it uses roughly the same pathways.

Berberine, a Natural Plant Product, Activates AMP-Activated Protein Kinase With Beneficial Metabolic Effects in Diabetic and Insulin-Resistant States [http://journal.9med.net/qikan/article.php?id=210309](http://http://journal.9med.net/qikan/article.php?id=210309)

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-18T18:38:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/good-explainaiton-on-why-metaformin-works-for-those-who-are-insulin-resistant.3074/post-45544>

I'm still blown away by all this...... fingers crossed PCP will jump on board w/metaformin - initially she was hesitant... but I think after the email I just sent her.... we'll be adding some more weapons to the arsenal...

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-18T18:47:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/good-explainaiton-on-why-metaformin-works-for-those-who-are-insulin-resistant.3074/post-45547>

anything that down regulates MTOr will use more methyl groups for detox phase one and two in liver. This is why.

# Thread: Quick Start Guide just for MDs?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quick-start-guide-just-for-mds.3075/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Lyndra — 2012-08-17T21:00:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quick-start-guide-just-for-mds.3075/post-45394>

I just visited the PCP Souldanzer is using and he is very interested in learning more about what Dr. Kruse is advocating. He seems to be one of the very few doctors who is not intimidated by the notion that there are things he doesn't know and he shows great curiosity about new ideas and learning more. There is only so much info I as a patient can communicate to him in an office visit, so he needs to go online to read for himself. He has visited the website and took a gander at the quilt but is a bit lost as to where to begin.

I know there is a getting started guide for laypeople, but I think it would be helpful to have something tailored to MDs and other medical professionals to help them ramp up. I would love to have him read all the blogs from the beginning, as the blogs build upon one another in a good sequence. Realistically, though, I think few MDs are going to invest the time in doing it that way, especially if their only knowledge of Dr. K is from a patient's endorsement.

I'm sure Dr. K is considering how best to develop his partnership with PCPs and perhaps a getting started guide would fit in with that.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-17T21:55:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quick-start-guide-just-for-mds.3075/post-45416>

He did really visit the website??? That is SO COOL!!! I encouraged him to on Thursday.... I said to approach it with a really open mind..... and to not try to get too much too fast. That he didn't need to buy into it all right away.

I'm so happy he actually did and had a positive reaction to it. I've been working on this for a while.....dropping just a little more info every time I was in to see him.... I wasn't sure he was going to check it out really as busy as he is. I can't get over he actually DID

You Lyndra, I think we as patients can help guide him a bit..... just by talking about our complaints and how we'd like to address them. We have read it all.... we can point him to the most important things and educate when we are in to see him.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-17T23:06:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quick-start-guide-just-for-mds.3075/post-45425>
