### CarolMorris — 2012-08-23T23:26:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quick-start-guide-just-for-mds.3075/post-46600>

I think that would be awesome! I have a hard time getting through everything on the site-so a busy Physician with a full practice--enough said. I don't post as much as I'd like because it's long after the fact or i feel there might already be something on the site I haven't made it to. Ask Jack is always my first stop--I'm guessing many other people's first stop as well. To have one for Physicians and a getting started page is brilliant.

I'm sitting here giggling...when I saw my PCP Tues. to go over what I learned in my consult #2, every so often during the visit she would ask, what did Dr. Kruse suggest? I like to present the issue and see what she suggests..she is right on with Dr. K on all my issues...leaky gut...metformin...cytomel...lab testing...she loved the morning cortisol solution...sun, sex, BAB!! what she's not familiar with she finds out and is not afraid to say she doesn't know...she's loving learning with me. I can't hug her enough.

I really felt like I won the wellness lottery when I found Dr. K and those of us that have a PCP who's on board...we are VERY fortunate!!

I work in a medical bldg. and I'm nominating my PCP for Physician of the month...#1 reason on the nomination form--she encourages me to actively participate in my own wellness. she even knew something about CT.

Thank you again Dr. K for all you do for us!!!!

### Paradox — 2012-09-25T07:41:14+0300

CarolM

I'm in the Palm Springs area too and have just given my PC doc a copy of The Quilt and she was very interested.. Will be seeing her in another week. Will be interesting to see how she reacts to it then. So far she has gone along with all the labs I have requested and has reluctantly given me additional progesterone and T3 . She is curious so I'm hoping she reads this website!

# Thread: Metformin alternative?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin-alternative.3079/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-18T02:40:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin-alternative.3079/post-45476>

[http://www.leanerlivingstore.com/Glycosolve--500-mg-of-berberine-per-serving\_p\_9.html](http://http://www.leanerlivingstore.com/Glycosolve--500-mg-of-berberine-per-serving_p_9.html)

Barbjean94 • 2 months ago

This is very interesting. And, if I'm not mistaken,, it's just as Dr. Atkins advised in his book. He said that some people will have trouble losing on a simple low carb diet. For these metabolically challenged folks, he recommended a very high fat diet, lowering both protein and carbs in conjunction. The blood ketone testing Is wonderful! It will really allow for our individual metabolic vagaraties. I do think that a damaged metabolism might need a little prescription help in the form of Metformin. I have also read some interesting work on Berberine, a naturally occurring derivative of goldenseal. Apparently it has similar positive metabolic effects on blood sugar, and less side effects.

I found this in the comments on livinlavidalowcarb.com [http://livinlavidalowcarb.com/blog/jimmy-moores-n1-experiments-nutritional-ketosis-day-1-30/14409](http://http://livinlavidalowcarb.com/blog/jimmy-moores-n1-experiments-nutritional-ketosis-day-1-30/14409)

Anyone heard of Berberine or Glycosolve? Would it work as well or possible better than Metformin? Differences and similarities?

Contemplating buying some since it seems I can get this without an RX.

# Thread: New egg yok study...the sky is falling!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-egg-yok-study-the-sky-is-falling.3090/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PrimalPam — 2012-08-18T21:53:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-egg-yok-study-the-sky-is-falling.3090/post-45594>

[http://articles.latimes.com/2012/aug/14/news/la-heb-egg-cholesterol-smoking-20120814](http://http://articles.latimes.com/2012/aug/14/news/la-heb-egg-cholesterol-smoking-20120814)

This kind of stuff makes me so mad I could spit....

I actually heard about this study this morning as I was listening to one of my favorite radio programs, NPR's "Wait Wait Don't Tell Me". The "intelligent minds" on that show actually defended the study, saying it was done by...duh...scientists. So it must be true!

Eating egg yolks almost as dangerous as smoking!!!!

Um, what else were the egg-eating folks doing? Were they eating toast with their eggs? Potatoes? Milk in their coffee? Coca-Cola? None of these other foods could have caused their increased risk levels. Nope, gotta be the eggs.

Correlation does not equal causation, and these studies (like the red meat one) irritate me to no end.

I actually posted this to "Ask Jack", and here was his response:

Dr. Kruse said, "That study is a joke......absolutely ridiculous it ever got published. This is why evidence based medicine is dangerous. If this can get published what other BS do you think gets in? Hint= cholesterol stories?"

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-18T21:58:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-egg-yok-study-the-sky-is-falling.3090/post-45597>

[http://www.westonaprice.org/blogs/cmasterjohn/2012/08/16/does-eating-egg-yolks-increase-arterial-plaque/](http://http://www.westonaprice.org/blogs/cmasterjohn/2012/08/16/does-eating-egg-yolks-increase-arterial-plaque/)

### Destiny — 2012-08-18T22:11:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-egg-yok-study-the-sky-is-falling.3090/post-45599>

> **PrimalPam;45968 wrote:** [http://articles.latimes.com/2012/aug/14/news/la-heb-egg-cholesterol-smoking-20120814](http://http://articles.latimes.com/2012/aug/14/news/la-heb-egg-cholesterol-smoking-20120814)
>
>
>
> This kind of stuff makes me so mad I could spit....
>
>
>
> I actually heard about this study this morning as I was listening to one of my favorite radio programs, NPR's "Wait Wait Don't Tell Me". The "intelligent minds" on that show actually defended the study, saying it was done by...duh...scientists. So it must be true!
>
>
>
> Eating egg yolks almost as dangerous as smoking!!!!
>
>
>
> Um, what else were the egg-eating folks doing? Were they eating toast with their eggs? Potatoes? Milk in their coffee? Coca-Cola? None of these other foods could have caused their increased risk levels. Nope, gotta be the eggs.

### Sem — 2012-08-19T01:23:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-hormone-labs.3096/post-45625>

I had some labs done about six weeks ago. My thyroid was in the bottom of the range and I had a positive ANA.

I went back for more hormone labs and my doctor says I need HRT. I am a 42 year old woman. Here are my results.

DHEA 188.24. Range 34.50-568.92

Estradiol 12.0 post menopausal is 0-32.3

FSH is 62.3 and post menopausal is 23.0-116.3

Progesterone

### Sem — 2012-08-19T01:48:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-hormone-labs.3096/post-45632>

I just wanted to add that my doctor wants me to go on estrogen patch and micronized progesterone.

Progesterone e with SLE have a positive ANA test result. If someone also has symptoms of SLE, such as arthritis, a rash, and autoimmune thrombocytopenia, then she probably has SLE. In cases such as these, a positive ANA result can be useful to support SLE diagnosis. Two subset tests for specific types of autoantibodies, such as anti-dsDNA and anti-SM, may be ordered (often as an ENA panel) to help confirm that the condition is SLE.

A positive ANA can also mean that the person has drug-induced lupus. This condition is associated with the development of autoantibodies to histones, which are water-soluble proteins rich in the amino acids lysine and arginine. An anti-histone test may be ordered to support the diagnosis of drug-induced lupus.

Other conditions in which a positive ANA test result may be seen include:

SjÃ¶gren syndrome: Between 40% and 70% of those with this condition have a positive ANA test result. While this finding supports the diagnosis, a negative result does not rule it out. The doctor may want to test for two subsets of ANA: Anti-SS-A (Ro) and Anti-SS-B (La). About 90% or more of people with SjÃ¶gren syndrome have autoantibodies to SSA.

Scleroderma (systemic sclerosis): About 60% to 90% of those with scleroderma have a positive ANA finding. In people who may have this condition, ANA subset tests can help distinguished two forms of the disease, limited versus diffuse. The diffuse form is more severe. Limited disease is most closely associated with the anticentromere pattern of ANA staining (and the anticentromere test), while the diffuse form is associated with autoantibodies to the anti–Scl-70.

A positive result on the ANA also may show up in people with Raynaud's disease, rheumatoid arthritis, dermatomyositis or polymyosis, mixed connective tissue disease, and other autoimmune conditions. For more on these conditions, visit the American Autoimmune Related Diseases Association patient information page.

A doctor must rely on test results, clinical symptoms, and the person's history for diagnosis. Because symptoms may come and go, it may take months or years to show a pattern that might suggest SLE or any of the other autoimmune diseases.

A negative ANA result makes SLE an unlikely diagnosis. It usually is not necessary to immediately repeat a negative ANA test; however, due to the episodic nature of autoimmune diseases, it may be worthwhile to repeat the ANA test at a future date if symptoms persist.

Aside from rare cases, further autoantibody (subset) testing is not necessary if a person has a negative ANA result.

### Me2 — 2012-08-20T03:25:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-in-colorado.3114/post-45773>

Hello you all,

I am a 32 yr old female w no children. I have completed the leptin reset and do a variation of cold CT which includes daily cold showers and spot ice packs as I have no bathtub. I have been primal for 1 yr and on GAPS for 1 yr, a total of 2 yrs in the paleosphere. Guess what? My hormones are still out of balance, and I have yet to lose one little pound. Granted I am about 27-30% body fat, which is lower-ish starting point but I would like to be even lower for health. I am even considering doing a half hour session w/ Dr. Kruse despite the fact that I am a teacher with very little $.

Hormonal issues: tender swollen breasts, bloating 2 wks into 28 day cycle.

What labs would you suggest?

Currently I take:

Protandim

high vitamin butter oil

fermented cod liver oil

biotin

b6

gotu cola

maka

selenium

iodine

digestive enzyme

probiotic

magnesium

geletin

msm

D

wowza.

thanks!

### vkiernan — 2012-08-20T03:50:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-in-colorado.3114/post-45779>

Welcome Me2. Just wondering, you mentioned you have "completed" the leptin reset. Does this mean you have all the signs and have reset and now moved on to the "post" reset? From my own experience, you sound like you have some est. dominance going on.

As for your question, a consult with Jack would help you decide which meaningful labs you should get so you don't waste money, time, etc. trying to figure this out. He also has a blog for labs. I would recommend a consult before doing anything. Write up an extensive history and have your consult. It won't be wasted dollars for sure. Will probably save you dollars on supps etc.

### Me2 — 2012-08-23T05:18:33+0300

### vkiernan — 2012-08-23T15:40:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-in-colorado.3114/post-46517>

> **Me2;46881 wrote:** By the way, what is the approximate amount people spend on the testing with insurance?

I don't think one can answer this as we all have something different. Whether it is a ppo, hmo, etc. I have an hmo so I only pay the copay for the office visit but not for lab draws. Some have a ppo and they get a nice discount. Others go out of network and pay quite a bit more. And last but not least, those w/out ins. pay it all. So, it really depends on "insurance." You can call them direct and ask how much is covered or ask the HR dept. if it is a work bennie.

### Me2 — 2012-08-24T04:55:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-in-colorado.3114/post-46649>

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-24T05:25:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-in-colorado.3114/post-46658>

There really isn't Me2. Everybody's got a different history and issues. Some people can fix themselves just by following the various rx's. Or you can take me, who's so broken it's not even funny anymore and I'm at my doctors' offices multiple times a week sometimes. If I told you my "range" of medical expenses, you'd fall over. Seriously. But one thing is for sure: the sooner you address things, the less damage there will be and the less it will cost. I do recommend getting a consult or participating in the email lab rec's option b/c a lot of us have spent money on things we eventually didn't need.

### Me2 — 2012-08-25T03:00:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-in-colorado.3114/post-46885>

Thanks Souldanzer, I hope everything goes well for you. I wish there was more transparency in our medical system.

### Me2 — 2012-08-25T03:36:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-in-colorado.3114/post-46916>

Okay one more thing, if I do the e-mail lab consult how does this help me if there is no follow up? What do the labs mean to me who knows nothing about labs?

### vkiernan — 2012-08-25T03:42:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-in-colorado.3114/post-46920>

> **Me2;47333 wrote:** Okay one more thing, if I do the e-mail lab consult how does this help me if there is no follow up? What do the labs mean to me who knows nothing about labs?

Exactly the reason you need a consult after the labs. But you also might need a doc to help you implement, if need be, after labs and a consult as Dr. K. cannot prescribe for you since you have not actually gone to him.

I know, this is all so frustrating but if you have issues the only way to really fix things is to know what is going on and symptoms alone do not tell the whole story.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-25T03:58:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-in-colorado.3114/post-46924>

> **Me2;47333 wrote:** Okay one more thing, if I do the e-mail lab consult how does this help me if there is no follow up? What do the labs mean to me who knows nothing about labs?

I'd say you need to think this through a bit before you act. Depending on your finances you might prefer different options. If you're really limited and not so sick, then you might just stick with the various rx's for now and see where that leads you.

The other cheaper option is to learn a hell of a lot about yourself and this world on here and the blogs and anywhere really you can find stuff. Then you figure out for yourself what you might need. You can then order your own labs or use your doc and interpret them yourself or post them here for us to help.

There's also \*us\*. We can help a lot  We've got labs experts, thyroid experts, eating disorder experts (lol), mothering experts, medical experts (that would be Dr K ), we got all sorts of people on here to help you figure things out if you want us to help.

Then there is the consult option. I'd say if you choose the email consult then you probably also want at least one follow up.

I probably forgot some other ways ... but those are some.

### Me2 — 2012-08-25T19:46:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-in-colorado.3114/post-47057>

Thanks soul, I am in awe of the kindness of strangers, and what a sweet community this is. Also filled with tons of expertise, as you pointed out.

I feel like I HAVE tried to figure it out on my own and now I'm at an impasse. Here's what I've done:

Primal blueprint

Primal blueprint no dairy

PB no dairy, nuts, fruit

PB very low carb (worst idea EVER!)

GAPS (since nov)

leptin reset while on GAPS (start May 2012)

daily cold showers and spot icing (since june 2012)

supplements galore

Now I'm just tired of trying things, but I still have hormonal disorders and my weight is not optimizing. I feel like I am so close but yet so far. I do have a new plan, I bought blue blocker glasses yesterday, and I'm going to buy amber bulbs for the house. I go to sleep before 10 & wake @6:30. I can't afford seafood every day, so I eat as much as I can afford.

To be honest, I'm over having plans, I'm ready to fly on the wings of an optimal Pegasus.

Maybe I just need to pony up the cash for the consult. Too bad the priced are going up, though goodness knows he is worth every penny.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-25T20:31:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-in-colorado.3114/post-47076>

> **Me2;46153 wrote:** Hello you all,
>
>
>
> I am a 32 yr old female w no children. I have completed the leptin reset and do a variation of cold CT which includes daily cold showers and spot ice packs as I have no bathtub. I have been primal for 1 yr and on GAPS for 1 yr, a total of 2 yrs in the paleosphere. Guess what? My hormones are still out of balance, and I have yet to lose one little pound. Granted I am about 27-30% body fat, which is lower-ish starting point but I would like to be even lower for health. I am even considering doing a half hour session w/ Dr. Kruse despite the fact that I am a teacher with very little $.
>
>
>
> Hormonal issues: tender swollen breasts, bloating 2 wks into 28 day cycle.
>
>
>
> What labs would you suggest?
>
>
>
> Currently I take:
>
> Protandim
>
> high vitamin butter oil
>
> fermented cod liver oil
>
> biotin
>
> b6
>
> gotu cola
>
> maka
>
> selenium
>
> iodine
>
> digestive enzyme
>
> probiotic
>
> magnesium
>
> geletin
>
> msm
>
> D
>
>
>
> wowza.
>
>
>
> thanks!

Based on the little info you have posted here, I'd say you probably haven't completed your leptin reset yet. Read the signs of leptin sensitivity and see if you qualify.

My suggestions:

\- go back on leptin reset until you have definite signs of leptin sensitivity. I've been on it for 10+months now but then, I'm kinda broken

\- add leaky gut rx/autoimmune protocol instead of GAPS

\- drop all your supplements (don't buy them anymore as they run out) and spend your money on better food (seafood) instead. I'm particularly looking at high vitamin butter oil and fermented cod liver oil here. They are super expensive and you could buy quite a bit of farmed (cheaper) seafood for that.

\- keep the progesterone, the vitamin D, and the magnesium. They are probably necessary.

\- Good food almost always takes priority over supplements.

\- eat as epi-paleo as you can. Even the worst fish is better than the best meat. Shop at asian markets. I can buy "wild-caught" (who knows) whole fish there for as little as two bucks a pound. Only the worst meat can beat that.

\- learn how to ferment your own vegetables for probiotics and digestive help. you can make spendy store-bought Kombucha for pennies at home and it's tasty!

\- Epi-paleo will take care of the selenium and iodine issue, most likely

\- eat a bit of offal/liver every week

\- Let go of the need to lose weight. Your body weight will adjust as you get healthier.

\- Read and understand the blogs (and comments) as much as you can. You will learn a lot about yourself this way. Keep re-reading them and pay attention to the parts that apply to you.

\- Get your morning sun.

\- Keep doing the CT.

\- probably a ton of other things

If you can barely come up with the money for a consult, then this might not be the good choice for you right now. Since the consult is sort of intended to get you the correct labs and such. Not to say Dr K won't help you just based on your history.... but you can probably find a lot of that info in the blogs and with help on the forums. Just from one poor person to another

If you shared more about your history, people on here could probably help you more with regards to your decision if you need a consult or not. Some people are more broken than others. But putting yourself into a stressful financial situation is probably not the answer either.

### Me2 — 2012-08-27T04:28:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-in-colorado.3114/post-47413>

I had a little breakdown today, my sister is GAPS too now and in 3 months she has lost so much weight. We use to be the same size (10), now she is probably a 4. Same w/ my dad. I have been really good about not fixating and trying to love myself as I am, but I embarked on this quest 2 years ago to lose weight and my weight is stuck. I have a long and storied relationship with my body image, and lately I have been at my best mentally, but today was a low.

While crying my husband said I should do what I need to and that we would make it work financially, but even with his blessing I started wondering if it was even worth it to have a consult. Would Jack just tell me to go on the Leptin reset again and eat epi-paleo just like Soul? That would not really be worth the $. I have till the end of the month to decide.

Until then, Okay Soul, I'm going to begrudgingly go on the leptin reset again. I say that because i really don't like it. Protein bomb in the AM is too much for me. I think I will follow Sisson's advice and only do 30 grams (that's all a person can process in one sitting w/o it turning to glucose). Does gelatin protein count? Also I like carbs, nothing crazy but some berries and coconut water here and there.

I am also going to source some seafood, maybe I can go to costco w/ my sis.

I will look into the leaky gut rx, though I do love GAPS, some days I fell better than I have since I was a kid.

We make our own brews here, honey kombucha, beet kvaas, and I just started some wild fermented pickles.

Soul: thank you x 100. I wish I had some sage advice for you but alas i do not. Perhaps one day I can repay the favor.

Attached files [](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/47859=439-283838_10151346180949418_1095981334_n.jpg)

I think it's a good idea to play this out like a game of chess, always ask - and then what?! Is there something you could find out from a particular test that would change what you do? OR would it simply be the mental ammo you need to actually do it? Coming into winter seems like a good time to really try to embrace your discomfort and be a sargent about what ever plan you go on.

The thing with the sis would drive anybody nuts, you get a day to be pissed about that

But, the next day comes. . .have you started the CT? The effects on mood and outlook of that are fabulous. I don't know if it will help right away with the weight - but you won't care as much, heheh.

Lots of people on here seem to start loving the seafood, eating less naturally and finding it's easier done than said once they start down the seafood path. . .might be worth spending the $ on the seafood up front and giving that a shot.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-27T05:16:27+0300

I have been really good about not fixating and trying to love myself as I am, but I embarked on this quest 2 years ago to lose weight and my weight is stuck. Would Jack just tell me to go on the Leptin reset again and eat epi-paleo just like Soul? That would not really be worth the $.** I have till the end of the month to decide.
>

> Until then, Okay Soul, I'm going to begrudgingly go on the leptin reset again. I say that because i really don't like it. Protein bomb in the AM is too much for me. I think I will follow Sisson's advice and only do 30 grams (that's all a person can process in one sitting w/o it turning to glucose). Does gelatin protein count? Also I like carbs, nothing crazy but some berries and coconut water here and there.
>
>
>
> I am also going to source some seafood, maybe I can go to costco w/ my sis.
>
>
>
> I will look into the leaky gut rx, though I do love GAPS, some days I fell better than I have since I was a kid.
>
>
>
> We make our own brews here, honey kombucha, beet kvaas, and I just started some wild fermented pickles.
>
>
>
> Soul: thank you x 100. I wish I had some sage advice for you but alas i do not. Perhaps one day I can repay the favor.
>
>
>
> Here is a pic of me and my husband cold CTing at 11,800 ft in cathedral lake. (don't be fooled by the flattering angle)
> [](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/47859=439-283838_10151346180949418_1095981334_n.jpg)

Sounds like you need to learn to be comfortable in the uncomfortable

About the consult or not: Dr K might tell you things based on your history. Thing is, if you are not able to get anywhere with the reset alone - most likely he will need labs to find out why you are stuck. If you need BHRT, there is a lot of testing involved to balance them correctly. If you have some other problem - you will need to run labs for that and then you will need a local doc who can rx things. What I'm trying to explain to you is that one consult isn't the answer to it all. The consult is the start to a bigger process. So unless you're really lucky and your problem is small, you probably will have an even bigger issue coming up with the $$ for the follow up care after your consult. If your problem is small, chances are people on here can help you figure it out.

If you "do the reset with 30grams of protein" then you're not doing the Leptin reset. You're doing a Sisson/Dr Kruse mix process that you put together yourself. Don't expect something to work that you are not doing correctly. There is an underlying reason for the big protein breakfast. Go lower and you're not doing the rx. IIRC, people with low morning cortisol do not like to eat after rising. One clue to your problem.

How severe are the body image issues? Any ED history?

No need to repay anything  We're all here to help and be helped.

If you have some other problem - you will need to run labs for that and then you will need a local doc who can rx things. What I'm trying to explain to you is that one consult isn't the answer to it all. The consult is the start to a bigger process. So unless you're really lucky and your problem is small, you probably will have an even bigger issue coming up with the $$ for the follow up care after your consult. If your problem is small, chances are people on here can help you figure it out.
>
>
>
> If you "do the reset with 30grams of protein" then you're not doing the Leptin reset. You're doing a Sisson/Dr Kruse mix process that you put together yourself. Don't expect something to work that you are not doing correctly. There is an underlying reason for the big protein breakfast. Go lower and you're not doing the rx. IIRC, people with low morning cortisol do not like to eat after rising. One clue to your problem.
>
>
>
> How severe are the body image issues? Any ED history?
>
>
>
>
>
> No need to repay anything  We're all here to help and be helped.

So, yeah. I'm going to go with your advice and try and hack my life myself. I will start delving into the threads and posts like you said. Do you have any suggestions about where to start? I will give it till Christmas and then reevaluate. Good thinking Soul, I never liked doctors too much anyway. Speaking of to people have success with alternative healing? Does acupuncture work? Also I will do the leptin reset correctly, though I am still not convinced that overeating is the key to success.

I have not had a diagnosable eating disorder, although I have heard ED defined as eating in a way that you would not around others, and that is true for me. I am an emotional/stress eater, and over eater, and have had a history of binging, though that hasn't happened since going on GAPS. My grandmother and aunt are anorexic and have tortured me about my weight since I was 3, yes my mother had a toddler come home from grandmas patting her thighs and calling them fat. Don't worry I have been in counseling for 7 yrs.

I dealt with my anxiety and depression by partying for a good decade. I do wonder if some of the issues I have is due to damage done that way. Have you heard anyone on the forums/Jack talk about recovering from the SAYWL (standard American young person weekend lifestyle, aka binge drinking and, well, yeah...). I don't do that anymore, i do still enjoy a glass of wine or two.

Yay, my blue blockers came today! Got them on Amazon for $7/pair.

### Me2 — 2012-09-08T19:35:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-in-colorado.3114/post-50277>

I can really see how stress effects my. My waist is building now that I am back to teaching (at risk kids). Am ever hopeful.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Proud Mama,  and fascination at live working with a ferrari engine. . . .
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/proud-mama-and-fascination-at-live-working-with-a-ferrari-engine.3115/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-08-20T04:43:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/proud-mama-and-fascination-at-live-working-with-a-ferrari-engine.3115/post-45792>

I just had to share. . .

We spend yesterday at the pool, got a little too much sun. After a week in lower altitude, I got all cocky about our superhero powers to avoid the ills of sunburn. And forgot we burn much faster up here closer to the sun. My son had some pinkish cheeks and upper shoulders.

This a.m., he gets up and goes and grabs cans of oysters, clams and sardines out of the pantry and proceeds to feed himself these for bfast (he just turned 6).

I guess he just craved what he needed (more seafood to balance the environmental impacts of life).

He decided on more clams and some berries for a snack.

It's just so awesome seeing my little human's body working so in sync with life and having the ability to desire what's good for his body.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-20T04:47:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/proud-mama-and-fascination-at-live-working-with-a-ferrari-engine.3115/post-45793>

And just because. . .

We harvested a boatload of basil yesterday. My son and I listened to podcast and picked basil. I thought he kinda looked like an anti child labor poster!

# Thread: Name that schrooom!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-08-20T19:30:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-45868>

I dared to venture to my lame attempt at a compost pile today. Found a boatload of these - any ideas? Seems like mushrooms could be something one just grows. Anybody done this? I want to - but as Lyndra can attest I'm fairly garden clueless.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-20T19:45:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-45877>

I have no idea about mushrooms and it's one of those things where i believe erring on the side of caution is best.

These look a lot like the kind that a friend of mine regularly picks all over the place - they grow in moist places like recently heavily watered lawns. She will pick them as we walk (our dogs) and then either eat them right away or cook them at home or dry them for winter use.

Don't try eating them b/c I say they might be edible..... my friend grew up in eastern Europe and that's how they got part of their food so she knows what she's doing since she was a little child.

### vkiernan — 2012-08-20T20:32:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-45889>

Never eat a wild mushroom that "you" did not start growing unless you are sure what it is. The are that poisonous as far as I know. At least that is the fear my parents put into us kids back then. You can buy kits to grow your own if you'd like to start. The only wild schroom I eat is a morel from my back woods. Everything else comes from the grocery store.

### Lyndra — 2012-08-20T20:51:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-45893>

> **Cavemam;46252 wrote:** I dared to venture to my lame attempt at a compost pile today. Found a boatload of these - any ideas? Seems like mushrooms could be something one just grows. Anybody done this? I want to - but as Lyndra can attest I'm fairly garden clueless.

You can definitely grow your own but as vkiernan said, you need to start out with known specimens. I have a friend who recently collected 10 pounds of wild mushrooms but she knows what she's doing. I would never eat a mushroom that I just found somewhere; too much risk. A friend's dog was poisoned by eating backyard mushrooms (and sadly, died).

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-22T17:29:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-46311>

I remember going mushroom picking w/my biotony teacher at USAFA - he was a fungiologist by trade - he did the actual picking - we didn't I clearly remember he said never to eat wild mushrooms, unless you're with an expert such as himself. HE also did provide fair warning that at time even experts can get some shrooms confused. stick w/what you can buy in the store - or what you can grow you're self from a "Starter kit"

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-22T17:50:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-46316>

Well, Y'all scared me enough to not eat them. But they look so lovely!!! And how cool would it be to have my own mushroom patch. I wonder if there is a way to "test" or an expert I can consult. Hmmmmmmm.

I am thinking of planting a blueberry bush in that spot - although I'm not sure what would grow. It's only $10, so what the hell. This spot is where I tossed "compostable" stuff for a couple of years with no idea wtf I was doing. It turned into a big moldy pile that none of us dare to approach. I will also need to buy a big sturdy steel rake and a shovel.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-22T18:22:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-46331>

Just FYI, blueberries don't grow in our neck of woods unless you get very lucky to have an unusually acid spot of dirt. We're really alkaline in CO..... blueberries like it acidic. YOu can create such a spot in your garden if you desire but it does take some upkeep b/c the soil will keep getting too alkaline for blueberries to thrive.

### SeaHorse — 2012-08-22T19:29:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-46362>

If you can get pine needles...they will acidify the soil, they are used as mulch for blueberries.

We have a lot of woods on our property and this fall I'm getting a mushroom expert to come and forage. He will get most of the mushrooms this year to sell, but I will get a free education and the knowledge to be safe. This guy is amazing, he sells mushrooms at the farmer's market that he harvests for free....no tricky ones though...only things like Hen of the Woods, Morels, Oyster, Chanterelles etc.

Cavemam...you could take your mushrooms to the nearest Botany dept at a University...they would love to id them for you.

### Lyndra — 2012-08-22T19:41:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-46371>

> **Cavemam;46705 wrote:** Well, Y'all scared me enough to not eat them. But they look so lovely!!! And how cool would it be to have my own mushroom patch. I wonder if there is a way to "test" or an expert I can consult. Hmmmmmmm.
>
>
>
> I am thinking of planting a blueberry bush in that spot - although I'm not sure what would grow. It's only $10, so what the hell. This spot is where I tossed "compostable" stuff for a couple of years with no idea wtf I was doing. It turned into a big moldy pile that none of us dare to approach. I will also need to buy a big sturdy steel rake and a shovel.

Step away from the shrooms. Seriously. If you want to grow them yourself, buy a kit. I've done it and it's quite fun.

Regarding blueberries, Soul is right, even with adding stuff to acidify the soil, they aren't likely to grow or thrive because of the alkaline soil. You may have noticed a number of trees, especially maples, that are chlorotic (green veins with yellow leaves). The alkaline soil prevents them from taking up iron and there's just no way to effectively change the soil. Grow something that does well here - blackberries or raspberries. It's more in tune with nature, anyway.

### Michael — 2012-08-22T19:47:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-46375>

I think the best guide is likely to be this one:

[http://www.amazon.com/Mushrooms-Other-Fungi-North-America/dp/155407651X/](http://http://www.amazon.com/Mushrooms-Other-Fungi-North-America/dp/155407651X/)

A lot of the older guides have watercolours, not always very well executed, and they could be just about anything. Roger Phillips uses photographs and does them well.

They look rather like the pictures of _Russala_ in his guide for Great Britain and Europe, which I have, though what's around in North America I don't know. There are dozens of Russala, some of which are edible and some not.

### Kiera2 — 2012-08-23T09:55:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-46497>

I agree, if you want to pick your own mushrooms, then grow your own from a kit.

[http://www.abc.net.au/news/2012-06-08/woman-dies-after-eating-death-cap-mushrooms/4061254](http://http://www.abc.net.au/news/2012-06-08/woman-dies-after-eating-death-cap-mushrooms/4061254)

### Dali Dula — 2012-08-23T15:01:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-46512>

There are some easily recognized mushrooms that are not easily confused with toxics. Chanterelles,coprinus. Most that grow on wood are safe. Don't mess with LBMs Little brown mushroms. They are difficult to identify and there are so many different kinds. There are some good field guides available as apps for ipod. Don't eat anything unless you are 100% sure what it is. If you are really interested there is probably a mushroom hunting club near you. These folks organize forays and probably know their stuff. The kits usually work well and are fun.

### caroline — 2012-08-24T01:53:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-46620>

> **Coriander;46895 wrote:** I agree, if you want to pick your own mushrooms, then grow your own from a kit.
>
>
>
> [http://www.abc.net.au/news/2012-06-08/woman-dies-after-eating-death-cap-mushrooms/4061254](http://http://www.abc.net.au/news/2012-06-08/woman-dies-after-eating-death-cap-mushrooms/4061254)

Sadly - didn't some folks die from mushrooms they had picked last Christmas/New years? - in your area?

### Kiera2 — 2012-08-24T02:55:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-46625>

In Canberra, a New Year's Eve party. Two of four people who ate the mushrooms died. Apparently the deathcap resembles a traditional chinese mushroom.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-24T04:25:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-that-schrooom.3123/post-46641>

This gives me a great idea for a murder mystery. . . .

# Thread: Comparing Nautropaths
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-nautropaths.3128/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### stephaniesherie — 2012-08-20T21:44:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-nautropaths.3128/post-45906>

So I love my Nautropathic Dr.'s! They rock.

I noticed a MAJOR difference in philosophie and would love some feedback other than what is in my own head.

1)Relies on doing the right testing (salivia, blood, etc.) and supports it w/muscle testing. Gives more nutritional supplements vs. herbs

2) uses this ZYTO bio feedback maching - which gives different results from blood tests (at least it did for me) and is used to suggest supplements based on the results of the biofeedback scan.

Any thoughts on these two approaches? I can't ask the Dr.'s cuze each thinks they are right and the other is wrong.

### Zophie — 2012-08-21T18:54:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-nautropaths.3128/post-46061>

My doctor is definitely in the first category. He also starts off by finding the miasim and takes that into account when suggesting herbs and supplements. I really love how I can muscle test something and then have the blood work show the exact same thing..... Amazing!

Bio feedback doesn't work on me either.

### stephaniesherie — 2012-08-21T19:41:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-nautropaths.3128/post-46079>

> **Zophie;46446 wrote:** My doctor is definitely in the first category. He also starts off by finding the miasim and takes that into account when suggesting herbs and supplements. I really love how I can muscle test something and then have the blood work show the exact same thing..... Amazing!
>
>
>
> Bio feedback doesn't work on me either.

HI Zophie,

Yes the bio feedback was WAY off - suggesting take supplements that contain the very thing the blood test showed me as SEVERE intolerant less than two weeks prior. SCARY! I felt like it was a way to push supplements unethically.

# Thread: Heart Rate and longevity
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-rate-and-longevity.3130/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ColdBren — 2012-08-20T22:27:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-rate-and-longevity.3130/post-45916>

My heart rate is too high. My dad's was high too and he passed away last year from a heart attack. My doctors never have said my heart rate is high but it has always been over 80 while resting. It is becoming clear that lower rates are associated with longevity. How do I bring my heart rate down other than "relaxing"?

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-21T01:59:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-rate-and-longevity.3130/post-45949>

I have a good friend who would be labeled all kinds of things (OCD, neurotic, anxioius, Type-A, anal). . .her resting is sky high, back when we took a boot camp together , we both bought Heart Rate Monitors. . .she would rocket up close to 200, and her resting was around 80-90. . .

It sounds like it's a correlation not causation kind of thing. . .but considering your panic attack and anxious stuff going on - I wonder. Do you know if your HR has always been high?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-21T03:14:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-rate-and-longevity.3130/post-45973>

meditation, optimal D levels, great PG/E2 ratio, optimal T3 levels all are keys to getting it down. It tells you your parasympathetic nervous system is not working. It also implies LR state.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-21T06:41:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-rate-and-longevity.3130/post-46010>

So Leptin RX/Keto-Epi-Paleo/Meditation in the sun /In a cold river/lake

### ealachan — 2012-08-21T17:45:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-rate-and-longevity.3130/post-46046>

> **ColdBren;46300 wrote:** My heart rate is too high. My dad's was high too and he passed away last year from a heart attack. My doctors never have said my heart rate is high but it has always been over 80 while resting. It is becoming clear that lower rates are associated with longevity. How do I bring my heart rate down other than "relaxing"?

Are you getting enough D3? My RHR was routinely in the middle to upper 80's until I started taking high doses of D3 and Omega-3's. It is now in the low to mid 70's most of the time. My doctor told me that the only way I could bring it down was with consistent exercising. Guess she was wrong.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-21T18:13:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-rate-and-longevity.3130/post-46051>

Good exercise probably wouldn't hurt though. I'm a big fan of Body By Science. Great book I just read it. The authors believe in Paleo so that helps. It goes into great detail why high intensity weight training works. Other topics include Epigenetics and High Intensity Interval training.

### Destiny — 2012-08-22T03:04:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-rate-and-longevity.3130/post-46194>

> **Jack Kruse;46357 wrote:** meditation, optimal D levels, great PG/E2 ratio, optimal T3 levels all are keys to getting it down. It tells you your parasympathetic nervous system is not working. It also implies LR state.

I have been on T3 for over a couple of weeks. I also started supplementing with Prometrium a week ago. This gives me a higher dose of progesterone compare to the one before. I believe this must be improving my PG/E2 ratio.

Something must be working, since my morning resting HR has been declining from 100's before to 80's this week.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-22T06:22:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-rate-and-longevity.3130/post-46240>

> **Destiny;46579 wrote:** I have been on T3 for over a couple of weeks. I also started supplementing with Prometrium a week ago. This gives me a higher dose of progesterone compare to the one before. I believe this must be improving my PG/E2 ratio.
>
>
>
> Something must be working, since my morning resting HR has been declining from 100's before to 80's this week.

Destiny keep watching your heart rate. I'm quite curious if you are like me and when you need to increase your T3 your heart rate goes up (and BP). Keep me posted!

### Destiny — 2012-08-22T13:07:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-rate-and-longevity.3130/post-46255>

> **Patty Cakes;46625 wrote:** Destiny keep watching your heart rate. I'm quite curious if you are like me and when you need to increase your T3 your heart rate goes up (and BP). Keep me posted!

Patty, I will let you know any changes!

### Destiny — 2012-09-14T13:57:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-rate-and-longevity.3130/post-51618>

> **Patty Cakes;46625 wrote:** Destiny keep watching your heart rate. I'm quite curious if you are like me and when you need to increase your T3 your heart rate goes up (and BP). Keep me posted!

Patty Cakes, my heart rate is consistently in 80's since I posted about this last time. What concerns me that I have been unable to move to a higher dose of T3. I am taking 6.25-6.25-6.25 and feel good on it. When I try to take more my inside feels in spasm and my heart gets some flutters. It is a very uncomfortable feeling.

I am concerned by staying at the same low dose for too long but it is hard to go higher when your body reacts like that.

### ATL_Paleo — 2012-09-15T16:22:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-rate-and-longevity.3130/post-51803>

Destiny ... your FT3 might be rising more than you think. I just discovered that fact. Before I started T3, my FT3 was low at 2.4. After taking T3 for several weeks, I increased the dose from 25mcg to 50mcg. Within severals days, I noticed heart palpitations, and had one night of semi insomina. The next morning I had my FT3 tested, and it came back high at 8.6 on a range of 2.0 to 4.4.

As a result of my labs, I dropped my T3 back to 25mcg / day (split into 2 doses) .... and my heart palpitations stopped, and sleep issues due T3 stopped the first night after lowering my T3 dose.

My reverse T3 was high when tested in July. Clearly something positive is happening. In the next month, I plan another test including the FT3, FT4, TSH, and reverse T3. I have read that clearing high RT3 can take up to 3 months ... based on how fast my FT3 rose, I am hopeful mine will clear faster.

So in summary, I would suggest testing your FT3 (only $ 31 at LEF) ... but I understand your challenges in NY.

### Destiny — 2012-09-15T19:41:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-rate-and-longevity.3130/post-51828>

>

>

I have read that clearing high RT3 can take up to 3 months ... based on how fast my FT3 rose, I am hopeful mine will clear faster.
>
>
>
> So in summary, I would suggest testing your FT3 (only $ 31 at LEF) ... but I understand your challenges in NY.

Thanks, ATLPaleo, for sharing your experience. Being careful is definitely very important. Actually, Patty Cakes had an excellent suggestion to all T3 newbies to take HR, BP and temperature to monitor your body reactions. I am taking it slow trying to get to 25 mcg per day.

My doctor just tested my rT3 and went down from 29 to 20. So even though, I am still taking under 20 mcg a day, it is working.

# Thread: Vision improvements
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vision-improvements.3134/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 37
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2012-08-21T00:49:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vision-improvements.3134/post-45934>

I have noticed lately that if I take off my glasses, I don't have to lean in to peer at my computer screen, unless there is something tiny. This is a BIG thing for me, since I've been wearing glasses for many decades.

I am nearsighted, and usually wear my glasses constantly while awake. When I went low-carb about 2 years ago, I noticed my night vision improved and my day vision was more vivid. And once in a great while, I'd take off my glasses if doing yard work. My optometrist last summer was puzzled at my vision change, and said I had "young eyes".

But now I am amazed at how sharp my vision is now. I am having to keep track of my glasses, an issue that has never been a problem for me before. (But with my improved short-term memory, it hasn't been a problem either.) The eyesight change seems to have started near the end of the 30-day seafood challenge. And it's improving all the time! I planned to see the optometrist again this winter, but I'm so curious as to what he will find.

Is anyone else seeing vision improvements with increased seafood consumption? I didn't notice any changes with CT + LR, but I didn't do many face dunks. I'm so happy that I didn't get my eyes lasered.

### melrito — 2012-08-21T01:09:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vision-improvements.3134/post-45938>

I actually stopped wearing my glasses about 4 months ago (I have worn glasses since I was 5). I have an optometrist appointment next week and am curious if there has been a measurable change - although since I can definitely see things clearly now that I could not before, I suspect there is.

I have the worst floaters since then, too. I'm pretty sure I had a vitreous detachment happen that night in both eyes. The floaters are so bad I've considered getting it fixed surgically  But that's scary.

### Michael — 2012-08-21T22:46:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vision-improvements.3134/post-46118>

This is a super-interesting topic.

I've kept half an eye on alternative explanations for eye problems over the years.

There's a general feeling among some yoga people that we spend too much on close-work -- reading and so on. That sounds fairly plausible. The suggested solution is regular rest for the eyes, including allowing them to focus on distant objects. Also, there's a yoga technique called "palming".

There's a similar notion that it's again what we do, and that specific exercises might help. Several people have had systems intended to help here. One, popularized by the novelist Aldous Huxley, was the Bates method:

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aldous\_Huxley#Eyesight](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aldous_Huxley#Eyesight)

There's a view that it's usually a miss-shapen eyeball at fault, and that what's needed to rectify that is something to push the eyeball back into the right shape. So you can wear special contact lenses at night that are intended to do this.

There's a (less sanguine) view held by some Weston-Price influenced people that a miss-shapen eyeball is probably the problem, but that the eyeball is likely to be miss-shapen because the eye-socket didn't grow as it should have (owing to insufficient minerals and fat-soluble vitamins in the diet.) See here for a fascinating discussion on that:

[http://www.westonaprice.org/dentistry/mental-or-dental](http://http://www.westonaprice.org/dentistry/mental-or-dental)

I say "less sanguine" because by the time one is full-grown, there's not much one can do about one's eye-sockets.

Then there's current diet, of course.

Maybe the truth lies somewhere between all these suggestions???

To cap off isn't it interesting to note what some of the early ethnographic sources say about the eyesight of native peoples eating their traditional diets?

> So it was with these Fuegians, and hence it was generally impossible to find out, by cross-questioning, whether one had rightly understood anything which they had asserted. Their sight was remarkably acute; it is well known that sailors, from long practice, can make out a distant object much better than a landsman; but **both York and Jemmy were much superior to any sailor on board: several times they have declared what some distant object has been, and though doubted by every one, they have proved right when it has been examined through a telescope. They were quite conscious of this power; and Jemmy, when he had any little quarrel with the officer on watch, would say, "Me see ship, me no tell."
>
>
> _The Voyage of the Beagle_ by Charles Darwin
>
>
>
> [http://www.gutenberg.org/files/3704/3704-h/3704-h.htm](http://http://www.gutenberg.org/files/3704/3704-h/3704-h.htm)
>
>
>
>
>
> And here's Weston Price:
>
>
>
> The marvelous vision of these primitive people is illustrated by the fact that they can see many stars that our race cannot see. In this connection it is authoritatively recorded regarding the Maori of New Zealand that they can see the satellites of Jupiter which are only visible to the white man's eye with the aid of telescopes. These people prove that they can see the satellites by telling the man at the telescope when the eclipse of one of the stars occurs. It is said of these primitive Aborigines of Australia that they can see animals moving at a distance of a mile which ordinary white people can not see at all.**

**

[http://journeytoforever.org/farm\_library/price/price10.html](http://http://journeytoforever.org/farm_library/price/price10.html)

(Oh, yeah, and while the most astonishing information there is on the Maori, that's in the chapter on Australian Aborigines not the Maori, but turning up the chapter on the Maori again reflect again how much seafood the Maori seem to have eaten.)

Again, as so often with Price, one is saddened by the thought of cultural** blindness and opportunities missed. He was recording this back in the 1920s and 30s and, he having done so, it's a terrible indictment of the mainstream that no-one paid any attention -- because if they had perhaps no child born today would need a dental brace or other orthodontic work, or a hearing aid, or special shoes ... or glasses.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-06T03:13:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vision-improvements.3134/post-49653>

[http://www.dhainmind.com/Portals/0/PDF%20Files/DHA\_inbrain\_and\_retinastructure.pdf](http://http://www.dhainmind.com/Portals/0/PDF%20Files/DHA_inbrain_and_retinastructure.pdf)

> **DHA & your eyes
>
>
>
> Did you know that your retina contains the highest concentration of Omega-3 DHA in your body? It is not surprising then to learn that DHA is a major structural component of your retinal photoreceptor membranes and plays an important role in retinal signalling mechanisms.
>
>
>
> **Studies have proven that you can modify the level of DHA in the tissues of your eyes through your dietary intake of Omega-3 DHA â€“ another reason to seek out new sources of Omega-3 DHA. Further, they have shown that changes in your dietary intake can affect retinal function and visual processing**. From infancy to the senior years, DHA is vital to the development and maintenance of your vision.
>
>
>
>
> DHA in brain and retina structure**
>
> A fundamental indication of the importance of DHA in healthy eye and brain
>
> function would be a high concentration of this fatty acid relative to others in
>
> these tissues. The first studies to establish that this is indeed the case were
>
> some of the earliest in the field of essential fatty acids.
>
> ....
>
> Early studies of mammalian retina revealed even higher concentrations of
>
> DHA, for example Anderson and Sperling (1971) examined bovine retina and
>
> reported that â€˜the levels of DHA in rod outer segment phospholipids are
>
> among the highest yet reported for membrane phospholipidsâ€™. Phosphatidyl
>
> serine and phosphatidylethanolamine were found, in retinas of six
>
> mammalian species including human, to contain high levels of DHA together
>
> with the saturated fatty acid stearic acid. As reported for rat brain
>
> phospholipids (Yabuuchi and Oâ€™Brien 1968) DHA was usually found in the 2-
>
> position of phosphatidylethanolamine and phosphatidylserine with saturated
>
> fatty acids in the 1-position (Anderson and Sperling 1971, Deese, Dratz,
>
> Dahlquist et al 1981, Fliesler and Anderson 1983, Stinson, Wiegand and Anderson 1991a, b, Wiegand and Anderson 1983). Experiments with DHA â€“
>
> deficient rats demonstrated the importance of DHA for normal retina function
>
> (Benolken, Anderson and Wheeler 1973, Wheeler, Benolken and Anderson
>
> 1975, Futterman, Downer and Hendrickson 1971).
>
> Later studies have confirmed that, over a wide range of species, the
>
> phospholipid and fatty acid composition of vertebrate retinal membranes is
>
> remarkably uniform (Fliesler and Anderson 1983). Phosphatidylcholine (PC),
>
> phosphat-idylethanolamine (PE) and phosphatidylserine (PS) account for 85-
>
> 90% of the phospholipids and in PE and PS, DHA accounts for half of the
>
> total fatty acid content (Stinson, Wiegand and Anderson 1991a). The
>
> functional importance of DHA in the retina may be related to the interaction of
>
> the photoactive protein rhodopsin with particular DHA-containing
>
> phospholipids (Gordon and Bazan 1990). It has also been found that
>
> phospholipid bilayers rich in DHA have high fluidity and enhanced rates of
>
> fusion and permeability, which are characteristics important in the normal
>
> functioning of photoreceptor cells (Giusto, Pasquare, Salvador et al 2000).
>
> .....

I thought I would add my recent experience I am very short sighted and have worn glasses since a teenager. I had recently been experiencing blurred vision..... I went for an eye test, the optician was amazed my right eye had improved from -8 to -4.5. So new glasses needed.

I have been eating epi-paleo since brain gut 6 was published 27th July just a coincidence I don't know.... I would have thought the change of diet was too soon to make these changes to my eye sight, but before BG6 I hardly ever ate fish or shellfish in my whole life.

The other major change is eating epi-paleo has stopped my binge eating in it's tracks. When I say binge I mean eating crap until nearly sick..... so this could be a factor in improving eyesight as well.

Best wishes

Kim

### nonchalant — 2015-05-09T00:54:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vision-improvements.3134/post-163643>

Does myopia result from the body needing to change the frequency of light it is exposed to? The eyeball flattens (or the cornea changes) to alter the focal point and put things out of focus, so that would be a good way to protect the retina from blue light damage.

Looks like we can alter our light intake by forming cataracts, losing hair, getting a suntan or sunburn, other skin coloration changes, dry hazy skin, and even changing eye color. Nearsightedness or farsightedness seems to be another good way of changing light intake. Perhaps a thick layer of fat cells (with water) would also help insulate us from those unwanted frequencies?

### Danny — 2015-05-09T22:59:42+0300

### nonchalant — 2015-05-10T02:46:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vision-improvements.3134/post-163750>

Yes! I posted somewhere the large improvement in both eyes as of last November. It really startled my long-time optometrist. Since then I believe there has been even more improvement. The glasses I'm wearing in my pic here feel even more uncomfortable. Like wearing binoculars, kinda.  I still wear them to drive, because my comfy glasses (former computer glasses) are yellow tinted. But otherwise I don't wear glasses usually, since I don't work. Besides vision discomfort, the metal frames bother my skin now, I think because of nn-emf.

Here is that other post:
[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/page-2#post-138459](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/page-2#post-138459)

One thing else, Danny, several months ago I noticed a sudden ability to focus sharply on the ground while walking, though I am nearsighted. I had just walked barefoot past a place where sand burs tend to grow (though I pull them out of the ground whenever I find one - ouch!) But the enhanced vision soon went away, when not needed. But the next time I walked there, I was able to focus well while I approached the spot. Nice to be able to spot those buggers ahead of time. After the stickers went away, I lost that ability to focus well at that distance. I still can't focus at that distance, darn it. Guess I'll have to look forward to sand bur season!

### nonchalant — 2015-05-10T17:59:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vision-improvements.3134/post-163777>

> [Danny said:](/goto/post?id=163769)
>
> What was the change in your eye measurements? I'm starting to go w/o my glasses whenever I can outside. My vision is 20/200 (-4.00) in both eyes so it's hard to go w/o glasses when I really need to see things for work.

My eyes were -3.50 (R) & -4.25 (L) for a long time. I have old prescriptions going back many years with approximately the same corrections. Last year the corrections were -3.25 (R) & -3.50 (L). It took me a while to stop using my glasses. I started taking them off while eating, because I seemed to enjoy my food more without them, and it gradually became an expanding habit.

When I was still working, I started wearing my yellow, computer glasses, which have corrections of 2.50 and 3.25. At first all the posters on the wall and such were slightly blurry, but after a while I noticed that the posters were coming into focus as I walked by. Perhaps if we are eating plenty of DHA, and the body sees a need to improve eyesight, the eyes will improve. So wearing glasses with perfect correction would be counterproductive.

# Thread: Struggling with T3
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 20
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-08-21T03:06:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-45965>

Both my current doctor (Dr. K said to consider) in my last consult have said I need T3. I started it several weeks ago, but messed up and added it with my Naturethroid instead of replacing the NT. I had labs run and ended up HYPER with very high T3 levels. I ended up stopping the T3 and just staying on NT because I was feeling pretty lousy, had major cold sensitivity and insomnia. Found out later that it was totally my fault because I was taking them together.

I've read the dosing info on Stop The Thyroid Madness website, but still feel unsure about how to proceed. I did start the T3 again a few days ago (without the NT) and have to say I'm already feeling like going back to the NT. I actually had been feeling pretty decent on it, but my labs still sucked. On the T3 I'm getting the major fatigue back along with more cold sensitivity, brain fog and just a general rage/irritable feeling.

Any tips/suggestions on figuring out the T3 doses? My doctor gave me no guidelines at all - just said start T3! Not much help! I was taking 25 mcg when I wake (around 6 AM usually) and another 25 mcg before bed, but after reading STTM I'm trying to take smaller doses when I feel the fatigue/brain fog kicking in. Today I took the normal 25 mcg when I woke and then 12.5 mcg around 1 PM and another 12.5 mcg around 5 PM and 3 hours later I still feel absolutely exhausted. I haven't been able to get my butt off the couch since I got home from work  The last few weeks prior to starting T3 I've had more energy than I had in the last few years. I'm sure this is what I need and I just need to figure out the right dose it just stinks that I have to go backwards first.

### shilohman — 2012-08-21T04:39:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-45991>

Sounds like your T3 is being converted to rT3. What is your cortisol level? Anything going on that is causing you to be stressed?

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-21T04:49:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-45995>

generally 1 grain of NDT = 25 mg of T3 in terms of effectiveness so if you were taking 2 grains of NDT you would look at taking 50 mg of T3. how much NDT were you on?

when you take the T3 as you are right now how is your pulse etc? if you are not displaying hyper symptoms and you doc is okay with the overall level of dosing you can consider adding in another 12.5 mg at the 5 pm mark.

alternatively there are those such as Owl who take both NDT and T3 but the amount of NDT is smaller than previous such as 1/2 grain NDT and 12.5 mg of T3 upon rising then T3 for the rest of the day dosing every 4-5 hours until bedtime. (this isn\`t Owl\`s dosing btw but rather a reflection of things I\`ve read)

a good spot for learning about thyroid dosing are thyroid forums such as realthyroidhelp dot com.

I was taking 1 grain of NT. I read on the STTM site that there's only 9 mcg of T3 in one grain of NT, so I'm assuming that's all I was getting. I don't seem to notice any changes in my pulse either way - with/without the extra T3, even when my labs showed my hyper.

I was feeling better with the combo of NT + T3, but it scares me that I went hyper. And during the last webinar I asked Dr K about that and he said it's not a good idea to mix the two. I have another consult with him in about a week and a half so I'm sure I'll get some clear answers then . . . the question is whether I'll manage until then! I just feel unbelievably drained of all life. I have so much to do and just can't get moving. Thank goodness I don't have to work today, but the rest of the week is super busy and I'm not sure I'll be able to function!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-21T19:31:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-46076>

This is just my personal experience with transitioning from NDT to T3. I wanted to start out slow. There are 9 mcg of T3 in each 1 grain NDT pill (plus the T4). I was on 2.5 grains so I started with approximately that amount of T3 (2.5 x 9 = 22mcg). My first dose was 12.5mcg in the morning then another dose of 6.25 and then another at 6.25 for a daily total of 25. I took my heart rate, BP, and temp almost every hour in the first couple of weeks to check dosage and watch for hyper symptoms. If you are clearing rT3 you definitely do not want to go hyper. Remember you still have some T4 in your system for approx 6-8 weeks due to its half life that may convert to T3.

I slowly raised to a dose of 85 mcg over 4 months. You raise based on the statistics of your vitals. I tried to stay lower in the beginning to clear as much of the rT3 I could and get the T4 converted (hopefully) to T3. I now have a rT3 ratio of 41 so I have accomplished the goal of ridding my body of excess rT3. I will go back to NDT after I get my ASI test and if my adrenals are OK.

I have heard many people mixing the NDT + T3 with good results although personally I am not sure that this is a good idea. Most do it with T3 in the morning and then NDT in the afternoon. I don't think anyone I've heard of has mixed the two in one dose. Maybe that was what Dr. K was referring to.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-21T19:32:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-46077>

hyper based on labs and not symptoms sounds like an RT3 issue, in other words you are showing hyper because the exogenous t3 is not being used. if you havent already go to rt3-thyroid.com and review the information there on treating RT3 with T3. the site is fairly comprehensive and covers symptoms, dosing etc

When you test for FT3 and Rt3 did you take the meds in the am then test? I used to do my labs first thing in am then take my T3 now Dzugan has his patients taking meds 1-2 before labs.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-21T19:53:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-46081>

I actually did NOT take the meds before I tested. This is my first T3 only lab test so I wanted to see what it would read with no morning meds. I came out low T3 as expected since I didn't have anything in my system since 3pm the day before. My next test will be with my morning meds taken. It should be higher on the scale. I have seen over the top range of T3 after taking meds and results like mine where the reading is low if you haven't taken meds. Dzugan probably wants to see the max you are reaching w/ the dose since t3 peaks usually 1-2 hours after ingestion. Obviously, it will be a very different reading, but keeping all that in mind you can interpret the labs better knowing when you took the meds.

### vkiernan — 2012-08-21T20:09:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-46087>

I took my Armour about 2-3 hrs before testing, does that mean they are not accurate? Is that why my FT3 was over the range?

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-21T20:38:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-46095>

> **vkiernan;46472 wrote:** I took my Armour about 2-3 hrs before testing, does that mean they are not accurate? Is that why my FT3 was over the range?

yes

T3 peaks in effectiveness at the 4-5 hour mark. if you want to know how the meds/doses are doing take a half dose 4.5 hours prior to testing. test before that 4.5 hour mark and you will show excess T3 in the blood.

if you want to know in general how your body is using the T3 take last meds 12 hours before testing.

T4 doesn't matter as the half life of this one is about 6 weeks which is why docs wait for 6 weeks to test once you are on NDT

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-21T20:52:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-46102>

on thyroid forums there is a big difference made between showing hyper on labs and hyper by symptoms.

hyper by labs can be a simple dosing issue or an indication of a big RT3 issue. hyper by symptoms is waay more useful as you try to find your sweet spot.

the other thing to remember is when you take exogenous thyroid meds, in particular when you add in T3 if you didn't have it before the body at first more or less ignores the exogenous T3 and you get both the incoming and what you produce. then in a couple of days or weeks (a YMMV situation) the body realizes that there is sufficient incoming T3 and drops it's own production/conversion thus giving the classic: I felt wonderful and then all of a sudden I was hypo again which shows at the 2 week mark (or so).

back in the day docs used to Rx Armour (the original) they would start with a small dose say 1/2-1 grain and then begin to up that dose within a 2-3 week period (often half the dose again or doubling in harder cases depending on symptoms) with full testing at the 6 week mark to see if the dose is correct. this is how my thyroid doc did it and he is in his mid 70's so not a TSH worshipper.

these days it seems to be some arbitrary dose that is given and people are told to come back in 3 months. unfortunately the body doesn't work this way.

> then in a couple of days or weeks (a YMMV situation) the body realizes that there is sufficient incoming T3 and drops it's own production/conversion thus giving the classic:

so how would a thyroid ever get better as we are optimizing if it downregulates with being exposed to exogenous T3?

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-21T23:53:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-46140>

> I have another consult with him in about a week and a half so I'm sure I'll get some clear answers then . . . the question is whether I'll manage until then!

Why don't you ask him if he can move it sooner for you if you're feeling that badly and think that he'll help you figure things out? I know he usually is pretty flexible - he's Mr Spontaneous after all  He got me in quickly both times I needed it.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-22T01:06:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/struggling-with-t3.3137/post-46172>

> **Souldanzer;46512 wrote:** so how would a thyroid ever get better as we are optimizing if it downregulates with being exposed to exogenous T3?

get the underlying stuff fixed and then reverse the process. a year ago I was on 75mg of T3, I'm now on 37.5mg T3 and feel better than I did last year. I'm hoping to go down to 25 mg (split 2x)

If the thyroid hasn't been destroyed by radiation or antibodies it will revive and begin to produce T4 and T3 again. frankly body builders do it all the time when they cycle T3 for cutting. (not the group I would suggest getting thyroid advice from but they do this process succesfully with some regularlity) the key is of course getting the rest of the stuff optimized.

fwiw it's the same with the adrenals and folks who are put on hydrocortisone. after a certain point hydrocortisone shuts down the bodies own production of cortisol and as you wean off hc the body begins to produce it again provided there has been sufficient time, hormone regulation and nutrition to strengthen the adrenals. AF groups refer to the process as giving your adrenals a rest

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sticky-suggestion.3139/post-45983>

I just got through reading the last couple of weeks of posts after my vacation. One topic that gets lots of questions, which could be addressed by a sticky. . .

1\. Email address to send labs

2\. Directions on how to book a time with Jack: go to tungle, request a time, he will respond accordingly.

3\. Helpful suggestions on presenting labs (scan to a pdf, make sure it's not too many MB's, take photo etc)

4\. What to include in history

I see these answered over and over again. Could be a lot more efficient.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-21T03:47:45+0300

# Thread: Decreased Serum Zinc in Fructose Malabsorbers  (and copper)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/decreased-serum-zinc-in-fructose-malabsorbers-and-copper.3140/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-08-21T04:14:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/decreased-serum-zinc-in-fructose-malabsorbers-and-copper.3140/post-45986>

[http://www.clinchem.org/content/47/4/745.full](http://http://www.clinchem.org/content/47/4/745.full)

> Overproduction of cytokines may be associated with decreased blood zinc concentrations (17) because of increased renal loss of zinc (18). We previously have shown that fructose malabsorption is a promoting factor for small intestinal bacterial overgrowth syndrome and is associated with higher serum neopterin concentrations (19), an indicator of immune activation. **The different bacterial composition of the gut could thus be a promoting factor for zinc deficiency in fructose malabsorbers by chronic immune stimulation. It has been shown that dietary carbohydrates, such as sucrose and fructose, reduce the bioavailability of copper by 30% in humans, but not in rats (20). Because zinc is handled very much like copper and high intestinal fructose concentrations are seen in fructose malabsorption, the same phenomenon could play a role in low zinc status in fructose malabsorption.**

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-21T04:52:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/decreased-serum-zinc-in-fructose-malabsorbers-and-copper.3140/post-45997>

I think you might like tonights blog post........for sure.
