# Thread: Spectracell Micronutrient Testing
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/spectracell-micronutrient-testing.3143/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Souldanzer — 2012-08-21T06:11:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/spectracell-micronutrient-testing.3143/post-46004>

Can someone help me with what the Spectracell Research Director is explaining here to me?

I asked whether or not their micronutrient panel will show a B6 to p5p (co-enzymated B6 or biologically active form of B6) conversion problem. Some people cannot do the conversion from B6 to p5p and that results in a deficiency of active B6 while there might be excess regular B6. They will still have symptoms of B6 deficiency b/c they cannot make use of the regular B6.

This is his answer:

> The way we perform all of our testing is by growing the patientâ€™s lymphocytes in the presence or absence of the analyte of interest, and looking at differences in cell proliferation under the two conditions. In the case of the B6 testing, we grow the patientâ€™s cells in **tissue culture grade Pyridoxine Hydrochloride \[regular or inactive B6\].
>
>
>
> Because we measure nutrient utilization for the purposes of cell proliferation, **the ability to mobilize nutrients, converting them to their active form, is one component of functional testing.** So we may not be specifically analyzing that particular pathway, but errors in that pathway might result in poor over all proliferative function**.
>
>
>
> Functional utilization of most nutrients appears to change very slowly â€“ on the order of months. A few days \[of supplementation\] shouldnâ€™t significantly shift results at all.
>
>
>
> I hope this addresses your question(s). Please let me know if you have any others.

I am not sure I'm understanding this correctly. I think he is saying that if there is a conversion problem my result will show up as deficient in B6. The way Spectracell does this test is by exposing the cell to an environment that either contains the particular nutrient or not. If the cell grows better with additional nutrient, then the person is considered deficient in this nutrient. If withdrawing the nutrient doesn't make a difference, than the person is considered sufficient in that nutrient.

If I have a conversion problem... then shouldn't both tests show the same result no matter if they add additional regular B6 to the growing medium or not? So I think my cells would proliferate poorly in \*both\* mediums but that wouldn't be reported on the test eventually b/c they report difference between normal medium and enriched medium?

I realize what he's saying is that somehow I'd get a poor result b/c there is a functional problem with the pathway. But how would it be reported? JanSz?

### kathylu — 2012-08-21T16:48:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/spectracell-micronutrient-testing.3143/post-46034>

I think if you want to talk to Jansz, you should pm him. He has stated several times that he is not reading most of the posts here anymore, too difficult to follow.

I think what the lab is saying is that if you are able to convert inactive B6 to the active form, cell proliferation will increase. They are not testing a specific component of the B6 metabolic pathway, just looking for the end result of increased growth in the presence of the nutrient. If the result is the same in both instances, you are B6 deficient, no matter what the specific metabolic defect is.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-21T17:27:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/spectracell-micronutrient-testing.3143/post-46040>

I think he is telling you its is not a test for you and I agree with him.

I'm not really looking at this test for this issue alone but I wanted to know if it would show in the results.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-22T04:02:27+0300

I was thinking of testing for regular B6 at the same time of the activated B6 test.... then, if I have normal or excess regular B6 but deficient p5p.... there's my answer for a conversion problem. Or I guess I could just test for the activated form b/c with all the b vitamins I've been taking there's no way I'm deficient in the regular B6.

[http://www.aruplab.com/guides/ug/tests/0080111.jsp](http://http://www.aruplab.com/guides/ug/tests/0080111.jsp)

> **Vitamin B6 (Pyridoxal 5-Phosphate) : 0080111
>
>
>
> Mnemonic: VIT B6
>
>
>
> Methodology: Quantitative High Performance Liquid Chromatography
>
> Performed: Sun-Sat
>
> Reported: 1-4 days
>
> Specimen Required: Patient Prep: Collect specimen after an overnight fast.
>
>
>
> Collect: Green (sodium or lithium heparin) or plasma separator tube. Also acceptable: Plain red.
>
>
>
> Specimen Preparation: Protect from light during collection, storage, and shipment. Separate plasma from cells ASAP or within 2 hours of collection. Transfer 1 mL plasma to in an ARUP Amber Transport Tube and freeze immediately. (Min: 0.5 mL)
>
>
>
> Storage/Transport Temperature: CRITICAL FROZEN. Separate specimens must be submitted when multiple tests are ordered.
>
>
>
> Unacceptable Conditions: Any specimens other than heparinized plasma or serum. Non-frozen specimens. Serum separator tubes. Specimens collected in EDTA yield a higher pyridoxal 5'-phosphate concentration; therefore, EDTA is not acceptable. Hemolyzed specimens.
>
>
>
> Stability (collection to initiation of testing): After separation from cells: Ambient: Unacceptable; Refrigerated: 4 hours; Frozen: 2 months
>
>
>
>
>
> Disease Topics
>
> Vitamins
>
> Reference Interval:
>
> 20-125 nmol/L
>
> Interpretive Data: Pyridoxal 5'-phosphate measured in a specimen collected following an 8 hour or overnight fast accurately indicates vitamin B6 nutritional status. Non-fasting specimen concentration reflects recent vitamin intake.
>
> Note: The biologically active form of vitamin B6, pyridoxal 5-phosphate, is measured in this assay.**
>
> CPT Code(s): 84207
>
> Cross References: Vitamin B6 (Vitamin B6 (Pyridoxal 5-Phosphate))

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-18T21:44:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/spectracell-micronutrient-testing.3143/post-52488>

Since the spectracell micronutrient panel tests for cell proliferation does that mean it tests for actual vitamin D activity (and therefore includes VDR receptor functioning) or does it test for level of vitamin D independent of VDR receptor function?

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-21T20:56:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/spectracell-micronutrient-testing.3143/post-53250>

Going to attempt to answer my own question here:

Spectracell uses a mix of T-lymphocytes isolated from blood to measure growth in various mediums. T-lymphocytes express the VDR.

> Vitamin D is an important regulator of immune function. **T cells express the vitamin D receptor (VDR) and have been shown to be direct and indirect vitamin D targets. Why should T cells be responsive to vitamin D? The data suggest that expression of the VDR is required for the development of two cell types, NKT cells and CD8αα T cells, which inhibit autoimmunity. In addition, effector T cell cytokine production is regulated by vitamin D. Available evidence suggests that NKT and CD8αα T cells express the VDR as part of the selection process to protect against the generation of autoimmunity, particularly in the gut.
>
>
> [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21114675](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21114675)
>
>
>
> Spectracell research director earlier said in an email that Because we measure nutrient utilization for the purposes of cell proliferation, the ability to mobilize nutrients, converting them to their active form, is one component of functional testing.** So we may not be specifically analyzing that particular pathway, but errors in that pathway might result in poor over all proliferative function.

I understand that as a "yes", if there is a VDR dysfunction that would show up on a spectracell micronutrient test. I guess the way to find out would be to get a D3 25 hydoxy at the same time. Then if sufficient on D3 but deficient on Spectracell, you have a VDR problem (or other pathway issue)? Am I off?

# Thread: Drink made from berry wine may provide tasty drug for diabetes
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/drink-made-from-berry-wine-may-provide-tasty-drug-for-diabetes.3146/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-08-21T15:07:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/drink-made-from-berry-wine-may-provide-tasty-drug-for-diabetes.3146/post-46029>

[http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/08/20/drink.made.berry.wine.may.provide.tasty.drug.diabetes?](http://http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/08/20/drink.made.berry.wine.may.provide.tasty.drug.diabetes?)

> Drink made from berry wine may provide tasty drug for diabetes
>
> Published: Monday, August 20, 2012 - 13:34 in Health & Medicine
>
>
>
> In evaluating the bioactive compounds of Illinois blueberry and blackberry wines, University of Illinois scientists have found compounds that inhibit enzymes responsible for carbohydrate absorption and assimilation. And that could mean a tasty way to help people with diabetes decrease their blood sugar. "We're thinking about a dealcoholized fermented fruit beverage that would optimize the inhibition of the alpha-amylase and alpha-glucosidase enzymes and also make use of the wines' other healthful bioactive components," said Elvira de Mejia, a U of I professor of food chemistry and food toxicology.
>
>
>
> Graduate student Michelle Johnson evaluated the nutritional value of 19 Illinois wines, deciding on a blueberry-blackberry blend for maximum effectiveness.
>
>
>
> In the in vitro study, the scientists compared the anti-carb effects of the alpha-amylase and alpha-glucosidase enzymes with acarbose, an anti-diabetes drug. The carb-degrading enzymes were inhibited in a range of 91.8 percent for alpha-amylase compared to acarbose and 103.2 percent for alpha-glucosidase compared to acarbose, de Mejia said.
>
>
>
> The study is the first to assess the effect of berry fermentation at different temperatures on these carb-inhibiting enzymes. At both room and cold (4ÂºC) temperatures, berry wine retained the ability to degrade the enzymes, she said.
>
>
>
> In a second study, Johnson quantified the antioxidant, polyphenol, and anthocyanin content of blueberry and blackberry wines. Her proposed blend contains an abundance of these bioactive compounds, which add to its healthful properties.
>
>
>
> The researchers are particularly interested in the ability of anthocyanins to reduce inflammation, which contributes to the development of many chronic illnesses, including cancer, metabolic disease, and cardiovascular disease. To that end, they are experimenting with the berries' effects on inflammatory cells, and they have found that anthocyanins reduce markers associated with the inflammatory response.
>
>
>
> "Preliminary studies have indicated that anthocyanins may have a positive effect on cognition and overall brain health while protecting against some of the effects of aging, such as Alzheimer's disease and memory loss. These berries have some very intriguing components," de Mejia said.
>
>
>
> A food chemist, de Mejia would like to remove the alcohol from the wines, leaving the carb-degrading enzyme compounds, the inflammation-fighting anthocyanins, and other beneficial bioactive components in a functional and flavorful drink for diabetics and others.

# Thread: Cruciferous Vegetables - who should eat, and who should avoid?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-08-21T17:38:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/post-46044>

I am not fully dialed in to who should eat them, who should avoid them, and it seems there may be sub groups of the veggies too, to be aware of. So what are the markers that would make cruciferous veggies not an "optimal" food choice? Should cruciferous veggies always be eaten cooked? Are there sub-sets (of the cruciferous veggies) that can consume and others not? I am particularly interested in their use (or avoidance) for a person with Hashimoto's, but would love to know the full details about this food group!

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-21T18:30:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/post-46055>

I'm E-dominant w/o an AI issues, Cruciferous veggies have always been my preferred veggies of choice intuitively. I'm moving now more to fermented versions of these veggies when I can get them... unfortunately my first attempt at sauerkraut was a total failure.... back to the drawing board....

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-21T19:03:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/post-46065>

I've had a hard time making sauerkraut in the past too (and so now I don't!). Too many bits floating to the top and getting moldy with the long fermentation time. I find cucumbers much easier. Cucumbers, garlic, dill, and coriander if you have it. Pour salt water to cover, and place a bag full of salt water on top to help keep them submerged. In the summer, these are usually "ready" in about 48ish hours.

Should someone with an AI disease avoid cruciferous vegetables?

### Endless — 2012-08-21T21:23:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/post-46105>

I follow the recipes in Sally Fallon's 'Nourishing Traditions' book for all my fermented veggies and they seem to work great, but they use a small amount of liquid whey as a 'starter' so if you want to do salt only they would not be suitable. It's so much quicker than just salt!

### Michael — 2012-08-22T15:37:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/post-46288>

> **bigknitwit;46429 wrote:** ... cruciferous veggies ... I am particularly interested in their use (or avoidance) for a person with Hashimoto's ...

You can try googling something like "goitrogenic foods" to get a list of foods that might need to be approached with a certain amount of caution by anyone who's hypothyroid. And, yes, it does include cruciferous vegetables. But they are OK if cooked.

But probably a little raw isn't likely to be a problem--I don't think anyone would need to pick young spinach leaves out of a mixed green salad, for example. You just don't want to eat a lot of raw cruciferous vegetables on a regular basis. And presumably people like some vegans who liquidize whole cabbages and drink the raw juice could run into problems, if not warned by their doctor. (But I guess any technology that lets you consume foods in forms or in quantities in which you couldn't in nature is _potentially problematic.) However, strangely, I've never heard of a doctor mentioning goitrogenic foods to a hypothyroid patient.

I do know that hypothyroidism makes the conversion of beta-carotene to vitamin A (not as good as its often been assumed to be at the best of times) more problematic. I'd have thought that would mean that someone who's hypothyroid should take particular care to occasionally eat foods containing pre-formed vitamin A (liver for example). But, again, I've never heard of a doctor telling a patient that. In fact, I've never heard of one giving any_ dietary or lifestyle advice to a patient. It seems they just hand over the medication and leave it at that.

It's rather shocking to me.

### Michael — 2012-08-22T22:38:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/post-46408>

BTW, I just grabbed a minute to see if there was anything online about whether fermenting vegetables that were goitrogenic was helpful.

The references I could find indicated not. (I just did a quick google search on "thyroid sauerkraut" -- nothing very technical; wasn't looking for papers or anything, and don't know if there is any academic literature on this.)

Someone at Paleo Hacks said that the WAPF says fermenting raw cabbage does not reduce its goitrogenic activity -- I presume that's accurate. I haven't gone to the WAPF site and checked whether that's an official WAPF statement and what evidence they adduce for it.

The search brought up what looks like quite a nice practical little piece here:

[http://nourishedandnurtured.blogspot.co.uk/2011/05/thyroid-goitrogens-and-recipe-for.html](http://http://nourishedandnurtured.blogspot.co.uk/2011/05/thyroid-goitrogens-and-recipe-for.html)

This is just internet stuff thrown up by google-searching, but it does sound like you might need to go a little easy on the sauerkraut.

### Endless — 2012-08-23T03:49:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/post-46461>

So cooking brassicas makes them thyroid-safe, but fermenting them does not. darn....

So if one is avoiding nightshades, brassicas AND starch, what the heck kind of veggies can you eat?? Suggestions please!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-08-23T08:58:41+0300

>
>
> So if one is avoiding nightshades, brassicas AND starch, what the heck kind of veggies can you eat?? Suggestions please!

I eat a lot of fresh herbs... cilantro, parsley, dill, rosemary, etc. And I eat garlic and sometimes onions. And lots of mushrooms.

### Dali Dula — 2012-08-23T13:50:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/post-46504>

I addition to what KiwiLauren mentioned there are greens like swiss chard, spinach, lettuces. red onion. These can be eaten raw, cooked, or lacto fermented.

### SeaHorse — 2012-08-23T14:52:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/post-46510>

Bok Choy...and some of the other asian greens too. Also, just to put another wrench in the works...some of these greens have very high oxalates...chard, spinach and some of the herbs mentioned. I have been avoiding these too...so bok choy starts to look really good after all these eliminations. I stopped feeling something was wrong about not eating vegetables earlier in the summer. I'm just going to wait for some more healing and then see how it goes....I think you have been down to very few foods too haven't you bigknitwit? Once the building blocks are being stacked up again, I think we will be able to branch out...but until then I'm just not going to worry about it.

### Michael — 2012-08-23T15:48:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/post-46519>

> **Endless;46856 wrote:** So cooking brassicas makes them thyroid-safe, but fermenting them does not. darn....
>
>
>
> So if one is avoiding nightshades, brassicas AND starch, what the heck kind of veggies can you eat?? Suggestions please!

Is it that much of a problem?

I mean I guess someone with thyroid issues might want to pass an eye on what's in a list of goitrogens. Those would one want to cook more often than not, although small amount raw sometimes would probably be OK.

The link I put up in post #7 is quite interesting. There's a suggestion there to have a little seaweed _with_ a (small) amount of sauerkraut. Sound like a good idea.

I don't think most of the stuff in question is usually traditionally eaten raw anyway ... And, of course, there would be reasons for that.

### Endless — 2012-08-23T20:40:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/post-46573>

Not raw, but I do like to make my own sauerkraut....

I like the idea of combining it with sea vegetables though, hmmm.

Can't say I've ever tried to lacto-ferment swiss chard or spinach, I'm not sure what that would even look like!

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-23T22:57:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cruciferous-vegetables-who-should-eat-and-who-should-avoid.3148/post-46595>

I remember reading somewhere ( I think something by Chris Masterjohn), that cooking the cruciferous veggies doesn't reduced their goitrogens significantly. He proposed including more iodine in the diet to counter the effect, if I remember correctly. I'll look it up and see if I can find the source.

I did lacto-fermented ginger carrots, but they turned out way too salty, and don't have any flavor except saltiness. I little tweaking (less salt, more innoculating brine, and it may get better results. My list of foods is small and dwindling, it seems.

**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-21T18:46:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fluoride-damage-permanent.3151/post-46059>

I was watching an anti-fluoride video and it quoted someone saying anyone drinking fluoridated water for a year will be forever changed...implying permanent brain damage...

I would hope this is untrue....and think it is. The human body is more resilient than people give it credit for. Not to mention the things you can do to improve the bodies ability to restore itself. There are obviously things that are permanent and no amount of good diet or good living can fix...not sure this is one of them. I suppose it would help to know what damage exactly fluoride causes and why. The root of the matter I suppose is can the body repair all of the damage done by fluoride once its removed...

Thoughts?

### chocolate — 2012-08-21T19:05:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fluoride-damage-permanent.3151/post-46066>

I read an article that said magnesium will displace fluoride. Here's hoping.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-21T19:11:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fluoride-damage-permanent.3151/post-46068>

> **chocolate;46451 wrote:** I read an article that said magnesium will displace fluoride. Here's hoping.

Haven't heard that one yet. I heard Iodine displaces fluoride. More reason to eat copious seafood. Wish it were cheaper for good quality fish/seafood.

### chocolate — 2012-08-22T00:01:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fluoride-damage-permanent.3151/post-46146>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21839622](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21839622)

> Biological effects of magnesium particles degradation on UMR-106 cell line: influence of fluoride treatments.
>
> Grillo CA, Alvarez F, de Mele MA.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Instituto de Investigaciones FisicoquÃ­micas TeÃ³ricas y Aplicadas (INIFTA, CCT La Plata-CONICET), Facultad de Ciencias Exactas, Universidad Nacional de La Plata, La Plata, Argentina.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Mg-based materials are promising for orthopedic, dental, and cardiovascular applications but their high degradation rate in vivo (release of Mg ions and debris particles) is cause of great concern. Protective treatments involving fluoride conversion coatings have been proposed in order to reduce corrosion rates. The aim of this study was to evaluate Mg debris biodegradation and its possible cytotoxic effects on osteoblastic cells in situ. Neutral Red dying and Acridine Orange staining techniques were used as endpoints to analyse the cytotoxic effects at 100-2000 μg/mL concentration range. Results showed a marked variation of Mg ion concentration in the culture medium after different exposure periods (1, 2, or 24h). Interestingly, the release rate of magnesium ions was dependent on the presence or absence fluoride treatment. Adverse effects induced by ≥1000 μg/mL MP doses and Mg ion concentrations higher than 480 μg/mL were observed on cells. Results showed significant differences between the concentration of Mg ions in the presence and absence of cells. This fact reveals a dynamic equilibrium mediated by Mg ion input and output in the cells that leads to the change in MP corrosion rates. **Fluoride release from conversion coatings did not show cytotoxic effects**

The article I read was older and kind of along the same lines. The magnesium released the fluoride somehow and when it did, there was no problem. I just don't remember how I searched it. I must have tripped over it. I almost may have misunderstood.

### chocolate — 2012-08-22T00:10:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fluoride-damage-permanent.3151/post-46157>

[http://jn.nutrition.org/content/116/6/1018.full.pdf](http://http://jn.nutrition.org/content/116/6/1018.full.pdf)

> ABSTRACT There is considerable uncertainty about the interrelated effects of
>
> calcium (Ca), magnesium (Mg) and fluoride (F) on hypocalcification of the skeleton
>
> and Ca accumulation in vital soft tissues. This paper describes experiments with rats
>
> fed a diet deficient in Ca and Mg, the latter deficiency being accentuated by a low
>
> potassium content. For different groups the drinking water was supplemented with
>
> Ca, Mg, F, Ca + F or Mg + F; Ca and F were supplied as chemically compatible
>
> compounds. At the end of the 54-d experiment, plasma Ca was strongly reduced in
>
> the Ca-deficient groups. **Plasma Mg was reduced particularly in the test groups sup
>
> plied with Ca, without any influence of F supply or varying plasma F. Ca accumula
>
> tion was much more pronounced in the kidneys than in the heart or aorta, but was
>
> **mitigated by both Mg and F supplementation. Bone mineralization disturbance due
>
> to the dietary imbalance was preventable only by Ca or Ca + F supplementation,
>
> and was less in alveolar bone than in femur. However, the Ca supplement was as
>
> sociated with the highest aorta and heart Ca contents, and Ca + F was associated
>
> with marked renal Ca accumulation; adequate Mg supply may be essential with**
>
> Ca + F administration against osteoporosis.** J. Nutr. 116: 1018-1027, 1986.

I think it can be manipulated, at least in rats.

# Thread: Coffee and Fat Make Blood Sugar Levels Spike
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coffee-and-fat-make-blood-sugar-levels-spike.3153/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-08-21T19:29:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coffee-and-fat-make-blood-sugar-levels-spike.3153/post-46075>

[http://www.healthytheory.com/coffee-and-fat-make-blood-sugar-levels-spike](http://http://www.healthytheory.com/coffee-and-fat-make-blood-sugar-levels-spike)

Home

Coffee and Fat Make Blood Sugar Levels Spike

Posted Thu, 04/07/2011 - 11:12am by Camilla Cheung

Filed Under:

antwerpenR / Flickr.com

The next time you order that burger and fries, you may want to skip the coffee. A new study published in the Journal of Nutrition suggest that not only does eating a fatty meal make your blood sugar levels spike, but drinking coffee along with your fatty meal doubles the sugar level increase.

University of Guelph researchers gave a fatty lipid-rich beverage to healthy men, and six hours later gave them a sugary beverage. The men's blood sugar levels, when measured after they ingested the fatty beverage and the sugary beverage, were 32% higher than when they ingested the sugary drink alone, and not the fat-rich beverage. The fat the men had consumed appeared to interfere with the body's ability to produce insulin and clear the excess sugar from the bloodstream.

The results were even greater when the men consumed caffeinated coffee after drinking the fatty beverage. In this second study, the men were given the fatty beverage, then five hours later consumed the equivalent of 2 cups of coffee, and when their blood sugar was tested an hour later (six hours from the consumption of the fatty drink), blood sugar levels were 65% higher than when the subjects drank only the sugar drink and not the fat and coffee.

Other studies have shown that people suffering from Type 2 diabetes should cut their caffeine intake, switching instead to decaffeinated coffee or tea, as the excess caffeine can lead to spikes in blood glucose. However, this is the first time the effect of a high-fat meal and the consumption of caffeine have been studied simultaneously. The results suggest that the effect of a fatty meal can have repercussions on your health even hours after it is consumed, especially if you consume caffeine with your meal or later that day.

(On the flip side, some studies have shown that drinking coffee can have a small protective effect against diabetes, but these studies suggest that the protective effect may be due to antioxidants in the coffee rather than to caffeine content. Decaffeinated coffee had as much of a preventative effect against diabetes as caffeinated.)

Experts at the Mayo Clinic also say that caffeine can impair insulin action in people with Type 2 diabetes, leading to rises in blood glucose levels.

For people with diabetes or at risk of developing diabetes, or for people interested in eating healthily in general, it is a good idea to cut high-fat foods from your diet, especially the unhealthy saturated fats found in processed food and fast food meals. And if you do indulge in a fast-food meal once in a while, you may wish to skip the caffeinated soda or coffee that comes with your meal. Fat, sugar, and caffeine? Not an optimal combination.

I guess lots of people get trouble with coffee and fat.

### ealachan — 2012-08-21T20:08:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coffee-and-fat-make-blood-sugar-levels-spike.3153/post-46086>

I put a bunch of fat IN my coffee every morning, and it doesn't spike me.

# Thread: Helping others become optimal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/helping-others-become-optimal.3154/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-21T20:19:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/helping-others-become-optimal.3154/post-46090>

This was posted by Peggy Holloway in the comments on [http://www.dietdoctor.com/a-new-toy-measuring-blood-ketones](http://http://www.dietdoctor.com/a-new-toy-measuring-blood-ketones)

If anyone has any thoughts maybe you could post a reply to her in the blog comments there. Sorry about the wall of text that's how it was in the comment section. Not sure how best to break it down so I'll just leave the formatting alone.

"This is a bit off subject, but I am desperate for some answers, so I'm posting on my favorite low-carb blogs. I have done this in the past, starting over a year ago and got some suggestions, but after a year, the situation has gotten worse instead of better. My sister was diagnosed as Type II (runs in the family - took the lives of our grandfather and father and all of us have carb intolerance) in 1999 after years of fighting weight issues with low-calorie, low-fat diets. She, my brother, and I all learned about low-carb diets about that time and all pursued low-carb lifestyles. My sister persevered despite reluctance from her physician to use diet to manage her blood sugar. She took Metformin from the start and other the years has been on Januvia. She has declined other medications and refused to take insulin. Her blood sugars have remained above normal and she has tried all sorts of "tweakings" of her low-carb diet. Last summer she went to see Mary Vernon (we live fairly close to her Kansas office) and had no success. The only recommendation Dr. Vernon and her associates made was a low-carb diet and they just couldn't understand that she'd been on a low-carb diet for 12 years! Jane also corresponded with Jackie Edelstein (spelling?) of the Atkins Society who suggested a yeast reducing diet, which Jane religiously followed. Not seeing great results, she, with my encouragement, has tried really upping her fat intake and lowering protein to replicate my ketogenic diet (which is the connection to this topic). Nevertheless, this past year, Jane's fasting blood sugars have risen exponentially, and her readings since attempting to go ketogenic are consistently between 180 and 250. Her last A1C was 10. This is really alarming and we have no idea what she should do. (My partner, who is also having great results from a ketogenic diet, losing 40 pounds in the last few months, is a retired Family Physician, so he has tried to help her since her own doctor is not sympathetic to the idea of treating with diet and avoiding medications. His only idea is to get her off her beta blocker which she claims she can't do without getting rapid heart palpatations and severe migraines.). Her doctor wants her to take Byetta and I'm sure will really start pushing insulin. Any ideas what is going on here? Thoughts about what she might do?"


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Ok, so I will book the email consult with Dr. K now that I have some info but from those who have been here what is the first step? I know I have alot of reading to do but my Dr. called today and said he doesn't know what to do about the antibodies reading. So, I need to either educate myself or find a new Dr.!

So, other questions, is there any chance this is a false reading?

Can allergy injections cause this reading to be off?

Shouldn't my TSH reading be really high for Hashi's?

Is there any chance that my diet is the cause of this reading?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-22T00:14:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-help-with-labs.3157/post-46159>

> **MartiD;46530 wrote:** So do I not need the readings on both Antibody tests? I know it was marked on form because it was same as hubbys which we still have. So frustrating that I can't get the lab to do what is marked. This is the second time!

You should have both tests in case one comes back showing no antibodies the other may show antibodies. I'm not sure if you need the other one since you have TPO antibodies right now you definitely have joined the club. Start eating the AI version of Epi-paleo Leptin Rx.

I suppose you can have a false reading not sure about that but your FT3 and 4 are both low so you have a thyroid issue right there. You also have a very low rT3 ratio ([http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/)) which means you are not converting that T4 into usable T3. You are probably fatigued, move slower than you used to, and have that darn foggy brain.

Go to [www.stopthethyroidmadness.com](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com) for info on Hashis and other thyroid related stuff. After reading the basics on STTM, go back to the blogs here and get the deeper info on how it all affects the whole body. I would find a new doc because Hashis is nothing you want to mess around with.

Don't know about the allergy injections...maybe.

Dr. K has said anything above 2.0 on the TSH can be a sign of a thyroid issue but I know of a doc that uses 1.5. Keep in mind TSH is not the best measure for your thyroid function. FT3, FT4, and rT3 tell you what the thyroid is really doing. The TSH is actually a pituitary reading. I actually don't pay any attn to my TSH any longer. If you are eating a strict paleo/epi-paleo diet it will help your readings and symptoms...at least that was my experience. This is just something you have to work with, shift your thoughts about and treat as necessary. I've been working with it for five years now.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-22T06:16:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-help-with-labs.3157/post-46239>

> **MartiD;46549 wrote:** So is it weird that I have been able to lose weight the last year with a hypothyroid issue?
>
>
>
> Also, what about CT with Hashi's ? Any rules on that? Have any relevance?

I lost weight with Hashis but it was a struggle, especially never having a weight problem or ever a problem losing 5#. But everyone is different. You are unique!

CT w/ Hashis is also an individual thing. I think some people can do it easier than others. I have gotten my temp down to 55-60 and I can do fine for 30 min but then I'm so cold I cannot stand it. Being on T3 has increased my internal temp so I can tolerate the cold water easier than some. The doc will probably have some suggestions for you on that during your consult.

Also the AI eating is referenced on the blog somewhere and in the forum in many places. Search AI and you should be able to find something. I just cannot remember where exactly it is.

# Thread: Mental Health
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mental-health.3158/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### mimi_92 — 2012-08-21T23:55:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mental-health.3158/post-46142>

Hey everyone! This is my first post

I've been lurking the forums and reading all of Dr. Kruses work for some time now and really feel I am ready and in a good place to give the Leptin Reset a go!

I am 19, and have suffered on and off with mental health issues pretty much since I was 12/13. I have been diagnosed with GAD and OCD (pure-O)...been suffering with panic attacks and bad intrusive thoughts and compulsions for some time now. My issues were bad when they first began, and then for a few years I was completely fine, and only when the new year began did my mental health issues return. I really don't know why they did : Also, I am about 10-15lbs overweight and have some small issues with my thyroid. No matter what I do I can't seem to lose the weight.

I'm hoping that perhaps my weight issues and thyroid issues have something to do with my mental health and am wondering if the Leptin Reset can help me with my anxiety and ocd at all. I am ready to start living an optimal life and am sick of my health issues that are holding me back from doing so many things. Is it possible that the Leptin Reset and a paleo diet could help?

Thank you for reading

Mimi

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-22T00:03:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mental-health.3158/post-46152>

Epi-paleo rx is what I would say  combined with leptin reset. Seafood will be your best new friend.

FYI, there was a thread on mental health on here a long time ago... some tips in there if you can dig it up.

### Lyndra — 2012-08-22T00:08:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mental-health.3158/post-46155>

Welcome! Glad you have found us. You may find the recent Brain-Gut series very enlightening. There's a huge connection between mental health, what you eat, and your gut & gut flora. Many people have been helped by following the leptin reset.

### mimi_92 — 2012-08-22T00:08:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mental-health.3158/post-46156>

Thank you for your reply!

How would I combine those two? Doing the leptin reset with the epi-paleo rx rules in there?

Yes, I did look through the search for other threads like this but wasn't able to find anything...will look again though with different search words. Thanks

# Thread: Doing Epi- Paleo in Richmond Virginia
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doing-epi-paleo-in-richmond-virginia.3160/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mar-see-a — 2012-08-22T00:52:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doing-epi-paleo-in-richmond-virginia.3160/post-46170>

Hello fellow Kruisers-

I am Marcia (aka Mar-see-a), and so excited to have the opportunity to FINALLY lose this weight! We won't mention how much I have to lose, as a woman needs a little mystery :0). I am a HCA graduate from the Medical College of Virginia, and now a SAHM after 10 years as an Administrator. Despite this past, Dr. Kruse has still agreed to help me, and was even very kind. If you wonder what I mean, you must understand that the relationship between Administrators and Doctors can be...... difficult :0).

I had my initial hour long consult last week, and wanted to share some random thoughts. Dr. Kruse's first words to me were that he was sure he could help me, as I was just like him only I had a vagina. Sweeter words have never been spoken, and you cannot imagine the impact such a simple statement has had on my life. My biggest surprise was that I did not expect Dr. Kruse to understand how I felt, but he did. I have been unable to lose weight for many years, but could not get a doctor to help me, as my labs always came back in the normal range. I am now on a regime recommended by Dr. Kruse and losing weight for the first time in years. I cannot tell you how good that feels.

I thought my biggest obstacle would be getting a doctor to work with me, while having Dr. Kruse as a consult, but I now have one. My approach was to offer a doctor the opportunity to work with me while I totally transformed my life based on a revolutionary protocol in the developmental stages by a neurosurgeon. In addition, I sent him a link to Dr. Kruse's website. To my surprise, the first doctor I approached called back a couple of days later to say he was on board. This doctor is a personal friend, which I am sure was a factor, but to be honest, Dr. Kruse's insights into cancer were a major factor for him as well (my doctor is a young oncologist).

So, I am sincerely looking forward to getting to know all of you fellow travelers. I have been paleo for a couple of years, so luckily not having any struggles with the foods- except I have no appetite at all. Isn't that strange? I am missing my fresh tomatoes, but that is nothing as long as I can lose weight. I know the importance of having a community to support you, and can only hope that I will be able to give something in return.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-22T03:27:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doing-epi-paleo-in-richmond-virginia.3160/post-46201>

Welcome. And I love a challenge.

### shilohman — 2012-08-22T04:03:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doing-epi-paleo-in-richmond-virginia.3160/post-46204>

Hope I had a dollar for every pound of fat that people on this forum have lost, I wouldn't have to work Welcome!

### caroline — 2012-08-22T10:15:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doing-epi-paleo-in-richmond-virginia.3160/post-46250>

welcome to this great forum - Good luck on your journey and please keep us posted!

### Mar-see-a — 2012-08-22T16:20:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doing-epi-paleo-in-richmond-virginia.3160/post-46298>

Thanks for the welcome, everyone. Caroline, the biggest update I can give you so far is that I am losing weight for the first time in years, although I am not eating anything different. I guess I really needed the thyroid meds that Dr. Kruse recommended, and shame on me for holding off as long as I did, trying to find a natural way.

I don't feel great yet, but do feel much better than I did. I am sleeping much more, around 8-9 hours a night. This is a really big change for a person who prided herself on how little sleep she needed :0). Nature has a way of teaching us a lesson-

Thanks Caroline, it's nice to know that I am in good company. I must admit, going to bed early is a big adjustment for me but so far so good. I think Dr. Kruse has more to say about sleep on the next Webinar. I feel like a kid whining about my bedtime .

I'm taking my temperature every morning to check if i can ovulate...

I went on leptin reset since april, wirh some times some "falls" from the line, but I'm almost always in the line of the reset. I used to be anorexic years ago, I aws always cold etc... Now I feel hot most of times, don't need a jacket. I've begun HIT, with lifting weights two times a weeks, and I sweat. I think that I becam leptino sensitive now, don't I?

Anyway, I would like to get pregnant. There is just a little problem : I don't have any ovulation, never get my period...

What can I do? I just buy Iodin to take, Vitex ect....

What do u think please?

Mel from paris

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-09-09T21:19:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fertility-il-tired-of-waiting.3170/post-50552>

> **melaniefrench1;46635 wrote:** Hi!
>
>
>
> I'm taking my temperature every morning to check if i can ovulate...
>
> I went on leptin reset since april, wirh some times some "falls" from the line, but I'm almost always in the line of the reset. I used to be anorexic years ago, I aws always cold etc... Now I feel hot most of times, don't need a jacket. I've begun HIT, with lifting weights two times a weeks, and I sweat. I think that I becam leptino sensitive now, don't I?
>
> Anyway, I would like to get pregnant. There is just a little problem : I don't have any ovulation, never get my period...
>
> What can I do? I just buy Iodin to take, Vitex ect....
>
>
>
> What do u think please?
>
>
>
> Mel from paris

Hi melaniefrench1,

I know this might sound strange but have you tried giving up on getting pregnant? If you have been trying for five years or more, I have seen more success with people that give up on getting pregnant and seriously looking at the option of adoption or filling their life with something other then a baby.

It's like the pressure is off. If you chart, stop charting. Have sex only for pleasure. Find an enjoyable hobby or work that is totally satisfying.

If having a baby is what you want to do with your time, raise someone else's unwanted child. That child will be a blessing to you and you will be a blessing to that "unwanted" child. Your heart will open and so may other parts of you.

From what I have seen, just starting the adoption process can get you pregnant or having an adopted child will open those closed parts of you. Regardless of if you ever get pregnant, you will have a child to raise and love. It will be your child no matter who gave birth to that child. We are all one big family anyway.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-10T01:56:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fertility-il-tired-of-waiting.3170/post-50596>

The evolutionary tale of fertility is quite simple. We are designed to change our LDL to pregnenolone using free T3 and Vitamin A as co factors from our diet. When the cell get pregnenolone it looks to the cytosol and assesses whether the environment is more oxidized (inflamed) or more reduced chemically(not inflamed). Once that decision is made pregnenolone is transfered down one of two paths depending upon the reduction/oxidation surroundings of the cell. If oxidized pregnenolone is shunted to cortisol to help us survive. If the cells are reduced pregnenolone is shunted to the pro- gestation hormone called progesterone. When proegesterone is high so is estrogen, testosterone, DHEA and vitamin D. That is why the distal hormone chain tells you how inflamed or reduced you really are. The giant circle of life. If you are not fertile your goal is to become reduced. It means you need to be come boring, cold embrace the dark and sleep and then begin to have mad sex once you adapt to this reduced life. The more you stress about not getting knocked up the more you will shunt your pregnenolone to cortisol for survival. This is the basic biochemistry of fertility. Inflamed means LR. LS = reduced. The giant circle of life all from the Hormone 101 blog. It is critical to all our biology and our species.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-10T02:00:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fertility-il-tired-of-waiting.3170/post-50597>

If you dont have free T3 (hypothyroidism of any cause) or Vitamin A (bad diet and is pretty rare even on a SAD) your cells will be constantly oxidized and over time chronic oxidation destroys cellular signaling. When signaling is broken nothing works.......never mind having a kid. This is why the obese and the anorexic have fertility issues. It is why the old and the sick also have trouble being fertile. This applies to both sexes. It is more critical in women.......but it is as deadly in men. Oxidation kills slowly and reduction increases longevity and fertility.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-09-10T17:44:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fertility-il-tired-of-waiting.3170/post-50723>

Having a baby is very stressful **unless it is the only thing you really want to do. I am speaking from my personal point of view. I have seen other women nest** and have the most beautiful time of their lives.

Dr Kruse is right. You need to reduce your stress. I would guess the people that "adopt a child" or "give up on having a child" are both reducing their stress. Just be careful what you choose because the other choice may just increase your stress. Everything comes down to knowing yourself.

### hope2conceive — 2012-09-11T02:49:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fertility-il-tired-of-waiting.3170/post-50856>

Hi Caroline,

what do you mean by nesting? you mean without a baby?

Dr Kruse,

would the inflammation waterfall also include FSH....and do you think once you become LS, FSH could be normalized from being too high, indicating diminished ovarian reserve?

Thanks!

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-09-13T23:34:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fertility-il-tired-of-waiting.3170/post-51509>

> **hope2conceive;51416 wrote:** Hi Caroline,
>
>
>
> what do you mean by nesting? you mean without a baby?

A mother needs a infant to "nest". I meant that some women find having a baby, their own or an adapted child, a wonderful experience. It is the most beautiful time of their life. These women are like songbirds sitting in their nest singing to the beauty of motherhood. It's beautiful to watch women that are so fulfilled by their infants.

Personally, I found a new baby stressful because I was so confined in a way I had never been before. I was an older mother too (35). I also felt "dependent" on my husband in a way that was new to our relationship, even though we had been together for more then ten years at that point. That make me feel crazy and a bit depressed. If that wasn't enough I was also sleep deprived for about 18 months. Ugh. I found motherhood didn't sit very well with me. But over the years I have grown into a loving devoted mother. Not being sleep deprived really helps with that!

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-09T17:19:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fertility-il-tired-of-waiting.3170/post-63128>

One thing I can say with certainty regarding getting pregnant is that functional does not equate to optimal...... If I could travel back in time with the knowledge I have now I would not settle for less than optimal body conditions for my babies.

Some sites says eggs and dairy are ok, even have them in recipes and others say to avoid. Just about the only consistency I've found is Soy which won't be an issue I don't think. So I thought I would start a list of thing Ive read about and get feedback from those who are dealing with Hashis in particular. I know each AI protocol can be different.

Eggs (all eggs, are yokes alone ok?)

Dairy (is grass-fed butter ok?)

Soy

Gluten (this is really confusing!)

Sugar

Things to add:

More seafood

Iodine rich foods such as?

Selenium

Up magnesium supps...any type better than others? I do malate now

I've read exercise is also strongly recommended but I assume nothing strenuous that will cause inflammation? Walking best? Thanks for help from all experts on this one!

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-22T18:40:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-to-hashis-whats-ok.3173/post-46340>

@ cavemam: Sorry Marti, maybe I should move this post to c's journal...

U might see me around tomorrow, lol. I gotta get a blood re-draw and it's getting awfully late today for that

Here's my tips in short: (unless you wanna hire me b/c I shure kuld use a few $$ lol)

\- decide ahead of time which issues to address

\- pick three in order of priority

\- start with the highest priority (focus your history on that issue since it's the intro appt)

\- be ready to drop the rest if necessary b/c of time limits

\- there's nothing wrong with bringing in an index card with the major points you want to make

FOr the thyroid testing:

\- say you have some symptoms of thyroid disease..... whatever applies for you inability to loose weight in combination with super-diet (low carb, paleo, fish rich, no grains, no sugar etc.), fatigue, just look up what applies to you. He will take notes on this to back up his lab order so be specific and clear. A list, on an index card, if you need it.

\- say you want a full panel including the following.... have your list ready. Say you want an antibody panel b/c you'd like to exclude AI disease. RT3 is never covered by insurance he will order it for you anyways, so don't complain about having to pay for it, or any lab he orders really. That's not his job unless it was coded incorrectly. The clearer you are in asking and backing up your claims, the higher the likelihood he will code correctly

\- when your tests are in, the office most likely will call you and say that "your labs came back great, everything is within range" and then you say thank you, that is great, may I have a copy mailed to me?"

\- once you get the labs, you look at them yourself, post them here, and then, if there is something to fix, make another appointment with Dr B and make your case why you need RX whatever. Basically, you start over at the top of this list.

Good luck! He's really nice and friendly... if you mess up you can just try again next time better prepared..... he's that kinda guy  Or, maybe then, you \*really\* should hire me

### melrito — 2012-08-22T19:26:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-to-hashis-whats-ok.3173/post-46359>

I am currently in the phase of not knowing whether I have hashis or not. I have low antibodies and dr. k said i need to retest, he cannot rule it out based on my current labs, but suspects i caught it in a "good" phase, since hashis fluctuates and antibodies increase and decrease - they don't remain constant. from what i understand, with hashis you need to follow an autoimmune diet - no grain (of course), sugar, eggs, dairy (yes, dairy includes butter, but not ghee provided it is casein-free), nuts, etc. if you didn't hear last month's webinar on the epi-paleo rx, you might want to think about becoming a member so you can listen to it - he talked a lot about diet related to hasis in that webinar and i found it very informative. he does recommend lots of seafood and sea veggies and of course to keep a close eye on how foods are affecting your labs to see what you can tolerate. Probably a consult with dr. k would be the best course of action - he will definitely answer all your questions and explain the reasoning for that answer.

### MartiD — 2012-08-27T05:18:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-to-hashis-whats-ok.3173/post-47426>

Interesting Colleen. For last few months I did have a bit of brain fog which I tributed to two teenagers, dry skin which I've had most my life, and some slight fatigue (see brain fog excuse). I stopped eggs, gy, hwc last Monday. I've been gluten free for a year for most part. I haven't noticed a change because I didn't have any pains before. However, today I've felt like crap...no idea why but felt anxious, tired but heartbeat seems to be up, dull headache, pressure behind eyes, heavy feeling. I've been reading so much I think it has mentally exhausted me especially since there is so much contradictory info out there.

### MartiD — 2012-09-01T16:51:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-to-hashis-whats-ok.3173/post-48724>

I have a question about corn. I found some sweet potato chips that are gluten free, actually everything free(lol), but made with corn. Is corn ok on AI protocol?

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-01T22:09:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-to-hashis-whats-ok.3173/post-48787>

> **MartiD;49217 wrote:** I have a question about corn. I found some sweet potato chips that are gluten free, actually everything free(lol), but made with corn. Is corn ok on AI protocol?

The thing about packages of crunchy, is they are usually cooked in a bad oil. Heated bad oils are as bad as sugar for inflamation.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-01T22:58:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-to-hashis-whats-ok.3173/post-48793>

Sometimes I make Kale chips. Fresh kale leaves deveined, tossed in oil (I use coconut, but butter used to be divine) and salted. Put in the oven at 350-375ish, and turn about every 5-7 minutes for even drying. You need to keep turning them! They take about 30 minutes, depending on how full the pan is. Lower temps taste better, but take a little longer. Common failure is not using enough oil (probably not a problem with this crowd!). When they're done, they're crispy and oily like a chip. I bet there all kinds of reasons why these are not good for you, probably to do with overcooking the greens. Seems every little crutch I use has something wrong with it!! But I don't know of anything just yet, and I use Kale fresh (unwashed) from our garden.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-01T23:01:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-to-hashis-whats-ok.3173/post-48794>

Lower temps taste better, but take a little longer. Common failure is not using enough oil (probably not a problem with this crowd!). When they're done, they're crispy and oily like a chip. I bet there all kinds of reasons why these are not good for you, probably to do with overcooking the greens. Seems every little crutch I use has something wrong with it!! But I don't know of anything just yet, and I use Kale fresh (unwashed) from our garden.

You can make these in the dehydrator if you're concerned about the cooking part. They are delish either way

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-01T23:03:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-to-hashis-whats-ok.3173/post-48795>

> **bigknitwit;49289 wrote:** Sometimes I make Kale chips. Fresh kale leaves deveined, tossed in oil (I use coconut, but butter used to be divine) and salted. Put in the oven at 350-375ish, and turn about every 5-7 minutes for even drying. You need to keep turning them! They take about 30 minutes, depending on how full the pan is. Lower temps taste better, but take a little longer. Common failure is not using enough oil (probably not a problem with this crowd!). When they're done, they're crispy and oily like a chip. I bet there all kinds of reasons why these are not good for you, probably to do with overcooking the greens. Seems every little crutch I use has something wrong with it!! But I don't know of anything just yet, and I use Kale fresh (unwashed) from our garden.

I second these! I read somewhere that it's really helpful to NOT salt until after baking, as the salt pulls the moisture to the surface and it crisps slower? I can't remember why, but I do it that way now and they do seem to go faster.

I also reallllllllly love coconut flakes fried up in butter (ghee will work, and still smells buttery) it's very pop-corny and snacky. they get brown and crispy and have a slight sweet from the coconut and salty/butter from the toasting.

Hmmm, I may have to make some of those tonight.

### LinD — 2012-09-01T23:25:32+0300

### hazyjane — 2012-09-02T01:34:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-to-hashis-whats-ok.3173/post-48821>

Hey Marti! Sorry to hear about your diagnosis

Here are my two cents regarding Hashi's (and what has benefited my Hashi's):

**Books: I recommend reading [Why Do I Still Have Thyroid Symptoms When My Lab Tests Are Normal?](http://http://www.amazon.com/Still-Thyroid-Symptoms-Tests-Normal/dp/1600376703/ref=sr_1_1?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1346537149&sr=1-1&keywords=why+are+my+lab+tests+normal), as it's specifically about Hashi's and all the related patterns of imbalance (hormones, neurotransmitters, etc.)

A new book that I've heard had quality info is [The Complete Idiot's Guide To Thyroid Disease.

](http://http://www.amazon.com/Complete-Idiots-Guide-Thyroid-Disease/dp/1615640541)

**Practitioner: It's important to work with a functional medicine practitioner who understands autoimmune disease and balancing the immune system. This is one of the keys to managing Hashi's. You have to determine if your immune system is TH-1 or TH-2 dominant and then you can treat it accordingly and you'll know which supplements to avoid and which will be beneficial.

I've been helped tremendously by this approach, but I'm going to be switching docs to someone who does more involved endocrine treatment than mine does. Drs. Shane Steadman and Mark Flannery were both recommended to me by a knowledgeable friend (they're both listed [here](http://http://thyroidbook.com/practitioner-locator.html)):

I've also heard good things about [Dr. Alan Christianson](http://http://myintegrativehealth.com/our_doctors/dr_alan_christianson).

Iodine:** This one is so individual, but I'd say to take selenium for awhile before trying any iodine and to go small and slow with the dose if you do try it. You can cause a major flare up, otherwise.

Diet:** This is also really individual, but gluten intolerance is nearly a given and casein intolerance is common. Some people cross-react to coffee and others don't. Corn cross-reactivity is very common. It would be ideal to get a [Cyrex Labs Gluten-Associated Cross-Reactive Foods and Foods Sensitivity panel](http://http://www.cyrexlabs.com/CyrexTestsArrays/tabid/136/Default.aspx) to see exactly what you react to.

I hope this is helpful!

### MartiD — 2012-09-26T17:08:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-to-hashis-whats-ok.3173/post-54420>

Thank HazyJane...just realized I never thanked you. I do have a question for those who are veteran Hashi's patients. What do you do when you have several days in a row of the very Hypo feelings. Tired, heavy arms and legs, bad brain fog, mild headache. My sleep is still really good, solid 8 hrs a night but I hit a wall around 4:00 p.m. occassionally. Most days I feel more hyper, but last few days have been very hypo. Any recommendations on foods or supplements? I am still total AI diet and have been 4 weeks.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-26T18:40:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-to-hashis-whats-ok.3173/post-54460>

Are you taking any T3 or Armour Marti? I'm taking 3 doses every day: one as I wake up, one before lunch time and one before dinner. It helps me to have energy and feeling great all day. I had to make some trials and error, but now I think I've found what works the best for me.

### MartiD — 2012-09-26T19:09:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-to-hashis-whats-ok.3173/post-54468>

Until I can see a new Dr. in December I am on 75 mcg of Synthroid. Only be on it about 2 weeks however so not sure it is even in my system yet.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-26T19:22:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-to-hashis-whats-ok.3173/post-54473>

Synthroid is T4. If you have a leaky gut or possible liver problem you may not be converting to FT3, the usable hormone. Did you read the STTM site on your symptoms. Many people don't convert well so they need to be on T3 or natural meds. It may not be you but may be the meds. My first day on natural meds I felt TERRIFIC and it just kept getting better!

### MartiD — 2012-09-26T19:27:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-to-hashis-whats-ok.3173/post-54474>

I've been all through the leaky gut symtoms and if I have one it is very minor because I have no stomach issues at all. As far as I can tell I am about a "normal" and "regular" as someone can be. I know the Dr. I will see in December has experience in compound T3/T4 and prescribes Armour some. There are so many mixed stories about what is best for Hashi's. I have read some horror stories of people on Armour so that makes me a bit nervous. I know any type of Iodine supplement is not good for me. Otherwise, I am still learning.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-26T19:44:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-to-hashis-whats-ok.3173/post-54478>

You're doing good just learning. I read for hours a day for a couple of years trying to figure this out. Sounds like this new doc may be able to help you more. A Hashis person don't have to feel tried, crabby, and bad. I know that now. Stay positive as there is always a new curve thrown in when you have Hashis.

### hazyjane — 2012-09-29T23:09:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-to-hashis-whats-ok.3173/post-55288>

> **MartiD;55190 wrote:** Thank HazyJane...just realized I never thanked you. I do have a question for those who are veteran Hashi's patients. What do you do when you have several days in a row of the very Hypo feelings. Tired, heavy arms and legs, bad brain fog, mild headache. My sleep is still really good, solid 8 hrs a night but I hit a wall around 4:00 p.m. occassionally. Most days I feel more hyper, but last few days have been very hypo. Any recommendations on foods or supplements? I am still total AI diet and have been 4 weeks.

That's where the TH1 balancing supplements and T3 conversion supplements have really helped me (everyone is going to have individual supplement needs with Hashi's).

Also, go easy on yourself. Chances are your adrenals are stressed from the hyper/hypo swings and it can take awhile to heal them (several months). I'm on custom Chinese herbs for that.

Oh, and you're welcome! I hope you get it all figured out. I know how it goes

### Ozzie_Kendall — 2018-12-27T03:57:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-to-hashis-whats-ok.3173/post-259035>

Something I’ve observed - you can have lowish levels of antibodies in Australia and they will say you definitely don’t have Hashimoto’s.

However, the Mayo clinic have a much lower cut off and will say you do indeed have it.

Lab “ranges” and “normals”...

# Thread: sleep stuff
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-stuff.3176/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ealachan — 2012-08-22T16:21:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-stuff.3176/post-46300>

I'm suddenly terribly curious - what's your current sleep schedule, and how does it work for you?

Right now, what I do is put my blue-blockers on as soon as it starts to get dark (around 7:30pm here in VT this time of year), and I'm usually in bed by 8pm or so. I often read for 15-20 minutes until my eyes get heavy (or if I'm lucky, I'll uhhh...get lucky!), then I zonk out. I usually wake up once or twice in the night very briefly (basically only long enough to realize I'm awake), then I go right back to sleep. I wake up around 4am and I'm ready to go. So far, I haven't been wearing my blue-blockers in the mornings because all summer long it was getting light out by 4:15-4:30, but given today's exchanges with Dr. K about waking up in the dark, I guess I better start making sure I wear them in the mornings now, too. I'm averaging 7-8 hours of sleep a night, and I actually have a much easier time waking up at 4am than I ever did waking up at 7am.

So tell us...when do you put your blue-blockers on, when do you go to bed, how long does it take you to get to sleep, do you sleep WELL, and what time do you get up in the morning?

I have a feeling that we're all going to get read a little bit of the riot act about this stuff during thursday's webinar...

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-22T16:23:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-stuff.3176/post-46301>

Invest + Develop+ Sustain= Gain when it comes to sleep

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-22T16:46:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-stuff.3176/post-46302>

Here's what I do - not saying its 100% optimal - its what has to happen.

Get home from work btwn 5-530pm, pick up kids, put dinner on, do a brtv or LHT session for 20min max, eat dinner btwn 630-7pm. If dinner is early, we do something as a family - go for a walk, play outside, or do puzzles/games.

NLT 8pm orange glasses go on me (Dh is later, and the kids aren't wearing them yet b/c when they do they're up at 4am which is too early - I will experiment w/them as it gets darker earlier as we move into fall/winter as well as using candles for lighting during that time frame), kids get bath time and get dressed for bed. Lights are out NLT 830, bedtime stories are done in the dark w/red lights. kids are asleep by 9pm at the latest after milk/nursing/teeth brushing etc.

I either read my kindle touch w/red light and orange glasses, clean up dinner dishes, prep for next day of work/school, do laundry (most like that is a load of cloth diapers). then I lay down in bed reading until I fall asleep - usually NLT 1030 - though we try to DTD every evening if we're not exhausted or TOTM

philips wake up light starts to turn on btwn 515-530 (of recent I've been waking up automatically at 5am ready to go but I stay in bed until the alarm goes off trying to get more sleep) I'm up at 530 at the latest - I pop my cycloset, and armour, go outside look for what little light is out there at that time, start breakfast, hop in shower, get ready (dry hair, makeup, get dressed) kids are up at this point - put breakfast on the table NLT 630. get kids dressed, pack optimal lunch for work, throw everything in backpack, walk down to bus stop for 720 bus to my client site.

What's going to be interesting is how we're going to work this next year - as dd starts school - every monday she'll get out of school at 1:30, and the remainder of the week she'll get out at 3 (going to register her for spanish - to help maintain her bilingual skills) which will keep her after for another 45mins. She'll have to do before and after school care - unless I change my schedule to be in the office at 6am (not too keen on that - but I would be done w/work at 3pm- but its not optimal) I think the key is figuring out what's the best way to make optimal work w/the requirements you have outside the home/family.

### ealachan — 2012-08-22T16:51:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-stuff.3176/post-46304>

Gretch, do you think you'll change your schedule around any as the days get shorter this fall / winter, or will you just start putting your blue-blockers on earlier in the evenings?

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-22T16:56:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-stuff.3176/post-46305>

> **ealachan;46693 wrote:** Gretch, do you think you'll change your schedule around any as the days get shorter this fall / winter, or will you just start putting your blue-blockers on earlier in the evenings?

I think what we'll do is use candles as much as possible in the fall/winter, and blue blockers at least for me & DH. the kids will depend on how they adjust to candles at 5pm vs artificial light. I may pick up some orange bulbs that don't have any blue light and put them in some of our light bulbs for use at night to make cooking a bit easier... we'll see. I think we're going to follow Lauren's method of candles in winter - and see how it goes.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-22T18:16:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-stuff.3176/post-46329>

For evening I put my orange glasses on when the sun goes down (I use an app to follow sunsets) and try to get most of the lights off. Dinner is already done and cleaned up at this point. I will usually go in the bedroom where we have this great electric candle that is set to turn on at 7 and go off at 10pm. I'll be either on my computer or reading until I go to bed around 10pm. I wake around 6am and immediately get up and get the BAB ready. Not all days are perfect like that but most are. I'm learning to be very conscientious of the light cycles. I even travel with my orange glasses!!

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-22T19:02:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-stuff.3176/post-46352>

I've been trying to get outside for a little walk to see the sunset each night. Doesn't always happen depending on schedule but I like to turn off all the lights at home and put on the red night lights, then leave to watch the sun set, then come back home, spend some time on the laptop with F.lux and blueblockers and dimmed screen, or chatting with the roomie, or texting Jennifer  and by 10pm lately I've been sleeping. I still sleep a ton, today I wasn't up until 8:30  and I slept through the night without waking from 10pm. I was awake by 8am but then I need some time to "wake up" before actually rising.

### vkiernan — 2012-08-22T20:42:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-stuff.3176/post-46394>

Our lights are always dim in the living room in the evening. I have only worn my glasses a few times lately. If dh isn't home, the lights stay off. I am always ready to go to bed by 9 but try to make it til almost 9:30 cuz I don't want to get up at 5. Doesn't take long to zonk out and I get up about 5:45-6 am. If I wake in the night, that is due to the dh rolling me over. I usually go right back to sleep. I have found if I have to work late at the office, my sleep generally sucks that night. Can we say good ole' flourescent lighting!!! I have to stop staying too late. I really try to avoid light at night and even while driving or riding in the vehicle. The radio/gps display can be very bright. I do my best to not look at it. I should probably try to turn down the brightness.

### ealachan — 2012-08-22T22:07:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-stuff.3176/post-46404>

> **vkiernan;46784 wrote:** Our lights are always dim in the living room in the evening. I have only worn my glasses a few times lately. If dh isn't home, the lights stay off. I am always ready to go to bed by 9 but try to make it til almost 9:30 cuz I don't want to get up at 5. Doesn't take long to zonk out and I get up about 5:45-6 am. If I wake in the night, that is due to the dh rolling me over. I usually go right back to sleep. I have found if I have to work late at the office, my sleep generally sucks that night. Can we say good ole' flourescent lighting!!! I have to stop staying too late. I really try to avoid light at night and even while driving or riding in the vehicle. The radio/gps display can be very bright. I do my best to not look at it. I should probably try to turn down the brightness.

I can't turn down the brightness on the displays in my car, but when I was having to wake up in the middle of the night to pick Hubs up from work, i'd wear my orange glasses while driving and it worked great! I need to start doing the same thing when I take him out at 4am now, I guess.

Also, I am...lucky?...that flourescent lights overhead when I'm looking at a computer monitor give me a migraine, so I talked my boss into just taking the bulbs out of the light above my desk. Everyone else's works fine, and their desks are all far enough away that I don't get flourescent spill-over. I have a little desk lamp here in my corner that I turn on if it gets too dim (which is really only late afternoons in the winter time. Corner desk means I have two windows. I have good Western and Southern exposure...).

### quelsen — 2012-08-22T22:14:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-stuff.3176/post-46405>

i hit the hay when the sun goes down, sleep till i wake to pee them sleep some more.

we use a red led light in the bedroom for the obligatory 2 chapter read before sleep. lights dont come one much anymore if at all.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-08-23T08:44:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-stuff.3176/post-46494>

> **Shijin13;46694 wrote:** I think what we'll do is use candles as much as possible in the fall/winter, and blue blockers at least for me & DH. the kids will depend on how they adjust to candles at 5pm vs artificial light. I may pick up some orange bulbs that don't have any blue light and put them in some of our light bulbs for use at night to make cooking a bit easier... we'll see. I think we're going to follow Lauren's method of candles in winter - and see how it goes.

I promise you your kids will adapt to candlelight only with total ease. We have now made it through an entire very long winter doing this (just heading into spring in the next few weeks). Sundown was as early as 4:30. It's now around 6:00pm. For us, it is simply normal to have candles going once dusk hits. We never bought any glasses to force us to be strict with the no-artificial-lights. No one has missed the computer screens, tv shows, etc. And we all sleep super well! Good luck!
