# Thread: Hello and a long introduction from Darlene in Montana.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-and-a-long-introduction-from-darlene-in-montana.3180/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 24
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### DJsmiles — 2012-08-22T17:59:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-and-a-long-introduction-from-darlene-in-montana.3180/post-46320>

Hello everyone. I am a 44 year old mom of two (all grown up), have been married 26 years, live in Montana, and unfortunately was diagnosed with Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) last Christmas.

I am a nursing student, and love medical research. Immediately upon diagnosis, I began eating a raw, vegan, gluten free diet. I then transformed that into a totally grain free diet. I felt amazing for about 6 months, when I started to get tired and run down. At that time, more research led me to the belief that I may not be doing myself such a great favor by the raw vegan diet, so I added bone broth, grass-fed beef, eggs etc. I also added coffee back into my morning routine with heavy cream (grass-fed organic).

I then came across the Perfect Health Diet and was allowing myself safe starches.

Just days ago I read The Caveman Doctor and Dr. Kruse's info on safe starches and realize now that they will have to be eliminated from my diet.

The type of cancer I was diagnosed with is usually seen in much older people, except that it seems lately, younger people are being diagnosed. CLL is a slow moving blood cancer (hopefully), and I have had time to experiment. It is a monoclonal proliferation of B-Cells. If any of you know anything about CLL, I do not meet any of the currently tested for FISH test criteria (13q deletion etc.) , which places my overall survival at about 5-9 years. They believe I have had this for about 2. For those of you that do not know about CLL, that will sound like gibberish.

My White Cell count is 29k, my absolute lymphocytes are about 85%. In December of 11 at diagnosis, it was 22k. It was stable while on the vegan diet, not increasing over three months. Then, after adding meat back in (and coffee) it increased to 29K.

There are SO many conflicting studies, articles, and opinions on what a person with cancer should eat. There is even more question when the cancer is a blood cancer. Not much information out there. I have even come across a study that shows that leukemic cell lines are immune to death by ketosis. They are able to maintain life by glycolysis or ketosis, either one. (I cannot relocate this study).

My prior history has been a mess. I had post-viral syndrome after a particularly bad case of influenza in 1990. I had not really been the same since. There were random diagnoses such as Chronic Fatigue, Fibromyalgia, non-differentiated connective tissue disease etc. I have had my gallbladder out, my appendix out, my tonsils out. During the appendix operation in 2008 I acquired Clostridium Difficile. I had this relapsing and reoccurring for 8 months. For 8 months my gut flora was assaulted with Vancomycin. When I asked for a stool transfer I was literally laughed at by the gastroenterologist. I gathered information on my own and did it myself using my daughter's stool. I was cured in one day. Dr. Kruse's latest blog post on gut flora has been eye opening.

All of these random problems cleared up when I eliminated grains from my diet and ate the paleo way.

So I am here. I have read lots of information by Dr. Kruse, but have not been entirely sure how much applies to me. With my student loan in October, I will be utilizing a consult. Until then, I will be devouring information where I can get it. Thanks for any advice or help you can throw my way. I find that the more I learn, the better I feel. The oncologist/hematologist cannot be relied on for helpful information, they tell me that what you eat doesn't matter!

I realize that my post makes me sound like an exhausted lost-cause, but that couldn't be further from the truth. I have tons of energy, workout cross-fit style, play, spend time in the sun, and enjoy every moment of my life. I have always fought back and I will continue to do so.

Thanks for reading!

Darlene

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-22T18:16:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-and-a-long-introduction-from-darlene-in-montana.3180/post-46328>

I loved your post and very glad you were rescued from safe starches......and cancer. This is why I vehemently oppose PHD in this respect. People like you can kill yourselves if you eat this way. Welcome.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-22T18:32:13+0300

Darlene can I post this on the comments of BG 9? Your story might save a few people. I admire you.

### DJsmiles — 2012-08-22T18:33:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-and-a-long-introduction-from-darlene-in-montana.3180/post-46335>

Thanks Dr. Kruse for the welcome. I laughed as I read your post, imagining myself rescued from a large knife wielding sweet potato, lurking in my kitchen with beady eyes and safe starch oozing from his body.

I'll be spending a lot of time here in research and asking questions.

Thank you again,

Darlene

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-22T18:49:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-and-a-long-introduction-from-darlene-in-montana.3180/post-46347>

Darlene.......the genetics of cancer are a result of an alteration of normal metabolic process not the proximate cause of the disease. We were all taught that genetics is the root cause. It said, Genomic determinism means DNA and genes control everything. Today we know that it’s 180 degrees opposite that. It is all about epigenetics. This means we have total control of our DNA if we learn how to use it.

Cancer researchers and oncologist have made this mistake in my opinion from the get go. It is all about epigenetics. The best diet for cancer is the best at controlling inflammation at the brain level to affect hormonal modulation and epigenetic expression. The moral of the story for your paleo template? Most folks are more interested in being effective than right, when it comes to their diet. There’s a huge difference between paleo solution and Epi paleo Rx when you are not well. My site focuses in those in our community who are not well or ideal as they want to be. You have an obligation to yourself only. You can only change yourself. You can’t let other people tell you who you are. You have to decide that for yourself. Health improvements always begin with I, not we.The Paleo template is a very good diet because it is is better at controlling inflammation than most other diets. When I discovered the Epi-paleo Rx seven years ago, I kept researching all the biochemistry links of why it worked so well in those with serious diseases and illness risks. It destroys inflammation while providing the human with massive quantities of brain specific nutrients to rebuild your neural circuitry to optimize your signaling. Degraded signaling is what causes cancer in my view.

Think of food as hormone information, not as a metabolic fuel. Think of the Epi-paleo template as human jet fuel for the human nervous system. Accept that your epigenome is a loaded gun, and your lifestyle is the trigger. Your lifestyle cant change you. It reveals who you really are. I promise you are in the right spot.

### DJsmiles — 2012-08-22T19:24:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-and-a-long-introduction-from-darlene-in-montana.3180/post-46358>

In response to Dr. Kruse's response. Thanks for this post. It truly is the conclusion I have been coming to, however it is nearly impossible not to doubt "little ol me" when compared with the massive sea of conflicting information that seems to exist. You have the raw vegan set, and their whole list of favorable outcomes for cancer. (I probably would have stuck with this if leukemia had not been the "bad boy" of the raw/juicing world, not responding well like a solid tumor might.) You have the macrobiotics, the "dirty paleo", the "clean paleo" etc. And then you have the authorities in health care, the oncologists who claim to be the last and final word, telling you that sugar and carbs do not effect cancer growth.

I know that I am in the right place at the right time. I have just read "The Biology of Belief" by Bruce Lipton. What I took away from it is that Epigenitics rule! My main concern is finding what I can and should "Epi" in order to create the right conditions for my body to respond in an appropriate and ultimately unfriendly manner to cancer cell proliferation.

I question everything, instead of relaxing into the experience. For instance...to coffee? Or not to coffee? I love the dark steamy brew, but if it meant my life, I could end my relationship with it in an instant.

So thank you again for being part of this educational and life-saving journey of mine. Just reading your post provides a bit of a stable base to stand on, and as I am sure you understand, that stable base is part of the optimization of the epigenetic soup that I want my cells swimming in.

Darlene

"Of course it might start a black market for Poo Zoo Goo (sorry)."

No sorry needed! I only wish that I had made a video (not of the actual transfer lol), but of me in my bathroom, mixing up a life-saving toxic soup of saline and sewage... I was a true mad scientist  My friends are still impressed, and eye me cautiously. I do reassure them that I disposed of the dedicated blender after the deed was done...

Dr. Kruse, I would be honored guest blog. Luckily, I took notes and photographs of the set-up in the event I would need them to help others through the process. Darlene

### DarleenMB — 2012-08-23T21:44:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-and-a-long-introduction-from-darlene-in-montana.3180/post-46579>

Darlene in Montana, welcome from Darleen in Wyoming!

On the poo subject ... I have been wondering why coconut oil has been causing me to bloat up like the Hindenburg. Then I remembered it is an antibacterial and I starting thinking maybe my gut has become populated with bad bac. I'm downing 6 caps a day of a combination of acidophilus and bifidus. I've also ordered some [Garden of Life Primal Defense Ultra](http://http://www.gardenoflife.com/ProductsforLife/DigestiveHealth/PrimalDefensesupsupULTRA/tabid/639/Default.aspx) and am hoping those will help. I've taken the primal defense in the past because of heavy antibiotic use. It worked wonders. I'm hoping that combined with drinking kombucha and eating pickled veggies will help me ... I'm not sure I'm ready to try your therapy. Sorry for being so squeamish.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-10-08T20:34:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-and-a-long-introduction-from-darlene-in-montana.3180/post-56920>

I wrote to someone privately about how I "cured" myself of "paleo-type constipation". (The one or two on the Bristol Stool Chart.) I cannot remember which probiotic I used. It was either the 6 or 12 strain probiotic from **Custom Probiotics. My guess is the 6 strain which I like the best. Here is the link. **Harry Bronozian would say not to do what I did. Nevertheless, it worked:

[http://www.customprobiotics.com/custom-probiotics-six-cultures.htm](http://http://www.customprobiotics.com/custom-probiotics-six-cultures.htm)

I have also considered doing a stool transplant from a family member. I have not taken that step yet, nor do I need to now. Here is the private letter. You will have to go to the book for the procedure:

_If you have gut dysbiosis, constipation can be a real problem. When I went on the SCD/GAPS program the only thing that didn't go well for me was constipation. I was having type 1 and on good days type 2 bowel movement using the **Bristol Stool Chart**. It wasn't very pleasant:

[http://thewvsr.com/bristolstoolchart.htm](http://http://thewvsr.com/bristolstoolchart.htm)

Dr Natasha Campbell-McBride_** _has a section on constipation in her book, Gut and Psychology Syndrome_**_. The sections starts on page 225. She gives detail instructions on how she does an enema and how to use probiotics. I know there can be a real yuck factor here and I wish I hadn't been so squeamish about trying it. I could have saved myself years of suffering. I guess it was thinking about my mom dying of bowel cancer that got me over the yuck factor.

The emema, the way she does it, seems to retrain the bowel to work properly again even if the constipation has been going on a long time. Well, it worked for me anyway. I now have type 4 or 5 most days. I now occasionally get constipated traveling but normally my bowel function is healthy._

# Thread: Foods to boost liver function
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/foods-to-boost-liver-function.3181/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-08-22T18:07:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/foods-to-boost-liver-function.3181/post-46322>

My youngest came up with some really nasty spontaneous bruising a week or so ago, and so our doctor ordered some blood tests. She's got to have another round of these, as her liver function test is abnormal (slightly raised ALTs), and I wondered if there were any specific foods to boost liver function. She eats well, a typical day would be coconut "cereal" (toasted coconut flakes) with coconut milk for breakfast, or eggs and bacon, soup, salad and cold meat or fish for lunch, with maybe some berries for pudding (usually with double cream, but no sugar) and meat or fish and vegetables for supper. She doesn't really snack, and she finds her asthma and eczema are more manageable if she is on the lower carb side of life (50 - 100 grams per day, and she's not underweight and is growing like a weed!). She occasionally like a mug of hot chocolate (made with unsweetened cocoa powder and coconut milk), and also occasionally like a couple of squares of 85% or higher chocolate, but in general avoids sugar like the plague. Is there anything I could be doing to help her bloods come back to normal?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-08-23T09:00:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/foods-to-boost-liver-function.3181/post-46496>

Olivia, I'm not sure I'm able to be helpful, but what about dandelion tea or a milk thistle supp? Also, have you read the latest blog? i'd worry more about her gut than just the liver. So minding light cycles big-time, eating epi-paleo, and working on gut flora (you could read the leaky gut Rx). Good luck.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-23T14:21:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/foods-to-boost-liver-function.3181/post-46508>

Haven't had enough time to really take in the latest blog posts, but I am most certainly mindful of circadian cycles for her. My biggest problem is that she returns to boarding school in two weeks (unless there is something major wrong), and whilst I'll make sure she's eating epi-paleo until then, I have no control once she returns to school ... milk thistle supplement might be a very good idea, and I'll get some whilst I'm out today! I doubt I could persuade her to drink dandelion tea, and I don't want her to really guess that there might be something seriously wrong with her unless we come to that point.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/podcasts-music-general-entertainment-to-occupy-your-mind.3182/post-46330>

Hi All,

I notice many of you are like me, and need mental stimulation all the time. ON that note, I find my podcast round up, as well as peppy music for HIIT training is getting mucho stale. I'd love you to say what podcasts, books, music you have that you would reccomend! I am always hooked up to the idevice and listening and I've run out of materials! Hoping you've got something good I've not heard of or tried.

I'll start:

Podcasts:

BTR Underground Wellness

Fatburning Man

ICU Rounds

Latest in Paleo

Livin la Vida Low Carb

Revolution Health

Ted stuff

Upgraded self

Non-health stuff just for fun:

APM Marketplace

Dave Ramsey

Clark Howard

Handel on the Law

Leo Laport

NPR stuff

This American Life

### ealachan — 2012-08-22T18:48:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/podcasts-music-general-entertainment-to-occupy-your-mind.3182/post-46344>

I can't focus on work when people are talking in my ear (either co-workers or podcasts), but I routinely rock out to Spotify all day long...usually quite loud to drown out the co-worker drama that I want no part of. I have lots of different playlists because my musical tastes are pretty diverse, but I tend to gravitate mostly toward classic rock, hard rock, funk and hip-hop. I am also a huge sucker for 80's hair-band music (omg Whitesnake, SQUEEEE!) and have a whole playlist dedicated just to that. I also have a really neat white noise / meditation music playlist that helps chill me out when I'm stressed at work.

At home, I just like quiet. I need either a fan or the A/C unit running at night while I sleep, otherwise I hear every little mouse-scuttle and faucet-drip.

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-22T18:26:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/things-that-drive-jack-crazy.3183/post-46333>

Ok - I'm a capitol offender... so this thread is to poke fun at the things we do that drive Jack Crazy!

I'll go first buying a consult that was intended to help some w/o labs

Spelling metformin incorrectly adding an a - to make it metaformin! hehehe thanks spell check.

asking consult questions in the Ask Jack (yup I've done that)

ok...what else drives Jack Crazy.

Misty we know you've got a list!!!!

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-23T03:18:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-iron-labs.3190/post-46456>

In the context of low Free T3, low Free T4 levels, can anyone give insight into my iron labs?

Total iron 241 (40-175)

Iron binding capacity 275 (250 - 450)

%saturation 88 (15-50%)

Ferritin 33 (10 - 154)

I have not been supplementing iron, but I am having a horrendous time dealing with thyroid supplementation. Not sure if there's anything I need to "do" about the iron issue in the mean time though...

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-23T04:15:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-iron-labs.3190/post-46470>

These labs are beyond me! This is what I see but don't know what it all means:

Total Iron looks very high

Sat% looks high (s/b 35-45%??)

Ferritin looks low

Have you checked your rT3? With those iron labs I would imagine that you would have a rT3 problem especially with a low FT3. Sorry not much help.

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-23T22:50:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-iron-labs.3190/post-46591>

Wow. I can't believe I'm looking at an iron overload condition, when what I really want is to improve my ferritin status. Just put in a call to my doctor about how to proceed forward with this. My iron levels in December were all in normal range (ferritin in the 20s though - not optimal). In May they were getting high (55% saturation, and total iron had climbed just out of range). This hasn't been a good week for lab news.

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-23T22:51:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-iron-labs.3190/post-46592>

Would having sky high iron levels be a reason for extremely poor absorption of thyroid meds, especially T4? I wonder.

### Destiny — 2012-08-24T02:43:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-iron-labs.3190/post-46621>

> **bigknitwit;46998 wrote:** Would having sky high iron levels be a reason for extremely poor absorption of thyroid meds, especially T4? I wonder.

I heard about low ferritin giving a trouble with thyroid meds absorption.What is ferritin? Ferritin is an iron-storage protein which keeps your iron in a dissolvable and usable state, making the iron non-toxic to cells around it. So when Ferritin is measured via a blood test, it is basically measuring the iron you have tucked away for safe use.

Why is measuring storage iron important? You can have so-called “normal” or even optimal serum iron and saturation levels, and even normal hemoglobin and hematocrit, yet a low Ferritin. And some patients report continued problems raising desiccated thyroid even with just low ferritin while the other labs look good. What causes low ferritin when other labs look great? It can point to bacteria and viruses feeding on iron. Even too much of certain herbs like Milk Thistle are said to lower ferritin.

# Thread: Food as hormone information.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-as-hormone-information.3197/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 26
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Souldanzer — 2012-08-23T14:11:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-as-hormone-information.3197/post-46507>

> **â€œThink of food as hormone information, not as a metabolic fuel. Think of the Epi-paleo template as human jet fuel for the human nervous system.â€￾**

Looking at your eating... habits, amounts, food choices, cravings, desires, dislikes, feelings after you eat, well-being in general and after you eat, etc...... what is your body telling you about your hormone status and nervous system? Can you decipher the messages?

How much do you listen to you inner voice when making choices and feeding yourself?

Do your labs and your inner voice agree? Or is there discrepancy? Are they at odds? Which one do you listen to if they are?

What happens if you take a look at your eating (troubles) through the lens of food as hormone information and human jet fuel for the nervous system?

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-23T14:26:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-as-hormone-information.3197/post-46509>

Going first:

Maybe I eat so much.... high quality food.... lbs of seafood a day.... b/c my hormones plain s\*ck and my body is trying to get the building blocks. Maybe it doesn't care so much about metabolic energy content. Maybe all it cares about is refilling those hormone stores.

Maybe I feel like I'm a junkie going through withdrawal without my daily can of coconut milk and coconut oil at every single meal b/c MCFAs are \*the\* preferred energy source for a bad brain.

Maybe I can eat 2lbs worth of salmon in raw jerky form in one sitting and think nothing of it b/c my brain has realized there is a plentiful source of DHA.

How much does my body/brain care about caloric content if it is missing nutrients. Can eating too much be a message other than leptin resistance or damaged hypocretin neurons.

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-23T16:38:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-as-hormone-information.3197/post-46526>

I follow my intuition when eating... seafood has become a priority in how I eat, with skeletal meats and organs interspersed throughout the week. Veggies of choice have always been broccoli, kale, collards, cabbage, cauliflower, Brussel sprouts etc... they are my favorite veggies - but then again I'm E-dominant... I can use all the help I can get.

I have discovered in the past 3days - that IF I DONT TAKE THE MAX DOSE OF K2 my FBG SPIKES! I knew I was running out and thought I ordered in plenty of time - but haven't received the order yet...grrr - 2 days w/o 45mg K2 - and my FBG this morning spiked to 109mg/dl!

I've also discovered since really pushing the seafood - that chicken is crap. I DO NOT LIKE IT. Who BRIAN WASHED me into thinking it actually tasted good. blech! I told DH I will not eat chicken any more. yucky. it doesn't satisfy me AT. ALL! And when I do eat it - I have to load it w/Ghee to even make it palatable and cover it in horseradish - which I dislike!

### chocolate — 2012-08-23T17:28:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-as-hormone-information.3197/post-46533>

I am scared of my inner voice. I dreamed about cookies. I try to think back to the food I was raised on and it doesn't necessarily agree either. I think the part that worked about the food was that it was huge hours between meals and only water. Now, I'm second guessing the only water part, just for the extra flush of digestive enzymes. I am estrogen dominant by appearances. Its the alcohol from the candida, I think. I've been trying to do things "right" and its been wrong. I have a friend that had metabolic trouble he couldn't nailed down and he always says, when in doubt, just fast. After seeing Dr Kruse's post on the study that says 4 days of VLC and your hypothalmus responds properly to insulin, I think I might just try that as a reboot. It seems like that would stop the candida very nicely. I know when you have limited calories, instinctively, carbs aren't on the menu.

I'm doing so many different things and breaking my own rules about one change at a time that I can't nail down what's working either. I do know the codfish helps and beef tastes nasty lately.

### nonchalant — 2012-08-23T18:17:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-as-hormone-information.3197/post-46543>

> **Shijin13;46928 wrote:** I follow my intuition when eating... seafood has become a priority in how I eat, with skeletal meats and organs interspersed throughout the week. Veggies of choice have always been broccoli, kale, collards, cabbage, cauliflower, Brussel sprouts etc... they are my favorite veggies - but then again I'm E-dominant... I can use all the help I can get.
>
>
>
> I have discovered in the past 3days - that IF I DONT TAKE THE MAX DOSE OF K2 my FBG SPIKES! I knew I was running out and thought I ordered in plenty of time - but haven't received the order yet...grrr - 2 days w/o 45mg K2 - and my FBG this morning spiked to 109mg/dl!
>
>
>
> I've also discovered since really pushing the seafood - that chicken is crap. I DO NOT LIKE IT. Who BRIAN WASHED me into thinking it actually tasted good. blech! I told DH I will not eat chicken any more. yucky. it doesn't satisfy me AT. ALL! And when I do eat it - I have to load it w/Ghee to even make it palatable and cover it in horseradish - which I dislike!

Interesting about the K2, Gretchen. I've started tracking my FBG and you're right, interesting patterns are appearing.

I'm in a love affair with seafood. Bacon & eggs are still good, veggies are iffy, other meats are ok once in a while. I have actually eaten 1 piece of chicken lately. It wasn't blech, but I had no desire for a second piece, or any more in the future actually. Lunch meats and hot dogs are blech now, and I can only eat a couple brands of already cooked sausage. I avoid dairy now, even the good raw stuff. I thought I'd miss it, but seafood has filled that niche as well. I have absolutely no desire for any grains any more. I'm free!!

### SeaHorse — 2012-08-24T20:15:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-as-hormone-information.3197/post-46767>

I roasted a chicken we had in the freezer from last summer..it was 8 pounds! Months ago it would have been one of my favorite meals..I couldn't stand it yesterday. It just doesn't taste like food anymore. Now...oysters make me completely happy. I still like organ meats and bacon. Veggies have been off the list except for a little lettuce and bok choy. What I miss is some textures...like crispy crunchy things, or cold crunchy things like cucumber...but salmon skin is better than potato chips for sure, I need to find somebody who wants to market salmon cracklings. I can just see it "Great for your hormones only 15.00 a bag!"

### ealachan — 2012-08-24T21:23:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-as-hormone-information.3197/post-46788>

I have been in situations several times in the last week and a half where I had myriad tasty options available to me, and I chose fish. It wasn't even that I was like "oh gods, better eat the fish, what a chore...", but rather "oooh, what's this fish thing they have? It sounds nummy, I shall try it!" That, to me, is like a complete 180 from even a couple months ago. I've always been ok with fish but basically avoided it if possible. Now I eat fish either breakfast or dinner every day...sometimes both.

I was brought up in the land of "anything fatty is bad" combined with a very big dose of "do as I say, not as I do", so I was always the kid who was sneaking naughty treats whenever I could. That turned into an obsession for tasty yummy goodness when I got to be an adult. Didn't matter what was in it or even if I was hungry - if it tasted good, I would eat it until it was gone. Tasty was my happy. To a certain degree, tasty is STILL kind of my happy because I love to cook...but nowadays I feel like I have control where I never did before. Now I can decide to not eat something even though I know it might taste good and give me a temporary happy, because I can recognize it as just that: temporary. There are other things that make me happy now, not JUST food.

These two points, especially taken together, tell me that there is indeed something quite profound going on inside of me, and I'm thrilled with that.

### ealachan — 2012-08-24T22:17:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-as-hormone-information.3197/post-46811>

> **Souldanzer;47215 wrote:** I have some of those experiences... funny day to write about this b/c today I feel quite a bit anorexic all of the sudden
>
> Or maybe I just feel \*bad\* and with that my appetite got up and left?
>

>
> But yeah, something profound has changed for me, too. I don't usually feel like starving, restricting, binging or purging anymore. It's very strange. I've just plain lost interest in it. When until recently I was obsessed with it all. Even what I am doing today doesn't feel like purposeful restriction. I just don't have an appetite.

I've obviously never been anorexic, but...maybe you're just not hungry? I think it's ok to just not be hungry sometimes, where as it's not ok to purposefully tell yourself or make yourself think you're not hungry when you are...if that makes sense? Does the sensation of hunger show up eventually, at which point you eat...or does it never really show up and you just kind of eat when you think / know you should?

Also, you posted elsewhere that your sleep has been screwed up lately. Is that maybe throwing your appetite for a loop?

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-24T23:32:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-as-hormone-information.3197/post-46834>

> **ealachan;47221 wrote:** I've obviously never been anorexic, but...maybe you're just not hungry? I think it's ok to just not be hungry sometimes, where as it's not ok to purposefully tell yourself or make yourself think you're not hungry when you are...if that makes sense? Does the sensation of hunger show up eventually, at which point you eat...or does it never really show up and you just kind of eat when you think / know you should?
>
>
>
> Also, you posted elsewhere that your sleep has been screwed up lately. Is that maybe throwing your appetite for a loop?

I think I just don't feel well today. Lack of sleep, missed BAB twice in a row b/c of blood draws, stressed about some stuff. But all that sets me up for an AN relapse... b/c it's all stress you know? I was wondering if maybe there's a level of hs CRP where I start to feel that way. Well, if there is, I'm going to get my answer soon b/c I got it drawn this morning at the docs!

It is really really hard to explain what goes on with anorexics when they don't eat. There was another post yesterday about ANs and how they just don't have any interest in food. It's not quite like that. ANs are OBSESSED with food. It's just another way of "filling" your body with food. You fill your mind with it. It's also not true that we are not hungry. We are deathly hungry, we are starving to death and we know it and feel it. For some reason though, the hunger isn't just bad. There is a very enjoyable side to it. It's a high a little bit to feel really hungry. When you're emaciated like some ANs, you don't really feel simple hunger. It's a world of a difference what we feel. It's more like... you feel your body eating itself..... hard to describe. It is beyond hunger. I did sometimes get a growling stomach when starving a lot. So grehlin is still sticking around. But the brain... the brain is kinda lost. You get low blood sugar (dangerously low) and you get shaky and dizzy and can't concentrate or focus and you know exactly what you need but you are very detached from it. The brain is happier when the rest of the body is hungry. There's a huge mismatch going on between the tummy, the rest of the body, and the brain. And the brain wins the battle most times. I used to fill the grehlin hunger with fluids or high-fiber foods to get it to go away, and I used to deal with the low BG by eating sugary little pieces of candy throughout the day. Not much calories but enough to keep me from passing out from low BG and to get me through my next class or whatever.

So yeah, we ARE hungry. But it's really screwed up and there's something very wrong. We feel best when hungry. But there's also a very nagging and uncomfortable side to the hunger.

These days, yes, I do get hungry but the sensation is a lot more subtle. I don't feel like I'm going to keel over if I don't eat NOW. Today I feel badly.... a little nauseous, no real appetite. Kinda feel like I've eaten enough for a while. No interest really. But there is also a little bit of that enjoyment of being hungry going on. Which really is a red flag for me. Yes, I do think me not sleeping correctly is a major major issue here. Not sleeping well used to set me up for some majorly bad eating. And I kinda feel like that today a bit. A bit at risk for some bad decisions.

Well, I wrote a whole lot and I'm not sure I really said anything that makes sense

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-08-26T16:51:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-as-hormone-information.3197/post-47225>

Soul, from the side of simple interventions, (as in not supplements or NT support etc.) I force myself no matter what to go to bed at 10pm. I've started (just two days lol) where I turn my computer off at least 1 hr before going to bed and instead chose to read a book by low yellow lighting...this makes me feel very tired very quickly. Since I don't look at any clock (all of them are digital) I never know, but I suspect I don't even make it to 10 even...but maybe close). I wake up around 6:30 w/o an alarm clock. I go outside and look at the sunrise. (See my room for the next week or so is east facing so I get the twilight and sun coming in in the morning). (And my new place after that is south-east facing...so WIN for that).

As someone suggested for you if you still need the 10 hrs of sleep, to be strict with the bed time and wake up...and then have a nap during the day for the extra needed sleep. I've done this a few times a few weeks back when my sleep got disrupted from external sources. (For example, former house mates were up until 2am laughing and being loud---therefore although in bed I didn't 'sleep' until 2am). So I stayed in bed at least knowing I was resting. Next day I woke up at the usual 7am (that house had a crappy west facing room with towels over the window due to TWO street lights shining in my room w/ loud traffic). So I took a nap the next day just to help 'recover', then resumed the 10pm bed time the next night.

Do you still take the colostrum? I ran out now...and will buy more in about a week or so. I feel like I miss it. I think it really helped, even just the one bottle...and feel like I need it again. (This time I will get goat colostrum if possible). I wonder...if since growth hormones surge at night...if it would be better to take it on empty stomach before bed...?

I really hope you can get the sleeping sorted out. Also, keep everyone updated if you decide to try cycloset. That seems so alluring to me because I know I have low brain dopamine...

I don't say my sleep is perfect, because I know that I wake up at whatever hour it is (again I don't actually check)...but in the early morning. I just lay around and eventually fall back asleep and then wake up at the 6:30am time.

Everyone: About chicken...ya...it's CERTAINLY not a preferred food of mine anymore. When I lived in the Bahamas I lived on chicken and pork...lol. I did eat seafood too...but I didn't 'think' to rely on it's use. My circadian rhythm was IMPECCABLE though, which I believe now really helped more than I ever thought.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-08-26T16:59:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-as-hormone-information.3197/post-47229>

O, ya regarding hunger or not-hunger.

In the past I used to hate eating...like not an AN type...but I was bored with the food...and I felt like it was a chore to eat...I mean I preferred to drink Milo instead...haha. When I cooked I hated almost everything I made...

So, I didn't feel "I shouldn't eat" but it was of no interest to me...probably only sweets were. LOL But it did feel good to feel a bit 'hungry' too...which I suppose it was a bit of the 'rush' or 'high' feeling. I just felt more clear in the head...so idk.

But now I enjoy seafood and fish so much.

Here's what I like to eat:

\-fish of all kinds

\-seafood of all kinds

\-dark leafy greens

\-sea veggies (honestly, seaweed really IS addicting)

\-coffee

\-coconut oil

\-butter from grass-fed (but lately I don't know if I need to switch to ghee)

\-\*want to try more goat products instead\*

\-olive oil

\-bacon (of course!)

\-eggs

\-sweet potatoes, squash and pumpkin (regular potatoes don't taste good...so bland and 'mushy').

\-Lindt dark chocolate (lol)

\-don't know if this is 'bad' but when I put sugar in my coffee, it's raw cane sugar

It's pretty much what everyone else eats here. lol It's so addicting though!

I know you guys mean good.... but I can not read. And while I know you all mean good keeping it getting suggested to me that I read instead of being on the computer.... it makes me real sad  I wish I could read. It was one of my favorite things to do before my brain went bye-bye. It gives me a major headache now and I won't retain anything anyways so why try. My major problem isn't falling asleep, it is staying asleep.

I'm deciding about the colostrum. I don't "take" colostrum, I eat it. I go through a huge amount (up to 2lbs of powder/months). But since the antibodies showed up on my thyroid panel I need to ask Dr K if the colostrum is really ok for me. I wouldn't take it before bed.... I take nothing for 4hrs before bed other than my progesterone.

No cycloset for now. I need to get my dopamine up first and then I will reevaluate. Also with regards to my rather big appetite. And like I mentioned somewhere else, I like to do things naturally.... so I'd rather go live in a tent in the mountains for a while to reset my circadian cycles. Not kidding.

If you have a neurotransmitter issue, you might want to check out Dr kalish's work. Skip the hormone doctoring and the diet advice with him, just stick to the Organic Cation Transporter stuff.

### Qodesh — 2012-08-27T18:20:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-as-hormone-information.3197/post-47494>

I'm finding I'm loosing my hunger. I eat my breakfast (usually two easy eggs and a meat of some kind) and am still full through lunch. When I do feel hungry at dinner, I find I fill up so fast I eat about 1/3 of what I used to. I'm learning to change my habit of a huge plate-full. Seems so odd. My brain and life-time programing thinks it's not enough, but my body and stomach are fine.

I CT around 50 degrees each morning, but only about 10 mins, working my up easily as my warmups speed up.

I LOVE raw oyster and can't wait until they come back in season. I very gently saute my shrimp in GF butter so they're barely done.

I love munching on my raw/dried dulse straight up as a snack/lunch.

I've been adding raw "high lamb heart" and dry aged, raw GF beef.

I have a buffalo kidney waiting for me, but it's a bit intense. Anyone have a good way to eat it?

# Thread: Nature | News In-law infighting boosted evolution of menopause
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nature-news-in-law-infighting-boosted-evolution-of-menopause.3200/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-08-23T19:11:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nature-news-in-law-infighting-boosted-evolution-of-menopause.3200/post-46550>

[http://www.nature.com/news/in-law-infighting-boosted-evolution-of-menopause-1.11253](http://http://www.nature.com/news/in-law-infighting-boosted-evolution-of-menopause-1.11253)

Conflict between women and their daughters-in-law could be a factor in explaining an evolutionary puzzle â€” the human menopause.

Humans, pilot whales and killer whales are the only animals known to stop being able to reproduce long before they die. In terms of evolution, where passing on your genes is the main reason for living, the menopause remains puzzling.

Mothers-in-law can help to care for their grandchildren â€” unless they have their own children at the same time.

B. Maurer/Getty

Now, using a large data set from Finland, researchers have for the first time been able to test a hypothesis that competition between different generations of genetically unrelated breeding women could have promoted the evolution of the menopause. The results are published today in Ecology Letters1.

Mirkka LahdenperÃ¤, an ecologist at the University of Turku in Finland, and her colleagues used data from meticulous birth, death and marriage records kept by the Lutheran church in the country between 1702 and 1908. As they dug into the data, the researchers found that the chances of children dying increased when mothers-in-law and daughters-in-law gave birth around the same time. For children of the older women, survival dropped by 50%. For children of the daughters-in-law, it dropped by 66%. However, if mothers and daughters had children at the same time, the survival of those children wasnâ€™t affected.

Family values

The results suggest that it would be beneficial to stop having children once your daughter-in-law entered the fray. â€œWe were surprised that the result was so strong,â€ says Andrew Russell, an ecologist at the University of Exeter, UK, who was part of the research team. He suggests that perhaps in-laws fought over food for their children instead of cooperating as mothers and daughters might.

Related stories

'Grandmother hypothesis' takes a hit

Spring birth leads to earlier menopause

When will you hit menopause?

More related stories

Other theories to explain the menopause include the mother hypothesis, which suggests that older women have an increased chance of dying in childbirth, and the grandmother hypothesis â€” that the benefits to the family when women care for their grandchildren provide an evolutionary reason to stay alive after reproductive age.

Using an inclusive-fitness model, which counts the number of gene equivalents passed from generation to generation, the team showed that when mothers and their sons' wives had children at the same time, there was strong selection against women remaining fertile past the age of 51.

The researchers also teased out how much each of the three hypotheses contributed to changes in inclusive fitness. The conflict hypothesis and the grandmother hypothesis both contributed about the same, whereas the mother hypothesis didnâ€™t have any real effect. â€œReproductive conflict and grandmothering are equally important,â€ says Russell.

Combined effect

Michael Cant, an evolutionary biologist at the University of Exeter, developed2 the intergenerational conflict theory with Rufus Johnstone, a zoologist at the University of Cambridge, UK, in 2008. He is pleased that the theory has been tested. â€œIâ€™m stunned by the size of the effect,â€ says Cant. â€œThe problem with most ideas about human life-history evolution is theyâ€™re so difficult to test.â€ By incorporating both grandmothering and conflict to explain menopause, â€œyou get to the point where it makes senseâ€, he says.

However, the effects studied in the Finnish data set arenâ€™t the only possible contributing factors to menopause, says Tom Kirkwood, who studies ageing at Newcastle University, UK. Kirkwood thinks that â€œmenopause will turn out to have multiple lines of explanationâ€, including some based on physiology. For example, to continue reproducing past the current age of menopause, humans would need larger ovaries that could produce viable eggs for longer.

There is still a lot of work to be done, adds Cant. â€œWhy is menopause hard-wired and inflexible? Why havenâ€™t we evolved the ability to switch off breeding in a flexible way?â€ he asks.

Russell agrees that a lot more testing is needed to understand the menopause, but not just for scientific completeness. â€œIf one day we are considering the possibility of reversing menopause medically, understanding the implications of this will require understanding what got us there in the first place,â€ he says.

It makes you wonder about the wisdom of food stamps.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-23T19:46:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nature-news-in-law-infighting-boosted-evolution-of-menopause.3200/post-46561>

This is why older moms have so much trouble......lower progesterone is why......most of the modern world is born to older moms and this is now being transmitted to these offspring.......epigenetics rears its head again. This is why I think we are seeing massive spikes in autism and spectrum disorders and also why infertility now affect 1 in 7 couples......

### CarolMorris — 2012-08-23T21:05:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nature-news-in-law-infighting-boosted-evolution-of-menopause.3200/post-46576>

Very interesting...I was having the "age" discussion with my daughter and daughter-in-law. My daughter who's 34 has a 17 month old and planning the second. She feels she needs to do it right away even if she's not ready. My comment..what's the difference if you're 35 or 36?

Sounds like I was wrong.

What age is older for a mom, in your opinion Dr. Jack?

### AKMan — 2012-08-24T08:05:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-know-youve-embraced-epi-paleo-when.3201/post-46683>

I'm heading out this weekend for my yearly moose hunt. When I was packing my food, I realized that last year I shot a moose on the first day, so all of last year's food was still packed in my camping gear. I laughed when I looked at it. I was firmly 'primal blueprint' at the time. Here's what I had for a week: 10 cans of Spam, 10 cans of Vienna Sausages, 5 bags of teryaki beef jerky, instant rice, and several bags of nuts. This year I am bringing 10lbs of dried raw salmon, 10 cans of oysters in water, 5 lbs of raw, dirty carrots and 5 lbs of tomatoes from my garden, 2 lbs of sauerkraut I just made in a stone crock.

I don't plan on shooting a moose right away this year, I want to enjoy the 40 degree nights sleeping in a tent. I'm also packing a cooler of ice so I can save the heart, kidneys, liver, brains, and all the fat I can find--this usually gets left behind for the bears.

Epi-paleo Alaskan Style!

they are washed..... no matter what you do, if you buy your veggies farmer's market, stand, booth, whatever, unless they come from your own garden, chances are, most of them are washed. Multiple times and/or with a pressurized water stream.

I know Dr K says different but I work on organic farms every season (except for this b/c of %$@# TBI) and we.... wash. Pretty much everything. No dirt is one of the things customers look for.

Maybe we should start having a special bin: Probiotic veggies. Comes with local soil bacteria. Crunchy between the teeth.

Lol. Less work for moi. Nicer skin for my hands, too.

### Stavity — 2012-09-01T21:43:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-know-youve-embraced-epi-paleo-when.3201/post-48781>

When I decided to buy some large Spotted Shrimp that has the roe in it and make a ceviche with it. Never would I have done this 6 months ago. From what I was told, this is the last of the season for Spotted Shrimp in Alaska and the closer to the end of the season the more shrimp have their roe. I just hope I don't become all squirrley and such over this and chicken out. I have only had scallop ceviche and salmon tartare so far. I love when seafood that smells of the ocean and not fishy. Finding that source has been a blessing to me.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-12T04:52:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-know-youve-embraced-epi-paleo-when.3201/post-51131>

> **caroline;51481 wrote:** can you tell me about the seaweed salad please - do you buy it ready made or make it? I haven't quite figured out what I am looking at yet at the Asian market. I did see a bag of seaweed salad in the cooler. Is that what I want? thanks

Caroline, be REALLY careful with ready-made seaweed salads. I'm guessing Aussie is like NZ and it is most usually with MSG added, but often called other things (there is a list of synonyms/numbers that can be used)... I've found this to be the case even at the small Asian markets. I have no idea if this is true in the US. Buy it dehydrated (health shops usually have pure stuff) and make the salad yourself to be sure.

thanks Lauren. so you buy dried seaweed and then rehydrate it and make a dressing for it?

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-12T14:01:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-know-youve-embraced-epi-paleo-when.3201/post-51155>

The best way to cook squid/octopus on the girl w/smoked wood chips. Brushed w/avocado oil salt pepper & garlic

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-12T23:49:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-know-youve-embraced-epi-paleo-when.3201/post-51262>

> **caroline;51732 wrote:** thanks Lauren. so you buy dried seaweed and then rehydrate it and make a dressing for it?

There is a great NZ company that sells mostly NZ-harvested seaweeds (with some imported from Europe) and I buy directly from them. In bulk! Then I rehydrate to make seaweed salads (and yes, then I make a dressing). But I also just sprinkle it directly on food or into soups. I eat handfuls every day. One easy way is to mash an avocado, add sea salt and some lemon juice and then chuck in a bunch of seaweed. Because it's not re-hydrated some of it (other than kelp, usually) will still be very crunchy (dulse especially) or chewy (like arame/wakame) but it still tastes great.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-13T04:18:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-know-youve-embraced-epi-paleo-when.3201/post-51361>

My version of the wakame salad. . . I looked at a bunch o recipes and came up with my own.

1 bag of wakame, crunched up into warm water till it's soft. You will know! doesn't take long. You need a bigger bowl than you think!!

2 tbs, rice vinegar

1 tbs, apple cider vinegar

1 tbs, raw local honey

1.5 tbs of actual soy sauce. . .it's expensive because it's an actual fermented thing. I think you could use coconut aminos.

2 tbs of sesame oil

Grated ginger, about a tbs - to taste.

I tasted and made adjustments as desired on the sauce. drained the seaweed in the salad spinner and mixed and refridgerated and sprinkled sesame seeds on when serving. Kiddo L O V E D it.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-17T20:24:51+0300

# Thread: Optimal Vitamin D from the Sun
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-vitamin-d-from-the-sun.3203/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-23T20:48:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-vitamin-d-from-the-sun.3203/post-46574>

So I was wondering if anyone has any links to websites which either list dates, times, locations for when the sun will be at a good angle for good Vitamin D production or have maps preferably. I would like the visual if possible.

Also if anyone has any advice on how to find tanning salons that use safe tanning beds or what questions to ask that would be great. I'd love to use tanning beds/natural light as much as possible. I've lately been thinking about doing tanning at a location for Vitamin D mainly but wouldn't mind a bit of darkening either. I also remember reading about someone saying that exposure all over could be beneficial....do any tanning places allow you to be naked?

If anyone has any more information regarding exposure to all areas that would be great. I guess at the very least I could buy a Speedo or something for almost complete exposure though I would put it on in privacy and switch back to something less embarrassing. I don't think I have the body for a Speedo but would like the benefits if there are any of full exposure.

My fence has wholes people can see through and my mom is not comfortable with me sun bathing naked in my back yard. At some point I hope to get a board on board type fence or something without the problem....not sure if I could convince my mom even with it because she would rather not see me either  but it might help.

### lioness7 — 2012-08-23T22:34:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-vitamin-d-from-the-sun.3203/post-46584>

[http://aa.usno.navy.mil/data/docs/AltAz.php](http://http://aa.usno.navy.mil/data/docs/AltAz.php)

This site will calculate your sun angle times. Add an hour for daylight savings. I have never been to a tanning salon nor plan on it...somehow just doesn't seem natural. Perhaps we don't need vit D exposure twelve mos of the year? I remember reading years ago that the front abdomen and back of knees has more vit d receptors...though I never followed up on this. The more naked the better I am sure. I have been tanning all summer, weather and housework permitting that is - 15 mins front, back and each side , half an hour before and after solar noon a few times a week. We have never used sunscreen. Can't do naked with 7 kids roaming around but I have learned how to be extremely discreet despite them. Also you can go here too

[http://nadir.nilu.no/~olaeng/fastrt/VitD\_quartMEDandMED\_v2.html](http://http://nadir.nilu.no/~olaeng/fastrt/VitD_quartMEDandMED_v2.html)

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-23T22:39:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-vitamin-d-from-the-sun.3203/post-46585>

You are right about not needing it 12 months out of the year if your level is good. Actually I recently got a Vitamin D test so what I do or don't do might change based on the result. Tanning beds are not natural but I've heard they are the next best thing after the real sun if you aren't optimal in the optimal range.

There are tanning beds using electronic ballasts and ones using magnetic ballasts. Also they have varying levels of UVA/UVB. If the ratio is good and they use electronic ballasts they are probably good....Optimal would of course be the sun and if your level is optimal in the fall and winter using a tanning bed or supplements probably isn't necessary....depending on how fast it degrades.

This I'm not sure of...what level do you need to be able to last through the fall and winter.

I'm not aware of what values need to be for good Vitamin D production...and what do I need to set tabular value at?

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-23T22:45:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-vitamin-d-from-the-sun.3203/post-46588>

On the second page they list 1000 IU every other day as ideal....I don't think this fits with Jack Kruse's or Dr. Mercola's ideal amounts.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-23T22:45:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-vitamin-d-from-the-sun.3203/post-46589>

I think the only safe tanning bed is getting your omega 3 to 6 ratio in check before using any.

You can get quite a bit of vitamin D from seafood. Fish roe rocks for that purpose.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-23T22:46:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-vitamin-d-from-the-sun.3203/post-46590>

Can you get the activated form of Vitamin D without sun or safe tanning bed exposure though? The activated form is sulferated. Sulferated doesn't come up as a dictionary word....maybe I'm not spelling it right or that's the incorrect term.

On the Omega 3/6 point I actually did a bloodspot test...hoping I did it right and should be getting results today if all goes well.

### Endless — 2012-08-24T19:37:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinar-on-light-and-andropause-menopause.3210/post-46755>

I was unable to make the webinar...can someone please tell me about SAFE souces of light? I have the UVEX orange lens glasses and am minimizing electric lights in my house. Is it ok to see by firelight or candlelight without using the glasses? What about an old fashioned kerosene lamp?

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-24T20:27:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinar-on-light-and-andropause-menopause.3210/post-46772>

> **Jack Kruse;47123 wrote:** Misty is working on it.......some issues with the recording I heard.

Boy, I sure don't like the sound of that.......

### ealachan — 2012-08-24T21:01:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinar-on-light-and-andropause-menopause.3210/post-46779>

> **Endless;47165 wrote:** I was unable to make the webinar...can someone please tell me about SAFE souces of light? I have the UVEX orange lens glasses and am minimizing electric lights in my house. Is it ok to see by firelight or candlelight without using the glasses? What about an old fashioned kerosene lamp?

The sun is pretty much the only totally safe light source, from what I gathered. My notes indicate that source-wise, the list from WORST to LESS BAD goes like this: xenon, halogen, florescent, incandescent, candles. LEDs were also mentioned as pretty bad, though I'm not sure what category they fall under. He did mention that amber / orange incandescents are slightly better than regular, and that one should always make sure to use BOTH f.lux AND wear orange glasses if having to work on a laptop at night. Light from the TV at night is bad even with orange glasses on, but he said it's more of an issue for someone who is very fat (there goes my football season, \*sob\*) or very sick than it is for someone healthy.

He said he's a fan of both candle light and fire light. I don't remember hearing anything about kerosene lamps, but my sound kept dropping out, so maybe someone else caught it.

Hope that helps!

### LinD — 2012-08-25T15:08:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinar-on-light-and-andropause-menopause.3210/post-46986>

We are building a new house and we had to make upfront decisions about a lot of derails. One item was a floor electric plug for the living room. DH was always annoyed with the one we had in the old house....always in the wrong spot no matter how the room was arranged. So he said, no, we don't want one. (I giggled inside.) The wall outlets will be enough. OK, honey, are you sure? Yes.

Two months later, he is asking the builder how much to add it now? Don't think we got an answer (afraid to really know), but in light (lol) of keeping to circadian and optimized living, not having the floor plug will work out just fine. Don't ya think so?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinar-on-light-and-andropause-menopause.3210/post-47029>

Well, windows are always a value! More windows, big ol picture windows where it cranks open so you can let in air and light without the filters from the UV coating

I love house building!!! It's my favorite thing in the world. My hubby is on board and once son is up and out (15 yrs from now!) we fully intend to buy our little mountain land and build an optimal oasis!

The other thing at this stage you might be able to have influence on is the placement of fire/smoke alarms. Put them just OUTSIDE the bedrooms, so you don't have that little light shining in the dark. Code might not be on your side, but you can ask!

# Thread: Scientists Clear Path to the Fountain of Eternal Youth
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scientists-clear-path-to-the-fountain-of-eternal-youth.3220/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### JanSz — 2012-08-24T15:40:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scientists-clear-path-to-the-fountain-of-eternal-youth.3220/post-46700>

[http://gizmodo.com/5937249/scientists-clear-a-path-to-the-fountain-of-eternal-youth?popular=true](http://http://gizmodo.com/5937249/scientists-clear-a-path-to-the-fountain-of-eternal-youth?popular=true)

Scientists Clear Path to the Fountain of Eternal Youth

Jesus Diaz

Researchers at Johns Hopkins have discovered an **efficient and totally safe method to turn adult blood cells "all the way back to the way \[they were\] when that person was a 6-day-old embryo."** The discovery could be the key to cure the incurableâ€”from heart attacks to severed spinal cord to cancerâ€”and open the door, some day, to eternal youth.

Scientists believe that stem cell therapy could change medicine forever. However, these therapies are impossible to implement on a large scale because you can't acquire embryonic stem cells without having to use actual human embryosâ€”an extremely controversial undertaking. The alternative has always been to use the stem cells found in umbilical cordsâ€”which is why rich people use umbilical cord storage facilities to guarantee future treatments for their kidsâ€”or use viruses to reprogram adult cells. These viruses can successfully return adult cells to their stem cell state, but the procedure opens the door to numerous complications as a result of potential DNA mutations. And those mutations could lead to cancer.

But this new method changes everything. To start with, it uses normal adult blood cells from the patient, so there's not need to keep umbilical cords in storage. It also doesn't use any virus reprogramming, so it's completely safe. It's also very efficient: researchers successfully transformed about 50 to 60 percent of adult blood cells into embryonic stem cells that can then be turn into any type of cellâ€”a heart muscle cell, a bone cell, a nerve cell, anything.

How it works

Described in the August 8 issue of the journal Public Library of Science, the rejuvenating method uses plasmids, DNA molecules that are usually present in bacteria and eukaryotic organisms. These plasmids can replicate themselves independently from the chromosomal DNA, disappearing after they complete their function.

Using electrical pulses, the researchers opened holes in the membrane of blood cells extracted from a patient's spinal cord. They used these tiny holes to inject plasmids loaded with four genes, programmed to make the cells revert to a primitive state. After the plasmids completed their function, they cultivated the cells with irradiated bone-marrow cells. Seven to 14 days later, the cultivated cells magically turned to embryonic stem cells.

The team is now evaluating the quality of these cells, but the potential to accelerate current and future stem cell treatments like never before is nothing sort of miraculous. By getting rid of all the barriers to entry, medical researchers could experiment at a faster pace. And once new therapies are in place, everyone on the planet would be able to receive self-transplants of embryonic cells to cure diseases, fix spinal cords or eye nerves, and rejuvenate organs by renewing tissues without rejection risks or any other side effect. Hypothetically, if you're able to perpetually fix any part of your body, there's no reason you wouldn't be able to live as long as you wanted.

We are not there yet, of course, but the path is more open and wider than ever. More importantly, this makes the whole political debate about embryonic stem cells absolutely pointless.

# Thread: Dr. Rosedale on safe starches
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-rosedale-on-safe-starches.3221/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 62
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-08-24T16:07:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-rosedale-on-safe-starches.3221/post-46705>

I tend to think that "safe starches" is a storm-in-a-teacup debate, and I don't know why the Paleosphere is so obsessed by it. This article by Dr. Rosedale is, however, quite interesting:

[http://drrosedale.com/blog/2012/08/18/a-conclusion-to-the-safe-starch-debate-by-answering-four-questions/](http://http://drrosedale.com/blog/2012/08/18/a-conclusion-to-the-safe-starch-debate-by-answering-four-questions/)

Among other things it introduces some information on other cultures I wasn't aware of. It's an obvious point that however much starch Okinawans eat (and actually they were eating unusually more in the immediate post-war period, for obvious reasons, as someone points out in Rosedale's comments), they eat less starch and more vegetables and fish than mainland Japanese. I already knew that one.

However, some of the stuff Rosedale has on the Kitavans I had not heard before -- and this is surprising when you think about it, considering how much their name is bandied around.

Has anyone else ever heard before that the average male height is 5' 4"?

I mean let's all eat like that so that we can all shrink over a few generations! Sounds like a really good idea.

Rosedale also says that claims about their longevity are dubious. Furthermore, he gives the interesting information that they only eat one large meal a day, so they're not continually thumping their systems with starch.

There's a lot going on there.

### Michael — 2012-08-24T17:04:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-rosedale-on-safe-starches.3221/post-46717>

I wasn't in Boston, but it sounds pretty weird to me.

For one thing it sounds as if people were taking a listing of what was in typical meals in Okinawa in the late 1940s, and saying _that's_ what Okinawan centenarians had to thank for their longevity. Maybe that's not what they were saying. But if it was, it's pretty crazy. If you were 100 at that time you obviously hadn't grown up on that. For most of your life you were eating whatever was around in the nineteenth century. The time-perspective matters.

And it's not as if the war in the Pacific theatre didn't change matters!

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Battle\_of\_Okinawa](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Battle_of_Okinawa)

Even Robb Wolff seemed to be dog-piling onto Dr. Ron (and Cate Shanahan was it?) in his latest podcast. And, sure, someone had referenced a paper, but paper's aren't free-standing, and if one's not understood properly in a wider context ...

### kathylu — 2012-08-24T18:04:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-rosedale-on-safe-starches.3221/post-46734>

The "Okinawan Weight Loss Diet" that seems to be so popular now (high carb, low fat, low protein) has nothing to do with the traditional Okinawan diet, which was quite heavy in pork and goat meat and their principle starch was sweet potato (some rice too). In fact there was a lot of cultural discrimination towards Okinawans from Japanese, who called them "pig eaters" as a pejorative. I know this is true, I grew up in Hawaii with a large Okinawan population and Japanese were openly critical of Okinawans, though I will spare you the actual comments. As for their diet, Okinawan sayings include the phrase that Okinawan cuisine "begins with pig and ends with pig" and "every part of a pig can be eaten except its hooves and its oink." Of course, they had access to great seafood as well.

So, maybe the lower carb, higher fat, whole food, pig and goat heavy diet has something to do with their longevity?

Even though they have favorable genetic differences in their MHC (major histocompatibility loci), their life expectancy has been dropping since they have adopted a low fat, Japanese style diet, a fact that is ignored by mainstream media and research.

No safe starches in the winter in my opinion, limited starch in the summer is probably fine as long as you are healthy.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-24T18:10:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-rosedale-on-safe-starches.3221/post-46737>

The reason Okinawans live long is because of a genetic defect they have in cytochrome 1. I wrote about it a long time ago. Tanaka in the Lancet 1998. I also mentioned it at Paleo Fx and it is in Nick Lane's books too. It just does not fit the paleo meme.........Therefore they ignore it.

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-24T18:24:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-rosedale-on-safe-starches.3221/post-46740>

When I lived in Okinawa, four things were always served when you went out to eat pork, seafood, seaweed and Awamori. (each family has their own Awamori recipe that is handed down) I will admit I seriously miss the okinawan purple sweet potato - there is nothing like it in the world...

though w/the influence of the mainland Japanese - there was a large prevalence of soba, and other starchy dishes.

### kathylu — 2012-08-24T19:27:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-rosedale-on-safe-starches.3221/post-46751>

I also mentioned it at Paleo Fx and it is in Nick Lane's books too. It just does not fit the paleo meme.........Therefore they ignore it.

Yes, but I wasn't really talking about their (unfair!) genetic advantage. I was trying to point out that the characterization of the Okinawan traditional diet has changed radically recently and that characterizing their diet as 85% carb (Kresser and Guyenet at AHS 2012) may be true now, but wasn't true previously. And their life expectancy has been dropping recently, though that may be tied to destruction of circadian rhythmicity, like it is for all of us suffering from the plague of artificial lights.

### kathylu — 2012-08-24T19:59:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-rosedale-on-safe-starches.3221/post-46758>

Working on a little thought experiment here...if most of the cellular damage of ageing is due to mitochondrial leakiness, then why not address that therapeutically? We know for instance, that calorie restriction reduces leakiness, so it makes a lot of sense to adopt CR by middle age or so in order to increase one's health span if not lifespan. So, might it be possible to take adult stem cells and insert a population of young, healthy mitochondria into them, which presumably would replicate. You wouldn't even need to eliminate the existing host cell mitochondria because the cell has a mechanism for choosing the "healthiest" mitochondria for replication. Then transplant the new stem cell population into the recipient and watch for improvement in markers of ageing. I think this could easily be done in a mouse model now.

Of course, doing it in humans would set off ethical warning bells, since it would effectively create a "transgenic" human (i.e. DNA from 3 people). However, if people thought they could get a transplant that might make them younger, you couldn't keep them away!

Ok, submitting this idea for the Methuselah prize...lol!

### Michael — 2012-08-24T22:20:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-rosedale-on-safe-starches.3221/post-46814>

Here's something else in Rosedale's article that piqued my interest: his concept of "commonality", his saying that in much we are all alike & in some respects even like earthworms.

I think that has the ring of truth about it.

Currently in Paleo there seems to be a pull in the opposite direction. I'm sure that's true up to a point. But eventually as one hears this more & more doubt kicks in.

Jimmy Moore, an important voice & justly revered for his hard work, seems nevertheless strangely nervous one under his very smooth & professional delivery. He went thru a phase of continually saying everyone's different & ANY given diet might be as good depending on who you were ... Until one day a guest called him on it. I always felt it was an attempt to retreat to a place where he couldn't be criticized, to say: "I offer help but assert nothing beyond that this works for me."

But surely we can say a LITTLE more than this & it's not as if Mary next door to me belongs to a different species or something.

Interesting to muse on why "everyone is different" is pushed so hard in Paleo at the moment. Yeah, that's true, but only up to a point.

### AKMan — 2012-08-24T23:42:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-rosedale-on-safe-starches.3221/post-46836>

[http://www.proteinpower.com/drmike/inflammation/can-your-food-make-you-fit/](http://http://www.proteinpower.com/drmike/inflammation/can-your-food-make-you-fit/)

Eades is excited about a recent study linking AMPK and low-carb...

"AMPK is one of the primary signaling proteins that monitors the ATP levels in the cells and signals for more energy when levels drop. When AMPK is activated indicating our cellular energy tanks are depleted, all kinds of good things happen. Here is a short list of metabolic efforts all kicked into action by activated AMPK and why they’re important.

\- Increases glucose uptake: We want to get glucose out of the blood and into the cells to burn.

\- Increases glycolysis: We need to break down glycogen (stored sugar) to get the glucose to burn.

\- Increases fatty acid oxidation: An obvious one. We want to start burning fat to replenish the depleted energy stores.

\- Increases mitochondrial biogenesis: we want to make more mitochondria to burn fat and generate as much ATP as possible.

\- Inhibits gluconeogenesis: We don’t want to spend energy making more sugar – we want to burn it.

\- Inhibits glycogen synthesis: Same thing – we don’t want to store sugar, we want to burn it.

\- Inhibits fatty acid and cholesterol synthesis: We don’t want to spend energy making fat and cholesterol.

\- Inhibits insulin secretion: We want insulin to be low, so that we can move stored fat and sugar to where it needs to be burned.

When our ATP tanks are filled to the bursting, as when we eat and are stuffed with food (especially carbohydrates) and/or we don’t exercise, all the above pathways go in the opposite direction. If we chronically overeat the wrong foods, our metabolic systems end up sending all the above pathways in the opposite direction most of the time.

This I struggle with....you say there are no safe starches, then say they are safe at certain times. How does the body change it's reaction(insulin, blood sugars etc) in differant seasons?!

For me I'm trying to get a bit of weight, or certainly not wanting to lose anymore weight(5'9", 140lbs), so I strive for around 2500-3000 cals per day.

So if, as per Rosedales article, I shoot for 1g pro per kg BW, which is around 63g, and around 50g carbs.

This comes to around 450cals, with the rest coming from fat, which is around 2000-2500 cals, depending on my daily workload.

That's 222-277g of fat, that's seems a heck of a lot to me! Do I just up the nuts, butter, cream, avocadoes etc?

Thanks
