> **bigknitwit;48548 wrote:** Thank you!! This was fantastic!
>
> Now how does insulin resistance start cmoing into the picture?

The question is how does IR come into the picture, per the following you become LR first, then IR and then AR. The following blog: Hormones 101 - Clinical thoughts revealed answers all the questions. It is a great blog. Always remember, rising cortisol assists in the "get fat" dept. per the following

I want to now give you some perspective as to WHY this matters.

We have established that as one gets fat, Leptin levels rise. Once they get high enough (around a Body Mass Index (BMI) of 20-24), Tumor Necrosis Factor (TNF) rises in several tissues. TNF quickly destroys normal hepatic homeostasis, which sets the stage for fatty liver disease and type two diabetes over time. This rise in TNF also biochemically changes Leptin receptor signaling and changes its quantum properties by changing its â€œresonanceâ€ (think of a vibration like effect in the receptor) at the hypothalamus level. Once TNF rises, it causes the liver to make an acute phase inflammatory protein called highly sensitive CRP. HS-CRP is therefore a very early biomarker for cellular inflammation before any disease is established. We have already established in earlier blogs that inflammation causes Leptin resistance. Leptin controls all energy production in the body. Inflammation stops T4 to T3 conversion in the liver and abruptly turns off your thyroidâ€™s ability to function properly despite normal thyroid labs. As an analogy, your car engine no longer has a gas pedal to use. Simultaneously Vitamin D production caves as well because TNF takes that out too. Immunity fails and bad things commence for the cellular terroir. (Levee 1)

In Summary, once Leptin resistance occurs centrally in the brain, the liver soon follows and then the peripheral tissues become resistant too. This effects fecundity, bone metabolism, cardiac metabolism, the thyroid, and the immune system in that order. Are you with me?

RESULTANT HORMONE CASCADE

Leptin resistance occurs first. Then insulin resistance happens next. This eventually leads to adrenal resistance. Cortisol is the stress hormone that allows for fight or flight syndrome (life or death). This is the hormone that allows you to run away from a hungry lion fast and live. Evolution always makes sure cortisol production stays ready for action at the expense of the other hormones that also are made from the same precursors. That precursor is pregnenolone. Pregnenolone is made from cholesterol, and cholesterol is made from LDL. So anytime the body is stressed or inflamed, it up-regulates cholesterol production to make more lifesaving hormones. It requires T3 and vitamin A as cofactors to complete this step. Blocking cholesterol production will increase cellular stress. This is why biochemically, to me, no statin drugs have ever made any sense under any circumstance in medicine. Moreover, this is why there is a chronic association of cancer to low cholesterol levels in the literature. In times of infection or stress, LDL levels always need to rise to protect the cell.

A diet should provide a substrate of animal protein to sustain the hormone substrates. This is why a Paleo diet is ideal for hormone health because it provides ample substrate for all hormones. A Paleolithic diet is best for longevity in my opinion. It supports all of our hormones that are present in abundance in youth and health. Many other physicians, like Dr. Ron Rosedale, tell folks that protein stimulates the mTOR pathway which leads to death. The real issue I have there is that he gives no context to the statement. Here is my context. His belief is only true if the background cellular terroir has a high HS CRP back drop, and if your gut is leaky as shown by a low HDL level. If your HS CRP is treated aggressively with a Paleo diet, your HS CRP will be low and your HDL will be high, and then a moderate protein diet is protective. So I go hard after HS CRP in all of the patients I work with. It is a staple in my practice. Once solved, it is hard-core Paleolithic Robb Wolf style advice to affect the most important part of our biology.

HORMONES. So anytime cellular stress is high (high HS CRP), it also forces all the hormone backbone substrate called pregnenolone to be shunted to cortisol production. This is called pregnenolone steal syndrome. What exactly does this mean, Doc? DHEA, Androstenedione, Vitamin D, testosterone, estrogen, and aldosterone production all fall dramatically. These are all of the hormones that are made from a common precursor. Remember that chronic Leptin resistance leads to huge hypercortisolism all the time! NEVER FORGET THIS BIOLOGIC FACT! So that means we need to understand well what high cortisol does to the cellular terroir as well. (Levee 1)

This also means that Leptin resistance clinically will lead to low vitamin D levels. This completely explains the epidemic that John Cannell, MD is reporting about in the Vitamin D council newsletters. This in turn down regulates T regulator cells of the immune system, and it will decrease bone metabolism as well since vitamin D is a cofactor in bone metabolism. Since DHEA and Androstenedione are lowered too, the sex steroids are also lowered because they are made from DHEA and Andro. In younger humans this leads to early andropause, low libido, and early onset perimenopause any time stress is present. When this occurs in older humans, like postmenopausal women, it destroys libido and electrolyte balance (low aldosterone effects) and causes osteopenia and osteoporosis. Younger females lose control of fecundity, oocyte maturation, and get amenorrhea. Evolution does not favor these activities when your stress hormone is high. It favors survival over these other activities. This observation was mind shattering to my practice when I realized it. In men, longevity is tied to their optimized testosterone levels. Here is a link that confirms this. [http://www.nature.com/ijo/journal/v27/n7/fig\_tab/0802292f1.html](http://http://www.nature.com/ijo/journal/v27/n7/fig_tab/0802292f1.html)

The high cortisol also directly affects the Hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis too. Cortisol directly blocks 5′ deiodinase enzyme that converts T4 to T3 (this occurs in the liver). Cortisol Releasing Hormone (CRH) (seen elevated in high cortisol production states) directly blocks TSH. The implication is huge because Thyroid Stimulating Hormone (TSH) and T3, the active thyroid hormone, are inhibited quickly in this process. Immediately, any excess T4 is then shunted to reverse T3. Reverse T3 is a COMPETITIVE inhibitor to T3, the active thyroid hormone. This basically turns off the thyroid! (Youâ€™re welcome ladies.), This is a biological switch needed to shut metabolism off in starvation mode. This is precisely what happens in starvation or in anorexia. Once T3 is turned off, no fat burning can occur at the muscles with UCP3. Remember UCP3 activation requires T4, T3 and Leptin to be working well. With high cortisol it cannot. Shutting off these things could be good biologically, if youâ€™re badly starving or if youâ€™re morbidly obese. CRH also directly inhibits TSH. This is why TSH is a horrible marker for thyroid status. If you do not know the cortisol status you can infer zero information from a thyroid panel. This is reason number one for many thyroid misdiagnoses. The sad part is that most endocrinologists seem to have forgotten this vital biochemical fact. It is the source of most patient frustrations with their thyroid condition.

CRH directly blocks secretion of Growth Hormone secretion as well. This means you get sarcopenia and osteopenia together! Low GH levels (IGF1) increase your body fat, decrease your lean muscle mass and increase your osteopenia to great degree. It also causes the cardiac muscle to fail and decreases the stroke volume. Sarcopenia is the result of low IGF1 or GH level, and is a harbinger of ensuing death, especially with respect to the heart. There is now excellent data to support the use of GH and testosterone for cardiac health in aging because of these effects. High cortisol comes from stress. The causes of stress in humans are: psychological, traumatic, infectious, allergic, electromagnetic (EMF), xenobiotic and geopathic, as well as Leptin resistance and dysbiosis.

Key points about cortisol.

1\. Anything that causes a chronic elevation of cortisol causes chronic disease.

2\. Any chronic elevation of cortisol and insulin will lead to some kind of chronic disease and death.

3\. Signs and symptoms that you really have stress and a cortisol problem will cause most medicine docs to put you on one or more of these ten medications: 1. Statin 2. Premarin 3. Synthroid 4. Prilosec 5. Hydrocodone 6.Norvasc 7. glucophage/metaformin 8. Albuterol 9. Claritin 10. Prozac/sleeping pills.

# Thread: World's Oldest People
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/worlds-oldest-people.3333/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-08-29T19:15:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/worlds-oldest-people.3333/post-48112>

Besse Cooper of Monroe, GA, the world's oldest woman, just turned 116 this week. [http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2012/08/27/besse-cooper-worlds-oldest-living-woman\_n\_1833864.html](http://http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2012/08/27/besse-cooper-worlds-oldest-living-woman_n_1833864.html) Her secret to longevity: "I mind my own business and don't eat junk food."

Jiroemon Kimura of Japan, the worlds's oldest man, just turned 115 this week. [http://www.ibtimes.com/articles/331132/20120420/jiroemon-kimura-worlds-oldest-man-diet-life.htm](http://http://www.ibtimes.com/articles/331132/20120420/jiroemon-kimura-worlds-oldest-man-diet-life.htm) In honor of his birthday, Kimura divulged some of his secrets to a long life, which he says is attributed to eating small portions with meals three times a day of red bean cake and rice. For his last birthday, he dined on grilled fish, steamed rice and red beans, a Japanese tradition on special occasions.

"I don't know exactly...maybe it's all thanks to the sun above me," a modest Kimura told The Telegraph. "I am always looking up towards the sky, that is how I am."

Thoughts?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-29T20:33:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/worlds-oldest-people.3333/post-48142>

Instincts they follow......

### fitness@home — 2012-08-29T20:34:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/worlds-oldest-people.3333/post-48144>

Interesting articles. Thank for posting the links. The older I get, the more interested I am in extending my "shelf life".

Real food (not processed) is definitely a factor. I love how the old man acknowledges the power of the sun.

My great-grandmother lived to age 101. She had breast cancer twice while in her 70s and 80s. Things she had in common with them were eating real foods, smaller portion sizes with no snacking between meals, and spending time outside in the sun. She also read her bible every afternoon, canned/preserved her home grown fruits & veggies and drank catnip tea (dried the leaves herself).

Simpler way of life? She was always in bed at dusk and up at dawn.

### ealachan — 2012-08-29T20:36:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/worlds-oldest-people.3333/post-48145>

> "I mind my own business and don't eat junk food."

I love this! She sounds like someone I'd like to hang out with.

### kathylu — 2012-09-02T19:29:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/worlds-oldest-people.3333/post-48992>

OMG, I followed some of the links above and looked at the world's oldest people. They look terrible! Do you want to live to be 116 if you look like Besse Cooper? I only want extreme old age if I am functional and feeling good. Too much to hope for, or not?

### AKMan — 2012-09-02T20:32:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/worlds-oldest-people.3333/post-49001>

> **kathylu;49492 wrote:** OMG, I followed some of the links above and looked at the world's oldest people. They look terrible! Do you want to live to be 116 if you look like Besse Cooper? I only want extreme old age if I am functional and feeling good. Too much to hope for, or not?

I think this is the point of "Living an Optimized Life". Aging without the neolithic aging diseases. If it's possible to live to be 116 by accident, what will it be like for us who are trying hard to optimize our guts, diets, hormones, etc...

As to smoking...nicotine is a powerful anti-inflammatory. That's why quitting results in weight gain. It seems to me that all the harmful chemicals added to smoking products are what gets you, but the nicotine actually extends the misery with it's anti-inflammatory action.

I've often thought there was probably a dose of smoking that could be beneficial. Native Americans used it for health and ceremony--maybe they were on to something.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-02T20:34:39+0300

nice observation AKman.......

### AKMan — 2012-09-03T02:13:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/worlds-oldest-people.3333/post-49076>

Please don't take up smoking because of this!

[http://suite101.com/article/nicotine-antiinflammatory-h1n1-cure-a114895](http://http://suite101.com/article/nicotine-antiinflammatory-h1n1-cure-a114895)

Nicotine stimulates the cholinergic anti-inflammatory pathway. At the end of this pathway are immune cells that produce anti-inflammatory cytokines that block inflammation. Thus, nicotine, although one of the most addictive chemicals, can have beneficial effects on inflammatory diseases, such as arthritis, asthma, cancer, inflammatory bowel diseases and perhaps, H1N1.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC404586/](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC404586/)

Tobacco smoking suppresses the immune system, and smokers have a higher incidence of diseases, such as lung cancer, cardiovascular disease, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and respiratory infections (38). Interestingly, smokers have a lower incidence of some diseases, including ulcerative colitis, sarcoidosis, endometriosis, uterine fibroids, farmers' lung, pigeon breeders' disease, Parkinson's disease, and SjÃ¶gren's syndrome (38). Many of these diseases are inflammatory in nature or have an inflammatory component. In animal studies, NT, a major constituent of cigarette smoke, suppresses the immune system when provided by subcutaneously implanted miniosmotic pumps (38). NT may help in human cutaneous inflammation (19) and ulcerative colitis

# Thread: Leptin reset and raised blood pressure
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-raised-blood-pressure.3335/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### somewhatchubby — 2012-08-29T19:53:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-raised-blood-pressure.3335/post-48127>

Has anyone experienced raised blood pressure from doing paleo or the Leptin reset.

I managed to get my husband to do the reset with me and today after rectal endoscopy (because his iron has been found to be low)after a prostate flare up. Nothing was found although they want to do the tests from the other end now.!! What they did find was that his usually normal blood pressure had risen. Of course the stress of the procedure could be a contributory factor .

He is three weeks into the diet.I seem to remember someone else saying there blood pressure had risen and was wondering if it became lowered again.

Also before going on the diet he used to eat a lot of antacids and Ive read somewhere that antacids can interupt iron absorbtion.

Since cutting carbs (apart from a beer) he hasnt needed to take antacids.

### ealachan — 2012-08-29T20:15:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-raised-blood-pressure.3335/post-48133>

My blood pressure has improved on the reset, to the point where I'm working my way off of medication now.

Did they tell you to keep track of his blood pressure at home for a while? Seems like it would be pretty hard to deem someone as having chronic elevated blood pressure just from one reading which happened to be in a stressful situation. I have "White Coat Syndrome", wherein my blood pressure is always higher at the doctor's office because I find it stressful no matter how much I try to convince myself that I'm calm. This is why I have my own cuff at home and keep daily track of my blood pressure there. If my doctor went only by the readings she got in the office when I go in, I'd be on even more medications than just the one I take.

Also, tell him to knock it off with the beer. It's like drinking liquid bread. I had to learn this the hard way. I love beer, but it doesn't love me.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-29T20:15:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-raised-blood-pressure.3335/post-48134>

My blood pressure went down within the first 30 days of doing the Leptin Rx. I was on a HBP medicine and now have been off for 7 months.

### somewhatchubby — 2012-08-29T20:24:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-raised-blood-pressure.3335/post-48138>

Hi ealachan and patty cakes, blimey that was quick !Great to hear that both of you have been able to get off your meds. Hubby is 64 and isnt on any meds at all which is why its come as a surprise.

What worries me most is that once the docs start you never know where it will end. Although I'm a believer and I have told him about things I have read in 'Trick or Treat' by Barry Groves. However ,for him,in a docs surgery , is he likely to be swayed. ?

I have tried to get him off the beer ,trouble is if I push too hard he may give up. I figure giving up all else should help .He does like a drink at night and half a pint ,although I know its bad could be worse.

He has lost weight already.

So, we may just have to wait and see what happens.

Are BP cuffs very expensive ?

thanks for your responses.

### ealachan — 2012-08-29T20:29:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-raised-blood-pressure.3335/post-48141>

> **somewhatchubby;48589 wrote:** Hi ealachan and patty cakes, blimey that was quick !Great to hear that both of you have been able to get off your meds. Hubby is 64 and isnt on any meds at all which is why its come as a surprise.
>
> What worries me most is that once the docs start you never know where it will end. Although I'm a believer and I have told him about things I have read in 'Trick or Treat' by Barry Groves. However ,for him,in a docs surgery , is he likely to be swayed. ?

>
> So, we may just have to wait and see what happens.

>
> thanks for your responses.

Mine was $70, but I had to get a special sized cuff because I have extra-big upper arms. If you get one, make sure you measure your DH's upper arm and get the correct cuff size...it makes a difference.

Also, unless the doctor is talking about dragging your DH back in to put him on meds for the blood pressure, I wouldn't worry about it too much. Like I said - it's hard for them to claim he has a chronic problem from just one whacked-out reading. Blood pressure is not a static thing - it changes constantly all day long, depending on what you're doing.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-29T21:05:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-raised-blood-pressure.3335/post-48155>

I use a wrist cuff by Omron. I've heard people say the wrist cuffs are not as reliable but I've tested mine against the drug store ones and its within a couple of points so mine is great. It's small enough to carry it anywhere, if I need to.

### ealachan — 2012-08-29T22:29:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-raised-blood-pressure.3335/post-48177>

> **somewhatchubby;48618 wrote:** hi ealachan, thanks for that. I will look into it.
>
> His BP was 165 /? he cant remember the other reading. I personally think this has been coming on for a year or more. He ate alot of sweet stuff, lots of spuds,bread , cakes ,wine, biscuits potato crisps. just thinking about it turns me queazy. So you see how I have to be clever about how I proceed.?
>
> I drink water but he wont ,he still needs some sweetness in there. I ponder over what I could get/make him to drink in the evening. Any ideas ?

It's not going to sound fun, but in my opinion the easiest way to make the change needed is to do it like ripping off a band-aid: quickly and decisively. It will hurt (figuratively) for a few days, but it's far easier on the system than trying to "ease" into it and prolonging the mental pain...see what I mean?

I couldn't get my husband on board with the new way of eating until he was diagnosed with T2D. At that point, I had to sit him down and say "look, all these things that you're eating that you think are not so bad because you're only eating a little of them...they all add up, big-time. If you keep eating them, they will kill you. I don't want you to suffer like others in your family are doing from diabetic complications. This new way of eating will help you." He agreed, and while he was a bit wibbly over losing his beer and soda and sweets for a few days, he started to feel better almost immediately, and now we don't usually have an issue.

# Thread: Estimated macronutrient and fatty acid intakes from an East African Paleolithic diet
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/estimated-macronutrient-and-fatty-acid-intakes-from-an-east-african-paleolithic-diet.3336/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-08-29T20:38:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/estimated-macronutrient-and-fatty-acid-intakes-from-an-east-african-paleolithic-diet.3336/post-48146>

This is interesting. This is an article to which both Remko S. Kuipers and Loren Cordain contributed.

[Estimated macronutrient and fatty acid intakes from an East African Paleolithic diet](http://http://journals.cambridge.org/download.php?file=%2F40019_F393045E162BC03BD0AFF6AC26AB2A7A_journals__BJN_BJN104_11_S0007114510002679a.pdf&cover=Y&code=cce1b7f1e41e422ed990e75cf703ee45)

I'd seen the [previous article from Cordain et al. from 2000](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/10702160), -- it's often cited in the Paleosphere -- but this is ten years on.

You have to wonder how accurate a picture you're getting with all the assumptions that are imbedded in these models, but they are complex and deeply impressive models. 172 references for the paper.

# Thread: How much protein?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-protein.3338/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 30
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-08-29T21:52:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-protein.3338/post-48164>

In reading Dr. Rosedale's site I see he recommends eating 1 gram/day per kg of body weight for protein. I eat MUCH more than that. In fact, my breakfast alone is pretty darn close! I know Dr. Kruse has never given specific guidelines for macronutrients other than for BAB and the general low carb guidelines, so this now has my curiosity piqued.

What do you all think of the 1 gram protein suggestion?

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-29T22:14:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-protein.3338/post-48173>

I asked him about that once and he said a great white shark doesn't count protein..... I'd not eat turkey breast all day but fatty fish etc.... I think that's pretty safe.

### Michael — 2012-08-29T22:32:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-protein.3338/post-48178>

I think Rosedale is currently saying 1 gram per day of _ideal bodyweight _minus ten percent. So a bit less than that on two counts. (I'd think the 10% is a rough way of getting a figure to correspond to lean_ bodymass.)

Some other low-carb advocates are around this figure. Kwasniewski says to take your height in centimeters, take off a hundred, and that's your allowance in grams of protein. That would give you something close to Rosedale's figure I guess.

Robert Atkins seems originally to have been fairly high-protein, and Kwasniewski corresponded with him on it:

[http://www.cybernaut.com.au/optimal\_nutrition/information/articles/optimal\_nutrition\_vs\_atkins.pdf](http://http://www.cybernaut.com.au/optimal_nutrition/information/articles/optimal_nutrition_vs_atkins.pdf)

I think Volek and Phinney, "modern Atkins", if you like, are saying a little more than Rosedale - 1.5 to 2 grams of protein per kg of lean_ bodymass.

Figures in the paleo world are higher. The recent paper that Cordain contributed to, that I inked below, estimates an intake of 25-29% protein. The estimated daily energy expenditure of a hunter-gatherer in the same paper is given as 12500KJ. That would be 2985 kcal. So up to 866 kcal as protein, which equals up to about 215 grams of protein a day.

If a man's lean bodymass were 150 lb - 68 kg - then I make that 3 g per kg of lean bodymass.

I guess Rosedale would probably say he wasn't trying to replicate how paleolithic humans ate but aiming to down-regulate the mTOR pathway or something.

And I guess Volek and Phinney would probably say that what they were interested in was what would get the subject into ketosis - or keep him out of it. Or maybe what would achieve nitrogen balance.

> What do you all think of the 1 gram protein suggestion?

I don't know. I know what some well-qualified people are saying and there's no agreement. What's anyone to think?

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-29T22:38:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-protein.3338/post-48179>

I shoot for at least what my lean body weight should be given my height ~ 125g/day I'm probably pretty close to that... considering I'm still averaging btwn 45-60g of protein for BAB depending on what I eat (salmon burger, 3eggs w/ guacamole & salsa ~ 60g), then there's lunch (which was ~20g of protein - smoked oysters, and upgraded Whey HWC shake and sauerkraut), dinner tonight will be pork chops, sauerkraut and apples (6oz of pork chops is about 16g -I'll probably eat 2 chops w/dinner)

so today that puts me at about 112g protein(assuming I eat what I think I'm gonna eat for dinner)...w/a small amt of carbs, the rest is FAT!

FWIW I do not want to weigh 125lbs I'd be too effing skinny... I think when I get to ideal body comp - I'll be some where btwn 130-145

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-30T01:11:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-protein.3338/post-48220>

Here is the key most I guess dont get. The high protein in the Leptin Rx is used to stop the cravings. Once that is solved you can move to higher fat and moderate protein......but I still disagree with Ron that protein is bad. He still thinks CROn is best but today in Nature journal an article came out and said CRON does work in rhesus monkey's, a primate. On PH Ron argued with me that the primates would be the same as us.......well guess who was right. I told ROn that just because CRON works in other species it is not shown to be correct in primates or humans......and the BG series is showing just how different primates and humans are. You can generalize via species. It is the same argument I have with obesity researchers who claim rat data is the same as humans......no it is not. It is interesting to draw a hypothesis but their biochemistry is way different than ours. That is how we wound up with food reward theory.

They end up with something like 30% protein, 30% fat, and 40% carbohydrate. (That sounds like the Zone!) And the other thing is that those reconstructed diets aren't seasonal.

### Butters — 2016-08-25T14:36:58+0300

### Jack Kruse — 2016-08-25T15:41:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-protein.3338/post-200182>

Depends upon your light mismatch and % heteroplasmy rate at present moment. The key is if you cannot fat burn you need most protein and fat intake to keep TCA cycling low. When mitochondria are damaged for any physiologic or environmental reason they cannot utilized beta oxidation to burn fat so they have to revert to the older evolutionary pathways in the TCA cycle. This older pathway is OK for simple organism like Bacteria and Archea but it is not good for chronic use in complex eukaryotic cells. If you rely on the TCA cycle for too long it will stimulate cell growth to try to offset the energy deficit. The reason for this is that simple life ability to make energy is limited by the geometry of their cell membranes. So they have to lean heavy on the TCA cycle to make up the energy deficit. When you have no ability to fat burn you become more bacteria like in energy generation because our mitochondria reverts to its ancient bacterial past. This is why the Warburg metabolism occurs. This amount of energy does not allow for FULL CELL function and this is why their disease phenotype manifest because the change in the mitochondria forces epigenetic changes in the nuclear genome to help offset the energy loss.

It is not that hard to see when you understand Dr. Doug Wallace's work. This is why patients with high % heteroplasmy crave carbohydrates and love blue light devices. You need to not allow these things to happen in your local environment because it deepens the "energy deficit" for these people to crawl out of. These two environmental ACTIONS act in unison to keep their gut microbiome simplified and allow for chronic rapid TCA cycling to make energy from carbohydrates. The problem is the longer they stay in this cycle the deeper the mitochondrial defects get the worse their mitochondria get in many tissues. This is why obesity leads to so many other diseases as more mitochondria are mowed down. Fixing neolithic disease is a mitochondrial Rx to regain fat burning, nothing else and nothing more. Blood glutamate and glucose are always raised in these people because glutamate and glucose are part of the TCA being overused because they cannot access beta oxidation of fats. The proof: Studies have shown that increased oxygen consumption and N-acetylglutamate and urea synthesis are coupled to agmatine-induced stimulation of mitochondrial fatty acid oxidation. IMPLICATIONS: So if you cannot use beta oxidation it means glutamate and glucose will rise in the blood plasma. This explains many disease because it is the common pathways seen in altered mitochondrial as laid out by Dr. Doug Wallace's 40 years of work in mitochondrial medicine. [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16452488](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16452488)

# Thread: Glucose Tolerance test and meter issues
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-tolerance-test-and-meter-issues.3339/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-08-29T23:51:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-tolerance-test-and-meter-issues.3339/post-48195>

I did a 2 hour oral glucose tolerance test last week, hoping to fail and convince my endocrinologist to give me cycloset. I didn't fail enough for that purpose, unfortunately. My results were:

A1C 5.1%

Fasting Glucose: 73

Fasting Insulin less than 2

1 hour: 134

2 hour: 80

I brought my meter with me, and had a similar reading at the 1 hour (130), but got 136 for the 2 hour, which is pretty different from 80. I've never seen my FBG as low as 73, or 70anything, or 80anything on that meter. I usually see a FBG of 89-93. Are the meters really that unreliable?

This is my second meter. I bought one last summer, and got am readings consistently in the 120s. I thought I had developped late onset type 1 diabetes. Did a serum test and got lower 80s as the result.

Are some meters more reliable than others?

I got home from the test 30minutes later, retested and got a reading of 84, then 30 minutes later started feeling pretty bad (low blood sugar feeling), and got a reading of 56. So the meter isn't completely out to lunch, but it doesn't seem like something reliable to work with.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-30T00:15:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-tolerance-test-and-meter-issues.3339/post-48204>

I really don't know about meters. Which one do you have? I occasionally use the True Test and it seems fairly i line with my fasting BG.

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-30T00:22:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-tolerance-test-and-meter-issues.3339/post-48205>

I have the Walmart one - ReliOn

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-30T00:27:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-tolerance-test-and-meter-issues.3339/post-48206>

Hopefully others will chime in and help. The ReliOn is different although I think Walgreens and CVS sell one like the one I have under a different name.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-02T02:55:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-tolerance-test-and-meter-issues.3339/post-48840>

I have had very bad luck with ReliOn meters! The numbers jump all over the damn place.

Try taking several readings in a row, from the same prick, one right after the other. You will be amazed at the variance you get. Take at least 5.

I got TrueTrack meter instead, and I'm quite happy with it. The TrueTrack has 4 electrodes in the strip, unlike the others, and it takes a full 10 seconds to analyze the sample. I took several readings in a row, and I still see variance, but it's considerably less. (Often, two readings in a row are within a few points of each other.)

The nominal accuracy of all of these meters is +/- 20% So if your actual BG is 100, you could get any random number between 80 and 120. That's not very useful! So if I want a really accurate reading, or I have reason to think my BG is out of whack, I take at least 3 readings and average them. Never trust a single reading!

### mambo — 2012-09-03T09:17:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-tolerance-test-and-meter-issues.3339/post-49149>

ReliOn is generally reliable but last year they had some bad batches of strips which they recalled. The most reliable meter on the market is thought to be Roche's Aviva. Dr. Bernstein's office recommends it.

> **bigknitwit;48648 wrote:** So the meter isn't completely out to lunch, but it doesn't seem like something reliable to work with.

### mambo — 2012-09-03T09:25:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-tolerance-test-and-meter-issues.3339/post-49151>

Your results look good for the most part, especially your fasting insulin. Now, what's a bit confounding is your 2h post-prandial number. Assuming you took the big fast-acting glucose carb they administer, it shouldn't really be at 136, right? But then your BG control really isn't impaired since your A1c is 5.1 My interpretation: you probably have something called "biphasic" glucose curve, where BG rises again (but not as much as the first spike) at the 1.5-2.5 hour mark. In other words, you probably dipped below 130 between 1.5 to 2 but your 2h reading caught the rebound. It's normal and quite a bit of people have this: in fact, many people with biphasic (rather than monophasic) BG readings seem to have better BG control overall (lower A1c, etc.) and become diabetic at a lower rate. You can confirm by measuring your BG at 15 minute intervals after ingesting 75g carbs. I did and confirmed through several trials that I indeed have a "second BG spike" -- though a lot smaller spike than the first spike. Anyway, that's called geeking out.

> **bigknitwit;48648 wrote:** My results were:
>
>
>
> A1C 5.1%
>
> Fasting Glucose: 73
>
> Fasting Insulin less than 2
>
> 1 hour: 134
>
> 2 hour: 80

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-03T19:21:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-tolerance-test-and-meter-issues.3339/post-49197>

Yuck - 20 off is material! I wonder if my bg isnt as awesome as i think!

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-03T20:42:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-tolerance-test-and-meter-issues.3339/post-49205>

> **Cavemam;49700 wrote:** Yuck - 20 off is material! I wonder if my bg isnt as awesome as i think!

20 off is the biggest error you \*could\* get but you rarely do. You can find out how accurate your own meter tends to be by retesting a few times and comparing numbers. That's why they tell you in the instructions to re-test before freaking out if you get a result that appears off. I have a Bayer Contour and if I do multiple tests they are usually within a few points. Have I had a 10 point difference happen... yep. It happens. I retest if it seems weird.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-03T21:00:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-tolerance-test-and-meter-issues.3339/post-49207>

One of the readings was really off though. My 2 hours blood level measured 80, but on the meter it measured 136. Unfortunately I didn't take the fasting on the meter for another data point. My FBG's are consistenly in the high 80s, low 90s on that meter, but now I'm wondering since here my fasting was 73. Maybe I'm not as badly off as I thought... Very annoying to have these discrepancies!

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-03T21:18:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-tolerance-test-and-meter-issues.3339/post-49211>

> **bigknitwit;49710 wrote:** One of the readings was really off though. My 2 hours blood level measured 80, but on the meter it measured 136. Unfortunately I didn't take the fasting on the meter for another data point. My FBG's are consistenly in the high 80s, low 90s on that meter, but now I'm wondering since here my fasting was 73. Maybe I'm not as badly off as I thought... Very annoying to have these discrepancies!

You can buy a test solution to test your meter's accuracy. If I got a reading like that I'd retest but I'm assuming you didn't get the lab results until later.

# Thread: Chocolate Prevents Strokes, Study finds
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate-prevents-strokes-study-finds.3340/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-08-30T00:10:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate-prevents-strokes-study-finds.3340/post-48199>

As if we need more reasons to like chocolate!

[http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-08-chocolate-sweet-method-men.html](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-08-chocolate-sweet-method-men.html)

In a larger analysis of five studies that included 4,260 stroke cases, the risk of stroke for individuals in the highest category of chocolate consumption was 19 percent lower compared to non-chocolate consumers. For every increase in chocolate consumption of 50 grams per week, or about a quarter cup of chocolate chips, the risk of stroke decreased by about 14 percent.

"The beneficial effect of chocolate consumption on stroke may be related to the flavonoids in chocolate. Flavonoids appear to be protective against cardiovascular disease through antioxidant, anti-clotting and anti-inflammatory properties. It's also possible that flavonoids in chocolate may decrease blood concentrations of bad cholesterol and reduce blood pressure," said Larsson.

"Interestingly, dark chocolate has previously been associated with heart health benefits, but about 90 percent of the chocolate intake in Sweden, including what was consumed during our study, is milk chocolate," Larsson added.

# Thread: Sous Vide and endocrine distrupting

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-30T01:53:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sous-vide-and-endocrine-distrupting.3343/post-48235>

So, I keep coming back to Sous Vide cooking as a way to "gently" cook things, as right now I grill just about anything moving in my path.

I've not tried Sous Vide because the thought of cooking something in the plastic bags seems like a bad idea. But is it worse than grilling? Of course, I'll probably grill finish my Sous Vide steaks - but hey, it could be a great way to cook fish.

I have a friend with one just sitting unused, and I'm thinking of borrowing. Any wisdom out there on the wisdom of eating food heated in the vacuum seal bags?

### Lyndra — 2012-08-30T02:15:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sous-vide-and-endocrine-distrupting.3343/post-48238>

No knowledge here but I'm distrustful when I hear, "don't worry, none of the bad chemicals will leach out." How about braising if you want to gently cook? Or obviously, a slow cooker. Or scrape off the brown bits after grilling. Later Paleo woman had fire but not plastic and BPA.

I'm transitioning away from plastic storage containers to glass and prefer aluminum foil over plastic wrap. But that's just me.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-30T02:33:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sous-vide-and-endocrine-distrupting.3343/post-48241>

I'm really curious about this too. DH offered to make me a Sous Vide about a year ago after we chatted with a waiter who said he made one. DH is really good with his hands! Anyway, I declined the wonderful offer because I didn't like the idea of plastic heated up and the xenoestrogens coming out. Don't need any more estrogen no matter where it's coming from.

### janagram — 2012-08-30T02:51:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sous-vide-and-endocrine-distrupting.3343/post-48253>

I do my grilled fish wrapped in parchment paper then wrapped in foil....comes out awesome!

### nonchalant — 2012-08-30T02:56:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sous-vide-and-endocrine-distrupting.3343/post-48256>

I would really like to get one of these, but I've been concerned, yes. The Sous Vide Supreme cooks food unlike anything else.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-30T03:01:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sous-vide-and-endocrine-distrupting.3343/post-48259>

I voiced concerned over the phythates in the plastic but they have say no?

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-30T03:55:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sous-vide-and-endocrine-distrupting.3343/post-48271>

IMO just say no and stick to traditional slow cooking methods or eat it raw

### kathylu — 2012-08-30T04:20:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sous-vide-and-endocrine-distrupting.3343/post-48281>

The safer option would be food grade silicone bags. They don't leach anything, but are rather expensive. Reusable though. Amazon sells them. I agree that sous vide is a lovely way to prepare food, but the plastic bags may be a concern.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-30T05:40:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.3347/post-48303>

I used to not like salmon very much.....until I cooked it in bacon fat!! Now THAT's what I have for breakfast 4-6 times a week with some sauteed kale.

### johnnyb — 2012-08-30T06:28:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.3347/post-48307>

My first question for eating is which meal will I be having seafood. For the seafood meal I try to avoid saturated fat. That usually means using bacon fat. So to breakfast. Weekday breakfast needs a certain amount of convenience. I prep stuff a head of time. I par cook 2 lbs of bacon at a time. That gives me a supply of bacon fat for seafood and vegetables. Finishing the slices leaves enough fat in the pan to cook eggs. I like to have sautÃ©ed onions and mushrooms, grounbeef made into taco meat, ready to go. Sausage often ready to go but not so much recently. So its 3-4 eggs made with the other stuff.

My meals keep evolving. I recently stopped eating night shades. I never knew how many tomatoes and bell peppers I ate until I stopped. So no more salsa or sautÃ©ed peppers. With the increase seafood consumption salmon patties have been breakfast too. Those little pre-cooked shrimp are pretty tasty with avocado in an omelette. I saw recipe for making shrimp sausage. They were served like a hot dog, but I plan to make some patty shaped ones to have in the freezer for any meal. Having having reasonable choices ready to go makes eating better way easier.

### BJK77 — 2012-08-30T15:21:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.3347/post-48342>

Since starting to eat more epi-paleo, I've gotten into a good routine with having my BAB my only non-seafood meal. I generally have some kind of red meat (chuck roast, burger, etc) with offal (heart or liver). I add a ton of CO and tallow since this is the only meal where I eat saturated fats. I always add a ton of sea veggies to this meal too and usually some mushrooms and greens like kale.

Both lunch and dinner are now always seafood for me. I do anything from sashimi to broiled salmon or scallops, shrimp, clams, oysters - whatever I'm in the mood for that day! I almost always have an avocado with lunch and dinner too along with bacon and a little more sea veg.

My only concern is that I just feel like I'm not getting enough fat. The last few weeks I've been starving all the time and just feel like I could eat and eat and eat! I'm hoping I just need to adjust to the new influx of nutrients and things will settle down soon.

### Stavity — 2012-08-30T15:25:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.3347/post-48343>

I eat seafood for breakfast and for dinner. I feel and sleep so much better now. I have decided to eat a rare steak once a week. I was advised by my doctor to eat this way over a year ago, but didn't actually start until Dr Kruse started hinting about epi paleo. I make a lot of ceviches, and salmon tartares ahead for the days I don't have time to cook. This morning it's raw oysters and cold cooked shrimp. I cannot believe the change in me in just two months. My hair stylist said she couldn't get over how shiny and healthy my hair is and I am getting compliments about how healthy I look. I still have a long long way to go, and much to improve, but can't see going back to the way I was eating.

### Endless — 2012-08-30T17:14:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.3347/post-48362>

Stavity, can you share your salmon tartare recipe? Do you use fresh or frozen?

### nonchalant — 2012-08-30T17:15:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.3347/post-48364>

Sem, you can also look at the member journal entries. Some of them list what they eat for each meal.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-30T18:02:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.3347/post-48381>

Here is my favorite salmon tartar recipe that I have used for years.

Salmon Tartar

3/4 # Salmon cut into 1/4" dice

1/4 cup capers, drained but not rinsed

1/4 cup green onions, finely chopped (both white and green parts)

2 Tbsp Dijon mustard (can use a little less)

2 tsp pureed Chipotles (come in a little can in Mex food section- I just chop mine)

2 Tbsp Olive Oil

3 Tbsp Cilantro chopped

Mix salmon, capers and onions.

Combine mustard and chipoltles. Drizzle mixture over salmon.

Drizzle olive oil and mix thoroughly.

Add cilantro and mix thoroughly

It should be refrigerated for a couple of hours if you have time.

### Stavity — 2012-08-30T18:05:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.3347/post-48382>

> **Endless;48833 wrote:** Stavity, can you share your salmon tartare recipe? Do you use fresh or frozen?

I use frozen, but I have used fresh from WF. The wild caught from Alaska frozen, tastes the best to me, but it is from an honest source.

Here are two of the recipes I love: [http://www.epicurious.com/recipes/food/views/Dilled-Salmon-Tartare-on-Whole-Grain-Bread-236498](http://http://www.epicurious.com/recipes/food/views/Dilled-Salmon-Tartare-on-Whole-Grain-Bread-236498), [http://www.epicurious.com/recipes/food/views/Salmon-Tartare-365150](http://http://www.epicurious.com/recipes/food/views/Salmon-Tartare-365150). I make my own mayo for the one and use macedamia for the other in place of the oil that is in the recipes. I also play around with different veggies, one of my favorite is to use fresh cucumbers right now since they are in season at my local market.

Tartare is mild tasting. It's when salmon is cooked to much that makes it strong. I prefer it raw for sure.

### Endless — 2012-08-30T18:44:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.3347/post-48394>

### vkiernan — 2012-08-31T05:01:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.3347/post-48515>

# Thread: Stroke and the Epi Paleo Rx.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stroke-and-the-epi-paleo-rx.3349/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-30T05:21:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stroke-and-the-epi-paleo-rx.3349/post-48292>

Impact of polyunsaturated fatty acid consumption prior to ischemic stroke

Acta Neurologica Scandinavica, 07/02/2012 Clinical Article

Mishina M et al. â€“ High eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA)/arachidonic acid (AA) ratio was associated with good outcome in ischemic stroke. The paper suggests that prestroke dietary habits affect the severity in patients with ischemic stroke.

Methods

The authors studied 75 patients with lacunar infarction (LI; n=25), atherothrombotic infarction (AT; n=32), and cardiogenic embolism (CE; n=18).

The patients underwent blood examinations in a fasting state next morning of hospitalization, including examination of low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL), high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL), triglyceride (TG), blood glucose, hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c), uric acid, and fatty acid fractions of EPA, DHA, dihomo-γ-linolenic acid (DGLA), and arachidonic acid (AA).

They used the modified Rankin Scale (mRS) to assess clinical severity at discharge.

Results

There was no significant difference in the EPA/AA and DHA/AA ratio among the three stroke subgroups, although the DGLA/AA ratio was significantly higher in patients with LI than in patients with CE.

Considering the confounding factors, the mRS was negatively correlated with EPA/AA and positively correlated with age, DHA/AA, and blood glucose

Read more: [http://www.mdlinx.com/neurology/news-article.cfm/4107572/stroke#ixzz24zZQru4Y](http://http://www.mdlinx.com/neurology/news-article.cfm/4107572/stroke#ixzz24zZQru4Y)

# Thread: Bone collectors get it wrong about birth?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-collectors-get-it-wrong-about-birth.3352/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TribalSpice — 2012-08-30T09:01:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-collectors-get-it-wrong-about-birth.3352/post-48314>

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/08/120827152037.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/08/120827152037.htm)

I am on my iWank, so I can't copy and paste, but the general thought is we are born prematurely due to metabolism, not because of defective pelvises. ( Pelvis open an enormous amount in birth and babies heads mould)

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-30T14:35:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-collectors-get-it-wrong-about-birth.3352/post-48334>

I wonder what Ina May would say about this???

Birth has become a small obsession for me as I had two distinctly different births - both were intended to be natural w/o medical interference but required it any way. I now sit back and wonder at how my own birth (emergency C-sec due to loss of fetal hb -my moms OB was attempting to deliver me breach) impacted my ability to birth trusting nature.

Dd was 7lbs 6oz total labor was 51 hrs (39 of which was natural) w/a vaginal delivery

Ds labor was great until about hr 7 when I had an urge to push but knew that it wasn't time -spent the next 6 hrs trying labor w/I pushing until my BP got too high and I had to ask for a transfer intuitively I knew something was wrong. Ds was trying come down like superman and his elbows got hung up on my pelvis. If that hadn't happened I would have successfully delivered a 9lb 5oz baby (how much of that weight was natural and how much was influenced by epidural, pit, & fluids) vaginally. Instead he was a C-sect baby.

Physically he could have passed through the birth canal as my maternal grand mother gave birth naturally to several 9-11 lb babies

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-30T15:01:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-collectors-get-it-wrong-about-birth.3352/post-48340>

Very interesting read, now I'm wondering about what each of children's birth tells me about my own metabolism at the time. I also wonder how the time of year (circadian cycles) affect gestation.

1st 3 weeks early 8 pounds 6 ounces (hospital, epidural) June, effortless weight loss

2nd 1 day late 8 pounds 8 ounces (home) June, effortless weight loss

3rd 10 days early 7 pounds 14 ounces (home) May, effortless weight loss

4th on time 9 pounds 6 ounces (home) December, no weight loss

The fourth was definitely harder to push out, she took 45 minutes, whereas the other 2 homebirths were 10-15 minutes of pushing. The hospital delivery was a nightmare. That's why I never repeated it.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-08-30T23:53:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-collectors-get-it-wrong-about-birth.3352/post-48445>

This makes such sense to me. It's never made sense that we would evolve (especially around something critical like birth!) in a way that compromised our development. Thanks for posting!!

### TribalSpice — 2012-09-06T03:48:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-collectors-get-it-wrong-about-birth.3352/post-49663>

It is interesting I have a winter baby and 2 summer babies and all of them I carried to just under 42 weeks. One thing I have observed though is women who give birth consistently "early", so before 36weeks, to healthy babies are often women who's family come from countries near the equator, Brazil, Indonesia, Egypt and clients that have carried babies longest were those from much colder climates, including a Russian woman who gestated to 44.5weeks.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-06T04:54:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-collectors-get-it-wrong-about-birth.3352/post-49685>

the colder the high the fasting blood sugar. Think back to the CPC evolutionary friend or foe blog.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-06T15:08:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-collectors-get-it-wrong-about-birth.3352/post-49724>

ok Jack... now you've got me thinking.... of a question for ask Jack......

### TribalSpice — 2012-09-07T02:29:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-collectors-get-it-wrong-about-birth.3352/post-49887>

Jack, I love your work!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-07T03:27:53+0300

### nonchalant — 2012-09-07T04:01:19+0300

And if the body does allow the pregnancy, there may be a good chance for problems, if DHA is scarce. Well, a certainty for problems.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-08T06:56:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-collectors-get-it-wrong-about-birth.3352/post-50201>

I wonder, does this also imply something about the future health of the mother. In other words, if she has sufficient energy and DHA on board to get pregnant and carry a healthy child to term, but not actually enough to support both her and the fetus, then pregnancy will create the next generation but potentially compromise mother's health. I ask because I see myself in this scenario. My health really declined after my second child was born. Yet I got pregnant very easily both times and breastfed both children for 3 years. Since nature designs pregnancy to take everything from mum and put it into baby's growth, if mum isn't optimal, her own health will then suffer. Yes?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-08T15:58:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-collectors-get-it-wrong-about-birth.3352/post-50243>

you my dear are going to love the next few blog posts with that question.......i think youre already feeling that answer with your intuition.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-08T19:59:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-collectors-get-it-wrong-about-birth.3352/post-50283>

This describes me pretty well too. No problems getting pregnant any of the 4 times. Easy pregnancies, and each child breastfed from 2-3 years, with pretty exclusive nursing until 18ish months (they didn't want to eat much until then). I also tandem nursed #1 and #2... My health started going downhill after baby #4. Everyone says it's because I was "older," (I was 33 when she was born), but I don't believe it for a minute. And then, when she was 18 months old (still nursing a ton), and I got really sick I lost a ton of weight very quickly. Then came the elimination diet to figure out her food sensitivities.... And then I really, really crashed and burned... That was 2 years ago and I'm still struggling to figure it out, let alone fix myself.

> **KiwiLauren;50750 wrote:** I wonder, does this also imply something about the future health of the mother. In other words, if she has sufficient energy and DHA on board to get pregnant and carry a healthy child to term, but not actually enough to support both her and the fetus, then pregnancy will create the next generation but potentially compromise mother's health. I ask because I see myself in this scenario. My health really declined after my second child was born. Yet I got pregnant very easily both times and breastfed both children for 3 years. Since nature designs pregnancy to take everything from mum and put it into baby's growth, if mum isn't optimal, her own health will then suffer. Yes?

Ive slept badly the last couple of nights due to my heart beating really hard ,making me feel quite funny.

I was looking forward to good sleep, had worn my orange specs and not eaten after 5 pm so was not amused to be still awake after and hour in bed.

### somewhatchubby — 2012-08-30T19:59:06+0300

Destiny, yes, I have noticed somethings already. My arms have been itching too. I have caught some insect bites just recently but I think there is something else goin on too.

Gladina, thanks ,you may well be close. I have wondered about thyroid ,i tracked my basal temperature first thing in the morning and am regularly 36.1 /36.2 .My thermometer is a few years old so need to get a new one. So, although that temp. does seem low I dont seem to have a lot of the obvious symptoms ,my hair is really thick and glossy with hardly any grey, my eyebrows are good.I dont have cold hands and feet.

What I do have is anxiety ,its been a stressful year(,lost several family members ,business worries) ,intermittant palpitations which I attributed to meno and my breasts do have fibrous areas but are no longer tender like before menopause.I need to lose about 14lbs of fat but its proving very tenacious. So, I seem to be a mixed bag. I think I will take myself off to the docs but I dont know how far I'll get.The UK dosnt seem to do it the same way as the US. Still, I'll withold judgement until I see what happens. As I sit here now,I have this full and slightly beating sensation below the ribs and it restricts my appetite as I feel full.

Hey ho , what it is to get old!

However, the noon cortisol is low, any idea how to bring it a bit up? Still waiting on additional labs .....

Salivary Test Units Range

Cortisol Morning (saliva) **7 ng/ml 3.7-9.5

Cortisol Noon (saliva) **1 ng/ml 1.2-3.0

B12 1500

folate 18.8

iron 147

saturation 38%

LabCorp have variety of tests containing same names.

Without specifying test number and CPT Code

you have high chance on getting something else in return.

Cortisol, AM-Test Number: 104018 CPT Code: 82533

### mambo — 2012-09-03T09:43:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/salivary-cortisol.3354/post-49152>

I would note that his 147 Ferritin is a bit high -- perhaps RBCs not turning over quickly. I believe HS CRP is only for cardiac. His BG control is good and fasting insulin is excellent. Given this, could you really conclude that he's hypothyroid? His FT3 is just a bit low. Unless he has symptoms, he may not be hypothyroid. His problem seems to be really low T. Assuming that his TG is overstated because of whatever medication he's taking for Crohn.

### mambo — 2012-09-03T19:03:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/salivary-cortisol.3354/post-49192>

I said his insulin is excellent, his BG control is good. I used to think that 4.5 was the optimal A1c. I no longer believe that. Most healthy people are actually closer to 5 than 4.5. 4.5 is really for T1 diabetics on a ketogenic diet. Healthy peopel eating moderate carbs should have A1c around 5.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-30T18:39:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-weird-lab-results.3358/post-48393>

ok, so got back a few lab things today - and interpreting this stuff is all running together. Comments? Help?

I'm battling absorption issues due to gastric bypass.

Vit-D is 21 with a range of 30-150. This one annoys the poop out of me. I've spent the last two months in the sun in addition to supplimenting to the tune of 20K-40K iu a day.

Any suggestions on other ways to up this?

Testosterone - I may be a man!

Free - 1.4 with a range of 0.2-2.0

Total - 152 with a range of 7-46. . . is this is as bad as it sounds? I am having hair loss, but I chalked it up to the

25 lbd weight loss with HCG, but perhaps it's the Testosterone.

On the anti-thyroid ABS

Thyr Perox AB - 11.8, with a range of 0-35

Thyroglob AB -

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-30T18:56:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-weird-lab-results.3358/post-48396>

Wow that testosterone is high! Maybe PCOS?

On the D, I am wondering if you need a different brand. Are you taking oil based or pills or what? Just thinking out loud here but maybe you need a different not just brand but makeup of the D itself. I think some absorb better than others....oil based.

OK, on the thyroid antibodies there are two schools of thought: 1) antibodies within range means no Hashis, 2) any antibodies means something is haywire and you should take the precautions as if you have Hashis. As someone who has Hashis I come from the second school of thought. I wish I had known sooner.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-30T19:05:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-weird-lab-results.3358/post-48397>

Thanks PK!

On Vit-D, taking Carlsons oil based. I will switch that up - certainly couldn't get worse, this is same level I had back in March before sun and supplements.

On Testosterone - I have been taking 25-50 mgs of the DHEA, which I will cease immediately. I see no reason to stop 7-Keto tho right? I've only been doing that about 3 weeks prior to test - seems weird. Testosterone wasn't reported on any prior bloodwork so I have nothing to compare it with.

On Antibodies - The Thyroglob AB, just gives "

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-30T19:09:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-weird-lab-results.3358/post-48399>

PCOS.... w/those T levels... are you dominate for E2? (what's your Pg:E2 ratio look like)

FWIW PCOS can also start the cascade w/in the Thyroid for Hashis

looking at your Vit D3 - its the final hormone that's made in the hormone cascade. so what the low level tells me is you've got some hormone converstion problems - think pregnenolone steal (need labs to verify - VAP, full hormone pannel etc)

so what's happening is what hormones are shunting away from making Pg, and going to cortisol, Testosterone, and most likely going to E2 as well... how are your FBG levels? Additionally what does your DHEA-S look like?

PCOS is a wicked thing - and it can kick off other diseases... its a hormone disruption (high T and E2), insulin resistance, leptin resistance, - the combination of which will turn off your thyroid (think idling doing nothing)

go back and read my blog - specifically June2012, and July 2012 entries [http://sunvalleysoul.blogspot.com](http://http://sunvalleysoul.blogspot.com) or [http://sunvalleysoul.wordpress.com/2012/06/20/my-journey-to-optimal-health/](http://http://sunvalleysoul.wordpress.com/2012/06/20/my-journey-to-optimal-health/) (pick which ever blog platform you prefer)

I'm getting ready to give an updated - based upon tracking FBG for the past month - and discovering that my FBG Spikes the last 5days of my cycle and into TOTM - b/c of decrease of Pg.

STTM - has an explaination of how PCOS can induce hashi's [http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/2012/05/30/hypothyroidism-insulin-resistance-and-metformin/](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/2012/05/30/hypothyroidism-insulin-resistance-and-metformin/)

to support Vitamin D3 conversion you need both K2 and A. I would look at the using the following product at the max dose 45mg/day [http://www.vrp.com/bone-and-joint/ultra-k2](http://http://www.vrp.com/bone-and-joint/ultra-k2), and increase your offal consumption, and up your seafood.

FWIW CT can raise T naturally.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-30T19:20:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-weird-lab-results.3358/post-48403>

Can I just say I learn so much about the interconnectedness of all these issues from you Shijin! I love to read your posts. Just wow!

Cavemam, as far as the antibodies goes if you are showing some in either category (TPO or TgAB) I would go to AI diet right now. You are like me (and most Hashis people) where you only show TPO and TgAB is really low.

I had always heard the Vit D oil based is better for absorption but I like Shijin's explanation above. Makes total sense also in my situation why my D has always been low. I also upped K2 to help with absorption.

I'm not sure about the DHEA but agree that if it were me I would continue with the 7-keto. Have you tested the DHEA? Sounds like you may need a total hormone panel so you can follow the cascade and see where the holes are. Plus an ASI test to see what is going on there.

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-30T19:47:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-weird-lab-results.3358/post-48407>

as for vitamin D3 - I've had great success w/Now brands - dirt freaking cheap too, I'm one of those who need additional VD3 support yr round - though now w/the BHRT on board - I'm hoping I can cut back... but looking at my history - D3 tanks after September, as does my T3. but when I get to winter I feel amazing - its my season.

in order to fix the problem.. you have to understand the underlying problem...

for those of us w/PCOS - did the PCOS come first (hormones) or did the LR IR come first? from my perspective it really is the perfect storm of what can go wrong will go wrong... I'm going to do as much as I can to learn how to combat this, so dd won't have these problems...

### TribalSpice — 2012-08-31T01:16:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-weird-lab-results.3358/post-48462>

With the VitD are you getting sunshine before you wash all the natural oils off your skin? When we strip away the oils, even with products without SLS's etc it makes it much more difficult for the body to synthesize vit D. Could you try getting sunlight to as much exposed skin as possible before showering?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-08-31T02:46:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-weird-lab-results.3358/post-48492>

+1 on everything Shijin said (Dr Gretchen i am now going to call her!). I also know that my Thyroid antibodies were MUCH lower than yours (almost zero on both) and Dr K told me I could NOT rule out Hashis until I had 3 thyroid antibody tests in a row (over 18 months) that showed those minimal levels. So I'd think about retesting a few times, getting the Vitamin D co-factors Gretchen mentioned, and working on hormones, specifically getting that Pg:E ratio testing done. (Also, given Gretchen's theory about the hormone steal, have you had the 4-panel ASI test?)

### kathylu — 2012-08-31T04:07:34+0300

### Glamazon — 2012-08-31T09:17:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-weird-lab-results.3358/post-48540>

I have to slam my body with D3 to keep it up. Here's where I get a good brand (Carlson) of high concentration, cheap. [http://www.vitacost.com/carlson-vitamin-d3-10000-iu-120-softgels](http://http://www.vitacost.com/carlson-vitamin-d3-10000-iu-120-softgels). So I am back to slamming with 30K/day.

Whew - I've been reading and reading. . .and there are so many things I have going for me that point AWAY from PCOS:

Low BP

Low HR

Blood Sugars fine/low, no signs of insulin resistance

On the other hand, the Thyroid antibodies could lead to something - doing more tests. . . obviously I need hormones done - expect to get that next week.

Right now my thyroid panel points to HYPER, not HYPO. . .but that too makes zero sense considering my symptoms (fat!).

I've read a lot that as things are starting to go wonky on the thyroid front, that it's not uncommon to see fluctuations - but to show as hyper?

He's now running the parathyroid tests (4 glands unrelated to thyroid). . .which sorta makes sense with my blood levels of calcium.

I just don't have the negative "symptoms" of either PCOS or Hyper/hypo thyroid - except my hair falling out, which still couldbe the testosterone jump from regular DHEA.

So, I do need all of your good advice. . .for my hormone blood draws do I need to wait till a certain day of the cycle?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-02T03:12:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-weird-lab-results.3358/post-48848>

Cavemam, if you do have Hashis you can go hyper and hypo back and forth in the beginning. Not all hyper people will lose weight either when its Hashis hyper. As a Hashi person myself, I don't remember ever having a hyper episode. I grew up being a person with lots of energy (nervous energy my mom called it). Always on the go so maybe my hyper episodes came early on a few years before I was diagnosed and I just thought everything was normal. However, I did go through a hair falling out stage and was told it was thyroid. But my estrogen was also almost at zero so it could have been that. Iron may have something to do with hair too IIRC.

It has been referenced that day 3 (I think) or 19-21 are fine for draws for the sex hormones. My doc likes day 21.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-03T21:20:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-weird-lab-results.3358/post-49212>

> Parathyroid hormone, which regulates calcium, is increased when magnesium is
>
> low. PTH is lowered when magnesium stores are high.

# Thread: Diverticulitis - need some help for a friend
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diverticulitis-need-some-help-for-a-friend.3360/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### vkiernan — 2012-08-30T20:10:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diverticulitis-need-some-help-for-a-friend.3360/post-48410>

My friend was just diagnosed with Diverticulitis yesterday. Her doc is no help and she is not supposed to go see him for 2 weeks. Her gut has been in pain for almost a month now. Anyone have any suggestions I can share with her? It is going in one end and coming right out the other. I feel really bad for her and sure wish I knew how to help. I have told her stop all grains but what else?

### Lyndra — 2012-08-30T20:35:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diverticulitis-need-some-help-for-a-friend.3360/post-48417>

Consider the Specific Carbohydrate Diet. Worked for my husband, but much different circumstances (SIBO). It is a gut-healing protocol which I would think couldn't hurt. Start with lots of bone broth.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-08-31T02:49:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diverticulitis-need-some-help-for-a-friend.3360/post-48493>

> **vkiernan;48883 wrote:** My friend was just diagnosed with Diverticulitis yesterday. Her doc is no help and she is not supposed to go see him for 2 weeks. Her gut has been in pain for almost a month now. Anyone have any suggestions I can share with her? It is going in one end and coming right out the other. I feel really bad for her and sure wish I knew how to help. I have told her stop all grains but what else?

How about reading the BG 9 gut post and listening to the webinar? No grains is for sure. But given what Dr K has taught us about gut, she's got 3 major things going on (likely): leaky gut, insufficient gut flora and insufficient gut variety. Not to mention potentially H Pylori and/or Candida. I'd be supplementing with the Leaky Gut supps (glutamine/bone broths, pro and prebiotics, DGL, aloe, glutathione, colostrum) and testing with a functional med doc. I'd cut all dairy and nightshades as well as the grains. It is a LOT to take on. Has she read any of Dr K? Maybe she could start by listening to his podcasts and/or the webinars?

### vkiernan — 2012-08-31T02:53:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diverticulitis-need-some-help-for-a-friend.3360/post-48495>

Thanks Lauren and Lyndra. I think she is in shock for the most part. She already has lyme disease and she has read that blog. I will mention a func. med for sure. That would be a great place for her to start. I will also mention everything you guys have mentioned to start healing. Really wondering if her lyme's is playing a part in all of this?
