# Thread: Stanford Invents 'Cooling Glove', Better Than Full-Body Immersion!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stanford-invents-cooling-glove-better-than-full-body-immersion.3363/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-08-30T22:16:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stanford-invents-cooling-glove-better-than-full-body-immersion.3363/post-48427>

[http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-08-stanford-cooling-glove-steroids.html](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-08-stanford-cooling-glove-steroids.html)

"Equal to or substantially better than steroids … and it's not illegal."

This is the sort of claim you see in spam email subject lines, not in discussions of mammalian thermoregulation. Even the man making the statement, Stanford biology researcher Dennis Grahn, seems bemused. "We really stumbled on this by accident," he said. "We wanted to get a model for studying heat dissipation."

But for more than a decade now, Grahn and biology Professor H. Craig Heller have been pursuing a serendipitous find: by taking advantage of specialized heat-transfer veins in the palms of hands, they can rapidly cool athletes' core temperatures – and dramatically improve exercise recovery and performance.

The team is finally nearing a commercial version of their specialized heat extraction device, known as "the glove," and they've seen their share of media coverage. But what hasn't been discussed is why the glove works the way it does, and what that tells us about why our muscles become fatigued.

The rapid thermal exchange device, nicknamed 'the glove,' creates a vacuum to draw blood to the surface of the palms. Cold circulating water cools the blood, which returns to the heart and rapidly lowers the body's core temperature.

Nature's radiator

For Heller and Grahn, the story starts, improbably, with a longstanding question about bears.

Black bears are extremely well-insulated animals, equipped with a heavy coat of fur and a thick layer of subcutaneous fat that help them maintain their body temperature as they hibernate through winter. But once spring arrives and temperatures rise, these same bears face a greater risk of overheating than of hypothermia. How do they dump heat without changing insulation layers?

Heller and Grahn discovered that bears and, in fact, nearly all mammals have built-in radiators: hairless areas of the body that feature extensive networks of veins very close to the surface of the skin.

Rabbits have them in their ears, rats have them in their tails, dogs have them in their tongues. Heat transfer with the environment overwhelmingly occurs on these relatively small patches of skin. When you look at a thermal scan of a bear, the animal is mostly indistinguishable from the background. But the pads of the bear's feet and the tip of the nose look like they're on fire.

These networks of veins, known as AVAs (arteriovenous anastomoses) seem exclusively devoted to rapid temperature management. They don't supply nutrition to the skin, and they have highly variable blood flow, ranging from negligible in cold weather to as much as 60 percent of total cardiac output during hot weather or exercise.

Coolers and vacuums

In humans, AVAs show up in several places, including the face and feet, but the researchers' glove targets our most prominent radiator structures – in the palms of our hands.

The newest version of the device is a rigid plastic mitt, attached by a hose to what looks like a portable cooler. When Grahn sticks his hand in the airtight glove, the device creates a slight vacuum. The veins in the palm expand, drawing blood into the AVAs, where it is rapidly cooled by water circulating through the glove's plastic lining.

The method is more convenient than, say, full-body submersion in ice water, and avoids the pitfalls of other rapid palm-cooling strategies. Because blood flow to the AVAs can be nearly shut off in cold weather, making the hand too cold will have almost no effect on core temperature. Cooling, Grahn says, is therefore a delicate balance.

"You have to stay above the local vasoconstriction threshold," said Grahn. "And what do you get if you go under? You get a cold hand."

Even in prototype form, the researchers' device proved enormously efficient at altering body temperature. The glove's early successes were actually in increasing the core temperature of surgery patients recovering from anesthesia.

"We built a silly device, took it over to the recovery room and, lo and behold, it worked beyond our wildest imaginations," Heller explained. "Whereas it was taking them hours to re-warm patients coming into the recovery room, we were doing it in eight, nine minutes."

But the glove's effects on athletic performance didn't become apparent until the researchers began using the glove to cool a member of the lab – the confessed "gym rat" and frequent coauthor Vinh Cao – between sets of pull-ups. The glove seemed to nearly erase his muscle fatigue; after multiple rounds, cooling allowed him to do just as many pull-ups as he did the first time around. So the researchers started cooling him after every other set of pull-ups.

"Then in the next six weeks he went from doing 180 pull-ups total to over 620," said Heller. "That was a rate of physical performance improvement that was just unprecedented."

The researchers applied the cooling method to other types of exercise – bench press, running, cycling. In every case, rates of gain in recovery were dramatic, without any evidence of the body being damaged by overwork – hence the "better than steroids" claim. Versions of the glove have since been adopted by the Stanford football and track and field teams, as well as other college athletics programs, the San Francisco 49ers, the Oakland Raiders and Manchester United soccer club.

The elegant muscle

But what does overheating have to do with fatigue in the first place?

Much of the lab's recent research can be summed up with Grahn's statement that "temperature is a primary limiting factor for performance." But the researchers were at a loss to understand why until recently.

In 2009, it was discovered that muscle pyruvate kinase, or MPK, an enzyme that muscles need in order to generate chemical energy, was highly temperature- sensitive. At normal body temperature, the enzyme is active – but as temperatures rise, some of the enzyme begins to deform into the inactive state. By the time muscle temperatures near 104 degrees Fahrenheit, MPK activity completely shuts down.

There's a very good biological reason for this shutdown. As a muscle cell increases its activity, it heats up. But if this process continues for too long, the cell will self-destruct. By shutting itself down below a critical temperature threshold, MPK serves as an elegant self-regulation system for the muscle.

"Your muscle cells are saying, "You can't work that hard anymore, because if you do you're going to cook and die,'" Grahn said.

When you cool the muscle cell, you return the enzyme to the active state, essentially resetting the muscle's state of fatigue.

The version of the device that will be made available commercially is still being tweaked, but the researchers see applications for heat extraction in areas more important than a simple performance boost. Hyperthermia and heat stress don't just lead to fatigue – they can become medical emergencies.

"And every year we hear stories about high school athletes beginning football practice in August in hot places in the country, and there are deaths due to hyperthermia," said Heller. "There's no reason why that should occur."

More information: H. Craig Heller and Dennis A. Grahn. Disruptive Science and Technology. 2012, 1(1): 11-19. doi:10.1089/dst.2012.0004.

Provided by Stanford University

### AKMan — 2012-09-22T00:14:07+0300

> **philerup;51959 wrote:** I have started experimenting with this a bit. I have been putting mini gel packs on my palms for about 10 min at a time (about most I can bear). I do this pretty much every night as I watch tv as well as doing regular CT. Now how to get a vacuum to expand the veins in my palms while also cooling them is another challenge. Of course, something like a dustbuster or small vacuum machine used over the ice pack on the palm would be an idea.

Here is a study done by Stanford in 2008. It explains in detail the pressures used and temps. I can't believe that holding an iced gel pack in your hands while exercising wouldn't give the same results.

"The heat extraction device (AVAcore Technologies, Ann Arbor, MI) consisted of a rigid chamber into which one hand was inserted through an elastic sleeve that formed a flexible airtight seal around the wrist. The rigid chamber was connected to a pressure sensor and a vacuum pump. The vacuum pump created a slight subatmospheric pressure environment inside the chamber (-40 mm Hg) where the palm rested on a curved metal surface that was maintained at 18–22Â°C by temperature-controlled water circulated beneath the metal surface. The device was suspended from the ceiling by an elastic cord during the exercises to reduce the impact of wearing the device on gait, stride, and posture while walking."

### Eric — 2012-09-02T11:03:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/59-99-ice-pack-vest.3365/post-48909>

This is Eric, creator of the "Cool Fat Burner." I want to thank AKMan for posting about my new invention, and am glad to see the interest in a cold thermogenesis / thermal loading device.

I made the Cool Fat Burner for myself, as I looked for a functional device online, but couldn't find anything close to what I wanted. (I knew I would never consistently stick to cold baths or showers for years and decades, but figured a simple device I could put on and "forget about" would be easy to wear for years to come)

Every aspect of the Cool Fat Burner is there intentionally, as I initially made what I wanted for my own CT usage: Large surface area of cold packs. Cold packs hold cold a long time. (2 hrs in neutral environment of 70'F) Covers primary BAT areas. (subclavical, spine, etc) Adjustable but also have optional compression. Basically, that's all the stuff I wanted for my own sessions, so that's why I made it that way. (my first couple prototypes didn't fit all those criteria, and my sessions weren't nearly as productive to say the least)

To address Endless' concerns: regarding the **"junk food" thing, first, I swear that's completely true and accurate. (the video and related claims) It's almost absurd the amount of empty carbs I can eat now, yet gain absolutely 0 fat, so long as I time and plan it properly. (That is, no/low/slow carb the _rest_ of the day, the empty carb "junk food" after 1 meal only, and that meal itself must fall between a power endurance workout before, and cold therapy after).

It actually started as a bit of a self-experiment, it's temporary, and I certainly don't have it there as a recommended dietary plan; it's there now only to show that if cold therapy with the CFB can negate all those empty carbs that otherwise would be stored as fat... just imagine how well it will work for someone who doesn't eat empty carbs each and every day!

Also please remember, there are various dietary plans out there that allow empty carbs. Various portion-control diets (weight watcher, jenny craig), the Slow-Carb diet and Metabolic diet (1 "cheat day" a week), the Renegade diet, Carb Back-Loading diet, etc. So being able to burn off empty carbs / glucose before it stores as fat could be helpful to folks on those types of diet plans.

As for the shipping**, thanks for the info, I'll get on that. We've had issues w/ exports (ie, outside US) having a high shipping rate, and can sometimes 'fix it' and get a much lower rate. (we're also looking into expanding our carriers as well)

It's kinda ridiculous that all because they cross over an imaginary line, that the rates should jump 4X as high. (US shipping is never over $9, even to Hawaii or Alaska -- and each CFB weighs 7lbs!!!)

If you want to email me (info CoolFatBurner.com) with your shipping address, I can talk to the USPS and see if I can get a much lower rate. (It would also help us adjust our settings for others in your region.)

Thanks again!

### Eric — 2012-09-03T11:52:01+0300

This whole field is so knew to most "regular" people, it's hard to get through their lack of knowledge without grabbing their attention first.

It is interesting, the vast difference in knowledge and experience and world-view of folks who know about CT (members here, those who have read the "4 Hour Body," readers of Ray Cronise's blog, etc) ...and those who don't even know CT _exists.

In my past, I used to compete for one of the top MMA and submission grappling teams in the world. I still keep in contact with many people who are literally world and Olympic -class athletes. Many of them have done ice baths in the past. Yet almost none of them had the first clue about thermal loading and cold thermogenesis!!!_ (though the only one who did, an absolute guru on general weight-loss and weight-cutting (for competition) is on this upcoming season of "The Ultimate Fighter," and he actually knew about BAT when I told him I was making the CFB.)

He greedily scarfed up a bunch of my samples, and is giving them out to his UFC clients. I'd laugh if we see him wearing one on TUF show LOL!!! (it premieres in a week or two)

> **AKMan;49692 wrote: Hey, Eric - Wow! Didn't realize you were a reader of this blog. Glad I posted your link. What got you interested in cold thermogenesis? Are you working on any other CT products?
>

> AKMan -- thanks for linking to my site!

>

> Around 2yrs ago I decided to get back in shape, and ran across CT mentioned in the "4 Hour Body" book. That re-sparked my interest.

> >

> >
> > AKMan;49692 wrote:** I really like your website, did you make that you-tube video? It's very well done!
>

> Thanks! There were a number of years where I was away from health, fitness, and competition. During that time I did a lot of civic theater and got into indie filmmaking. That's where I got some skills to throw together videos like on the site.
>
>
>
> AKMan;49692 wrote:** Do you use your vest much? Maybe you could tell us how often and when you wear it.

I use the Cool Fat Burner almost every day. I always wear it for at least an hour, and always go to at least "Moderate" intensity (strong goosebumps for the duration). My personal "sweet spot" for CT is high Moderate intensity, right on the verge of "Hardcore" intensity (shivering).

So long as I have a real slight, subtle pre-shiver going on deep in the gut, I feel like I'm at my most productive level of CT. If I'm in the mood, I'll push into a full shiver state. Those are really good sessions!

If I only do 1 session a day, I do it in the evenings, typically an hour or so after a meal (usually the biggest meal, which I eat after a ~ 25 min power-endurance workout, usually involving kettlebells)

I'm more or less at my [target weight/physique](http://http://i1173.photobucket.com/albums/r591/CoolFatBurner/Cool-Fat-Burner-usage--Aug-30th.jpg), so I use the CFB to help maintain and keep my metabolism boosted, as well as for the inner "emotional" experience... there's something very 'cleansing' about a good CT session. When I skip a day, I often miss it.

Thanks again for helping to get the info out!

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-21T06:19:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/59-99-ice-pack-vest.3365/post-53118>

> **Endless;52371 wrote:** Looking forward to a review!

Being only a week or so, I can't yet say anything about results, but I can talk about the user experience.

The cold packs are nice. In fact, they're the best thing about the whole gizmo. I actually use them to supplement my ice for cooling down my bath water for tub immersion CT. I'll probably actually use the cold packs for this purpose more often than their intended purpose, haha.

Already being used to 46 degree water for immersion CT, I didn't experience the vest (used as directed) as too intense, even after a couple of hours (the instructions indicated that this would be considered "hardcore"). Mind you, my Southern California apartment is currently stifling (high 90s inside), so at this point all the vest is doing is bringing things back down to a sane temperature. I suspect I'll have to wait for cooler weather before I can really get the chance to experience its cooling effect.

I'm also using the cold packs separately for spot-CT. They're worth buying the vest just to get them.

One issue I had--though my fault, not the vests--was that my confidence from the cold-ass CT tub immersions made me disregard the cautions about cryoburn. I go to sleep in the vest at times (a bit awkward, but so is wrangling 30 1-quart freezer containers every other day for CT baths) and once woke up to extreme tenderness on the tips of my shoulder blades. It didn't feel like a muscular soreness, and I had the same thing develop in a spot on my belly were I had also put the large pack later on. After reading the thread about where someone accident froze their fat (and Jack actually intimated that this was a good thing), I concluded that I must have inadvertently frozen the fat under those areas. The spots in question stayed painful for a few days but gradually got better.

So go for it, if just for the ice packs to use in spot-CT and to aid in immersion CT.

As for how the website explains it works...I'm not sure I buy the explanation. Thermal loading just to burn calories won't result in permanent fat loss--your hypothalamus will make damn sure you re-eat or down-regulate to recoup those calories one way or another to get back to your current homeostatic set-point. It's going to have to be the BAT activation and ensuring hormonal changes that would make the fat loss permanent. Kinda like how Jack's CT isn't about burning calories through cold, but sending information to the brain that it's cold outside so that it will switch over to the winter-mode hormone regime.

So for that, the vest does specifically target BAT areas, so hopefully intense use of the vest to activate BAT would help us with our Jack-style CT. I won't use it to replace immersion CT as 1) I'm not sure it's doing the same thing metabolically, and 2) I'm addicted to the sub-50's immersive experience.  But this is an excellent adjunct for the 2-day downtime between ice baths when my 60 lbs of ice (for horridly warm SoCal tap water) is busy getting frozen.

It does seem to take a while for the packs to freeze, but that's probably due to the fact that they have to compete with 30 1-quart water-filled food containers to have their heat taken away by my poor overworked freezer.

So overall, two thumbs up. I'll be using in ways indicated and ways not directed as long the packs last.

### Eric — 2012-09-26T04:43:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/59-99-ice-pack-vest.3365/post-54337>

CuiviÃ«,

Thanks again for the thorough review! It makes me me good to come here and read Dr Kruse's work and to the forums and see other folks who are knowledgeable about all this, because we're all still in the minority; the world hasn't caught up yet.

I wanted to address 2 points:

> **CuiviÃ«;53753 wrote: Already being used to 46 degree water for immersion CT, I didn't experience the vest (used as directed) as too intense, even after a couple of hours **(the instructions indicated that this would be considered "hardcore"). Mind you, my Southern California apartment is currently stifling (high 90s inside), so at this point all the vest is doing is bringing things back down to a sane temperature. I suspect I'll have to wait for cooler weather before I can really get the chance to experience its cooling effect.
>
>
> That's true... The reason I did this, I wanted to give users the choice. Which is to say, I didn't want to force them onto the highest level.
>
>
>
> So everyone starts at "Casual," assuming a 70' room.
>
>
>
> It takes conscious user effort then to effect and choose a higher level of intensity. That way, it puts the final choice and outcome in the user's hands. (and reduces my chances of being sued!!!)
>
>
>
> Also note, intensity is based on sensation**, not time. "Hardcore" intensity is near-to-all-out-shivering.
>
>
>
> Personally, for me to currently hit an all out shivering state (38 yrs old, down to 185lbs, probably around 10% or less bodyfat, 32" waist -- the same as high school (walk around with soft 4-pack)), it takes either 69' on the A/C, OR around 1/2 gallon of ice water, OR wearing a wet T-shirt. \[\[\[ keep in mind, over the last month or so, I've kinda cold-adapted, despite eating garbage as part of the junk food experiment, to the point where I gotta crank the A/C to max to even be able to sleep due to feeling overheated while I sleep\]\]\]) So I have to really employ the "supporting factors" to push me into the shivers.
>
>
>
> Since I'm more or less at my target physique, I rarely go to Hardcore shiver state nowadays, unless I'm in the mood for a good "cleansing."
>
>
>
>
>
> CuiviÃ«;53753 wrote:**
>
> As for how the website explains it works...I'm not sure I buy the explanation. Thermal loading just to burn calories won't result in permanent fat loss--your hypothalamus will make damn sure you re-eat or down-regulate to recoup those calories one way or another to get back to your current homeostatic set-point. It's going to have to be the BAT activation and ensuring hormonal changes that would make the fat loss permanent. Kinda like how Jack's CT isn't about burning calories through cold, but sending information to the brain that it's cold outside so that it will switch over to the winter-mode hormone regime.

Yeah, I want to add this aspect, but not completely sure how to cite Jack's work and not confuse "regular folks." I'm working on it.

There's something to consider... you're all ahead of the curve. Waaayyyy ahead of the curve.

I used to train and compete in MMA before it was called MMA, for one of the top MMA and submission grappling schools in the USA.

Anyhow, I know a bunch of world-class and Olympic athletes... _and they don't know anything about cold thermogenesis!!!!!!!!_ And half these folks even take ice baths from time to time! YOU PEOPLE ARE AHEAD OF THEM!

Simply put, for now, the "calories in vs calories out" model is the easiest to use.

But you're right... I need to include and cite Dr Kruse's primal pathway as well. (and I intend to!)

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-03T04:18:34+0300

I am more then thrilled to death with this. I would recommend it to anyone

I tried using the vest outside where it's in the low 40's - strip down naked with only socks and gloves.Â  Plus, I'm wearing my blue light blocking eyewear.Â  Very sexy stuffÂ  lolÂ Â Â  now - I'm not extremely cold but I do have goose bumps on my entire body and oddly enough I'm sweating?!???Â Â  My BO is worse than if I ran the Boston Marathon.Â  I ment to ask Eric if anyone else notices that?Â  weird.

### [email redacted] — 2012-12-13T10:20:45+0200

### endless — 2012-12-14T14:00:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/59-99-ice-pack-vest.3365/post-67511>

I got mine a couple of days ago. I was resisting because of the shipping price to Canada but with no trips to the US upcoming I finally gave in!

First impressions: It looks homemade...my photocopied sheet says that it is part of the introductory run and is unique. I am cool with home made, as long as it is rugged enough to stand up to daily use. The fabric is pretty thin, but seems strong enough.

It's quite small. I am 5'1" and 148lbs, so by no means a big person, and I find it hard to use the straps when the ice packs are inside it. Almost like the straps should be a wee bit longer. Once the packs soften it fits better.

The fabric ices up after a few minutes, so it's a bit messy...I shed 'snow' if I sit against something or just walking around. Maybe that's because the house temp was fairly warm?

I wore a very thin tshirt under it and it took about 15 minutes to feel the cold...I got some goosebumps on my arms. I had to take it off after 20 minutes to go somewhere, and after I took it off I felt some numbness around my shoulderblades, which was not noticeable while I was wearing the vest. It went away quickly.

I am really looking forward to this being my supplemental CT! I know that I will be able to use this in the evening when I can't bear the thought of full immersion. I might even use it on the upper torso while I do ice water to the waist.

All in all, as long as the longevity is there, I'm happy with it. I would recommend consulting with Eric about sizes if you are larger though.

ETA: the link to the 'Start Up Guide' on the sheet I recieved seems to be defunct.

### Eric — 2012-12-31T11:41:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/59-99-ice-pack-vest.3365/post-68808>

_I finally started up my ice pack vest.

_I like the design - simple but seems to work well.

_Â 

However, do the packs actually make anyone cold?_

I've tried them out 3 times now, 60 minutes each time, and I don't really feel much - no goosebumps, etc._

Hey V,

It depends largely on each person, but the CFB was made so that an "average user" will still have to do at least a little bit_ more to push them into Moderate (goosebumps) and Hardcore (shivering) levels of intensity.Â  This way, each person isn't forced into the higher levels; they get to choose exactly what level they experience, and for how long.Â 

For example, sometimes I like blasting out a Hardcore level for the first ~ 45-60minutes, then reel back and relax at Moderate for the next ~ 45-60minutes.Â  (1.5 to 2hr sessions, then relax in the same room for 1-5hrs)Â  The first Hardcore round "brute forcing" the cals/fat off via shivering, as well as releasing adiponectin, and the emotional 'cleansing' it provides.Â  The second Moderate round activating BAT, which as I become more and more cold-apapted, will keep staying active for hours after.

Of course, for practical reasons, allowing users to dictate the intensity also keeps me from being sued if it forced one into a shiver-level Hardcore intensity as default, and someone out there went and got hypothermia!Â 

EDIT:Â  you could also experiment w/ the "supporting factors" such as chugging a bunch of ice-water to jump start the session, lowering the temp of the room you're in bit a degree or two, wearing a wet T-shirt, etc.

### Eric — 2012-12-31T12:11:34+0200

The initial inventory of the vest portion is actually being made by local seamstresses...Â  After testing for around 4-6months last year, I went back and forced them to make various "reinforcements" to several places.Â  They said it might not "look nice," but I was worried about function over form, and wanted to make sure it held together forever.Â 

Â  The material was actually some specialized, temp-treated stuff that was initially used in garments for L.A. firefighters LOL!Â  Ironic, eh?Â  (if anything ever 'comes loose' or whatever, let me know for a quick replacement!)

_It's quite small. I am 5'1" and 148lbs, so by no means a big person, and I find it hard to use the straps when the ice packs are inside it. Almost like the straps should be a wee bit longer. Once the packs soften it fits better.

The next, formal run will have more size options for sure...Â  though it's strange that they should be too tight; there have been lots of bigger folks using the same size vest w/o problem.Â  Weird.

_I wore a very thin tshirt under it and it took about 15 minutes to feel the cold...I got some goosebumps on my arms. I had to take it off after 20 minutes to go somewhere, and after I took it off I felt some numbness around my shoulderblades, which was not noticeable while I was wearing the vest. It went away quickly.

Also note:Â  the first 5-15 minutes is the "Cool Down" phase.Â  Just like exercise has a 'warm-up' that doesn't count as part of the workout, the Cool Down phase probably shouldn't be counted in one's session time.Â  (the "Supporting Factors" -- ice water, room temp, etc -- will determine how long one's Cool Down is.

I am really looking forward to this being my supplemental CT! I know that I will be able to use this in the evening when I can't bear the thought of full immersion. I might even use it on the upper torso while I do ice water to the waist._

Â 

All in all, as long as the longevity is there, I'm happy with it. I would recommend consulting with Eric about sizes if you are larger though._

### hellojtm — 2012-12-31T19:43:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/59-99-ice-pack-vest.3365/post-68872>

cross-posting this from my own post on a DIY ice vest thread:

> hellojtm said:
> So far I have had mixed results with the cool fat burner. Â It's very tight and uncomfortable until the gel pads get quite thawed. Â So the initial 20-30 minutes are with minimal contact to my body as the stiff gel pads only touch on a couple of areas. Â  This leaves me with laying back against a large pillow to try to maximize surface contact. Â That's no better than sitting in the tub for me. Â The point of the device was to make me a mobile thermogenerator but for the first 1/2 of the session I'm tied to the couch. Â When it does thaw somewhat I can start attaching the velcro straps, but when you cross over the gel paks you get a really unbalanced cold effect as the left side (which has the velcro on the outside) is close to the chest by the right side (with it's velcro on the inside) is held away from the chest. Â This resulted in a skin temp of 50F on the left and 67F on the right. Â  Not ideal. Â  A bit frustrating after spending good money and I'll likely return it (they have, seemingly, a great return policy).
>
>
> I only post this here, not as a dig against the company that I have no qualms with, but to encourage folks that may be on the fence about trying to design their own DIY solution that it may be comparable to what's available commercially. Â I'll crosspost this to the thread discussing cool fat burner too.

Hope this helps!

Jesse

### endless — 2013-01-01T02:50:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/59-99-ice-pack-vest.3365/post-68890>

Jesse, this is the same issue I have noticed with CFB...the packs are solid and not very easily conformed to your shape until they soften.Â

### Eric — 2013-01-01T12:19:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/59-99-ice-pack-vest.3365/post-68899>

Hey guys, thanks for the feedback... Gotta run, so a quick response:

Here's why I made the packs start solid for the first ~ 20-30min or so...
Study proves solid ice packs work better than regular gel packs for quick cold transfer.
http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16003673

Of course, a person can just let the packs sit out for a while first, if they don't want to start w/ them solid.

Also please make sure you're not crossing them until they soften. (start with them "resting," side-by-side, but still with straps attached; they should not be crossed in the beginning, assuming you start w/ them solid)

ps. couldn't quote responses, or highlight link, for some reasons; none of the icons will appear in on the forum for me to use

### Thor — 2013-01-01T12:41:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/59-99-ice-pack-vest.3365/post-68903>

.
@Eric - Can't you design a vest that makes 50 lbs of fat just instantly fall off on the first use ???

Just kidding - would be nice but nope.... takes work folks.

### endless — 2013-01-01T13:00:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/59-99-ice-pack-vest.3365/post-68905>

Thanks, Eric. I'm going to try softening them a bit before I put the vest on, at least for times when I need to be moving around more.

### ashryn — 2013-01-02T22:12:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/59-99-ice-pack-vest.3365/post-69073>

I just got mine yesterday.. Since I've been spot icing with a great bag of ice strapped to my belly for two weeks, this is noticeably colder, and more convenient since I can walk around in it. The solid packs are a little uncomfortable, but not unbearable... It wont take them long to soften up in any case... I've had it on for 5 mins so far, so no updates whether I get to shivering or not. Then again, I am in Western Australia, and its damn hot here, so I figure as cold as I can get it has to be better than nothing.

Eric, I have a suggestion for a super-deluxe version that includes belly packs.

(My n-1 experience with spot icing the belly during my ToM is a complete lack of pain compared to the usual experience of the type of agony that necessitated 4 days of non-stop high strength painkillers.)

You could modify the existing vest by making the back section longer to create two wide straps that would wrap around the waist and attach to each other with Velcro at the front across the belly. If you made the pouch for the ice packs just one long tube, people could adjust the position of the ice depending on their personal shape, and it would continue to be useful once any excess belly has been burned away.

I'd be happy to test out a prototype for you if you think this is a workable idea.. Or perhaps I'll sew one myself and hit you up for a couple of ice packs to fill it

So far I'm very happy with my purchase, and am looking forward to getting results like Thor!

### hellojtm — 2013-01-03T21:12:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/59-99-ice-pack-vest.3365/post-69240>

Hey, thanks for the responses.

Pro-tip. After my initial use I purposely froze the bags into a shape that was more conducive to my body shape... Mainly I folded the rear bags and placed them longwise on their end in the freezer door, this made them just as long (width of the back) but narrower which gave more slack to the overall vest. But more importantly I accidentally let the two front bags bend a little in the freezer. This made them somewhat c-shaped and then were able to rest past my collarbones and onto my chest with a nice initial surface patch that increased as it thawed.

Going to keep working with it. Endless, maybe give it a try. Right now my packs are in the freezer in a specific shape that I believe will optimize for a solid bag with a good sized contact patch.

Eric, appreciate your response, just to clarify I wasn't strapping or crossing until it thawed once I realized that it didn't make sense to do so. Then I read the PDF that is linked in this thread too

I second the previous posters idea about getting it onto the belly too, but I like the logic of hitting the BAT areas directly. Much like we do squats to stimulate the big muscles, why not put the cold on the area that will likely have the most BAT?

So, I'm cooling off a little bit on my earlier dissatisfaction as I get better at using the tool provided. I do think that the design could be better, but everyone's a critic.....

jesse

### Eric — 2013-02-12T00:46:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/59-99-ice-pack-vest.3365/post-75458>

Just finished up what was, in total, a [4-month experiment](https://coolfatburner.com/Blog.htm) with the Cool Fat Burner.

While this will come as no surprise to most here, I was able to more or less "prove" that **cold-thermogenesis burns off excess glucose**. In fact, I was able to lower glucose levels as much from using the Cool Fat Burner as I was from jogging!

Anyhow, this was a simple and cheap (albeit, long) experiment.

I plan on doing more "impressive" (and expensive) tests and experiments in the future.

EDIT: if I get a chance, gonna throw together a video summarizing the whole thing, put it on the landing page

### caroline — 2013-02-12T01:06:37+0200

### RiverNeighbor — 2013-02-12T04:23:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/59-99-ice-pack-vest.3365/post-75488>

I was having trouble getting ct in the tub every day with the twins still demanding so much time but I am loving my cool fat burner. I can put it on and play with the kids, fix meals, do laundry. Looking forward to an enhanced version. Haven't checked my blood glucose since starting but I will have to tomorrow. Now when I go in the cold my back heats up and my husband has started commenting on how I am radiating heat at night. Good stuff.

### freesia — 2013-02-12T07:18:09+0200

...I even encountered someone (who may or may not have read the blogs..hmm probably not actually) who tossed aside cold adaptation as having _any_ potential benefit because she'd lived in a really cold country and it had done nothing for her. Simple explanations are most powerful. So is what happened to your abs LOL.

Good luck continuing to develop your vests. I might even get one myself next (Sthn hemisphere) summer so I don't have to persevere with getting enough ice to make 46 F baths when the water is really too warm for CT.

>
> Good luck continuing to develop your vests. I might even get one myself next (Sthn hemisphere) summer so I don't have to persevere with getting enough ice to make 46 F baths when the water is really too warm for CT.

Thanks freesia!

I have to question that she was cold-adapted... I can't imagine someone experiencing it, yet not immediately realizing the absurd benefits... Even if one doesn't know (via research) all the health benefits -- immune system, hormonal, longevity, etc -- just the whole "boosted metabolism" thing, feeling the efforts of diet and exercise being magnified, the fat coming off way faster, feeling the metabolism revved up... I'd think that alone would be proof enough of it's benefit.

I wonder how many folks living in colder climates just dress real warm all the time? They're barely getting any more cold exposure than someone living at the equator. (also, the shorter daylight hours might lead to SAD in some, which of course will mess w/ energy levels, mood, appetite, etc)

### Eric — 2013-02-13T04:56:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/59-99-ice-pack-vest.3365/post-75661>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=75513)
>
> Wow.
> Thank you for sharing Eric! And doing such a experiment! I love biohacks...!

I have no clue when I'll be able to do it, but I want to do a PET scan / BAT test experiment. But I'd like to add something new.

First, since the CFB is the first BAT device, that in itself would be novel. But I want to scan not just immediately after the CFB session, (like established studies do, after having people in cooling suits and cold chambers, etc) but I want to also test several hours after the CFB session ends! I'm convinced, as I mentioned on the blog (Cold-Adapted entry) that my BAT is staying active for hours and hours after the session.

Just the other morning, I did a session from ~ 10-11:30am. (fasted; first half shiver, last half goosebumps) Not only did I feel overly warm for the hours after, that warmth came and went throughout the whole day, into that nite trying to sleep! That's somewhere around 16yrs later!!!

I don't always get that reaction though, but I'm certain I could keep the BAT activated for at least 2 hrs after the CFB session ends.

(note: even though the room I'm in is cooler, it's not cold enough to activate BAT on it's own; but once I do a CFB session, the room seems cool enough to keep BAT activated, almost like I keep "coasting" after the CFB gives the initial "push")

As far as I know, few if any studies, have shown that BAT can keep being active after the initial cooling stimulus. (they always test right after)

### freesia — 2013-02-13T05:52:12+0200

I agree 100%. Even if you're breathing really cold air, and experiencing freezing ambient temps on what skin you do keep regularly exposed (what, the face?) - if your energy metabolism is off and you suffer chronic ill health it can take quite a determined appraoch to pull your thermoadaptive capacity and all the rest(!!!) back into line.

No, one mention of cold baths and people (some people more than others!) turn off to any possibility it might be a very effective tool. I think for people with low basal temperature maybe their reaction to the idea of bathing in icy water is partly driven by some natural protective reflex?
Your vests are a less threatening way for some people to get comfortable with the idea of CT, and the practice of it too. I hope people will flock to buy because like you said the benefits of cold are absurd.

### Jasmin — 2012-08-31T10:14:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-blocker.3374/post-48542>

Can someone help me with finding out where to buy blue blocker glasses and bulbs. I have found different glasses at amazon but dont know wich one I should choose and I have not found the bulbs yet

### Kiera2 — 2012-08-31T10:54:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-blocker.3374/post-48543>

Hi Jasmin, I'm in australia; the cheapest I could find were these (for over my reading glasses) [http://www.ebay.com.au/itm/Vent-Safety-Glasses-w-Blue-Blocker-Lens-/220917710721?pt=AU\_MensSunglasses&var=&hash=item7915ffa28e](http://http://www.ebay.com.au/itm/Vent-Safety-Glasses-w-Blue-Blocker-Lens-/220917710721?pt=AU_MensSunglasses&var=&hash=item7915ffa28e)

### Optimalbound — 2012-08-31T11:07:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-blocker.3374/post-48544>

I'm using the [Uvex S1933X "Skyper"](http://http://www.amazon.com/Uvex-S1933X-Eyewear-SCT-Orange-Anti-Fog/dp/B000USRG90/ref=pd_bxgy_hi_img_y) which works well. (Everything blue appears black when seen through these)

They are super cheap, so I have several pairs at home, in my car, in my boat etc. They are not exactly fashionable, though..

### Endless — 2012-08-31T11:29:32+0300

I have those too, but I can still see some blue....wonder if that means they are not enough?

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-31T14:08:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-blocker.3374/post-48551>

> **Endless;49022 wrote:** I have those too, but I can still see some blue....wonder if that means they are not enough?

[http://rs.nationalsafetyinc.com/company\_79//UvexLensTechnology.pdf](http://http://rs.nationalsafetyinc.com/company_79//UvexLensTechnology.pdf)

Check out the charts they have for the different color lenses. The SCT ones are about as good as you could get for blocking blue, I'd think. Did Jack put a wavelength limit on it? For some reason 520nm sticks in my head as a cutoff point?

Either way, you're good up to 540 w/ these. For the price, they certainly can't be beat.

# Thread: Odd statement in Cunnane
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-statement-in-cunnane.3375/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 26
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-08-31T11:47:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-statement-in-cunnane.3375/post-48546>

So he says:

> Like Phinney, Loren Cordain ... has ... cautioned that meat intake exceeding about 100 grams per day causes metabolic problems and cannot be sustained by humans

In the first place, I suppose Cunnane means "protein" not "meat".

Secondly, where does Cordain say this? AFAIK, he \*doesn't\*. In fact, as we know, he recommends anything up to 30% protein, has research that appears to back that, and offers sample meal plans that deliver that and more.

In fact, in his own way he started a kind of (polite) war with the low-carb movement by describing their regimen as a "fad" in _The Paleo Diet_ and saying a "high protein, low glycemic index" diet was superior.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-31T14:46:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-statement-in-cunnane.3375/post-48554>

He has mentioned it in several of his research papers. Most of the articles were pre 2004 however.

### Michael — 2012-08-31T18:40:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-statement-in-cunnane.3375/post-48585>

There are so many fascinating and deep things in Cunnane's book that posting about macronutrient ratios seems absurd.

Yet paleo seems to be waging something of an undeclared war on the low carb people. Take the "safe starches" nonsense out of the picture and it's STILL there. I say "undeclared" but Cordain effectively did declare it.

So he's said 100g protein per day approx max in some paper somewhere but MUCH more than that all over the place, including the paper from 2000 as well as the one from 2010. Weird disconnect.

And it's all (seemingly) based on his models. But what if the models contain a mistaken assumption?

He seems to offer no PHYSIOLOGICAL argument for saying up to 30% or 33% or 35% or whatever.

Damn expensive, too.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-01T03:00:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-statement-in-cunnane.3375/post-48679>

Michael now you are finding out why the assumptions in all the paleo leaders books.......might be a house of cards.

### Michael — 2012-09-01T10:35:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-statement-in-cunnane.3375/post-48695>

Sisson excepted on this point. I guess that's down to his inborn shrewdness.

I don't know I should mention people's personalities but it inevitably does come down to that.

If people could only be calm and level-headed and ask themselves the right questions.

Here's a good illustration. So recently Sisson was quietly laughing in his column about an attack launched by John McDougall on the paleo movement. McDougall was so wrong on so many levels that reading his stuff was like watching a train wreck happening in slow-motion.

But here's just one thing (and relevant to the above). McDougall is saying that ketosis is "a state of illness". Well, everything else aside, he obviously doesn't know that babies spend a fair amount of time in it. (I didn't fully understand _why till I read Cunnane, but at least I knew that_ they did.) And this man's an MD. Hilarious!

But why's he getting all exercised over ketosis, anyway? The answer seems to be that in his mind he's associated paleo with Atkins ... because that's too much meat, too, you know ...

He doesn't stop to ask himself: "How do you get into ketosis on 40,30,30 (the East African paper) or even on 22%-40% carbs and a similar level of protein (the earlier paper)?" Cordain never discusses ketosis in his books - and no wonder.

It never ceases to surprise me how people want to start shouting the odds before informing themselves even minimimally.

Even if you didn't really want to get at the truth but merely want a ruck you might as well do that otherwise you're just punching the air.

But, actually, let's stop, get calm, and actually notice a few interesting phenomena and ask ourselves some questions about them, like Cunnane did.

Why are babies fat? How is the fat distributed and what's it's composition?

Until the 1930s cretinism was disturbingly common in inland areas of Europe and the US (till government mandated the iodization of salt). But why should that be? Whence the importance of iodine?

We might all get somewhere then.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-01T16:37:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-statement-in-cunnane.3375/post-48719>

Now you are beginning to see what the science is behind what I am saying. Cordain is a great researcher but he and his followers do not realize his research blind spots. I do and I point them out and because I do so I get painted by a broad paleo brush. Why? Because their franchises might be at risk. My job is to lead people to the science and let them make their own decisions. In the paleo community they are being successful because they are comparing themselves to a SAD.......but they are not looking to see if there is something more optimal for our species. Now the meme in paleo is about nutrient density. Well guess what? Nutrient density has to be put in context of the organs needs of that specific mammal. The human brain has different nutrient density requirements than other animals. The key is understanding what the organ specifically requires. The human brain separates us. The best nutrient density for the human brain is found in seafood. Oysters specificially, not liver. BG 5 and 6 are critical in this area.

### AKMan — 2012-09-01T17:52:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-statement-in-cunnane.3375/post-48736>

If the paleo gurus start preaching to eat more seafood, avoid light after dark, embrace the chill and get quarterly labs, they would still argue what seafood, what light, how cold and what labs--but at least they'd be a step closer to the best solution for population-wide health, I believe.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-01T18:37:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-statement-in-cunnane.3375/post-48755>

### Michael — 2012-09-01T20:49:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-statement-in-cunnane.3375/post-48775>

> **Jack Kruse;49212 wrote:** Now you are beginning to see what the science is behind what I am saying. Cordain is a great researcher but he and his followers do not realize his research blind spots. I do and I point them out and because I do so I get painted by a broad paleo brush. Why? Because their franchises might be at risk. My job is to lead people to the science and let them make their own decisions. In the paleo community they are being successful because they are comparing themselves to a SAD.......but they are not looking to see if there is something more optimal for our species. Now the meme in paleo is about nutrient density. Well guess what? Nutrient density has to be put in context of the organs needs of that specific mammal. The human brain has different nutrient density requirements than other animals. The key is understanding what the organ specifically requires. The human brain separates us. The best nutrient density for the human brain is found in seafood. Oysters specificially, not liver. BG 5 and 6 are critical in this area.

I wonder if Cordain is entirely wise to keep pushing low salt given the importance to the brain of iodine. I might go back to his books and have a look at what, if anything, he says about iodine. Given that the iodination of salt seems to be all that is standing between modern man and increased cretinism, and perhaps a lot of other less-severe problems, this needs caution. I think after that discussion of Cunnane's nobody should be saying "cut your salt" without adding the rider "increase your intake of seafood".

Of course, paleo can and does change over time. Case in point. Jimmy Moore has a line about how paleo has taught him about "food quality". If he's not just using that as a formula to try to please everyone then he's forgetting something. Food quality matters in the movement _now, but it seems to have crept in sideways via the WAPF. There's an old book review by Sally Fallon of one of the original books, possibly Boyd Eaton's, possibly the first edition of Cordain's, or maybe it was a survey and pulled in both. And she ridicules them on just this point. It's actually quite a funny piece, although it was taken as a case of lese majeste_ in the paleo movement. I think the criticism was probably silenty if grudgingly noted.

Also there are people who've moved across from WAPF and brought their organ meats and bone-broth and organic and biodynamic interests with them.

At one time if you'd have gone somewhere like Mark's Daily Apple and said "Why not try a little liver?" people would have said, "No, no, no, you can't do that; the Vitamin D Council says vitamin A is dangerous." Whether it had said quite that, and even if it had ...

Paleo was all meat - sorry lean meat - and vegetables and fruit. But we've moved a little since then.

My only complaint about Cunnane: he seems a little careless with numbers. There's a table in which he shows different foods in terms of their content of brain-specific nutrients. He seems to be matching them against the RDA. Now he says that shellfish, the best of them, requires 900 grams to deliver them all, other foods being far less nutritious. But how can that be? That's two pounds of shellfish! Almost none of us would be anywhere near even the RDA for these nutrients (inadaequate as the RDA might possibly be) and that seems unlikely.

But I'm quibbling. At the end of the day that doesn't matter. What's important is that he has identified the brain specific nutrients and pointed to the best sources of them.

It changes the way at which we have to look at things. It's a shame Jimmy hasn't interviewed him.

### Michael — 2012-09-02T21:17:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-statement-in-cunnane.3375/post-49023>

> Another illustration of the wisdom of the native Indians of that far north country came to me through two prospectors whom we rescued and brought out with us just before the fall freeze-up. ...
>
>
>
> They accordingly abandoned everything, and rather than remain in the country with very uncertain facilities and prospects for obtaining food and shelter, made a forced march to the Liard River with the hope that some expedition might be in that territory. One of the men told me the following tragic story. **While they were crossing the high plateau he nearly went blind with so violent a pain in his eyes that he feared he would go insane. It was not snow blindness, for they were equipped with glasses. **It was xeropthalmia, due to lack of vitamin A.** One day he almost ran into a mother grizzly bear and her two cubs. Fortunately, they did not attack him but moved off. He sat down on a stone and wept in despair of ever seeing his family again. As he sat there holding his throbbing head, he heard a voice and looked up. It was an old Indian who had been tracking that grizzly bear. He recognized this prospector's plight and while neither could understand the language of the other, the Indian after making an examination of his eyes, took him by the hand and led him to a stream that was coursing its way down the mountain. Here as the prospector sat waiting the Indian built a trap of stones across the stream. He then went upstream and waded down splashing as he came and thus drove the trout into the trap. He threw the fish out on the bank and told the prospector to eat the flesh of the head and the tissues back of the eyes, including the eyes, with the result that in a few hours his pain had largely subsided. In one day his sight was rapidly returning, and in two days his eyes were nearly normal.** He told me with profound emotion and gratitude that that Indian had certainly saved his life.
>
>
> This is Weston Price, who continues:
>
>
>
> I have been impressed to find that primitive racial stocks in various parts of the world are familiar with the fact that eyes constitute an invaluable adjunct for nutrition. Even the one time cannibals of the Fiji Islands, and the hereditary king of the Fiji Islands, told me in detail of the practices with regard to the use of eyes as an adjunct to diet. The chief, his father, and grandfather had the privilege of reserving the eyes of captives for their personal use. When among the natives of the islands north of Australia, I learned to enjoy greatly fish head soup made from certain selected tissues. After the fish had been cleaned the heads were split and the eyes left in.

[http://www.journeytoforever.org/farm\_library/price/price15.html](http://http://www.journeytoforever.org/farm_library/price/price15.html)

### Michael — 2012-09-03T22:15:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-statement-in-cunnane.3375/post-49222>

> **Anna K.;49725 wrote:** actually he did, not every tribe he observed had access to the seafood. Some did indeed eat the liver and other organs of large game.

No, the Indian, Anna. The Indian didn't say, "Hang on while I catch you a ruminant, and get ahold of its liver." He started pulling fish out and telling him to eat the eyes and the flesh in back of the eyes.

I think that tells you two interesting things. First, you notice that there's a very good source of the vitamin A. Secondly, it reminds you of how much easier it can be to acquire food resources from a river - which is a point of great interest from the view of human evolution.

### Michael — 2012-09-03T22:33:45+0300

I think the point is that you can include food from _both_ water and land resources - shoreline scenario - but that the former, although better, has been rather neglected by the paleo movement.

I'd eat calves' liver, although I usually go for lambs' since they tend to be very unintensively reared.

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-31T20:15:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epipaleo-rx-and-increases-senses.3381/post-48598>

any one noticing an increase in their overall senses since switching to Epi-Pale RX?

the reason I ask... is well...  lets just say I've started noticing the opposite sex - as in I can instinctively tell which men have high T vs not having High T. I work in a building w/major eye candy for both men/women, as well as a representation of WDW and Walmart populations that Jack talks about. There are some very fit men and women running around the work place.

its crazy - there are guys who are no where fall into "my type" and I wouldn't consider them attractive but damn I've been doing the head swivel thing alot... hello there good looking...  the only thing I can think of is my senses have increased, and I can ID those men who have high T. there's no rhyme or reason to the build, height, coloring of the individual, most are lean and fit...but not all lean/fit and good looking turn my head...if you know what I'm saying

I really noticed it the other day as I was walking into the building - suddenly I was all aware and looking around, and this big (heck anyone's big given my height!), well defined guy walked past me and I about swooned (if I wasn't married I would have chased after him and given him my #  ). OMG WTF did that come from...the only thing I could think of was Inger's post on hormones smelling. so it seem my Radar has picked up who's got game vs those that done...

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?2185-Do-hormones-smell](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?2185-Do-hormones-smell)

obviously those in the WDW/Walmart category aren't setting off the "spidey" sense. there's even some fit/good looking guys not setting it off either....

DH is still working on raising his T...so the zing is coming back regarding my radar for him specifically... I can tell when he gets home based on his smell/aura etc if I'm going to get lucky at night....

I have also noticed, I'm getting more looks from both sexes as I carry out my day(even thought I've had no real change in weight in several months)... I think my mojo is coming back up

# Thread: PRICELESS!  My vet is talking about epigenetics
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/priceless-my-vet-is-talking-about-epigenetics.3382/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### indigogirl — 2012-08-31T20:19:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/priceless-my-vet-is-talking-about-epigenetics.3382/post-48600>

In my veterinarian's newsletter she talks about epigenetics!

Epigenetics - Or, Does My Labrador Have to Have Hip Dysplasia?

We all know of breeds that are genetically predisposed to certain diseases: labradors get hip dysplasia, rottweilers and goldens get cancer, chihuahuas have kneecaps that pop in and out. But what if there were things we could change so that those diseases didn't have to happen? When the Human Genome Project was completed, the researchers realized that genes were responsible not only for telling our cells how to look and act, making up each individuals unique appearance and biochemistry, but also what proteins, hormones, and co-factors to make or not make, influencing how our individual cells behave.

"Epigenetic changes represent a biological response to an environmental stressor that can be inherited through many generations via epigenetic marks. But if you remove the environmental pressure - the epigenetic marks will eventually fade and the DNA code - over time - will begin to revert to its original programming." {1}

So if our genome is influenced by our environment, what does that mean? There are some parts of our genome we have no control over, such as the air we breathe and the places in which we work. For our pets, life is much the same, but they have fewer controls over the environment we create for them. We decide what they eat, what they drink and how much they exercise. The other key piece of epigenetics is that each organism's response to the environment is individualistic.

The study of epigenetics has spawned several other fields of study. Nutrigenomics looks at how food and nutrients affect how our dna is expressed. Proteomics looks at how multiple proteins affect the body, or protein expression/activity patterns. Metabolomics looks at the effect of metabolites, or how products of biochemical reactions affect the body. We are familiar with the effect of eating broccoli on the body, knowing that it provides us with phytochemicals, many of which are not identified, but that protect our cells from cancer. Xenobiotics are the toxins in our environment that interact with our genes to cause certain proteins and cofactors to biochemical reactions to be released.

This is very confusing, so a simplistic explanation goes like this: if plastic residue that you drink in water causes a certain sequence of genes to produce a protein that causes cancer cells to not know to die, then a tumor has the opportunity to grow. If you eat broccoli, it down regulates the sequence of genes the plastic upregulates, and then upregulates a different sequence of genes that causes the individual cancer cells to die faster, because they recognize that they are abnormal. So, good nutrition in this example is offsetting harmful reactions.

Functional medicine for humans is a way to look at each individual and help their body function better by making specific recommendations about diet, supplements, exercise and lifestyle changes to get to the best level of health for each individual. It utilizes the concepts learned from epigenetics, metabolomics, and nutrigenomics to formulate a health plan, and recognizes that there is a gap between overt disease and wellness that is not being addressed. Like Traditional Chinese Medicine, Functional Medicine looks at the roots of disease rather than the symptoms or branches waving in the breeze. On September 10th, I am going to the Institute of Functional Medicine's Applying Functional Medicine in Clinical Practice Seminar. This course for human doctors (MDs) is designed to help more effectively integrate science, research, and clinical insights to treat and prevent disease and maintain health. Established and emerging diagnostics, therapeutics, and prevention strategies are extensively covered, including the use of diet, nutraceuticals, exercise, body/mind techniques, and the adaptation of lifestyle to an individual's genetic risks and environmental exposures. I hope to adapt this information for pets to help provide relief for several conditions that plague pets that are difficult to treat regardless of the way they are tackled, including skin disease, cancer and allergies."

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-31T23:41:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/priceless-my-vet-is-talking-about-epigenetics.3382/post-48653>

I'm seriously thinking about this as a second career.

T. Field

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-31T22:39:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-vitamin-d-injections.3386/post-48621>

> **bigknitwit;49094 wrote:** Interesting. If you take vitamin D, do you need the cofactors (K2 and A) for better absorption, or is that to aid in it's production with sunlight (or pregnenelone)? Also, do you need good pregnenelone to make D from sun exposure, or is it only needed to make D without sun?

yes. here's why (Jack correct me if I miss a step!) you need Vitamin A and T3 to convert LDL to Pregnenolone. Pregnenolone then has 2 choices as to where it goes "survival" (cortisol production) or "reproduction" (progesterone production) as part of that cascade -here's a pic - Jansz has posted this many times [http://img146.imageshack.us/img146/2681/pregnenolonesteal.jpg](http://http://img146.imageshack.us/img146/2681/pregnenolonesteal.jpg)

VitaminD3 is made alongside the hormone cascade [http://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S1570963910001810-gr7.jpg](http://http://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S1570963910001810-gr7.jpg)

I haven't quite figured out how it converts... that's some serious bio chem and I'm not there yet! but if you can't convert your LDL to pregnenolone you can't make Vitamin D3 either... there's a relationship...and to me that relationship is K2 and A. guess Its time to go back and read sunshine of your life here: [http://jackkruse.com/the-sunshine-of-your-life/](http://http://jackkruse.com/the-sunshine-of-your-life/)

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-01T01:13:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-vitamin-d-injections.3386/post-48669>

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?3475-Depression-Vitamin-D-and-the-August-webinar](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?3475-Depression-Vitamin-D-and-the-August-webinar)

> Depression, Vitamin D and the August webinar.
>
> So you go here and read this [http://evolutionarypsychiatry.blogsp...ctive-for.html](http://http://evolutionarypsychiatry.blogsp...ctive-for.html) and you come away with your own ideas of what is trying to conveyed here. The bigger question to be answered is why is low vitamin D an epidemic in the entire USA and especially in places like the Southwest where sun light is present in abundance. If you listened to the webinar I gave you that context.
>
>
>
> Then you listen to the webinar on the biochemistry of the Hormone 101 blog post. What biochemical step is four step ahead of endogenous Vitamin D production? Pregnenolone to progesterone conversion. That is the key missing step why the finding in the above said study and blog were found. Not putting that in proper context might give you a false impression that using exogenous Vitamin D is bad for you. A meme that may subjugate your paleolithic genes, mind you. When you fill the gas tank up the way evolution says it should be done, magical things begin to happen for our brain and our mood. exogenous Vitamin D3, by itself, is no pancea and most of you have heard that in your educational consults with me. I always tell you how and why it all fits in an evolutionary framework. Sometimes dissecting a study alone without your context in place can lead you astray. I want none of you lead astray. Understanding why the relationships between the hormones exist and how the work to improve brain function is how evolutionary medicine work.........only getting part of the story leads to moderation and may even hurt you. CONTEXT!!!!

Some of the things we find in our labs are like road signs to me.... it's like take this turn.... keep going here..... keep looking.... and if you do it long enough and well enough, eventually, you will get to the destination, and you find the true cause of your ailment that ties all the little mysterious arrows and hints on your personal treasure map together. And then it all makes sense.

Hi cave, I just downloaded an app for iPhone called D minder. my daughter thinks its hilarious it tells you when uv b rays are optimal based on location and profile...as in color of skin, age and what you are wearing. it times your sessions and beeps and then tells you how much vitamin d you got. lol so my point is that if one stays out ALL freak'n day it doesnt matter...there is a window of time to get vit d.

# Thread: Getting warm post CT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-warm-post-ct.3390/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TribalSpice — 2012-09-01T06:50:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-warm-post-ct.3390/post-48690>

I have been doing 30-40 minute ice baths around 12C. I stay cold for a number of hours after. I am not sure if I should be just embracing it or actively warming myself up with exercise, sitting in the sun etc.

Thanks.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-01T06:56:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-warm-post-ct.3390/post-48691>

Hi TS. This was discussed a lot when the CT blogs first started coming out. I think the general consensus was to 'embrace the cold'... I distinctly remember Dr K talking about drying off next to an air conditioner! I confess, however, that I needed to do squats or some other movement (walking) to warm up in order to tolerate it and hasten the warm-up time.

### freesia — 2012-09-01T09:31:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-warm-post-ct.3390/post-48694>

Hi, TribalSpice,

I couldn't exercise back in the beginning due to illness, and I did just what I needed to to get through the first few weeks. Warm up time was miserable and took 6 or 8 hours the first week, and I resorted to a warm bath one day when the sun didn't shine as an option. By week 3 warm up was 2 hours and much easier though some...ok most (!) Winter nights I did actively encourage my dog to snuggle up on my feet.

My advice is to embrace the cold post CT as much as possible...but don't push it to the extent it threatens to turn you off CT. How much you can tolerate may be different to others. For me everything took a little longer but I've just spent the whole Winter enjoying 7C baths and the many and varied benefits they bring...so I reckon I did get there.

### TribalSpice — 2012-09-01T12:33:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-warm-post-ct.3390/post-48698>

Thank you. I have just been embracing it, so I shall keep it up. I am not doing CT for weight loss (BMI of about 19), I actually stumbled across this site when I was googling cold adaption. I have always hated the cold and I am doing "Tough Mudder" in 3 weeks and there are a few ice bath type obstacles. So glad to find all this though as for a long time I have been preaching the leptin thing with out knowing exactly how in depth LR was.

### AKMan — 2012-09-01T17:57:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-warm-post-ct.3390/post-48739>

What warms me up the quickest is to stand in bright sunlight. The air temp isn't that important, the UV rays will really penetrate that cold skin and make you feel warm. If you are CTing in the day, give it a try.

### popcornsally — 2012-09-03T02:55:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-warm-post-ct.3390/post-49084>

> **AKMan;49233 wrote:** What warms me up the quickest is to stand in bright sunlight. The air temp isn't that important, the UV rays will really penetrate that cold skin and make you feel warm. If you are CTing in the day, give it a try.

My air temps are over 100. I go outside, jump in 90 degree pool, sit in sun. Nice and warm within a few minutes. Maybe I shouldn't be doing that. Shucks, after getting real cold with CT is the only time it feels good to get in the pool right now.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-06T21:55:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-warm-post-ct.3390/post-49832>

During and after CT, I've found that I'm just fine. No shivering. That is until I actually put on clothes, or worse, if I lay down on the bed. Even if its an hour after the bath, the shivering will then start in about 5-10 seconds. I think Jack said the slight warmth shuts down heat production by the BAT, and the muscles have to take over.

### Qodesh — 2012-09-06T23:24:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-warm-post-ct.3390/post-49851>

> **Nonchalant;50375 wrote:** During and after CT, I've found that I'm just fine. No shivering. That is until I actually put on clothes, or worse, if I lay down on the bed. Even if its an hour after the bath, the shivering will then start in about 5-10 seconds. I think Jack said the slight warmth shuts down heat production by the BAT, and the muscles have to take over.

Yes, I find the same thing. I can't even wrap a towel around myself or it causes me to feel colder.

### TribalSpice — 2012-09-06T23:37:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-warm-post-ct.3390/post-49854>

I have only shivered once. I jumped out of the bath and took my daughter to Little Athletics, it was 4:30pm and the sun was going down and the wind was picking up. I spent the 2 hours standing there shaking uncontrolably while all the other parents looked at me funny as it wasn't really that cold.

Really embracing it now, but I am also finding I am warming up much quicker.

### PrimalPam — 2012-09-07T02:44:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-warm-post-ct.3390/post-49890>

Yes, Dr. K. has definitely said "embrace the cold". He's gone as far as saying don't even dry off. Just walk around naked for the next several hours. Relating to what Nonchalant said...warmth shuts down heat production by BAT? I think.

I actually enjoy the way my cold body feels and looking at my pink skin.

My fingers and toes go numb about an hour after I'm out. I think it's because all the blood in my limbs is moving to my core to warm me down. So I find it hard to type or write...lol.

On days where I just have to get somewhere, I'll jump in a hot shower or sit in the hot sun for a while, which does work. But most days, I try to just deal with being really cold.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
