### [email redacted] — 2012-09-01T21:07:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-saturday.3397/post-48777>

Put each type of fish in a different jar. Poured marinade of lime juice, red onion, garlic, and red pepper flakes over it.

Putting this on ice in fridge for about 5 hrs.

This was very time intensive!

### ealachan — 2012-09-01T21:35:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-saturday.3397/post-48778>

5 hours?! Why so long? All the recipes I've ever been given for it say to "cook" it for no longer than 45 minutes or it will turn to mush. Curious to see how yours comes out.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-01T21:53:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-saturday.3397/post-48784>

> **ealachan;49274 wrote:** 5 hours?! Why so long? All the recipes I've ever been given for it say to "cook" it for no longer than 45 minutes or it will turn to mush. Curious to see how yours comes out.

ruh roh, I best go back and read! I just ate a burger for lunch and I'm full - so I hope it's not done! Plus, I haven't gone to the store for the rest of the ingredients. Who said this was easy?!

Ok, here is where I got the 5 hrs, I clearly didn't pay enough attention - each food has different times.

[http://www.customcatering.net/Rec\_hdo/Creole\_Ceviche\_Recipe.html#Basic\_Marinade:](http://http://www.customcatering.net/Rec_hdo/Creole_Ceviche_Recipe.html#Basic_Marinade:)

I think scallops should be about done. Good Gravy.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-02T02:35:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-saturday.3397/post-48826>

I've let my ceviche "cook" anywhere from 1-5 hours. Also when done I just drain the juice, add the cilantro, and it's good for another day. Much more than that and it continues to cook and get rubbery. Luckily I've never had mushy ceviche, just rubbery! Let us know how it is! I just found a new fish store only 25 min away that had some beautiful stuff.

Found a fish store for Souldanzer too! They had a whole container of heads in the fish case. they were not small fish heads like the mackerel but between a mackerel and a salmon. Too big for my blender so I didn't get any.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-02T02:47:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-saturday.3397/post-48834>

> **Patty Cakes;49325 wrote:**
>
> Found a fish store for Souldanzer too! They had a whole container of heads in the fish case. they were not small fish heads like the mackerel but between a mackerel and a salmon. Too big for my blender so I didn't get any.

I have another Asian store in the area that does sell those big fish heads. I've refrained so far for exactly that reason.... I don't want to break my blender and I would have to chop them up before throwing them in.... I'll stick with the smaller stuf. THough I did find a couple of cod fish heads at the Denver Asian store. And now that I know that Cavemam's local fish store can order fish heads for me...I will try that out, too.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-02T02:54:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-saturday.3397/post-48839>

This was a little Filipino market and they had some great looking smaller fish heads and all. I bought a couple of Sculpans (or something liek that) that the guy said he would clean and fillet for me. I wanted just some white fish no smoothie stuff. I get a sliced headless fish, w/ skin and fins still on. Very bizarre but it cooked up nicely in olive oil and tasted great. Probably a culture difference and he thought he was doing exactly what I wanted. Doesn't matter as the taste was there, although did require a little extra work. But fresh (clear eyes) and small fish. I know where I'm going to get small fish next time for smoothies!!!

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-02T02:58:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-saturday.3397/post-48844>

Left them all about 3.5 hrs. The halibut and scallops seemed fine, shrimp not quite enough cooked me thinks. . .I added tomatoes and cilantro (from my garden!) along with avocado and a little rice wine vinegar. Looked horrid! Next time I cut into littler chunks I think. .

Might try my fancy food processor that is supposed to chop into even pieces. . probably will marinate faster too.

In the end, it was all floating in this grey liquid and looked unappetizing. It tasted awesome tho!!! I ate as much as I could stand (I didn't think there was a limit to my ceviche hunger!) and made my son eat it for dinner, too.

I hope it keeps till tomorrow, that is what he gets for bfast!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-02T03:01:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-saturday.3397/post-48845>

Looks great! Wish I could have me some of them there nightshades. Ceviche really isn't the same w/o tomatoes and some jalapenos chopped in.

### Endless — 2012-09-02T03:36:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-saturday.3397/post-48860>

I used fresh scallops for a quick ceviche this morning....chopped them up, squeezed 1/2 a big lime over them, and left them while I did the dishes, about 15 min. Then I added pepper, salt, chopped cilantro and a couple drops of tobasco (a no-no for me but wanted to see how it tasted...)

I can see how much more 'cooked' it would be after an hour, but I liked it with just the outside texture changed and the insides still soft.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-02T03:37:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-saturday.3397/post-48861>

> **Endless;49359 wrote:** I used fresh scallops for a quick ceviche this morning....chopped them up, squeezed 1/2 a big lime over them, and left them while I did the dishes, about 15 min. Then I added pepper, salt, chopped cilantro and a couple drops of tobasco (a no-no for me but wanted to see how it tasted...)
>
>
>
> I can see how much more 'cooked' it would be after an hour, but I liked it with just the outside texture changed and the insides still soft.

I'll try this next time! Scallops are my favorite and I hated limes after squeezing 20 of them to get a pint of juice!

### Endless — 2012-09-02T03:43:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ceviche-saturday.3397/post-48863>

I don't think you need that much juice....I think cutting the fish smaller will make it 'cook' pretty quickly. That's how it is usually served in Mexico anyway...fine enough to scoop up with tortilla chips.

It was pretty pricey for the fresh scallops though...definitely going to try with frozen next time. I have not tried shrimp yet but I am looking forward to that!

# Thread: Ketogenic Seafood Nutrition
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketogenic-seafood-nutrition.3398/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 33
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-09-01T20:11:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketogenic-seafood-nutrition.3398/post-48771>

All summer, I have been eating plenty of seafod and fruit/veg. I checked the nutrition profile several times with FitDay.com and have easily been busting 100% RDA for everything (except maybe fiber/carbs).

As I transistion into winter eating, I will be dropping most fruit and vegetables. When I plug a sample day into Fitday, I find I am lacking in quite a few nutrients.

For instance; 8 oz raw oysters, 8 oz raw salmon, 1 oz mac nuts, 3TBS butter, 3TBS CO Oil, and 4 eggs is very low on Vit C, Vit E, Calcium, and Magnesium. It is also super-high in Vit B12, Copper, Iron, Selenium and Zinc.

The only way I can come up with to get C, E, Calcium and Magnesium is to add in a bunch of leafy greens, but that ups the carb count quite high.

Have you all been giving any thought to nutrients like this?

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-01T21:35:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketogenic-seafood-nutrition.3398/post-48779>

I think some of those nutrients are found in the organs of fish/meat and the bones..... so you'd have to add bones in some way for cal (bone broth or fish small enough to be eaten whole?). Vitamin E and C is in some organs I think.... adrenals/liver for C? Mg you would need less if you're keto b/c you're not dumping it due to high BG maybe? You also don't need as much of some nutrients if you're eating the correct things, calcium I think is one of those.

I guess what I'm saying is that your analysis might be a bit skewed b/c you didn't plug in the whole fish. In winter you'd also replace mac nuts and CO b/c that's out of season and eggs are technically a spring food. And I would never make it through the day on that little food

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-02T02:33:07+0300

### AKMan — 2012-09-02T03:42:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketogenic-seafood-nutrition.3398/post-48862>

I have access to a lot of wild things high in vit C and polyphenols, namely rose hips, cranberries, and spruce needles--all which are easily found in Winter here in AK. The point of this thread, I guess, is that people should not get a false sense of security if they are eating fish fillets, grass-fed beef and eggs. It's not that hard to get the whole range of micronutrients without bananas and potatoes, but it does require some thought.

The best thing I have in my winter arsenal is a mushroom called Chaga. It only grows on Birch trees in the far north and is being studied extensively for it's anti-cancer, anti-diabetes, anti-just-about-everything. Click here to read a wiki article on this miracle mushroom. It is found online for sale from Russia mostly. I get it free!

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chaga\_mushroom](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chaga_mushroom)

"Since the 16th century, chaga mushrooms were recorded as being used in folk medicine and the botanical medicine of the Eastern European countries as a remedy for cancer, gastritis, ulcers, and tuberculosis of the bones. A review from 2010 stated, "As early as in the 16th century, chaga was used as an effective folk medicine in Russia and Northern Europe to treat several human malicious tumors and other diseases in the absence of any unacceptable toxic side effects."

Chemical investigations show I. obliquus produces a range of secondary metabolites, including phenolic compounds, melanins, and lanostane-type triterpenoids, including a small percentage of betulinic acid. Among these are the active components for antioxidant, antitumoral, and antiviral activities and for triggering the human immune system."

### Glen PDQ — 2012-09-02T04:27:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketogenic-seafood-nutrition.3398/post-48876>

Beef spleen supplies a good amount of vitamin C. There's one in my freezer as I type this.

What about cold brewed rose hip tea for C? Has anyone tried blanching parsley/cilantro and then doing a cold brew method on it overnight for a tea?

Annatto is supposed to contain exclusively delta and gamma tocotrienals as opposed to tocopherol vitamin E. Not sure if tocotrienols replace the function of tocopherols. I read somewhere that tocopherols inhibit the absorption of tocotrienols. Perhaps one could infuse a bottle of macadamia oil or avocado oil using ground annatto since it is fat soluble, then filter. I hear the tocotrienols are great in their own way.

### Michael — 2012-09-02T18:52:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketogenic-seafood-nutrition.3398/post-48975>

> **AKMan;49266 wrote:**
>
> The only way I can come up with to get C, E, Calcium and Magnesium is to add in a bunch of leafy greens, but that ups the carb count quite high.

I don't think you can consider Ca and Mg apart.

My guess is that the current RDA for Ca may be too high. Cordain is adamant that it's very difficult to get up to the level of the RDA _unless you include dairy products. He says it's not difficult to hit around 60% of the RDA, however. Maybe that's telling us something.

I guess it would be interesting to go back and look up the paperwork the RDA is based on and see _why_ they set the figure where they did.

It does seem awfully high, and countries where the Ca/Mg ratio is particularly high seem to have a range of problems like heart disease and atherosclerosis (c.f. Finland).

I don't know how much solid anthropological data there is on intake of these minerals. Weston Price claimed that the intake of most minerals (including calcium) was several times higher in the populations he looked at compared with the average American diet. However, that leaves one with no quantifiable figure if the figure for the "average American diet" (of the 1930s) isn't stated, which it seems not to be in his published work. I think he does say somewhere that half to one gram per day of Ca is necessary.

Where the RDA of 1.2 grams comes from I just don't know.

I think there are good reasons for thinking that magnesium must have been more abundant in our past than it is now. Supplementing it seems to fix so many issues that the suspicion has to be strong. I believe it's there in reasonable concentrations in seawater. If our ancestors were not only eating food from shorelines but wading, and perhaps diving to get it, in estuarine areas, I'd think they were getting plenty of Mg. I suppose that you would get some transdermally just by being in the water.

Also, modern farming methods deplete magnesium from the soil, as does acid rain. And stress, which modern life certainly brings, causes you to burn through it.

I think I might be content with less than RDA as regards Ca but I think I would supplement Mg.

Vitamin C is an interesting one. Can you consider this one apart from the context of the whole diet? As has been said there are animal sources that are high, such as adrenal glands. But it seems that less might be needed where the consumption of carbohydrate is lower. If Nansen and Johannsen overwintering on the push "furthest north" and eating only_ freshly-killed undercooked meat -

[http://www.amazon.com/Farthest-North-Adventure-Visionary-Explorer/dp/1602392374/](http://http://www.amazon.com/Farthest-North-Adventure-Visionary-Explorer/dp/1602392374/)

\- didn't show that, then I guess that the Bellevue Hospital experiment did:

[http://highsteaks.com/the-fat-of-the-land-not-by-bread-alone-vilhjalmur-stefansson.pdf](http://http://highsteaks.com/the-fat-of-the-land-not-by-bread-alone-vilhjalmur-stefansson.pdf)

I don't think anyone knows why this is, but I think it's been said that glucose and vitamin C use the same transport system and it might be linked to that.

But it's possible to get a relatively high intake of vitamin C without pushing the carbohydrate content too high, anyway.

The other one's E?

That's fairly high in almonds. Maybe you could swap your macadamias out for almonds. Almonds would probably give you a little more in the way of calcium, too. Olive oil is a reasonable source of E, as well. You could swap some of those saturated fats out in favour of some olive oil.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-03T02:03:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketogenic-seafood-nutrition.3398/post-49074>

I'm wondering about the concept of 'RDA' in general. Recommended daily allowance. As if every day is the same. If we are minding circadian cycles and seasonal temperature cycles, as well as seasonal eating, doesn't it stand to reason that our need for certain vitamins and minerals will be up at some times of year and lower at others? I'm not sure how to carry this through to particular recommendations, but I'm guessing they are individual based both on time of year and the context of one's own state of health and particular needs.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-03T07:04:58+0300

> **Anna K.;49623 wrote:**
>
>
>
> What is a good source of vit E on epi-paleo?

"Seaweed contains several vitamins both water soluble such as B and C and lipid soluble such as A and E at varying levels. Brown seaweed contained 14.5mg/100g vitamine E and that was much higher than the vitamin E content in peanuts. This high vitamin E content helps protect PUFA in seaweed and to maintain their nutritional benefits."

from Marine Medicinal Foods, p. 20, Elsevier Press, 2011

### Michael — 2012-09-03T22:48:00+0300

> **Anna K.;49734 wrote:**
>
> Nuts, seeds and grains seems to be the highest in vit E, may be these foods need vitamin E to protect the Pufa from oxidizing, as you said, but if you don't eat them, you don't need as much vit E as RDA recommends.

it's an interesting point. IOW, have they set the level of E where they've set it, because they told people to eat lots of PUFAs (sunflower oil and so on)?

I don't actually know.

As Lauren said, what about 'the concept of 'RDA' in general? I think some of these levels are based on recommending a level of a nutrient where an intake of at least that level seems not to result in overt deficiency symptoms related to that nutrient. This seems to be why the dietary level of D is set as low as it is. But while you may not get overt symptoms at the RDA, you'd seem to be very far from optimal at that level.

# Thread: Modern medicine catching on
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/modern-medicine-catching-on.3399/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### msclman — 2012-09-01T21:01:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/modern-medicine-catching-on.3399/post-48776>

small step but a step.

Fever Control Using External Cooling in Septic Shock

A Randomized Controlled Trial

FrÃ©dÃ©rique Schortgen1,2, Karine Clabault3, Sandrine Katsahian4, Jerome Devaquet5, Alain Mercat6, Nicolas Deye7, Jean Dellamonica8, Lila Bouadma9, Fabrice Cook10, Olfa Beji1, Christian Brun-Buisson1, FranÃ§ois Lemaire1 and Laurent Brochard1,2,11

\+ Author Affiliations

Rationale: Fever control may improve vascular tone and decrease oxygen consumption, but fever may contribute to combat infection.

Objectives: To determine whether fever control by external cooling diminishes vasopressor requirements in septic shock.

Methods: In a multicenter randomized controlled trial, febrile patients with septic shock requiring vasopressors, mechanical ventilation, and sedation were allocated to external cooling (n = 101) to achieve normothermia (36.5â€“37Â°C) for 48 hours or no external cooling (n = 99). Vasopressors were tapered to maintain the same blood pressure target in the two groups. The primary endpoint was the number of patients with a 50% decrease in baseline vasopressor dose after 48 hours.

Measurements and Main Results: Body temperature was significantly lower in the cooling group after 2 hours of treatment (36.8 Â± 0.7 vs. 38.4 Â± 1.1Â°C; P < 0.01). A 50% vasopressor dose decrease was significantly more common with external cooling from 12 hours of treatment (54 vs. 20%; absolute difference, 34%; 95% confidence interval \[95% CI\], −46 to −21; P < 0.001) but not at 48 hours (72 vs. 61%; absolute difference, 11%; 95% CI, −23 to 2). Shock reversal during the intensive care unit stay was significantly more common with cooling (86 vs. 73%; absolute difference, 13%; 95% CI, 2 to 25; P = 0.021). Day-14 mortality was significantly lower in the cooling group (19 vs. 34%; absolute difference, â€“16%; 95% CI, −28 to −4; P = 0.013).

Conclusions: In this study, fever control using external cooling was safe and decreased vasopressor requirements and early mortality in septic shock.

[http://ajrccm.atsjournals.org/content/185/10/1088.abstract?sid=907d8d9a-c1f3-4d55-bd88-786877bfee0c](http://http://ajrccm.atsjournals.org/content/185/10/1088.abstract?sid=907d8d9a-c1f3-4d55-bd88-786877bfee0c)


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Confused about Webinars
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-about-webinars.3404/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-09-02T04:27:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-about-webinars.3404/post-48875>

Hi, Would someone out there please, please, please explain to me how the heck to access the webinars???? Are they archived anywhere? Thanks.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-02T04:45:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-about-webinars.3404/post-48878>

Jerry, it has to do with your membership status. If you read the "Members' tab on the site it will explain what different membership confers. FYI, membership prices are set to change mid-September. If you are not a paid member you can't get webinars for download. However, you can buy one-off 'tickets' to individual webinars (though I think you need to sign up for the free membership to get the emails announcing upcoming webinars). Good luck.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-02T05:03:45+0300

After the changes, you will only have access to the previous three months. So as time goes by, access to earlier webinars will drop off. If you want to see, and/or download all the webinars, become a Karnivore now!

If you have questions contact misty at [[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted]) or [[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted])

# Thread: Magnesium Malate vs. Magnesium Oxide
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-malate-vs-magnesium-oxide.3406/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### mambo — 2012-09-02T07:26:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-malate-vs-magnesium-oxide.3406/post-48902>

Started taking 400mg of Magnesium Malate after hearing that it might help with diabetic neuropathy. I have peripheral and possibly small fiber neuropathy arising from diabetes and/or autoimmune conditions.

I tried Magnesium Malate but I did not like it as much as NatureMade's Magnesium Oxide 400mg, which I had been taking before. 400mg of Magnesium Oxide is really helpful in making me fall and stay asleep. Anyone with experience with Magnesium Malate vs. Magnesium Aspartate?

I'm thinking about going back to Magensium Oxide. I also take 900mg of Alpha Lipoic Acid (with 10mg of Biotin) for neuropathy.

### AKMan — 2012-09-02T07:36:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-malate-vs-magnesium-oxide.3406/post-48903>

A smart man once said:

Remember from the mitochondrial series or the Gnoll’s post that Magnesium is a co factor in making every last bit of ATP in the mitochondria. So if you don’t have optimal magnesium you are not going to be energy positive or optimal. T2D have the lowest levels of Mg I have ever seen. When people begin to eat paleo they usually are depleted in magnesium. They complain of poor sleep and develop constipation and muscle aches pretty often when they first adopt a paleo diet within the first few weeks. I offset this with a pretty hefty supplemented dose of Magnesium malate usually two hours prior to bed. If they need more I add a morning dose. This should never be taken with AM Vitamin D3 because they compete with one another for absorption. I am also a big believer in the use of Epsom salts topically in those with big depletion risks. There is another big benefit that comes with topical epsom salt use and that is increasing the sulfur intake topically as well. Those neolithic diseases include T2D, sleep apnea sufferers, restless leg syndrome, and those with fibromyalgia. Sulfur intake is notoriously poor in vegetarian diets and helps explain why they suffer from so many skin and connective tissue disorders (psoriasis, eczema, dandruff, folliculitis (infected hair follicles), warts, and pityriasis versicolor, vitalize are some). Sulfur is an important component of our body’s proteins. It is present in our hair, skin, nails and tendons, and plays a critical role in maintaining the integrity and elasticity of connective tissues too. After adopting a paleo diet, if the patient still complains of persistent skin issues I usually suspect a sulphur deficiency and recommend Epsom salt baths. Sulfur is an element that is part of four amino acids called methionine, cysteine, cystine and taurine. Sulfur is found in many animal meats and eggs, so longer term it need not to be supplemented on a long term paleo diet. This is very common in veggie to paleo conversions because they are so deficient and tend not to become ravenous carnivores right off the bat. So this is a real nice way to get some sulfur into your body to get optimal. If you need more you can consider a sulfur supplement like dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and methylsulfonylmethane (MSM). I like Magnesium malate 800 mgs two hours prior to bed. If you need more add 400 mgs in the AM or eat dark chocolate daily. For those with inflammatory brain diseases, like obesity, MS, AD, PD, and most any AI disease I use a special version of mag called Magnesium Threonate because it penetrates the CSF of the central nervous system while no other formulation can.

From [http://jackkruse.com/what-are-the-top-ten-paleo-supplements/](http://http://jackkruse.com/what-are-the-top-ten-paleo-supplements/)

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-02T17:15:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-malate-vs-magnesium-oxide.3406/post-48942>

iirc mg oxide is the least well absorbed form of mg, and the most difficult for the GI tract to handle. I would stick with Dr K's rec's above - mg malate probably is a good choice, and also add the mg threonate. I think mg glycinate is another good from that is easy on the GI tract.

If you have T2D you also want to add topical mg oil.

### mambo — 2012-09-19T06:14:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-malate-vs-magnesium-oxide.3406/post-52638>

I hear ya. But for some reason, I seem to fall asleep better with oxide than with malate. Maybe I jus handle the capsule (oxide) better than the tablet (malate).

> **Souldanzer;49442 wrote:** iirc mg oxide is the least well absorbed form of mg, and the most difficult for the GI tract to handle.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-09-19T16:34:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-malate-vs-magnesium-oxide.3406/post-52682>

mambo - just a thought. But malate is a large compound - so are you getting the equivalent amount of magnesium between the two?

Often the supplements list the mg of magnesium malate - but the mg of the amount of magnesium you're getting is much much less.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-19T16:38:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-malate-vs-magnesium-oxide.3406/post-52684>

> **mambo;53262 wrote:** I hear ya. But for some reason, I seem to fall asleep better with oxide than with malate. Maybe I jus handle the capsule (oxide) better than the tablet (malate).

I hedge my bets and mix it up, taking both malate and chelated at bedtime. Take both!

### ealachan — 2012-09-19T16:51:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-malate-vs-magnesium-oxide.3406/post-52687>

I take threonate and while it doesn't make me straight-up sleepy like oxide did, it does relax me and I do notice a difference if I go a few days without it. Another difference I've noticed is that oxide made me have loose bowels (possibly because it had stearate in it?), where as threonate doesn't do that at all.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-19T16:57:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-malate-vs-magnesium-oxide.3406/post-52689>

I take Mg Threonate midday (btwn 3-6pm) then I take Mg Malate at night 1-2hrs before bed (usually 750mg but sometimes I up it to 1000mg)

### kathylu — 2012-09-19T17:08:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-malate-vs-magnesium-oxide.3406/post-52693>

I have read that mg oxide has only 10% of the absorbability of some of the other forms, so it is really a waste of money if you are trying to up your intracellular Mg levels. I'd follow the above advice, mg threonate in the morning, malate at night and work on your sleep issue in some other fashion. That's what I do.

### Jonathin — 2012-09-19T17:38:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-malate-vs-magnesium-oxide.3406/post-52702>

Magnesium oxide needs a very acidic environment to to turn into an absorbable salt. Your stomach may not have that environment. I determined this by mixing it with citric acid crystals and had to mix it with only a tiny amount of water to get it to react. If you can find it, getting pure magnesium malate powder might be the answer to tabs with added ingredients. Asparatate can be a neuro-stimlatant - something like glutamate. Besides the mag threonate, I am liking the mag glycinate powder from Nutrabio.

### mambo — 2012-10-08T06:32:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-malate-vs-magnesium-oxide.3406/post-56864>

That I agree with and maybe that's the reason I fall asleep well with Oxide: I get sleepy far quicker and easier. I dont' get that same effect with Malate and that's why I've been relying on Oxide --- send me off to a tranquil sleep.

> **ealachan;53313 wrote:** I take threonate and while it doesn't make me straight-up sleepy like oxide did, it does relax me and I do notice a difference if I go a few days without it. Another difference I've noticed is that oxide made me have loose bowels (possibly because it had stearate in it?), where as threonate doesn't do that at all.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-10-08T07:54:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-malate-vs-magnesium-oxide.3406/post-56867>

Glycinate form releases mg ions more readily than alot of other forms including malate. Oxide form only has a 4% absorption rate from what I've read. Don't get me wrong as malate is still a decent form. I asked a local compounding pharmacy if they could make mg threonate for me and they said they were not able, and I haven't gone to the trouble to order online since I can get glycinate at my local HFS.

### Dean6789 — 2017-07-04T00:17:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-malate-vs-magnesium-oxide.3406/post-217874>

I found this magnesium malate powder: [https://www.vitacost.com/natures-life-magnesium-malate-12-7-oz](https://www.vitacost.com/natures-life-magnesium-malate-12-7-oz) which has malic acid in it. I'm unfamiliar with malic acid. Is it a healthy substance?

# Thread: Help.. looks like I am far from optimal...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-looks-like-i-am-far-from-optimal.3407/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Inger — 2012-09-02T10:07:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-looks-like-i-am-far-from-optimal.3407/post-48907>

If someone have a clue about how to convert these labs / or how bad are they actually.. I just cannot find the right converter online.

I totally forgot to take the bloodtests at day 21 of my period so I did them day 11. Bummer. I need to do new ones I think. I wonder if my labs still can tell something about whats up with my hormones? First time ever I took these labs so I have nothing to compare with sadly, I will test again in spring after a long, cold, dark winter.. HA. It will be my N1. test very scientifically. I just love it!

Anyway, here are the results;

Estradiol 1,1 nmol/l (299,6 pg/ml) (1100 pmol)

Progesterone 2,4 nmol/l (755 pg/ml) (2400 pmol)

Testosteron 1,2 nmol/l (1200 pmol)

Testosteron free 13 pmol/l

Testosteron free % 1,1

DHEA 9,0 (nmol/l..? not sure about this as she said it so unclear on the phone, but range=1,9 - 17)

Glucose 5,3 nmol/l (90 mg/dl)

HS CRP 0,22 mg/l

I guess my hormones are far from optimal still. But not nonexistent though. DHEA is way too little I guess? And my clucose is too high..?

And my HS CRP could be much closer to 0? Should it be 0 actually? I live in a little moldy house I wonder if that has any impact but I feel great though. I hope the swimming in he cold river every morning makes the mold not hurt me.. and I always have my windows wide open all day long and I have lots of green plants in my appartement too!

But considering I have separated this spring, loosing my home.. living quite stressful times.. and yeah.. I do cheat a little at times with some HWC and dark chocolate they both do me not good I know that so I guess it is time to get 100 % strict! I need optimal hormones, nothing less!!!

I will do more labs like vit D. cholesterol, B12 and such when I have the money in a few weeks.

I guess my vit D is very good, since I have been tanning a lot... at least it was around 90 last year without supplements and my HDL was very good too back then.

Pics from my butt these days.. I always had the fat go to my lower body and struggled with cellulite. It is so much better now but still room for much improvement. I could need to do some exercise and I bet next spring will be improvement to see!

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

..

I am sure when my hormones optimize further it will fill out nice and curvy and get more firm!

Uh.. I wish I knew how to convert to the units that are used in this forum! Any help much appreciated.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-02T19:23:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-looks-like-i-am-far-from-optimal.3407/post-48989>

Inger - the first thing needed is the ranges - without these the figures are difficult to interpret. Each laboratory is different.

Progesterone 2.4nmol/l converts to 0.75ng/ml - low but where should it be for day 11?

Testosterone 1.2nmol/l converts to 34.5ng/dl - low but where should it be for day 11?

Your Estradiol conversion to pg/ml looks fine.

I'm not an expert on P/E ratios so hopefully one of the experts will calculate and comment.

DHEA - I think this might be in ng/ml. You need to be in the top 25% of the range - so 14 plus to be optimal.

HS-CRP - you are correct - this needs to be as low as possible. My last labs came in at 0.38. Jack told me he wants me to be aiming at 0.03.

Glucose - I think the units should be mmol/l and i'm guessing the range is roughly from 4 to 6. Your figure is 5.3 and so converts to 95.5mg/dl. Anything over 99mg/dl is a definite no so your figure is quite high. Definitely a fasting blood test?

Body comp will improve as hormones improve but you look like you are well on the way and looking great with your eating and CT. Hormones may have suffered due to the stress you were/are under. Sex hormones are sacrificed to make more cortisol. A salivary cortisol would be interesting.

I wouldn't start taking out the odd treat at this point in my opinion - it mght stress you more!

### Inger — 2012-09-02T19:53:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-looks-like-i-am-far-from-optimal.3407/post-48994>

Thank you so much Corvus! I will do all to get HS CRP to 0,03! Wow that is really low! But I can see it doable, as I still see room for improvement with my foods! Like quitting dairy 100% (I have it occasionally, not often but it must be enough to mess up). I guess I should, when, only use this raw organic goatmilk that I can buy here, it is from a very old Finnish breed. No 85% black and green chocolate eather, or 90% Lindt. It is NOT good for me, I notice it on my skin fast!

Corvus what are you doing now to get it that low? I am curious! Do you have any advice for me except of what I mentioned above?

Here are the Hormone ranges that was on the lab-paper;

Estradiol 1,1 nmol/l (299,6 pg/ml) (1100 pmol)

Range;

Follikular 0.07-0.53 nmol/l

Ovulation 0.23-1.31 nmol/l

Luteal 0.20-0.79 nmol/l

Progesterone 2,4 nmol/l (755 pg/ml) (2400 pmol)

Range;

Follikular

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-02T22:44:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-looks-like-i-am-far-from-optimal.3407/post-49044>

Day 11 is most likely follicular phase, depending on your cycle.

FBG should be under 85 but I think you really want for it to be in the 70s.

I think your results just mean that you need to fix some more things. Don't despair. Knowing you used to have an ED - I think we are some of the more broken people. I think your body might need a winter... were you eating carbs during the summer? CT and epi-paleo rx will get your hs CRP down.... and fixing your hormones. Keep eating your seafood....

Are you taking magnesium? Good for the blood sugar. As is vitamin K.

### mambo — 2012-09-03T10:13:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-looks-like-i-am-far-from-optimal.3407/post-49155>

Fasting BG numbers are just a snapshot on that particular morning, so I wouldn't worry too much over a 96 reading; it will be high depending on what you ate the night before, your level of stress, liver dump, etc. If you want to conclude something from FBG, measure a weekly average and see where that falls. Under 90, good. Over 95 and closer to 125, closer to insulin resistance but there is such a thing as physiological insulin resistance if you're doing very low carb.

Also, 0.22 in HS CRP should be regarded as excellent. I don't know anyone with zero CRP. Some labs (LabCorp) may not even measure something in increments that small (as .03).

> **Inger;49406 wrote:**
>
> Glucose 5,3 nmol/l (90 mg/dl)
>
> HS CRP 0,22 mg/l And my HS CRP could be much closer to 0? Should it be 0 actually?

# Thread: Iodine again
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-again.3408/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-09-02T10:43:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-again.3408/post-48908>

So here's another thing. I now understand why Jack said that while Steve Phinney often cited the Innuit he "doesn't get it". The Innuit, or most of them, eat plentifully of seafood.

Phinney also refers to the Plains Indians. These are two groups most of the paleosphere would probably rather forget about.

Looked at from the point of brain-selective nutrients, I guess the Plains Indians with plenty of meat & that coming from bison grazing on rich, undisturbed prairie would be fine. And they'd have no goitrogens in their diet.

Here's another thought. It may be totally wrong. The low carb people say to take 3 to 5 grams of salt a day. That seems to be because VLC makes the kidneys dump sodium.

However, Cunnane says that SEVERAL steps in ketosis are iodine-dependent. Is it possible that many people starting VLC are marginally deficient in iodine and that that three-quarters of a teaspoon of salt is also enough to ease them into ketosis through providing that? But maybe I'm getting far-fetched here.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-02T16:39:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-again.3408/post-48931>

You mike are getting it...........Jimmy Moore, Volek, Rosedale, Phinney.......none of them talk about it. If you go back and look at my top ten supplement post.......guess what is up high? Iodine. I also put a thread on PH a yr ago saying the same thing. No one gets it because the meme of paleo is meat and liver and bacon........and it misses the key points of what a human brain needs. In Matt LaLonde's recent AHS talk he was lauded for his nutrient density idea. Let me let you know what he missed on big time. Nutrient density has to be placed in context of what the mammal is. When the mammal has an organ like a brain that is an energy hog, this implies that the nutrient density has to take the energy hog into account. There is a meme in paleo set in force by Wolf, Cordain and others that say eating meat is what humans became human. Nikoly just tweeted that out not too long ago and I responded to the tweet this answer: Not exactly. Rich and Robb are good friends. Rich knows that Robb makes his money from the paleo community. This is major source of bias. When people become aware of what you are you now will begin to realize the meme has been wrong from the get go. What happens then? The key is to get it right. When you are the guy telling the community its leader may not be correct on major points.......well you can bet you will have a problem. He is a nice guy and very likeable but he does not like someone who is stirring a hornets nest that could upset the franchise he and Cordain has built. To Cordain's credit his first book warned about saturated fats........but for the wrong reasons. They are not good with seafood. They matter little with offal or meat. Matt LaLonde has just re enforced that mistake with his latest speech at AHS. People now think sat is a OK all the time.........well it is not. And when you realize the work of Cunnane, Crawford, Tobias and Kuipers (and many more) you have to begin to look at things differently. I do.......because I take care of sick people. The leaders do not. They are just driving the movement. I am OK with it because it is a better lifestyle than a SAD any day of the week. But when you are sick and ill......you should not settle for better than a SAD.......you should get optimal. That is the source of my concern. Iodine is critical.......but so is foods with all the brain specific nutrients I laid out in Brain Gut 5. I just want my readers dialed into this.........information and then decide for themselves. The most ironic thing about this community is that all on their sites their are thousands of people who are not getting the "expected paleo results" that Sisson and Wolf trump every week. My question is why? The answer on PH is work out more eat less and eat more carbs.........Try it. See what happens. Then try my way. See what happens. When you test smartly and see what is under the hood things kind change your perspective. The leaders of paleo are not clinicians..........and that is a major problem for patients. Patients know their is a disconnect and they are trying to find people to help them. There is a vacuum void for patients with clinicians. Listen to people who own gyms, are nice folks, and write books are great.......but when your labs are trashed and your hormone panel is a mess and you fell like \*\*\*\*........what are those things going to get you?

### Michael — 2012-09-02T17:26:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-again.3408/post-48948>

_The Paleo Answer_ Cordain's latest book. In the index under "Iodine" we have 65-67 and 142.

I looked those up: those references are discussing it in the context of vegetarianism.

> The good news is that antinutritional compounds have virtually no effect on our thyroid glands when out bodies' stores of iodine are normal and full. Because meat, fish, eggs, and poultry are rich sources of iodine, you will never have to worry about this nutrient when you eat Paleo style.

Never is a long time.

The reader, passing over that passage is then going to forget the issue.

But wouldn't that rather depend on the level of intake of fish (and eggs)? Would it not be affected by what the animals and birds from which the meat and poultry (less good sources anyway) come have been fed on? And there's no mention of shellfish or seaweed as desirable foods to include in the diet.

One might say the issue of iodine is raised only for it to be skated over and seen solely as a problem for vegetarians.

No hint either that Cunnane has identified it as \*the\* most important brain-specific nutrient out of five very important ones. No discussion of the role it looks to have played in encephalization. And it's not just anybody hypothesizing all this. Cunanne is an expert in fatty acid metabolism. His foreword is by Professor Michael Crawford, PhD, CBiol, FIBio, FRCPath previously director of a institute of brain chemistry and human nutrition, now, by the looks, at Imperial College, London, one of the most prestigious science places in the world.

Yes. Since our bodies expect a constant supply, we need a constant seafood diet.

Long, long ago, I found it puzzling why iodine was added to salt. Mom told me it was to prevent goiter, and wasn't available in most food. What is goiter? Where does iodine come from?

### Michael — 2012-09-06T10:35:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-again.3408/post-49700>

Nearly got to the end of the book.

There's some interesting if very speculative stuff coming from a U.S. scientist called Jerome Dobson summarized.

He's interesting on the Venus figurines. I'd always assumed that these were highly stylized representations. I have heard people say they showed that Paleolithic women must have been fat, but that never made any sense to me. Some of them have no faces, so would that indicate that some Paleolithic women had no faces? Absurd.

It seems Dobson claims to find four different morphological features in them, including the protuberant belly, that he says are indicative of cretinism. If it were just the body-fat, it would be one thing, but that's a more detailed and complex claim. I think one would have to look carefully at a lot of these figurines to see whether what he thinks he sees could be susbstantiated, but at least there is something sort of testable there.

If he is right, what would be interesting would be that it would show that the Paleolithic diet - the actual thing not "paleo" - was not always optimal for human beings.

### Glamazon — 2012-09-06T15:49:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-again.3408/post-49736>

One it is a masturbatory image; small, portable, no face. Second, it a representation of stylized feminine fertility goddess. An estrogen dominant female body?

### Michael — 2012-09-06T17:09:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-again.3408/post-49744>

That such a figure represents a deity seems intuitively plausible to me.

That it's the other thing ... Well, perhaps it takes a certain sort of lady anthropologist to come up with something like that. It's their notion of what would appeal to a man. Did they think to ask one?

Why would they need an image at all for that - unless their minds were very different from ours? And if they were really very different, then we're not going to be able to jump to immediate conclusions regarding the nature and purpose of their artifacts.

Assuming it's not sometimes just too difficult to carve the face, maybe it sometimes wasn't done because to do so would be to approach the tremendous mystery of things too closely.

The idols of some peoples, including the Celts and Old Norse, haven't recognizably human faces and are perhaps all the more numinous for that.

But that anthropologists of all people should think to look for possible ethnographic parallels rather than theorizing in vacuo!

That one seems implausible to me.

What's interesting to me about Dobson's hypothesis is that he claims to identify SEVERAL traits in the figures.

And that it possibly might reflect certain tendencies in some of the women of the age doesn't of course preclude it from being meant to represent a goddess.

### Glamazon — 2012-09-06T17:56:41+0300

A big concern about anthropology is that is is androcentric like much science. I think we have masculine viewpoint dominant opinion in all cultures. Asking a man what his masturbatory fantasy image is would be highly subjective. If man did not need an image for masturbation would there even be masturbation and the porn industry? Pocket art porn has been around for ages. What appeals to a man in this time period may not appeal to a man in a different time period. Even now that female body shape of the Venus statues is very attractive to men. Must we say that only one shape can be attractive to men?

Interbreeding did take place. Did Paleolithic man find Neanderthal body type attractive?

To say, "did they even think to ask one (man)" shows the inadequacies of science even now. I don't think male or female anthropologists or scientists in general ever thought in the past to think it is important to survey women for their opinions and consider how that might affect data and outcomes. That is slowly changing. Medical science has come to recognize relatively recently that disease process manifest differently in genders. Medication is processed differently. But, for a long time, modern medicine treated women like men in health care.

Per Dobson, I found this: "The famous Venus statues bear other physical features associated with cretinism: protruding bellies, large, sagging breasts, short trunks, curved spine and short stubby fingers. Critics of Dobson's work point out that some Neanderthal sites are near the sea, where people usually get enough iodine from fish and other sea creatures, and some physical traits of cretinism are not found in Neanderthals." [http://factsanddetails.com/world.php?itemid=1476&catid=56&subcatid=361](http://http://factsanddetails.com/world.php?itemid=1476&catid=56&subcatid=361)

Dobson is only looking at physical signs. No where does he indicate other symptoms such as neurological and cognitive impairment. I don't know how well a Neanderthal could hunt, create and use stone tools with cretinism. Hypothryroidism includes sluggishness, low energy, increased sensitivity to cold, muscle weakness, fatigue so I don't know how they could have survived during the Ice Age like they did. How did they carve those figurines?

Venus figurines started showing up 40k years ago. Neanderthals became extinct roughly 30k years ago but had been around since about 200k years. Why are there no older figurines from over 100k years ago. Altho the figurines do not show true steatopygia, steatopygia is considered a desirable feminine trait today and even in American hip hop culture.

Neanderthal body structure has been proposed to conserve body heat by minimizing body surface area with short limbs and a deep wide rib cage. This would protect the body organs within. The heavier, well muscled body would last longer in the cold than a longer limbed, athletic person. But this body would also need much more food. The body that the neanderthal had may have been what caused his demise. To maintain that kind of body structure required a too high of energy cost in an environment that was changing.

[http://www.bbc.co.uk/sn/tvradio/programmes/horizon/neanderthal\_prog\_summary.shtml](http://http://www.bbc.co.uk/sn/tvradio/programmes/horizon/neanderthal_prog_summary.shtml)

### Michael — 2012-09-06T23:19:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-again.3408/post-49849>

> **Glamazon;50385 wrote:** You zeroed in on "androcentric, judged my whole response, and didn't look at the rest of what I wrote?

How did you establish that?

Of course, I had begun by saying that what Dobson said seemed to me to be "very speculative stuff". But that got lost somewhere. Nevertheless, he has got something vaguely testable there. it would be equally satisfactory to me if someone were to show he's seeing what's not there as it would be if someone were to show that his four criteria are pretty much always present.

I have a great -- no, very great -- respect for Cunnane, but even he seems to be moving onto thin ice at this point in the book. His suggestion is that the figurines represent Neanderthals. I guess it's possible, though I don't see quite why Cro Magnons would want to carry around portable art featuring Neanderthals. But maybe they would.

I don't know. It's fascinating. I would tend to have thought of all Paleolithic diets as pretty close to optimal, and maybe they were. OTOH, I can now conceive that iodine-deficiency _may_ have been stalking us around for much of our historical, and even prehistoric past, like a ghost in the room.

Either way I prefer to think of people's having faces. I think anything else would freak me out.

Not exactly lifelike, but, again, maybe more otherworldly, more numinous, for just that reason:

[http://www.monaghan.ie/contentv3/media/monaghanie/content/images/museum/collections/celtic\_stone\_head\_1\_922\_lo\_res.jpg](http://http://www.monaghan.ie/contentv3/media/monaghanie/content/images/museum/collections/celtic_stone_head_1_922_lo_res.jpg)

Consider the figurines a goddess for women to pray to for protection like Tauret (Egyptian goddess who's carvings look very similar to the Donii goddess figurines only difference is the tools used in carving). Just a different perspective looking at the same issue When these people started looking for explanations as to why things happen they were searching out side their normal paradigms.

**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/falling-back-asleep-after-breakfast.3413/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### hope2conceive — 2012-09-02T18:33:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/falling-back-asleep-after-breakfast.3413/post-48968>

so I'm very early in my LRx, only day 5. I'm trying really hard to follow the circadian rhythm rules...well today, I woke up earlier than normal, and was famished. So I had breakfast earlier than normal (630am), within the 30 minutes of waking up....but afterwards I was still so tired, I fell back asleep for a couple hours. Is this bad? Will it screw up my circadian rhythm reset I'm trying to do?

I normally can NEVER go back to sleep in the morning...but maybe cause it's Sunday? I don't know.

I'm hoping someone can give me some feedback. Thanks much!

### Glamazon — 2012-09-02T20:36:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/falling-back-asleep-after-breakfast.3413/post-49004>

If your body needs sleep, get it. You can never sleep too much in this process. The reason you are tired is probably because you are running on yesterday's proteins that weren't autophaged during your sleep cycle. In most recent webinar, Dr. K. went into this in great detail. It is a must listen to for your journey to optimal. Also what is your most hormone panel, your vit D3 status, and your HS crp level. Other wise we are just peeing in the wind with narrowing down options.

For me, and this may not be you because I am have my own individual probs with my hormones and leaky gut, I slept constantly thru the day off and on for hours when I started the LRx. It took about a month before I quit doing so.

# Thread: When to get lab tests drawn
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-get-lab-tests-drawn.3420/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### popcornsally — 2012-09-03T00:10:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-get-lab-tests-drawn.3420/post-49062>

I'm planning on having lab tests drawn in the next couple of months for estradiol, progesterone, pregnenolone, testosterone free and total, DHEAs, cortisol AM, TSH, free T4 and free T3 (plus vit. b12, vit. d, CMP, CBC, and lipid panel). These are the tests my naturopathic doc is ordering. I did my own panel via mymedlab a couple months ago. I know the a.m. cortisol, TSH and some of the other things may not be that helpful, but as long as my insurance will pay for it, I'm not going to argue.

I am most interested in optimizing my cortisol, DHEA and sex hormones at this time. **My question is when is the REAL best time to draw the blood?** My doc said do it during 3rd week of menstrual cycle (43 y.o. pre-menopausal female). I've read here to do it on day 2-4 of cycle. Last fall my tests showed ALL hormones (including cortisol) were low. I am supplementing with pregnenolone and DHEA in addition to natural desiccated thyroid. Maybe I should do just a progesterone and estradiol level on day 2-4 and again with everything else around day 21? By the way, I will do a saliva adrenal stress index I ordered soon. Doctor sort of poo-poo'd that. Whatevs!

I don't really trust my doc to be in the driver's seat. She's nice and agreeable, but has not been very pro-active in helping me improve my health! I've figured out most of this stuff on my own by doing hours of reading on the internet and in books. It's so disappointing that my self-prescribed treatment has done much more for me than any doctor's ever has. I am a veterinarian so I do have some understanding of endocrinology. Just never thought I'd have to use it to treat myself! I feel sorry for people who don't have the gumption to do their own research and just continue to suffer.

Thanks for your help!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-03T03:13:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-get-lab-tests-drawn.3420/post-49086>

From my understanding, you want to draw the E and P and T (sex hormones) on day 21 of your cycle or day 3/4. I don't think this applies to DHEAs. Nor do I think it matters what day you do a thyroid panel or your vitamin levels or cholesterol. TSH is good to get as long as you get the free T3 and free T4. FYI, the cortisol and melatonin need to be saliva, and ideally a 4-panel test. I think that's what you mean - definitely get the melatonin not just the cortisol. Good luck.

### popcornsally — 2012-09-03T07:10:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-get-lab-tests-drawn.3420/post-49144>

Thanks, KiwiLauren. Yes, I do mean mainly the sex hormones. So you think it's either day 3/4 OR day 21? It seemed to me like it would be better to test estrogen early and progesterone later, but I'm not sure.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-03T04:03:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-other-trip-through-the-rabbit-hole-danielles-story.3422/post-49099>

She asked me to pull it because the cops ask them to pull it........so I led with the next blog.......Danielle Story about spending 24 hours with me......and MJ. I think you'll all like it. Please read her account. I think when I post BG series to follow.......it will flow great.
